## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Quality according to **Philip Crosby** and **Joseph Juran**. Which one do you think is more applicable to a software engineering process, and why?

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

- **Philip Crosby:** *"Quality is conformance to requirements."* The requirements must be clearly documented, and any deviation is a defect. Crosby believed defects are preventable and must be driven to zero.
- **Joseph Juran:** *"Quality is fitness for use."* The product or service must meet the customer's actual purpose in their real environment.
- **Applicability to Software:** Crosby's definition is **more directly applicable** to software engineering because software defects are largely conformance-based — code either meets the specification or it does not. Bugs are measured as deviations from the requirements document. Juran's definition, while important, is harder to measure objectively in software since "fitness for use" depends on evolving customer expectations.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the **Cost of Quality (COQ)**? List its **four components** with one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

The Cost of Quality is the total cost incurred to ensure and verify that a product meets quality requirements. It has four components:

1. **Prevention Costs** — e.g., employee training, quality engineering, design reviews.
2. **Appraisal Costs** — e.g., inspection, testing, supplier audits.
3. **Internal Failure Costs** — e.g., scrap, rework, machine downtime due to defects.
4. **External Failure Costs** — e.g., warranty claims, returns, product recalls, customer compensation.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** Discuss in detail the **various definitions of Quality** given by different quality gurus. Compare their views and explain which definition best fits a **modern manufacturing environment**. **(14 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a) Definitions of Quality by the Gurus (7 Marks):**

1. **Joseph M. Juran** — *"Quality is Fitness for Use."* Decomposed into five sub-qualities: design quality, conformance quality, availability, safety, and field use.
2. **Philip B. Crosby** — *"Quality is Conformance to Requirements."* Introduced the *Zero Defects* philosophy and the principle *Quality is Free* — the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure.
3. **W. Edwards Deming** — *"Quality should be aimed at the needs of the customer, present and future."* Deming argued 85% of quality problems are system-related; only 15% are worker-related. Proposed the **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle and the **14 Points for Management**.
4. **Armand V. Feigenbaum** — *"Quality is a way of managing the organization."* Pioneered **Total Quality Control (TQC)** — quality is the responsibility of every department, not just the QC department.
5. **Kaoru Ishikawa** — *"Quality is a continuous process of improvement."* Developed the **Fishbone Diagram** and championed **Quality Circles** in Japan.
6. **Genichi Taguchi** — *"Quality is the loss imparted to society from the time a product is shipped."* Any deviation from target causes loss, formalized by the **Quality Loss Function** $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$.

**(b) Comparison and Best Fit (7 Marks):**

| Guru | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Juran | Customer-centric, actionable | Difficult to measure objectively |
| Crosby | Clear, measurable via defects | Ignores subjective quality dimensions |
| Deming | Statistical, system-focused | Requires cultural change |
| Feigenbaum | Holistic, organization-wide | Implementation is complex |
| Ishikawa | Empowers workers | Quality circles can be slow |
| Taguchi | Quantifiable loss | Requires prior calibration of $k$ |

**Best Fit for Modern Manufacturing:** **Taguchi's definition** combined with **Deming's system thinking** is most appropriate. Modern Industry 4.0 manufacturing is highly data-driven — every deviation from target is captured by IoT sensors, and the Quality Loss Function directly quantifies that deviation in monetary terms. Deming's system thinking complements it by ensuring process improvements are sustained at the systemic level rather than at individual operator level.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** Explain the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** concept in detail. With a suitable numerical example, compute the **total COQ, COPQ, and Quality Ratio**, and suggest improvements. **(14 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a) Concept and Components of COQ (7 Marks):**

Cost of Quality is the total financial impact of quality-related activities — both those that ensure quality and those that result from lack of quality. It has four components grouped into two categories:

**Category 1 — Cost of Conformance:**
- **Prevention Costs (P):** Money spent before defects occur — training, quality planning, process design, supplier capability building.
- **Appraisal Costs (A):** Money spent to detect defects — inspection, testing, audits.

**Category 2 — Cost of Non-Conformance (COPQ):**
- **Internal Failure Costs (IF):** Costs of defects found before customer receipt — scrap, rework, re-inspection.
- **External Failure Costs (EF):** Costs of defects found after delivery — warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits, brand damage.

The mathematical relationships are:

$$
\text{COQ} = P + A + IF + EF
$$

$$
\text{COPQ} = IF + EF
$$

$$
\text{COGQ} = P + A
$$

**(b) Numerical Example and Improvement Suggestions (7 Marks):**

A KTU exam-favorite dataset — given for one quarter of production:

| Component | Symbol | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Training and Quality Engineering | P | 1,50,000 |
| Inspection and Testing | A | 2,50,000 |
| Rework and Scrap | IF | 6,00,000 |
| Warranty and Returns | EF | 10,00,000 |

**Step 1 — Compute COQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ} &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute COPQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ} &= IF + EF = 6{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Quality Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quality Ratio} &= \frac{P + A}{IF + EF} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000} = 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation and Recommendations:**

The Quality Ratio of 0.25 (≪ 1) shows the organization is heavily **failure-driven**. **Recommendations:**

1. **Increase Prevention investment by 50%** — invest in Six Sigma Green Belt training, robust supplier development, and **DFSS (Design for Six Sigma)**.
2. **Automate inspection** to reduce A without losing detection capability.
3. **Apply DMAIC** to the top three failure modes identified by **Pareto Analysis** of IF and EF.
4. **Target ratio ≥ 1** within 12 months — i.e., the cost of conformance should equal or exceed the cost of failure. This typically reduces COQ by 20–40%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Cost of Quality**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake:** Mixing up Prevention + Appraisal (conformance) with Internal + External Failure (non-conformance). Many students incorrectly place *rework* under Appraisal Costs. **Rework = Internal Failure**, NOT Appraisal.
> 2. **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to scale up correctly. **DPMO uses × 10⁶**, not × 10³. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 3. **Common Mistake:** Writing the Taguchi Loss Function as $L(x) = k(x - T)$ (linear). It is **quadratic — the square is essential**. Losing the squared term will cost full marks.
> 4. **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the 1.5σ shift when computing Sigma levels from DPMO. KTU explicitly tests this in the Dec 2023 paper.
> 5. **Common Mistake:** Listing gurus without year/contribution. Always pair the **Guru + Definition + Key Contribution** (e.g., *Ishikawa — Quality Circles — Fishbone Diagram*).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Quality Concepts and Definition: 30-Second Revision**
>
> - **Quality = Conformance to Requirements + Fitness for Use + Customer Satisfaction.** Multiple definitions are valid simultaneously.
> - **Three views of Quality:** User-based, Product-based, Manufacturing-based.
> - **Five Gurus to memorize by name and definition:** Juran, Crosby, Deming, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa (+ Taguchi as the modern sixth).
> - **Garvin's 8 Dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality (mnemonic: *PFRCDSAP*).
> - **SERVQUAL dimensions:** RATER — Reliability, Assurance, Tangibles, Empathy, Responsiveness.
> - **Cost of Quality = P + A + IF + EF**, where **COPQ = IF + EF** and **COGQ = P + A**.
> - **Quality Ratio = (P + A) / (IF + EF)** — a Six Sigma mature organization has ratio ≥ 1.
> - **Taguchi Loss Function:** $\,L(x) = k(x - T)^2\,$ — quadratic, symmetric about target.
> - **DPMO formula:** $\,\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^{6}\,$.
> - **Six Sigma target:** 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield.
> - **1.5σ shift** is always added in long-term Sigma calculations.
> - **Quality is free** (Crosby): prevention saves ₹₹₹₹ downstream — emphasize this in long answers.
> - **Cost ratio rule:** $1\text{ prevention} = 10\times\text{ appraisal} = 100\times\text{ internal failure} = 1000\times\text{ external failure}$.
> - **Juran's Quality Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement.
> - **Deming's PDCA cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (continuous loop).
> - **Ishikawa's Fishbone Diagram** identifies 6M causes: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
> - **Crosby's 5 Absolutes of Quality:** Quality = Conformance; Prevention not Inspection; Zero Defects performance standard; Measure quality by price of non-conformance; Quality is FREE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: History of Continuous Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** History of Continuous Improvement # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** is a systematic, ongoing effort to identify, analyse, and incrementally enhance products, services, and processes by eliminating non-value-adding activities, reducing variation, and improving quality, cost, and delivery performance. In the KTU 2024 Six Sigma syllabus, it represents the philosophical and operational foundation upon which methodologies such as **TQM**, **Lean**, and **Six Sigma (DMAIC/DMADV)** are constructed. The formal Japanese term **Kaizen (改善)** — meaning *"change for the better"* — was popularised by **Masaaki Imai** in his landmark 1986 book *"Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success"*. It embodies the principle that *small, continuous, incremental improvements*, compounded over time, yield transformational results. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Continuous Improvement is treated as the *historical and philosophical parent* of Six Sigma. Module 1 explicitly traces its evolution from pre-industrial craftsmanship to modern data-driven Six Sigma deployment, ensuring students understand *why* Six Sigma exists and *what problem it solves*. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation) > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Fitness Journey** > > Imagine a person who wants to become healthier. They don't go from sedentary to running a marathon in one day. Instead, they take small, consistent steps: > - **Day 1:** Walk 1 km > - **Week 2:** Walk 2 km > - **Month 3:** Jog 3 km > - **Year 1:** Run 10 km > > This is **Continuous Improvement** — *small, consistent, never-ending improvements*. > > Now imagine a *different* person who, after years of poor health, undergoes a single massive surgery. That's a **radical breakthrough** — important, but risky. > > **Six Sigma combines BOTH:** continuous incremental gains (Kaizen) **AND** breakthrough projects (DMAIC). Understanding this blend is central to Module 1. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Box > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Terminology (Memorise These):** > > | Term | Meaning | > |---|---| > | **Kaizen** | Japanese for "continuous improvement" | > | **QC** | Quality Control | > | **QA** | Quality Assurance | > | **SQC** | Statistical Quality Control | > | **TQM** | Total Quality Management | > | **PDCA** | Plan-Do-Check-Act (Deming Cycle) | > | **DMAIC** | Define-Measure-Analyse-Improve-Control | > | **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities | > | **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality | > | **BPO** | Business Process Orientation | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Evolutionary Eras of Continuous Improvement Continuous Improvement did not emerge overnight. Its history spans **over a century**, evolving through six distinct eras: ### Era 1 — Craftsmanship Era (Pre-1900) - Quality maintained by **individual artisans** through personal pride. - No formal systems; quality was tied to the worker's skill. - Apprentices learned through observation and practice. ### Era 2 — Inspection Era (1900–1930) - **Frederick W. Taylor** introduced scientific management (1911). - **Henry Ford's** moving assembly line (1913) demanded inspection at every stage. - Quality became a function of **separate inspection departments** — *reactive*, not preventive. ### Era 3 — Statistical Quality Control Era (1930–1950) - **Walter A. Shewhart** (Bell Labs, 1924) developed the **Control Chart** — the foundation of SQC. - Introduced the **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act). - **W. Edwards Deming** and **Joseph Juran** refined Shewhart's ideas and later took them to **post-war Japan** (1950s). ### Era 4 — Quality Assurance Era (1950–1980) - **Kaoru Ishikawa** developed the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** and **Quality Circles** in Japan. - **Armand V. Feigenbaum** coined **Total Quality Control (TQC)** in 1956. - **Joseph Juran** published the *Juran Trilogy* — Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement. - **Genichi Taguchi** introduced **Taguchi Loss Function** (quality loss begins the moment a product deviates from target, even within specs). - **Philip B. Crosby** popularised **"Quality is Free"** (1979) and *Zero Defects*. ### Era 5 — Strategic Quality / TQM Era (1980–1992) - **Total Quality Management (TQM)** emerged as a **company-wide strategic philosophy**. - **W. Edwards Deming's 14 Points for Management** (1982) became the most cited management framework. - US Baldrige Award (1987) and European EFQM Excellence Model (1988) institutionalised quality. - **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — Taiichi Ohno's *Lean* principles — emphasised waste elimination. ### Era 6 — Six Sigma Era (1986–Present) - **Motorola**, under **Bob Galvin**, developed Six Sigma in **1986**. - **Bill Smith** (Motorola engineer) and **Mikel Harry** (founder of Six Sigma Academy) formalised the methodology. - **Jack Welch** made Six Sigma a corporate-wide initiative at **General Electric (1995)** — generating an estimated **$12 billion in savings** over five years. - **Lean Six Sigma** (late 1990s) merged Six Sigma's data discipline with Lean's speed. - Today, Six Sigma is integrated with **AI, Industry 4.0, and Digital Transformation**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet Although "History" is conceptual, the *context* for Module 1 includes foundational Six Sigma metrics. These formulas will appear across all modules: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Essential Six Sigma Formulas (Carry Forward for All Modules)** $$ \text{DPU} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units}} $$ $$ \text{DPO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Number of Opportunities per Unit}} $$ $$ \text{DPMO} = \text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000 $$ $$ \text{First-Time Yield (FTY)} = \frac{\text{Units without Defects}}{\text{Total Units Processed}} $$ $$ \text{Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FTY}_i $$ $$ \text{Defect Rate (\%)} = \left(1 - \text{Yield}\right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Reference (Yield to Sigma Level):** > - Yield ≈ 99.9997 % ⇒ **6 Sigma** (3.4 DPMO) > - Yield ≈ 99.999 % ⇒ **5 Sigma** (233 DPMO) > - Yield ≈ 99.99 % ⇒ **4 Sigma** (6,210 DPMO) > - Yield ≈ 99.9 % ⇒ **3 Sigma** (66,807 DPMO) ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Continuous Improvement | |---|---| | **Manufacturing** | Reducing weld defects, lowering scrap rates, improving OEE | | **Software** | DevOps CI/CD pipelines, defect reduction in releases | | **Healthcare** | Reducing hospital-acquired infections, wait-time optimisation | | **Banking/Finance** | Reducing loan-processing errors, fraud detection | | **Civil Engineering** | Concrete quality consistency, project schedule adherence | | **Electronics** | PCB soldering defect reduction, yield improvement in fabs | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The Gurus and Their Contributions > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board-Favourite Table** — Examiners frequently award 7–10 marks for a clean comparative table of quality gurus. Memorise the **Year + Contribution + Country** mapping. | Guru | Period | Country | Key Contribution | KTU 2024 Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Walter A. Shewhart** | 1924–1930 | USA | Control Charts, PDCA origin | Father of Statistical Quality Control | | **W. Edwards Deming** | 1950–1993 | USA → Japan | 14 Points, PDCA popularisation, System of Profound Knowledge | Bridge between US/Japan quality | | **Joseph M. Juran** | 1951–2000 | USA → Japan | Juran Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) | Pioneer of quality as a business function | | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1962–1989 | Japan | Fishbone Diagram, Quality Circles | Bottom-up employee involvement | | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1956–1990 | USA | Total Quality Control (TQC) | *Quality is everyone's job* | | **Genichi Taguchi** | 1960–1995 | Japan | Loss Function, Robust Design | Quality loss beyond specs | | **Philip B. Crosby** | 1979–2001 | USA | Zero Defects, Quality is Free, 4 Absolutes | Cultural transformation | | **Taiichi Ohno** | 1978–1988 | Japan | Toyota Production System (Lean) | Waste (Muda) elimination | | **Masaaki Imai** | 1986 | Japan | Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success | Coined *Kaizen* globally | | **Bill Smith & Mikel Harry** | 1986 | USA (Motorola) | Six Sigma methodology, DMAIC | Originators of Six Sigma | | **Jack Welch** | 1995–2001 | USA (GE) | GE Six Sigma rollout | Made Six Sigma mainstream corporate strategy | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — DPMO to Process Sigma > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example for 14-Mark Question Type:** > A manufacturing line produces **5,000 units/day** over **20 working days**. Each unit has **15 opportunities for defects**. A total of **1,800 defects** are recorded in the audit log. Compute the **DPMO** and the **process Sigma level**. ### Step 1 — Compute Total Units Produced $$ \text{Total Units} = 5{,}000 \times 20 = 100{,}000 \text{ units} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Total Opportunities $$ \text{Total Opportunities} = 100{,}000 \times 15 = 1{,}500{,}000 $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the DPMO Formula $$ \text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Opportunities}} \times 1{,}000{,}000 $$ $$ \text{DPMO} = \frac{1{,}800}{1{,}500{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000 $$ $$ \text{DPMO} = 1.2 \times 1{,}000 = 1{,}200 \text{ DPMO} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert DPMO to Yield $$ \text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1 - \frac{1{,}200}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.9988 = 99.88\% $$ ### Step 5 — Map Yield to Process Sigma Using the standard Motorola Sigma Conversion Table (1.5 sigma shift assumed): $$ \text{Yield} \approx 99.88\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Process Sigma Level} \approx 4.4 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A process operating at **4.4 Sigma** is competent but below Six Sigma's gold standard of 6σ. Continuous Improvement projects (DMAIC) would target the *gap* between 4.4σ and 6σ. ## 3.3 The Juran Trilogy — Conceptual Derivation > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield for 14-Mark Theory Questions** The Juran Trilogy describes quality management as three interconnected processes: $$ \text{Quality Management} = f(\text{Quality Planning},\; \text{Quality Control},\; \text{Quality Improvement}) $$ ### 3.3.1 Quality Planning - Identify **customers** and their needs. - Develop **product features** that respond to those needs. - Establish **quality goals** and design **processes** capable of meeting them. ### 3.3.2 Quality Control - Evaluate **actual performance** vs. **quality goals**. - Compare measurements to specifications. - Act on the **difference** (corrective action when out of control). ### 3.3.3 Quality Improvement - Establish **infrastructure** (councils, teams, training, measures). - Identify **specific improvement projects**. - Establish **project teams**, provide **resources**, and **motivate** them. The trilogy forms a *closed loop*, much like the Deming PDCA cycle, but at a strategic level. ## 3.4 Deming's 14 Points — Condensed Framework > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** List *any 5* of Deming's 14 Points for a clean 5-mark answer. 1. Create **constancy of purpose** for improvement. 2. Adopt the **new philosophy**. 3. Cease dependence on **inspection to achieve quality**. 4. End the practice of awarding business on **price alone**. 5. Improve **constantly every process**. 6. Institute **training on the job**. 7. Institute **leadership**. 8. Drive out **fear**. 9. Break down **barriers between departments**. 10. Eliminate **slogans and exhortations**. 11. Eliminate **numerical quotas**. 12. Remove **barriers to pride of workmanship**. 13. Institute a **vigorous programme of education and self-improvement**. 14. Put **everyone to work** to accomplish the transformation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Timeline of Continuous Improvement (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Craft Era
Pre-1900
Artisan Pride"] --> A2["Inspection Era
1900-1930
Taylor, Ford"] A2 --> A3["SQC Era
1930-1950
Shewhart, Deming, Juran"] A3 --> A4["QA Era
1950-1980
Ishikawa, Feigenbaum, Taguchi, Crosby"] A4 --> A5["TQM Era
1980-1992
Deming 14 Points, Baldrige, TPS"] A5 --> A6["Six Sigma Era
1986-Present
Motorola, GE, Lean Six Sigma"] A6 --> A7["AI Six Sigma
2020-Future
Industry 4.0, Digital Quality"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Influence Map of Quality Gurus ```mermaid flowchart TB Shewhart["Walter Shewhart
Control Charts + PDCA"] --> Deming["W Edwards Deming
14 Points + System"] Shewhart --> Juran["Joseph Juran
Juran Trilogy"] Deming --> Ishikawa["Kaoru Ishikawa
Fishbone + Quality Circles"] Juran --> Feigenbaum["Armand Feigenbaum
Total Quality Control"] Ishikawa --> Imai["Masaaki Imai
Kaizen Philosophy"] Juran --> Taguchi["Genichi Taguchi
Loss Function"] Feigenbaum --> Crosby["Philip Crosby
Zero Defects"] Imai --> Ohno["Taiichi Ohno
Toyota Lean System"] Deming --> Harry["Bill Smith and Mikel Harry
Six Sigma at Motorola"] Taguchi --> Harry Ohno --> Harry Harry --> Welch["Jack Welch
GE Six Sigma Rollout 1995"] Welch --> LSix["Lean Six Sigma
Late 1990s"] LSix --> AI["AI Powered Six Sigma
2020s Industry 4.0"] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Six Sigma DMAIC as the Operational Outcome of Continuous Improvement History ```mermaid flowchart LR D["DEFINE
Problem Voice of Customer Project Charter"] --> M["MEASURE
Baseline CTQ Data Plan Capability"] M --> A["ANALYSE
Root Cause Hypothesis Tests"] A --> I["IMPROVE
Pilot Solutions Implementation"] I --> C["CONTROL
Standardisation Mistake Proofing"] C -.->|"Continuous Loop"| D ``` > [!TIP] > **How to Read This Diagram:** > The DMAIC cycle itself is the *operational manifestation* of 100+ years of continuous improvement history. The Control (C) phase loops back to Define (D) — this is the **DNA of Kaizen** embedded inside Six Sigma. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Define Continuous Improvement and explain the term Kaizen in the context of Six Sigma.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - (1 mark) **Continuous Improvement** is the ongoing, incremental enhancement of products, services, and processes by eliminating waste, reducing variation, and improving quality. - (1 mark) **Kaizen** is the Japanese term (改善) meaning *"change for the better"*, popularised by **Masaaki Imai (1986)**. - (1 mark) In Six Sigma, Kaizen philosophy underpins the *Control* phase of DMAIC and enables *breakthrough* gains when combined with *Kaizen Events*. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **List any three quality gurus and their key contributions.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **W. Edwards Deming** — 14 Points for Management, PDCA cycle, System of Profound Knowledge. 2. **Joseph Juran** — Juran Trilogy (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement). 3. **Philip B. Crosby** — *Quality is Free* and the concept of *Zero Defects*. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Discuss the evolution of Continuous Improvement from the Inspection Era to the Six Sigma Era. Highlight at least four quality gurus and their key contributions. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Inspection Era (1900–1930):** - Frederick Taylor's *Scientific Management* (1911) and Henry Ford's assembly line (1913) introduced the concept of separate inspection departments. - Quality was *reactive* — defects were identified after production, not prevented. **2. Statistical Quality Control Era (1930–1950):** - **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)** developed the *Control Chart* at Bell Labs — the foundation of modern SQC. - **W. Edwards Deming** and **Joseph Juran** taught SQC to Japanese engineers after WWII, planting the seeds of Japanese quality excellence. **3. Quality Assurance Era (1950–1980):** - **Kaoru Ishikawa** created the *Fishbone Diagram* and popularised *Quality Circles* (1962). - **Armand V. Feigenbaum** introduced *Total Quality Control (TQC)* in 1956 — the idea that quality is everyone's responsibility. - **Genichi Taguchi** developed the *Loss Function*, arguing that quality loss begins the moment a product deviates from its target. - **Philip B. Crosby** published *Quality is Free* (1979) and the *Zero Defects* philosophy. **4. TQM Era (1980–1992):** - Deming's *14 Points* (1982) became a global reference. - The **Baldrige Award** (1987, USA) and **EFQM Model** (1988, Europe) institutionalised quality excellence. - **Toyota Production System (TPS)** by Taiichi Ohno introduced *Lean* principles — eliminating the 7 Wastes (*Muda*). **5. Six Sigma Era (1986–Present):** - **Motorola** (Bob Galvin) launched Six Sigma in **1986**. - **Bill Smith** and **Mikel Harry** developed the DMAIC framework. - **Jack Welch** adopted Six Sigma at **GE (1995)**, generating over $12 billion in savings. - **Lean Six Sigma** integrated speed (Lean) with accuracy (Six Sigma) in the late 1990s. **(b)** A process produces **8,000 units/day** for **25 days**. Each unit has **10 defect opportunities**. During the audit, **3,000 defects** were found. Calculate **DPMO, Yield %, and Process Sigma Level**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Total Units** $$ \text{Total Units} = 8{,}000 \times 25 = 200{,}000 $$ **Step 2: Total Opportunities** $$ \text{Total Opportunities} = 200{,}000 \times 10 = 2{,}000{,}000 $$ **Step 3: DPMO** $$ \text{DPMO} = \frac{3{,}000}{2{,}000{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}500 $$ **Step 4: Yield** $$ \text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{1{,}500}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.9985 = 99.85\% $$ **Step 5: Process Sigma Level** Using Motorola's 1.5σ shift table: $$ \text{Process Sigma} \approx 4.4\sigma $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes (Valuation Pitfalls):** > 1. **Unit mistake:** Forgetting to multiply *days × units/day* before computing DPMO. Loss: 2 marks. > 2. **Multiplier error:** Using 100,000 instead of 1,000,000 in DPMO formula. Loss: 1 mark. > 3. **Sigma table omission:** Writing DPMO without the final *Process Sigma* answer. Loss: 1 mark. > 4. **No units shown:** Examiner may deduct 0.5–1 mark for *missing units* in intermediate steps. ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Explain the **Juran Trilogy** of quality management with a neat diagram. Discuss how it applies to a Six Sigma project. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The Juran Trilogy consists of three universal processes applicable to *any* industry: **1. Quality Planning:** - Identify customers (internal & external). - Determine customer needs (translated to CTQs in Six Sigma). - Develop product/process features to meet those needs. - Establish quality goals. **2. Quality Control:** - Evaluate actual performance against quality goals. - Compare actual vs. planned (this is the *Measure* phase of DMAIC). - Take corrective action on the difference. **3. Quality Improvement:** - Build the infrastructure (Six Sigma project teams, belts). - Identify specific improvement projects. - Assign teams, give resources, and motivate. **Application to Six Sigma Project:** - **Quality Planning ↔ Define + Measure** phases. - **Quality Control ↔ Control** phase. - **Quality Improvement ↔ Analyse + Improve** phases. Thus, Juran's framework provides the *strategic envelope* inside which DMAIC operates. **(b)** Differentiate between **TQM, Six Sigma, and Lean Six Sigma** in a comparative table. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **TQM** | **Six Sigma** | **Lean Six Sigma** | |---|---|---|---| | **Origin** | USA / Japan (1980s) | Motorola (1986) | Late 1990s (SS + Lean) | | **Focus** | Customer satisfaction, culture | Defect reduction, variation | Speed + defect reduction | | **Tools** | 7 QC Tools, Pareto, Fishbone | DMAIC, DPMO, Regression | DMAIC + Value Stream Mapping, 5S, Kanban | | **Approach** | Cultural, organisation-wide | Data-driven, project-based | Hybrid: data + flow | | **Metric** | Customer satisfaction index | Sigma level, DPMO | Takt time, RTY, Sigma | | **Belt System** | No formal belts | Green, Black, Master Black | Same as Six Sigma | | **Strength** | Long-term culture change | Statistical rigour | Speed + accuracy | | **Limitation** | Difficult to sustain without metrics | Resource-intensive training | Complexity of dual framework | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Continuous Improvement** = ongoing, incremental, never-ending improvement. - **Kaizen** (改善) = Japanese for "change for the better", coined by **Masaaki Imai (1986)**. - **Shewhart** = Father of SQC; invented the **Control Chart (1924)**. - **Deming** = 14 Points, PDCA cycle, post-war Japanese quality revolution. - **Juran** = Juran Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement). - **Ishikawa** = Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram, Quality Circles. - **Feigenbaum** = Total Quality Control (TQC) — *"Quality is everyone's job."* - **Taguchi** = Loss Function — quality loss begins at any deviation. - **Crosby** = *Quality is Free*, Zero Defects. - **Taiichi Ohno** = Toyota Production System (Lean), 7 Wastes (*Muda*). - **Bill Smith + Mikel Harry** = Six Sigma at **Motorola (1986)**. - **Jack Welch** = Made Six Sigma a corporate strategy at **GE (1995)**. - **DMAIC** = Define → Measure → Analyse → Improve → Control. - **DPMO formula:** `(Defects ÷ Opportunities) × 10^6`. - **6-Sigma benchmark:** 3.4 DPMO, 99.9997 % yield. - **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs. - **Lean Six Sigma** = Lean's speed + Six Sigma's accuracy (late 1990s). - **Modern era:** AI + Six Sigma + Industry 4.0. > [!TIP] > **One-Line Board Answer (Write this if nothing else is remembered):** > *"Continuous Improvement evolved from Shewhart's Statistical Quality Control through Deming, Juran, and Ishikawa's philosophies into TQM, and finally crystallised as Six Sigma at Motorola in 1986 — providing the data-driven DMAIC framework that defines modern quality management."*

topics.title: Six Sigma Principles and Focus Areas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma Principles and Focus Areas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Six Sigma? — Formal Definition

**Six Sigma** is a rigorous, data-driven methodology and disciplined statistical quality improvement philosophy aimed at eliminating defects, reducing process variation, and driving sustainable, near-perfect operational performance. Formally, Six Sigma is a measure of process quality that represents a defect rate of **no more than 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.

It was originally pioneered at **Motorola** in 1986 by engineer **Bill Smith** under the leadership of CEO **Bob Galvin**, and later popularized globally by **General Electric (Jack Welch, 1995)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim spirit):**
> *Six Sigma is a statistical measure expressing how far a given process deviates from perfection — a "Six Sigma process" is one in which 99.99966% of the products manufactured are statistically expected to be free of defects (3.4 DPMO).*

## 1.2 Origin & Evolution

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1986** | Bill Smith introduces Six Sigma at Motorola |
| **1987** | Motorola formally launches the "Six Sigma Quality Program" |
| **1995** | Jack Welch adopts Six Sigma at General Electric — global expansion |
| **2000s** | Adoption across manufacturing, IT, BPO, healthcare, finance |
| **2010s+** | Integration with **Lean** → **Lean Six Sigma** (LSS) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Archery Target" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine an archer shooting at a target.**
> - **A 1-Sigma archer** hits the bullseye only **31%** of the time — most arrows scatter.
> - **A 3-Sigma archer** hits **93.3%** of the time — good, but not great.
> - **A 6-Sigma archer** hits **99.99966%** of the time — virtually every arrow is on target.
>
> In business terms, Six Sigma asks: *"If you produced 1 million mobile phones, fewer than 4 would be defective."* That's the level of excellence Six Sigma demands.

## 1.4 The Two-Dimensional Nature of Six Sigma

Six Sigma is simultaneously:
1. **A Statistical Metric** — measuring process capability in **sigma (σ)** units.
2. **A Management Philosophy** — driving business strategy through structured problem-solving.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Defect** | Any non-conformity that fails to meet Customer's Critical-To-Quality (CTQ) requirements |
| **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities — the core Six Sigma metric |
| **CTQ** | Critical-To-Quality — measurable characteristics most important to the customer |
| **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality — money lost due to defects, rework, and warranty claims |
| **DMAIC** | Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control (the core improvement cycle) |
| **SIPOC** | Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers (process scoping tool) |
| **VOC** | Voice of the Customer — the customer's stated and unstated needs |
| **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Major Pillars — *Principles* vs. *Focus Areas*

Many students confuse "Principles" with "Focus Areas." KTU specifically tests this distinction.

### 2.1.1 The **Principles** of Six Sigma (The "How we think")

These are the guiding beliefs — the philosophical core:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield: The 7 Principles of Six Sigma (commit to memory)**
>
> 1. **Always Focus on the Customer** — every action must create customer value.
> 2. **Data- and Fact-Driven Management** — decisions are based on statistics, not intuition.
> 3. **Process Focus, Not Blame** — 85% of problems are process-related, not people-related (Deming's 85/15 rule).
> 4. **Proactive Management** — anticipate and prevent defects, not just detect them.
> 5. **Boundaryless Collaboration** — break down silos between departments.
> 6. **Aim for Perfection; Tolerate Failure** — perfection is the target; mistakes during the journey are learning opportunities ("Fail Forward").
> 7. **Driving for Sustained, Breakthrough Change** — incrementalism is insufficient; transformational change is required.

### 2.1.2 The **Focus Areas** of Six Sigma (The "Where we apply effort")

The **Five Key Focus Areas** form the operational blueprint:

| # | Focus Area | Core Question Answered |
|---|-----------|------------------------|
| **1** | **Customer Focus** | What does the customer value? (VOC → CTQ translation) |
| **2** | **Process Focus** | Which process generates the defect? (SIPOC mapping) |
| **3** | **Data-Driven Decisions** | What do the numbers prove? (Statistical validation) |
| **4** | **Teamwork & Leadership** | Who owns the improvement? (Belts, champions, sponsors) |
| **5** | **Strategic Alignment** | How does this tie to business goals? (Project selection) |

## 2.2 DMAIC — The Foundational Roadmap

DMAIC is the structured, statistical improvement cycle for **existing processes**:

| Phase | Purpose | Key Tools |
|-------|---------|-----------|
| **D – Define** | Identify the problem, customer, and project scope | Project Charter, VOC, SIPOC, COPQ |
| **M – Measure** | Quantify the current process performance | Data collection plan, Process mapping, MSA, Capability indices (Cp, Cpk) |
| **A – Analyze** | Identify the root causes of defects | Pareto, Fishbone (Ishikawa), 5-Whys, Hypothesis testing, Regression |
| **I – Improve** | Implement solutions to address root causes | DOE, Pilot testing, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) |
| **C – Control** | Sustain the improvements | Control charts, SOPs, FMEA, Standardization, Response plans |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse DMAIC with DMADV.** DMADV is used for **designing new processes/products** — *Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify*. DMAIC is for *improving existing* processes. KTU exams love this distinction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | defects/M-opp |
| 2 | $$\text{First Pass Yield (FPY)} = \frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$$ | Fraction of units passing without rework | dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | $$\text{Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$$ | Cumulative yield across $n$ process steps | dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $$\text{Process Yield} = (1 - \text{DPMO}/1{,}000{,}000) \times 100\%$$ | Percentage of defect-free units | % |
| 5 | $$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$ | Sample standard deviation | variable units |
| 6 | $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$ | Process capability (spread only) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $$C_{pk} = \min\left\{\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right\}$$ | Process capability (centered) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $$\text{Short-Term Yield} = \Phi(\text{USL}; \mu, \sigma) - \Phi(\text{LSL}; \mu, \sigma)$$ | Probability within specification | probability |
| 9 | $$\text{Defect Rate (Short-Term)} = 1 - \text{Yield}_{\text{ST}}$$ | Proportion of defects (raw) | proportion |
| 10 | $$\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$$ | Cost of Poor Quality | currency |
| 11 | $$\text{Z-Score} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$ | Standardized distance from mean | dimensionless |
| 12 | $$\text{Six Sigma Level} \equiv 3.4 \text{ DPMO (with } 1.5\sigma \text{ shift)}$$ | Master definition | defects/M-opp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Numericals:** The famous **3.4 DPMO** at Six Sigma includes Motorola's **1.5-sigma long-term shift** convention. Statistically, a true 6σ process has **0.002 DPMO**, but Motorola accounted for real-world drift and set 3.4 as the operational benchmark. **Both numbers (3.4 and 0.002) may appear in exam questions** — read the question carefully.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Six Sigma

Six Sigma is applied across industries:
- **Manufacturing** (Motorola, GE, Boeing) — defect reduction in assembly lines
- **Healthcare** (Mount Sinai, Mayo Clinic) — reducing surgical errors
- **Banking/Finance** (JPMorgan Chase) — minimizing loan processing errors
- **IT/Software** (Accenture, TCS) — defect reduction in software delivery (CMMI integration)
- **Hospitality** (Starwood Hotels) — improving service quality

## 2.5 The Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) — Hidden Gold Mine

COPQ is the *price of doing things wrong*. It is divided into four categories:

```
COPQ = Prevention Cost + Appraisal Cost + Internal Failure + External Failure
        ↑ Investing to      ↑ Detecting       ↑ Defects found    ↑ Defects reaching
          prevent defects     defects           before delivery    the customer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Prevention and Appraisal costs *increase* as Six Sigma matures, while Internal/External Failure costs *decrease* dramatically. The net effect is a steep drop in total COPQ — a key selling point for Six Sigma to top management.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Defect Data to Six Sigma Level

This is the **most important KTU numerical pattern** for Module 1. Let us work through it exhaustively.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A manufacturing line produces **5,000 units per day** of a particular component. Each unit has **8 opportunities** to be defective. Over a 7-day period, **196 defects** are recorded. **Determine the DPMO and the corresponding Sigma Level.**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities Generated**

The total opportunities (O) over the 7-day period are calculated by multiplying the units produced per day by the number of opportunities per unit, then by the number of days.

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= \text{Units per day} \times \text{Opportunities per unit} \times \text{Number of days} \\[4pt]
O &= 5{,}000 \times 8 \times 7 \\[4pt]
O &= 280{,}000 \text{ total opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Defects Observed**

We are given directly that $D = 196$ defects. This is a single observed count — no further derivation needed.

**Step 3 — Compute DPMO**

Substitute the defect count and total opportunities into the DPMO formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{O} \\[4pt]
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{196 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{280{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{196{,}000{,}000}{280{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{DPMO} &= 700 \text{ defects per million opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert DPMO to Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{Yield} &= 1 - \frac{700}{1{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{Yield} &= 1 - 0.0007 \\[4pt]
\text{Yield} &= 0.9993 = 99.93\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert Yield to a Z-Score (Sigma Level)**

We need to find the number of standard deviations ($\sigma$) from the mean to the nearest specification limit. Since this is a one-sided problem (defects on the bad side), we look up the yield in the **standard normal cumulative distribution table**.

For a yield of 0.9993 (or 99.93%), the area under the right tail is $1 - 0.9993 = 0.0007$. The Z-score corresponding to a tail area of 0.0007 is approximately **$Z = 3.0$**.

In the Six Sigma convention, we **add the long-term shift of 1.5** to obtain the reported sigma level:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sigma Level} &= Z_{\text{short-term}} + 1.5 \\[4pt]
\text{Sigma Level} &= 3.0 + 1.5 \\[4pt]
\text{Sigma Level} &= 4.5\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the **+1.5σ shift** when reporting the final sigma level. Without the shift, your answer is *statistically correct but operationally incomplete* in Six Sigma terminology. Always include it.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sigma Levels at a Glance

This matrix is **critical** for KTU Part A (3-mark) questions and is frequently asked.

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) | Defects per 1 Million | Relative Cost |
|:-----------:|:----:|:---------:|:---------------------:|:-------------:|
| 1σ | 691,462 | 30.85% | 691,462 | Very High |
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.15% | 308,537 | High |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32% | 66,807 | Moderate |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% | 6,210 | Low |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977% | 233 | Very Low |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** | **3.4** | **Near Zero** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** *"From 3 to 6, defects drop by 4 zeros."*
> - 3σ → ~66,807 DPMO
> - 6σ → 3.4 DPMO
> - A jump of **5 orders of magnitude** in quality.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison — Principles vs. Focus Areas vs. DMAIC

This three-way distinction is **a guaranteed KTU exam question**.

| Dimension | Principles | Focus Areas | DMAIC |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| **What it is** | Belief system | Operational priorities | Execution roadmap |
| **Count** | 7 principles | 5 focus areas | 5 phases |
| **Question** | "How do we think?" | "Where do we focus?" | "What do we do?" |
| **Audience** | Executives & culture | Strategy team | Project practitioners |
| **Example** | Data-driven decisions | Process Focus | Analyze phase root-cause tools |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

A process has four sequential steps with First Pass Yields of 95%, 92%, 98%, and 99%. **Find the overall RTY.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \text{FPY}_3 \times \text{FPY}_4 \\[4pt]
\text{RTY} &= 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \times 0.99 \\[4pt]
\text{RTY} &= 0.8468 \\[4pt]
\text{RTY} &= 84.68\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Although each step has >92% yield, the cumulative process yields only 84.68%. This demonstrates the **multiplicative nature of process steps** — even small drops compound, and it is the *system*, not the individual step, that matters in Six Sigma.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six Sigma DMAIC Flow Architecture

This diagram captures the iterative, project-driven DMAIC cycle with embedded tools.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Business Problem / CTQ] --> B[DEFINE: Project Charter, VOC, SIPOC, COPQ]
    B --> C[MEASURE: Data Plan, MSA, Cp/Cpk, Baseline Sigma]
    C --> D[ANALYZE: Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Whys, Hypothesis Test]
    D --> E[IMPROVE: DOE, Pilot, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke]
    E --> F[CONTROL: Control Charts, SOPs, FMEA, Standardize]
    F --> G{Goal Achieved?}
    G -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[END: Sustained Improvement, Cost Savings]
```

## 4.2 The Five Focus Areas — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STRATEGY[STRATEGIC LAYER]
        A1[Strategic Alignment] --> A2[Project Selection: COPQ-driven]
    end

    subgraph CUSTOMER[CUSTOMER LAYER]
        B1[Customer Focus] --> B2[VOC to CTQ Translation]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[PROCESS LAYER]
        C1[Process Focus] --> C2[SIPOC and Process Mapping]
    end

    subgraph DATA[DATA LAYER]
        D1[Data-Driven Decisions] --> D2[Statistical Tools: Cp, Cpk, DOE]
    end

    subgraph PEOPLE[PEOPLE LAYER]
        E1[Teamwork and Leadership] --> E2[Champions, MBB, BB, GB]
    end

    STRATEGY --> CUSTOMER --> PROCESS --> DATA --> PEOPLE
```

## 4.3 Six Sigma Principles — Foundational Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[1. Customer Focus] --> P2[2. Data and Fact Driven Management]
    P2 --> P3[3. Process Focus not Blame]
    P3 --> P4[4. Proactive Management]
    P4 --> P5[5. Boundaryless Collaboration]
    P5 --> P6[6. Aim Perfection Tolerate Failure]
    P6 --> P7[7. Sustained Breakthrough Change]
    P7 --> OUT[Six Sigma Excellence]
```

## 4.4 Six Sigma Belts — Organizational Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXEC[Executive Leadership: CEO and COO] --> CHAMP[Champions: Project Selectors and Sponsors]
    CHAMP --> MBB[Master Black Belt: Senior Mentor and Strategist]
    MBB --> BB[Black Belt: Full-time Project Leader]
    BB --> GB[Green Belt: Part-time Team Member]
    GB --> YB[Yellow Belt: Awareness Level Participants]
```

## 4.5 DMAIC vs. DMADV — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Process exists?] -->|Yes| Q2{Meets customer spec?}
    Q2 -->|No| A[DMAIC: Improve Existing Process]
    Q2 -->|Yes| B[Re-evaluate need]
    Q1 -->|No| C[DMADV: Design New Process]
    C --> D1[Define] --> D2[Measure] --> D3[Analyze] --> D4[Design] --> D5[Verify]
    A --> P1[Define] --> P2[Measure] --> P3[Analyze] --> P4[Improve] --> P5[Control]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define Six Sigma. Explain the significance of "3.4 defects per million opportunities" (DPMO) in quality management.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Six Sigma is a disciplined, data-driven quality management methodology that aims to reduce process variation and eliminate defects to a near-zero level. The metric **3.4 DPMO** represents the maximum allowable defect rate in a Six Sigma process — meaning for every one million opportunities, no more than 3.4 defects occur. This translates to a process yield of **99.99966%**. The figure 3.4 includes Motorola's **1.5-sigma long-term drift** to account for real-world process variation over time. The significance lies in driving near-perfect operational excellence, drastically reducing waste, lowering the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**, and improving customer satisfaction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students who state *"Six Sigma means zero defects"* lose a mark. The correct number is **3.4 DPMO**, and you must mention the **1.5σ shift** to earn full credit.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**List and briefly explain the five focus areas of Six Sigma.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The five focus areas of Six Sigma are:

1. **Customer Focus** — Identifying Voice of the Customer (VOC) and translating it into Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) requirements.
2. **Process Focus** — Mapping and improving the actual process flow (using SIPOC) rather than blaming individuals.
3. **Data-Driven Decisions** — Using statistical tools (Cp, Cpk, regression, DOE) to make objective decisions.
4. **Teamwork and Leadership** — Engaging Champions, Black Belts, Green Belts, and Yellow Belts for project execution.
5. **Strategic Alignment** — Ensuring every Six Sigma project aligns with business goals and reduces COPQ.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **DMAIC methodology** of Six Sigma. List the tools used in each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A process produces 4,200 units per shift. Each unit has 5 opportunities for defects. Over 5 shifts, 210 defects are observed. **Calculate the DPMO, process yield, and the corresponding Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift).** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — DMAIC Methodology

**Define (1.5 Marks):**
The Define phase identifies the problem, scope, customer requirements (VOC), and project goals. Outputs include the **Project Charter**, **SIPOC diagram**, **VOC → CTQ translation**, and a preliminary **COPQ analysis**. Tools: Project Charter template, SIPOC, Gantt chart, VOC surveys.

**Measure (1.5 Marks):**
The Measure phase quantifies the current process performance and establishes a baseline. It involves data collection planning, **Measurement System Analysis (MSA)**, and calculation of process capability indices (**Cp, Cpk**). Tools: Data collection plan, Gage R&R, histograms, control charts.

**Analyze (1.5 Marks):**
The Analyze phase identifies the **root causes** of defects and process variation. Tools include the **Pareto chart** (80/20 rule), **Ishikawa fishbone diagram**, **5-Whys analysis**, **scatter plots**, and **hypothesis testing** (t-test, chi-square, ANOVA).

**Improve (1.5 Marks):**
The Improve phase develops, tests, and implements solutions that address the root causes. Tools include **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, **pilot studies**, **Kaizen events**, and **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)**.

**Control (1 Mark):**
The Control phase ensures that improvements are sustained over time. Tools include **control charts** (X-bar, R, p, c), **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**, **FMEA**, and **response plans** for out-of-control conditions.

#### Model Solution (b) — DPMO & Sigma Level Calculation

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= 4{,}200 \times 5 \times 5 \\[4pt]
O &= 105{,}000 \text{ total opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DPMO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{210 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{105{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{DPMO} &= 2{,}000 \text{ defects per million opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Process Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= 1 - \frac{2{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{Yield} &= 1 - 0.002 \\[4pt]
\text{Yield} &= 0.998 = 99.80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine Sigma Level**

For a yield of 99.80%, the tail area is $1 - 0.998 = 0.002$. From the standard normal table, $Z \approx 3.0$ (one-sided). Apply the **1.5σ shift**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sigma Level} &= 3.0 + 1.5 = 4.5\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Students often multiply daily units × opportunities **once**, forgetting to multiply by the **number of days/shifts**. Result: wrong DPMO. Always check: "Is the data given for one period or multiple periods?"

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Differentiate between the **DMAIC** and **DMADV** methodologies. Under what circumstances is each applied? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **seven principles of Six Sigma** in detail, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — DMAIC vs. DMADV

| Dimension | DMAIC | DMADV |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| **Full Form** | Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control | Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify |
| **Purpose** | Improve an existing process | Design a new process or product |
| **Application** | Process exists but underperforms | Process doesn't exist or needs redesign |
| **Focus** | Root cause elimination | Customer requirements built-in by design |
| **Key tools** | Pareto, Fishbone, DOE, Control charts | QFD, FMEA, Robust design, Tolerance analysis |
| **Outcome** | Incremental/sustained improvement | New product/process creation |
| **Example** | Reducing billing errors in an existing system | Designing a brand-new credit card approval process |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU:** "Existing and failing → DMAIC. New and needed → DMADV."

#### Model Solution (b) — Seven Principles of Six Sigma

1. **Always Focus on the Customer (1 Mark):** Every improvement must translate into measurable customer value. *Example:* Reducing call wait time from 5 min to 1 min.
2. **Data- and Fact-Driven Management (1 Mark):** Decisions are based on statistical evidence. *Example:* Using a control chart to validate process stability.
3. **Process Focus, Not Blame (1 Mark):** Deming's 85/15 rule — 85% of issues are process-related. *Example:* Rework the process flow, not punish the worker.
4. **Proactive Management (1 Mark):** Prevent defects before they occur. *Example:* Predictive maintenance over reactive maintenance.
5. **Boundaryless Collaboration (1 Mark):** Break silos between departments. *Example:* Joint R&D, manufacturing, and marketing teams for a new product launch.
6. **Aim for Perfection; Tolerate Failure (1 Mark):** Set ambitious targets, but accept small setbacks. *Example:* Pilot programs that "fail fast, learn fast."
7. **Driving Sustained, Breakthrough Change (1 Mark):** Six Sigma demands transformational, not incremental, results. *Example:* Achieving 50% defect reduction, not 5%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Alt):** Students often write *one-word answers* like "Customer Focus" without an **example**. KTU's 2024 scheme demands **applied understanding** — always pair a principle with a real or hypothetical industry example.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% Yield = 4.5σ short-term**.
> - The **1.5σ long-term shift** is Motorola's convention — **must be added** to Z-score.
> - **7 Principles** (philosophy), **5 Focus Areas** (operations), **5 DMAIC phases** (execution).
> - **DMAIC** is for *existing* processes; **DMADV** is for *new* processes.
> - **DPMO formula:** $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times \text{OPU}}$.
> - **RTY** is *multiplicative*: $\text{RTY} = \prod \text{FPY}_i$.
> - **Cp** measures *spread*; **Cpk** measures *spread + centering*.
> - **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
> - **Belts:** Yellow < Green < Black < Master Black Belt < Champion.
> - **SIPOC:** Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers.
> - **Voc → CTQ** flow is the *bridge* between customer voice and engineering specs.
> - **Deming's 85/15 Rule** is a frequently asked 3-mark question.
> - **DMAIC phase tools** (know at least 3 per phase for 7-mark questions).
> - **3.4 DPMO** is for *one-sided* (or two-sided symmetric) specs — read the question carefully.
> - **Six Sigma's origin:** Motorola, 1986, Bill Smith — *not* Toyota (that's Lean).
> - **Lean Six Sigma** = Lean (waste elimination) + Six Sigma (variation reduction).
> - **CTQ** = Critical to Quality; **COPQ** = Cost of Poor Quality; **VOC** = Voice of Customer.
- **Six Sigma is a metric AND a philosophy** — both definitions are required for full marks.
- **FMEA, DOE, Pareto, Fishbone, Control Charts** are the Big-5 Six Sigma tools — expect at least one in every KTU paper.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers: DPMO is *"defects per million opportunities,"* Yield is in **%**, Sigma Level is in **σ units**.
- **The 1.5σ shift is not a fudge factor** — it is a *modeled* adjustment for process mean drift in long-term production, supported by Motorola's empirical data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DMAIC Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DMAIC Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)** is a closed-loop, data-driven, five-phase problem-solving roadmap that forms the structural backbone of the **Six Sigma** improvement framework. It is a **project management methodology** rooted in the **DMAIC roadmap** and aligned with the **IDEF0 (Integration Definition for Function Modeling)** philosophy, used to systematically improve existing processes by identifying and eliminating root causes of defects and minimizing process variability.

The DMAIC methodology is executed by a certified **Green Belt** or **Black Belt** practitioner under the sponsorship of a **Champion** and the supervision of a **Master Black Belt**. Each phase has a distinct entry criterion, set of tools, exit deliverable, and review gate (often called a **tollgate review**).

| Phase | Full Name | Core Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | Define | Frame the business problem, customer, and project scope |
| **M** | Measure | Quantify the current state and baseline performance |
| **A** | Analyze | Discover and validate the root causes of defects |
| **I** | Improve | Implement solutions that eliminate root causes |
| **C** | Control | Lock in the gains and sustain the improvements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** DMAIC is *not* a one-time fix. It is a **cyclic, iterative** methodology. Once the *Control* phase is reached, the improved process becomes the new baseline, and the cycle may restart to drive further optimization (continuous improvement / Kaizen philosophy).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor treating a patient** with chronic fatigue:

1. **DEFINE** — You ask the patient questions: *"What hurts? When did it start? How does it affect your daily life?"* (Defining the problem)
2. **MEASURE** — You run blood tests, take blood pressure, measure heart rate. (Quantifying the baseline)
3. **ANALYZE** — You cross-reference symptoms and discover a Vitamin D deficiency is the root cause. (Root cause analysis)
4. **IMPROVE** — You prescribe supplements, a diet plan, and sunlight exposure. (Implementing solutions)
5. **CONTROL** — You schedule a follow-up in 3 months and ask the patient to maintain a health log. (Sustaining the improvement)

> **Six Sigma DMAIC treats your business process exactly like a patient.** You do not guess — you define, measure, analyze, improve, and control.

### 1.3 Key Performance Constants & Standard Six Sigma Metrics

- **3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — the universal Six Sigma benchmark of world-class quality.
- **1.5 Sigma Shift** — Motorola's empirical long-term drift allowance built into the **Yield Table**.
- **DPMO = (Number of Defects × 1,000,000) / (Number of Units × Number of Opportunities per Unit)**
- **Process Capability Indices:** $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$ — used to quantify how well a process performs relative to specification limits.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** DMAIC is the *problem-solving engine* of Six Sigma. **DMADV** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify), on the other hand, is used to *design new processes* — do not confuse the two in your exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases — Structured Logical Breakdown

#### **PHASE 1 — DEFINE**
**Objective:** Convert a vague business problem into a tightly scoped project.

**Why:** Without a sharp definition, projects drift, scope creeps, and the team solves the *wrong* problem. The Define phase establishes *what success looks like*.

**Key Activities:**
- Identify the **Customer (VOC — Voice of the Customer)** and the **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** characteristics.
- Develop the **Project Charter** (problem statement, scope, goals, team, timeline).
- Construct a high-level **SIPOC** diagram (Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers).
- Calculate the **business case** (cost of poor quality / COPQ).

**Deliverable:** Approved Project Charter + SIPOC + Initial Problem Statement.

---

#### **PHASE 2 — MEASURE**
**Objective:** Establish a quantified baseline of the current process performance.

**Why:** *"You cannot manage what you do not measure."* The Measure phase converts qualitative complaints into statistical baselines.

**Key Activities:**
- Develop the **Operational Definition** for each CTQ (precise, unambiguous).
- Create a **Data Collection Plan** (what, who, when, where, how, why).
- Validate the **Measurement System** using **Gage R&R** (Repeatability and Reproducibility).
- Establish the **Process Baseline** ($DPMO_{baseline}$, $\sigma_{baseline}$, $C_{pk\ baseline}$).
- Construct **Process Maps** and **Value Stream Maps**.

**Deliverable:** Validated baseline metrics + MSA results + Process Map.

---

#### **PHASE 3 — ANALYZE**
**Objective:** Identify, statistically verify, and isolate the **Vital Few X's** (root cause input variables) that drive the **Y** (output defect).

**Why:** This is the *diagnostic* phase — separating correlation from causation.

**Key Activities:**
- Apply **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** and **5-Why Analysis** for hypothesis generation.
- Use **Hypothesis Testing** (t-test, ANOVA, Chi-square, proportion tests).
- Use **Regression Analysis** to quantify the relationship between X's and Y.
- Apply **Pareto Analysis (80/20 Rule)** to separate the *Vital Few* from the *Trivial Many* root causes.
- Construct **Multi-Vari Charts**, **Scatter Plots**, and **Box Plots**.

**Deliverable:** Validated list of root causes with statistical evidence.

---

#### **PHASE 4 — IMPROVE**
**Objective:** Generate, pilot, and implement solutions that eliminate the validated root causes.

**Why:** This is the *therapeutic* phase — applying targeted interventions to the diagnosed root causes.

**Key Activities:**
- **Brainstorm & Screen** potential solutions using **Pugh Matrix** or **Design of Experiments (DOE)**.
- Run **Pilot Tests** to validate improvement on a small scale.
- Calculate the **Improvement ROI (Return on Investment)** and projected savings.
- Implement the chosen solution at full scale.
- Re-measure the **Process Capability** ($C_{pk\ improved}$).

**Deliverable:** Implemented solution + new capability data + ROI analysis.

---

#### **PHASE 5 — CONTROL**
**Objective:** Lock in the gains, prevent regression, and transfer ownership to the process owner.

**Why:** Without control, processes *regress to the mean* within 6–12 months. This phase ensures sustainability.

**Key Activities:**
- Develop and document the **Updated Process Map** and **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.
- Install **Statistical Process Control (SPC) Charts** (X-bar, R, p, c, u charts).
- Create a **Control Plan** listing critical X's, their specifications, measurement methods, and reaction plans.
- Establish an **Ownership Transfer Plan** (training the process owner).
- Document **Lessons Learned** and celebrate the project closure.

**Deliverable:** Live SPC system + Control Plan + Project Closure Report.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are universally tested in KTU Six Sigma examinations. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \\
\\
\text{Defect Rate} &= \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}} \\
\\
\text{Yield (Rolled Throughput Yield, RTY)} &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i \\
\\
\text{Rework Cost} &= \text{Direct Cost} + \text{Indirect Cost} \\
\\
\text{Process Capability (Cp)} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\
\\
\text{Process Capability Index (Cpk)} &= \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right) \\
\\
\text{Sigma Level} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{\sigma} \\
\\
\text{Defects \%} &= \left(1 - \text{Yield}\right) \times 100 \\
\\
\text{ROI (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

| **Metric** | **Formula Symbol** | **Standard Unit** | **Boundary Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Defects Per Million Opportunities | DPMO | defects per million | $\geq 0$ |
| Process Capability | $C_p$ | dimensionless | $C_p \geq 1.00$ (capable), $C_p \geq 1.33$ (preferred) |
| Process Capability Index | $C_{pk}$ | dimensionless | $C_{pk} \geq 1.00$ (capable) |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | RTY | percentage | $0\% \leq \text{RTY} \leq 100\%$ |
| Sigma Level | $\sigma$ | standard deviations | $6\sigma$ corresponds to 3.4 DPMO |
| Gage R\&R | \%R\&R | percentage of study variation | $\leq 10\%$ (acceptable), $10\%-30\%$ (marginal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** Absolute value bars like $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert y \vert$ are written using the `\vert` command to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a table cell.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DMAIC methodology is deployed across virtually every engineering and business domain:

- **Manufacturing Engineering:** Reducing weld defects in automotive assembly (Ford, Toyota).
- **Software Engineering:** Reducing bug escape rate in code releases.
- **Healthcare Engineering:** Reducing patient wait time in emergency departments.
- **Financial Services:** Reducing loan processing cycle time.
- **Aerospace:** Reducing turbine blade non-conformance rates.
- **Telecommunications:** Reducing call drop rate in cellular networks.

The framework is **discipline-agnostic** — wherever a measurable, repeatable process exists, DMAIC can be applied.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Computing DPMO and Sigma Level

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Typical Style):**
> A manufacturing line produces **5,000 mobile phone casings per day**. Each casing is inspected against **8 quality characteristics** (opportunities). Over a 5-day week, inspectors found **150 defects**. Compute:
> 1. Total number of opportunities
> 2. Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)
> 3. The corresponding **Sigma Level** (assume Motorola's short-term yield table).

---

#### **Step 1 — Calculate Total Number of Opportunities**

Let:
- $U$ = Number of units produced per week
- $O$ = Number of opportunities per unit

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 5{,}000 \text{ units/day} \times 5 \text{ days} = 25{,}000 \text{ units} \\
O &= 8 \text{ opportunities/unit} \\
\\
\text{Total Opportunities} &= U \times O \\
&= 25{,}000 \times 8 \\
&= 200{,}000 \text{ opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each casing is checked against 8 features, so 25,000 casings yield 200,000 total check points.

---

#### **Step 2 — Compute the DPMO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{\text{Total Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Total Opportunities}} \\
&= \frac{150 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{200{,}000} \\
&= \frac{150{,}000{,}000}{200{,}000} \\
&= 750 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Out of 1 million opportunities, this process would produce 750 defects. This is a **world-class** performance level.

---

#### **Step 3 — Determine the Sigma Level Using the Standard Yield Table**

We first compute the **Defects Rate** and **Yield**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Rate} &= \frac{150}{200{,}000} = 0.00075 \\
\text{Yield} &= 1 - 0.00075 = 0.99925 = 99.925\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, using the **Motorola Short-Term Sigma Yield Table** (standard reference table memorized for KTU exams):

| Sigma Level | Yield (%) | DPMO |
|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 69.1% | 308,537 |
| 3σ | 93.3% | 66,807 |
| 4σ | 99.38% | 6,210 |
| **5σ** | **99.977%** | **233** |
| **5.2σ** | **99.9993%** | **~\,750** |
| 6σ | 99.99966% | 3.4 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interpolating between 5σ and 5.2σ:} \\
\text{DPMO} &= 750 \Rightarrow \text{Sigma Level} \approx 5.18\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A DPMO of 750 falls between 5σ (233 DPMO) and 5.2σ (≈ 1,000 DPMO), placing the process at approximately **5.18σ** — meaning the process operates between 5 and 6 sigma capability.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Computing Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)

**Problem Statement:**
> A CNC milling machine produces shafts with diameter specification: **LSL = 24.90 mm, USL = 25.10 mm**. From a sample of 50 shafts, the mean diameter is **$\mu = 25.02$ mm** and the standard deviation is **$\sigma = 0.03$ mm**. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

---

#### **Step 1 — Compute $C_p$ (Process Capability)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\
&= \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.03} \\
&= \frac{0.20}{0.18} \\
&= 1.11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $C_p > 1.00$ means the *spread* of the process fits inside the specification window. $C_p = 1.11$ is acceptable but below the preferred 1.33 benchmark.

---

#### **Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (Process Capability Index)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right) \\
\\
\text{Upper Capability} &= \frac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.08}{0.09} = 0.889 \\
\text{Lower Capability} &= \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.12}{0.09} = 1.333 \\
\\
C_{pk} &= \min(0.889,\ 1.333) = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $C_{pk} = 0.889 < 1.00$ means the process is **not capable** — the mean is shifted toward the USL, and the lower $C_{pk}$ value dictates the index. The Six Sigma project must focus on:
- Centering the process (shift $\mu$ toward the target of 25.00 mm)
- Reducing variation (lower $\sigma$)

---

### 3.3 The DMAIC Analytical Matrix — Phase-to-Tool Mapping

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam answer** — examiners routinely award 7 marks for clean, comprehensive phase-tool mappings.

| DMAIC Phase | Primary Statistical Tools | Key Documents Produced | Exit Tollgate Review |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D — Define** | VOC, CTQ Tree, Affinity Diagram, SIPOC, COPQ analysis | Project Charter, SIPOC, Problem Statement | Charter Approval |
| **M — Measure** | Gage R\&R, Data Collection Plan, Pareto Chart, Process Mapping, Capability Analysis ($C_{pk}$) | Baseline Metrics, MSA Report, Process Map | Baseline Sign-Off |
| **A — Analyze** | Ishikawa, 5-Why, Hypothesis Tests, Regression, ANOVA, Multi-Vari, Scatter Plot | Validated Root Cause List, Fitted Model | Root Cause Validation |
| **I — Improve** | DOE, Pugh Matrix, Pilot Study, Cost-Benefit Analysis, FMEA | Pilot Results, ROI Report, Updated SOPs | Solution Approval |
| **C — Control** | SPC Charts (X-bar, R, p, c), Control Plan, Mistake Proofing (Poka-Yoke) | Control Plan, SPC Charts, Closure Report | Project Closure |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"D-Movies Always Improve Cinema"** → **D**efine, **M**easure, **A**nalyze, **I**mprove, **C**ontrol.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The DMAIC Cyclical Flow — Master Process Diagram

The diagram below illustrates the **iterative, closed-loop nature** of DMAIC. Each phase feeds its output into the next, and the *Control* phase loops back to *Define* to drive continuous improvement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PHASE 1 DEFINE Identify Problem Charter SIPOC"] --> B["PHASE 2 MEASURE Baseline Data MSA Capability"]
    B --> C["PHASE 3 ANALYZE Root Cause Hypothesis Tests"]
    C --> D["PHASE 4 IMPROVE Pilot DOE Solution Design"]
    D --> E["PHASE 5 CONTROL SPC Control Plan SOPs"]
    E --> F{"Gains Sustained?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Project Closure Document Lessons Learned"]
    F -- "No Recurrence Detected" --> A
    G --> H["New Baseline Established"]
    H --> A
```

> **Interpretation:** The *Control* phase is the **final tollgate** before closure. If monitoring reveals regression, the loop restarts from *Define* with a refined problem statement — this is the essence of **continuous improvement (Kaizen)**.

---

### 4.2 DMAIC Phase Deliverables & Tollgate Architecture

The block diagram below maps the **input → process → output → review** architecture of each DMAIC phase, highlighting the tollgate review checkpoints.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPONSOR["SPONSOR AND CHAMPION LAYER"]
        S1["Champion Approval Gate"]
    end
    subgraph TEAM["BLACK BELT AND GREEN BELT EXECUTION LAYER"]
        T1["Project Charter"]
        T2["Baseline DPMO and Cpk"]
        T3["Validated Root Causes"]
        T4["Pilot Solution Results"]
        T5["SPC Charts and Control Plan"]
    end
    subgraph DELIV["DELIVERABLES OUTPUT LAYER"]
        D1["Approved Charter"]
        D2["Validated Baseline"]
        D3["Root Cause Evidence"]
        D4["Full Scale Implementation"]
        D5["Sustained Process Control"]
    end
    T1 --> D1
    T2 --> D2
    T3 --> D3
    T4 --> D4
    T5 --> D5
    D1 --> S1
    S1 -- "Charter Approved" --> T2
    S1 -- "Baseline Approved" --> T3
    S1 -- "Root Cause Approved" --> T4
    S1 -- "Pilot Approved" --> T5
    S1 -- "Control Plan Approved" --> Z["Project Closure"]
```

> **Interpretation:** Each phase produces a tangible deliverable that must pass through a **Champion tollgate review** before the team is permitted to advance. This is the **governance mechanism** of DMAIC.

---

### 4.3 The DMAIC–VOC–CTQ Cascade Diagram

This diagram shows how customer voices (VOC) cascade down into measurable CTQs and then into process input variables (X's) and output responses (Y).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VOC["Voice of the Customer VOC Qualitative Complaints and Surveys"] --> CTQ["Critical to Quality CTQ Tree Measurable Specifications"]
    CTQ --> Y["Output Variable Y Defect Rate or Cycle Time"]
    Y -- "Influenced by" --> X1["Input X1 Material Hardness"]
    Y -- "Influenced by" --> X2["Input X2 Operator Skill"]
    Y -- "Influenced by" --> X3["Input X3 Machine Speed"]
    Y -- "Influenced by" --> X4["Input X4 Ambient Temperature"]
    X1 --> A["Analyze Phase Identifies Vital Few"]
    X2 --> A
    X3 --> A
    X4 --> A
    A --> I["Improve Phase Targets Vital Few Xs"]
```

> **Interpretation:** The Define phase translates VOC into CTQs. The Measure phase quantifies Y. The Analyze phase statistically identifies the *Vital Few* X's. The Improve phase targets those X's. The Control phase monitors them via SPC.

---

### 4.4 DMAIC vs DMADV — Comparative Block Diagram

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Students confuse **DMAIC** (improve existing) with **DMADV** (design new). Use this block to disambiguate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DMAIC["DMAIC FOR EXISTING PROCESSES"]
        D1["Define the Problem"] --> M1["Measure Baseline"]
        M1 --> A1["Analyze Root Cause"]
        A1 --> I1["Improve the Process"]
        I1 --> C1["Control the Gains"]
    end
    subgraph DMADV["DMADV FOR NEW PROCESSES ALSO KNOWN AS DFSS"]
        D2["Define Design Goals"] --> M2["Measure CTQs"]
        M2 --> A2["Analyze Design Options"]
        A2 --> D3["Design the Process"]
        D3 --> V["Verify the Design"]
    end
```

> **Interpretation:** DMAIC is *problem-solving*. DMADV (Design for Six Sigma / DFSS) is *process creation*. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction in Part A (3-mark) questions.
