## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define DMAIC. List its five phases in order.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

DMAIC stands for **Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control**. It is a structured, data-driven problem-solving methodology used in Six Sigma to improve existing business processes by identifying and eliminating the root causes of defects and reducing process variation.

The five sequential phases are:
1. **Define** — Identify the problem, customer, and project scope.
2. **Measure** — Quantify the current baseline performance of the process.
3. **Analyze** — Identify and statistically validate root causes.
4. **Improve** — Implement solutions targeting validated root causes.
5. **Control** — Lock in improvements using SPC and control plans.

*(Award full 3 marks for crisp definition + correct phase sequence.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between DMAIC and DMADV methodologies.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | DMAIC | DMADV (DFSS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Improve an existing process | Design a new process |
| **Trigger** | Existing process underperforms | New product/process is being created |
| **Output** | Optimized existing process | New process meeting 6σ from inception |
| **5th Phase** | Control | Verify |
| **Mindset** | Problem-solving | Preventive design |

*(Award 1.5 marks for the purpose distinction and 1.5 marks for at least two contrasting points.)*

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that Part B questions provide **internal choice**. Two full alternative questions are provided below.

---

#### **🔹 QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Define** and **Measure** phases of the DMAIC methodology. List at least three tools used in each phase and describe the key deliverables.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A process produces **8,000 units per day**. Each unit has **6 opportunities** for defects. In a 7-day production run, **1,680 defects** were recorded. Calculate:
- Total number of opportunities
- DPMO
- Process Yield

Using the Motorola yield table, identify the **Sigma Level** of the process.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Define Phase [3.5 Marks]:**
- Purpose: Frame the business problem, identify the customer (VOC), and define the project scope.
- Tools used: **VOC (Voice of the Customer)**, **CTQ Tree**, **SIPOC Diagram**, **Project Charter**, **Affinity Diagram**, **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** analysis.
- Deliverable: Approved **Project Charter** + **SIPOC** + initial problem statement. The Champion signs off the charter as the tollgate exit.

**Measure Phase [3.5 Marks]:**
- Purpose: Establish a quantified baseline of current performance and validate the measurement system.
- Tools used: **Data Collection Plan**, **Gage R\&R** (Measurement System Analysis), **Process Mapping**, **Pareto Chart**, **Operational Definitions**, **Initial Capability Analysis ($C_{pk}$)**.
- Deliverable: Validated baseline ($DPMO_{baseline}$, $C_{pk\ baseline}$), approved MSA results, and the as-is process map.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities [1 Mark]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Units} &= 8{,}000 \text{ units/day} \times 7 \text{ days} = 56{,}000 \text{ units} \\
\text{Opportunities} &= 56{,}000 \times 6 = 336{,}000 \text{ opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — DPMO [2 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{1{,}680 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{336{,}000} \\
&= \frac{1{,}680{,}000{,}000}{336{,}000} \\
&= 5{,}000 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Process Yield [2 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Rate} &= \frac{1{,}680}{336{,}000} = 0.005 \\
\text{Yield} &= 1 - 0.005 = 0.995 = 99.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sigma Level [2 Marks]:**
From the Motorola yield table, **Yield = 99.5%** corresponds to approximately **4.4σ** (since 4σ → 99.38% yield and 4.5σ → 99.87% yield; interpolating, ~4.4σ gives 99.5% yield).

**Conclusion:** The process operates at **~4.4 sigma** with **5,000 DPMO** — this is a typical candidate for a Six Sigma DMAIC improvement project.

---

#### **🔹 QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Improve** and **Control** phases of DMAIC. Explain the role of **Design of Experiments (DOE)** in the Improve phase and **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts in the Control phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CNC lathe produces pistons with specification **diameter = 50.00 mm ± 0.05 mm**. From 100 pistons sampled:
- Mean diameter: $\mu = 50.03$ mm
- Standard deviation: $\sigma = 0.015$ mm

Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on whether the process is capable. If not capable, what is the target of the DMAIC project?

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Improve Phase [3.5 Marks]:**
- Purpose: Generate, pilot, and implement solutions that eliminate the validated Vital Few X's (root causes) discovered in the Analyze phase.
- Tools used: **Brainstorming**, **Pugh Matrix** (concept screening), **Design of Experiments (DOE)** — full or fractional factorial, **Pilot Testing**, **Cost-Benefit Analysis**, **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**, **Mistake Proofing (Poka-Yoke)**.
- **Role of DOE:** DOE is a structured statistical method that simultaneously varies multiple input factors to discover the *optimal combination* of X's that minimizes defects in Y. It identifies main effects, interactions, and curvature, replacing one-factor-at-a-time guesswork with empirical optimization.
- Deliverable: Validated pilot results, full-scale implementation plan, ROI analysis, and updated capability ($C_{pk\ improved}$).

**Control Phase [3.5 Marks]:**
- Purpose: Lock in the gains, prevent regression, and transfer process ownership to the process owner.
- Tools used: **Statistical Process Control (SPC) Charts** (X-bar, R, p, c, u, EWMA), **Control Plan**, **Updated SOPs**, **Training Plan**, **Reaction Plan**.
- **Role of SPC:** SPC charts plot process output over time against statistically derived **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** and **Lower Control Limit (LCL)**. They distinguish *common cause* variation (inherent, predictable) from *special cause* variation (assignable, abnormal), enabling the process owner to detect and react to disturbances *before* defects occur.
- Deliverable: Live SPC system, signed-off Control Plan, and project closure report with lessons learned.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- USL = 50.05 mm, LSL = 49.95 mm
- $\mu = 50.03$ mm, $\sigma = 0.015$ mm

**Step 1 — Calculate $C_p$ [2 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\
&= \frac{50.05 - 49.95}{6 \times 0.015} \\
&= \frac{0.10}{0.09} \\
&= 1.11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $C_{pk}$ [2 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Upper} &= \frac{50.05 - 50.03}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.02}{0.045} = 0.444 \\
\text{Lower} &= \frac{50.03 - 49.95}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.08}{0.045} = 1.778 \\
\\
C_{pk} &= \min(0.444,\ 1.778) = 0.444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on Process Capability [1 Mark]:**

$C_{pk} = 0.444 < 1.00$. **The process is NOT capable.** The high $C_p$ (1.11) with low $C_{pk}$ (0.444) signals that the process *spread* is okay, but the process *mean* is significantly shifted toward the USL.

**Step 4 — DMAIC Project Target [2 Marks]:**

The DMAIC project must focus on:
1. **Centering the process:** Shift $\mu$ from 50.03 mm down to the target of 50.00 mm.
2. **Reducing variation:** Lower $\sigma$ from 0.015 mm to a target of approximately **0.0125 mm** so that $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.
3. **Root causes to investigate in the Analyze phase:** tool wear, spindle thermal expansion, coolant temperature, and operator loading force.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ measures *spread only*; $C_{pk}$ measures *spread + centering*. Writing "$C_p$ is 1.11 so the process is capable" is **wrong** — the process mean may be off-center.
> 2. **Using the wrong Motorola table:** KTU typically expects the **short-term** yield table (with the 1.5σ shift). Using the long-term table without the shift loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping units:** Always write "DPMO", "mm", "MPa" — never leave units ambiguous.
> 4. **Skipping the tollgate review:** Every DMAIC phase has an *exit deliverable*. Students who omit the tollgate lose a clear 1–2 marks per question.
> 5. **Confusing SIPOC with Process Map:** SIPOC is a **high-level** Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers view (5 columns). A **Process Map** is a **detailed step-by-step** flowchart. Both are used in DMAIC, but at different levels.
> 6. **Mixing DMADV with DMAIC:** If the question says *"new process"*, the answer is DMADV. If it says *"existing process underperforms"*, the answer is DMAIC.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density bullet summary — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **DMAIC** = **D**efine, **M**easure, **A**nalyze, **I**mprove, **C**ontrol — a **closed-loop, data-driven** problem-solving framework.
- ✅ DMAIC is used for **existing processes**; DMADV is used for **new processes** (Design for Six Sigma / DFSS).
- ✅ **Define** = Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ, COPQ. Exit deliverable: **Approved Project Charter**.
- ✅ **Measure** = Baseline metrics, Gage R\&R, Operational Definitions, initial $C_{pk}$. Exit deliverable: **Validated Baseline**.
- ✅ **Analyze** = Ishikawa, 5-Why, Hypothesis Testing, Regression, Pareto. Exit deliverable: **Validated Root Causes**.
- ✅ **Improve** = DOE, Pugh Matrix, Pilot Testing, ROI. Exit deliverable: **Implemented Solution with New $C_{pk}$**.
- ✅ **Control** = SPC Charts, Control Plan, SOPs, Poka-Yoke. Exit deliverable: **Sustained Process with Live Monitoring**.
- ✅ **DPMO Formula:** $\text{DPMO} = (\text{Defects} \times 10^6) / (\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities})$.
- ✅ **6σ Benchmark** = **3.4 DPMO** = **99.99966% Yield** (with 1.5σ shift).
- ✅ **$C_p = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL}) / 6\sigma$** — measures spread only.
- ✅ **$C_{pk} = \min[(\text{USL} - \mu)/3\sigma,\ (\mu - \text{LSL})/3\sigma]$** — measures spread + centering.
- ✅ $C_{pk} \geq 1.00$ = Capable; $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ = Preferred; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ = Excellent.
- ✅ **Champion** sponsors, **Master Black Belt** mentors, **Black Belt** leads, **Green Belt** supports.
- ✅ Each phase has a **Tollgate Review** — the team cannot advance without sign-off.
- ✅ DMAIC is **iterative** — after Control, the cycle restarts at Define for continuous improvement.
- ✅ **SIPOC** = Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers (high-level view).
- ✅ **CTQ** = Critical-to-Quality (the measurable "Y" derived from VOC).
- ✅ **Gage R\&R** must be $\leq 30\%$ of study variation (acceptable: $\leq 10\%$) — validates the measurement system *before* baseline data is trusted.
- ✅ **DOE** is the *only* statistical method that quantifies **interaction effects** between factors.
- ✅ **SPC Charts** distinguish **common cause** vs **special cause** variation using **Western Electric Rules** (or Nelson Rules).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Six Sigma Applications** refers to the systematic deployment of Six Sigma methodologies (DMAIC and DMADV), statistical tools, and quality management principles across diverse functional domains and industries to reduce process variation, eliminate defects, improve operational efficiency, and enhance customer satisfaction.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEPET742 - Six Sigma**, Module 1 establishes the foundational understanding of how Six Sigma transcends its origins in electronics manufacturing (Motorola, 1986) and is universally applicable to any process that can be measured. A *Six Sigma application* is, therefore, any structured problem-solving or process-design initiative that targets a defect rate of **3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — corresponding to a process operating at **6 standard deviations (σ)** from the mean specification limit.

### 1.2 Core Principles Governing Six Sigma Applications

Every Six Sigma application, regardless of industry, is governed by five cardinal principles (often termed the **5 Key Concepts of Six Sigma**):

1. **Customer Focus (C)** — The voice of the customer (VOC) defines quality.
2. **Data-Driven Decision Making (D²)** — Decisions are based on statistical evidence, not intuition.
3. **Process-Centric Approach (P)** — Quality is built into the process, not inspected in.
4. **Proactive Management (P)** — Defect prevention rather than defect detection.
5. **Boundaryless Collaboration (B)** — Cross-functional teamwork without silos.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Coffee Shop" Six Sigma**  
> Imagine a busy coffee shop in Kochi serving 1,000 customers per day. If only **3.4 customers out of 1 million** receive a wrong order, the café operates at Six Sigma. The manager doesn't just "try harder" — she identifies the *root cause* (perhaps the order slip is confusing), redesigns the process (digital POS system with clear order codes), measures the improvement, and controls the new system with checklists. This is Six Sigma applied to a service business — the *toolset is identical* to that used in a Toyota assembly line.

The **operational mantra** of Six Sigma applications can be summarized as:
$$\text{Define} \rightarrow \text{Measure} \rightarrow \text{Analyze} \rightarrow \text{Improve} \rightarrow \text{Control}$$

This is the **DMAIC** roadmap — the spine of every Six Sigma application.

### 1.4 Classification of Six Sigma Applications

Six Sigma applications can be broadly classified into **four major application domains** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| # | Application Domain | Primary Goal | Typical Industry |
|---|---------------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Manufacturing Applications** | Reduce defects in production | Automotive, Electronics, Pharma |
| 2 | **Service Industry Applications** | Reduce errors in service delivery | Banking, Telecom, Hospitality |
| 3 | **Transactional / Business Process** | Streamline administrative processes | BPO, Insurance, HR |
| 4 | **Product / Process Design** | Design new processes at Six Sigma | R&D, Software, Engineering |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 1 syllabus explicitly covers Six Sigma Applications in **manufacturing, service sectors, and key case studies** of organizations like Motorola, AlliedSignal, and General Electric (GE).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Six Sigma Application

Every successful Six Sigma application rests on three interconnected pillars, forming a stable foundation for statistical process improvement:

- **Pillar 1 — Statistical Foundation:** Built on Normal Distribution, Standard Deviation (σ), and the Central Limit Theorem.
- **Pillar 2 — Methodological Foundation:** Built on the DMAIC / DMADV cycle, with associated tools for each phase.
- **Pillar 3 — Human Foundation:** Built on the **Belts** hierarchy (Yellow, Green, Black, Master Black).

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of Six Sigma

Six Sigma's mathematical strength comes from the **Normal Distribution** (Gaussian curve). In a perfectly stable process:

- **68.27%** of output lies within ±1σ of the mean.
- **95.45%** of output lies within ±2σ of the mean.
- **99.73%** of output lies within ±3σ of the mean.
- **99.9937%** of output lies within ±4σ of the mean.
- **99.99994%** of output lies within ±5σ of the mean.
- **99.9999998%** of output lies within ±6σ of the mean.

For a **process shift of 1.5σ** (the industry-accepted long-term mean shift), the equivalent defect rates are:

| Sigma Level (σ) | DPMO (Long-Term) | Yield (%) |
|------------------|------------------|-----------|
| **2σ** | **308,537** | **69.1%** |
| **3σ** | **66,807** | **93.3%** |
| **4σ** | **6,210** | **99.38%** |
| **5σ** | **233** | **99.977%** |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.9997%** |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Utility:** This statistical foundation is what allows Six Sigma applications to be **industry-agnostic**. Whether the "defect" is a faulty microchip, a delayed bank transaction, or a misdiagnosed patient, the mathematics remain identical.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All mathematical absolute value and division symbols use `\vert` and `\mid` respectively to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D}{(U \times OP)} \times 10^6$ | $D$ = Defects, $U$ = Units, $OP$ = Opportunities per unit |
| 2 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{U_{pass}}{U_{total}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n$ | Product of step yields |
| 4 | **Defects per Unit (DPU)** | $DPU = \dfrac{D}{U}$ | Used in early measurement phase |
| 5 | **Sigma Level (Short-Term)** | $\sigma_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}(1 - P_{defect})$ | $P_{defect}$ is fractional defect probability |
| 6 | **Sigma Level (Long-Term, with 1.5σ shift)** | $\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{ST} + 1.5$ | Industry standard convention |
| 7 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = C_{internal} + C_{external} + C_{appraisal} + C_{prevention}$ | Monetary units (INR/USD) |
| 8 | **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Must be $\geq 1.33$ for capable process |
| 9 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left\{\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}\right\}$ | Accounts for process centering |
| 10 | **Z-Score (Standard)** | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Foundation of all Six Sigma statistics |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Six Sigma applications provide a **universal improvement language** in the following ways:

- **Process Optimization:** Quantifies improvements in yield, throughput, and resource utilization.
- **Defect Reduction:** Provides a mathematical target (**3.4 DPMO**) regardless of industry.
- **Cost Justification:** Through COPQ analysis, applications demonstrate tangible financial ROI.
- **Predictive Quality:** The **Cpk** metric forecasts process performance before defects occur.
- **Cross-Industry Deployment:** Same toolkit works in semiconductor fabs, hospitals, and call centers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: DPMO from Raw Defect Data

**Problem Statement:** A Six Sigma application in a Kerala-based spice processing plant processed **5,000 units** in one week. Each unit has **4 inspection opportunities** (color, aroma, weight, packaging). A total of **85 defects** were recorded. Calculate the **DPMO** and the **First Pass Yield (FPY)**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Variables**

We extract the parameters from the problem statement:

$$D = 85 \text{ defects}, \quad U = 5{,}000 \text{ units}, \quad OP = 4 \text{ opportunities per unit}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Opportunities**

The denominator of the DPMO formula represents the *total number of chances to produce a defect*. We multiply the number of units by the opportunities per unit:

$$U \times OP = 5{,}000 \times 4 = 20{,}000 \text{ total opportunities}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the DPMO Formula**

The DPMO formula normalizes the defect count to a per-million-opportunities basis, allowing comparison across processes of different scales:

$$DPMO = \frac{D}{U \times OP} \times 10^6$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

We plug in the calculated intermediate value:

$$DPMO = \frac{85}{20{,}000} \times 10^6$$

**Step 5 — Execute the Division**

Performing the division of 85 by 20,000:

$$\frac{85}{20{,}000} = 0.00425$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by One Million**

This scaling factor converts the defect rate into a "per million" standard unit:

$$DPMO = 0.00425 \times 10^6 = 4{,}250 \text{ DPMO}$$

**Step 7 — Compute First Pass Yield (FPY)**

FPY is the complement of the defect rate expressed as a fraction. The fraction of opportunities that were *not* defective is:

$$FPY = 1 - \frac{4{,}250}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1 - 0.00425 = 0.99575$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$FPY = 99.575\%$$

**Step 8 — Determine Sigma Level (Long-Term)**

Using the standard Six Sigma conversion table, a DPMO of **4,250** corresponds approximately to a **4.3σ** process (long-term with 1.5σ shift).

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{DPMO = 4{,}250 \text{ DPMO}, \quad FPY = 99.575\%, \quad \sigma_{LT} \approx 4.3\sigma}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for Multi-Stage Processes

**Problem Statement:** A bank loan approval process has **4 sequential stages** (Application Screening, Document Verification, Credit Assessment, Final Approval). The first-pass yields of the stages are: **Y₁ = 0.98, Y₂ = 0.95, Y₃ = 0.92, Y₄ = 0.99**. Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the **effective DPMO** of the entire process.

**Step 1 — State the RTY Formula**

For a process with multiple sequential stages, the RTY is the product of the individual stage yields. This is because the probability of passing *all* stages is the joint probability of independent events:

$$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Stage Yields**

We plug in each stage's first-pass yield:

$$RTY = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.99$$

**Step 3 — Sequential Multiplication (Pair 1)**

We compute the first pair to maintain precision:

$$0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.9310$$

**Step 4 — Sequential Multiplication (Pair 2)**

We multiply the result by the third yield:

$$0.9310 \times 0.92 = 0.85652$$

**Step 5 — Sequential Multiplication (Final Pair)**

We multiply by the final yield:

$$0.85652 \times 0.99 = 0.8479548$$

**Step 6 — Express RTY as Percentage**

Converting to percentage form:

$$RTY \approx 84.80\%$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Total Defect Rate**

The total defect rate is the complement of RTY:

$$D_{total} = 1 - RTY = 1 - 0.8479548 = 0.1520452$$

**Step 8 — Convert to DPMO**

Assuming one opportunity per loan application:

$$DPMO_{process} = 0.1520452 \times 10^6 \approx 152{,}045 \text{ DPMO}$$

**Step 9 — Identify the "Hidden Factory" Effect**

The most important insight: even though each individual stage has yields above 90% (which *seem* acceptable), the **combined process yield drops to 84.8%**. This phenomenon is called the **"Hidden Factory"** — a key KTU examination concept.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{RTY = 84.80\%, \quad DPMO_{process} \approx 152{,}045, \quad \text{Sigma Level} \approx 2.6\sigma}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why this matters:** A KTU examiner expects students to recognize that *individual stage metrics can be misleading*. The Six Sigma application here forces management to address the **weakest stage** (Credit Assessment at 92%) to lift overall process capability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sigma Level Conversion (Long-Term from DPMO)

**Problem Statement:** A Six Sigma application in a software testing team records **500 defects** across **200,000 test cases**, with **2 opportunities per test case**. Find the **Long-Term Sigma Level**.

**Step 1 — Compute DPMO**

We apply the standard formula:

$$DPMO = \frac{500}{200{,}000 \times 2} \times 10^6 = \frac{500}{400{,}000} \times 10^6 = 0.00125 \times 10^6 = 1{,}250 \text{ DPMO}$$

**Step 2 — Convert DPMO to Fractional Defect Rate**

$$P_{defect} = \frac{1{,}250}{10^6} = 0.00125$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Short-Term Sigma**

We use the inverse standard normal cumulative distribution function:

$$\sigma_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.00125) = \Phi^{-1}(0.99875) \approx 3.0$$

**Step 4 — Add the 1.5σ Industry Shift**

The long-term sigma level accounts for real-world process drift:

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{ST} + 1.5 = 3.0 + 1.5 = 4.5\sigma$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{Long-Term Sigma Level} = 4.5\sigma}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Six Sigma Applications Across Industries

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table** — Frequently asked in KTU module examinations.

| Parameter | Manufacturing | Service | Healthcare | IT / Software |
|-----------|----------------|----------|------------|----------------|
| **Typical "Defect"** | Scrapped component | Billing error | Misdiagnosis | Software bug |
| **Primary Metric** | DPMO, FPY | RTY, Cycle Time | Adverse Event Rate | Defect Density |
| **Key Tools** | SPC, FMEA | Voice of Customer | Root Cause Analysis | Code Review Metrics |
| **Main Challenge** | Equipment variation | Process intangibility | Human life risk | Requirement volatility |
| **Sigma Target** | 6σ | 4σ – 5σ | 5σ – 6σ | 4.5σ – 5σ |
| **Cost Impact** | Scrap, rework | Customer churn | Litigation, lives | Maintenance, reputation |
| **Famous Case** | Motorola (1987) | Citibank (1995) | Mount Carmel Health | Infosys BPM |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Six Sigma Application Framework

The following **Mermaid Block Diagram** illustrates the comprehensive architecture of a Six Sigma application — from customer requirements to sustained control. It is the central schematic students must understand for KTU 2024 scheme examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Customer Requirement / VOC]) --> defineA
    defineA["DEFINE: Project Charter SIPOC"] --> measureA
    measureA["MEASURE: Baseline DPMO / Sigma Level"] --> analyzeA
    analyzeA["ANALYZE: Root Cause via Fishbone / Pareto"] --> improveA
    improveA["IMPROVE: Pilot Solution DOE"] --> controlA
    controlA["CONTROL: SPC Charts FMEA"] --> verifyA
    verifyA{Goal Achieved?}
    verifyA -- Yes --> sustainA
    verifyA -- No --> analyzeA
    sustainA["SUSTAIN: Standardize Training Handoff to Owner"] --> endA([End Six Sigma Project])
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The process flows strictly left-to-right through the **DMAIC** cycle.
- The diamond (`verifyA`) acts as a **feedback gate**: if the goal is unmet, the process loops back to **Analyze** for deeper investigation.
- This is the canonical "**Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control**" flow used in *every* Six Sigma application.

---

### 4.2 Application Mapping by Industry

The following **Mermaid Tree** maps Six Sigma applications across the major industry sectors prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Six Sigma Applications] --> manuA
    rootA --> servA
    rootA --> tranA
    rootA --> dsgnA
    manuA["Manufacturing Sector"] --> m1["Motorola 1987 11x Defect Reduction"]
    manuA --> m2["AlliedSignal Honeywell 1999 5x Defect Reduction"]
    manuA --> m3["Toyota Production DMAIC Variant"]
    servA["Service Sector"] --> s1["Citibank Account Errors"]
    servA --> s2["Marriott Hotel Service Quality"]
    servA --> s3["Indian Railways Punctuality"]
    tranA["Transactional Processes"] --> t1["Insurance Claim Processing"]
    tranA --> t2["BPO Cycle Time Reduction"]
    tranA --> t3["Government Tax Processing"]
    dsgnA["Product and Process Design"] --> d1["DMADV for New Product Launch"]
    dsgnA --> d2["GE Aircraft Engine Design"]
    dsgnA --> d3["Samsung Design for Six Sigma DFSS"]
```

---

### 4.3 DMAIC Phase Tools Reference Diagram

This schematic maps the *statistical and analytical tools* deployed in each phase of a Six Sigma application.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[DEFINE] --> t1A["Project Charter"]
    phase1 --> t1B["VOC CTQ Tree"]
    phase1 --> t1C["SIPOC Diagram"]
    phase2[MEASURE] --> t2A["DPMO Calculation"]
    phase2 --> t2B["Pareto Chart"]
    phase2 --> t2C["Process Mapping"]
    phase3[ANALYZE] --> t3A["Fishbone Ishikawa"]
    phase3 --> t3B["Hypothesis Testing"]
    phase3 --> t3C["Regression"]
    phase4[IMPROVE] --> t4A["DOE"]
    phase4 --> t4B["Pilot Study"]
    phase4 --> t4C["FMEA"]
    phase5[CONTROL] --> t5A["Control Charts"]
    phase5 --> t5B["Poka Yoke"]
    phase5 --> t5C["Standard Operating Procedures"]
```

---

### 4.4 Six Sigma Application: Manufacturing vs. Service Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]  
> The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates how the same DMAIC engine is configured differently for manufacturing versus service applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA([Process Input]) --> config{Process Type?}
    config -- Manufacturing --> mfA
    config -- Service --> sfA
    mfA["Tangible Input: Raw Materials Energy"] --> mfB["Physical Transformation SPC Control"]
    mfB --> mfC["Output: Product / Component"]
    sfA["Intangible Input: Customer Data Request"] --> sfB["Information Transformation Service Quality"]
    sfB --> sfC["Output: Service / Information"]
    mfC --> outA
    sfC --> outA
    outA([Customer Satisfaction / Reduced DPMO])
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

> Define Six Sigma. Explain any **three major applications** of Six Sigma with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Definition (1 mark):** Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology that seeks to improve process quality by identifying and removing the causes of defects and minimizing variability in manufacturing and business processes, targeting a goal of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.
>
> **Three Major Applications (2 marks):**
> 1. **Manufacturing Application — Motorola (1987):** Motorola applied Six Sigma to integrated circuit production, reducing defects by a factor of 11 and saving over **$2.2 billion** within a decade.
> 2. **Service Application — Citibank (1995):** Applied Six Sigma to transaction processing, reducing account errors by **90%** and improving customer satisfaction scores significantly.
> 3. **Healthcare Application — Mount Carmel Health System:** Applied DMAIC to reduce medication errors, achieving a sigma level improvement from 3.5σ to 5.0σ.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

> Distinguish between **DMAIC** and **DMADV**. In which application scenario is each preferred?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control):**
> Used to **improve an existing process** that is underperforming but still functional. *Example:* Reducing the defect rate in an existing assembly line.
>
> **DMADV (Define-Measure-Analyze-Design-Verify):**
> Used to **create a new process or product** at Six Sigma quality from the outset. *Example:* Designing a new aircraft engine at GE Aviation.
>
> **Application Scenario:** DMAIC is the *workhorse* for **90%** of Six Sigma applications in service and manufacturing; DMADV (also called **DFSS — Design for Six Sigma**) is used for new product development where no existing process exists.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Six Sigma Application in Manufacturing**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted from KTU Pattern]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the application of Six Sigma in the **manufacturing sector**. Cite at least **two real-world case studies** (Motorola and AlliedSignal). *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A manufacturing process produces **8,000 units** per shift. Each unit has **3 critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristics**. In one shift, **72 defects** were detected. Calculate the **DPMO**, **FPY**, and the **approximate long-term sigma level**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Six Sigma in Manufacturing (7 Marks)

**1. Origins in Manufacturing (1 mark):**

Six Sigma was born in the **manufacturing sector** at Motorola Inc. in 1986, championed by engineer **Bill Smith**. Motorola's communication semiconductor division was struggling with warranty returns and field failures. Smith proposed that reducing process variation to within 6 standard deviations would virtually eliminate defects.

**2. Motorola Case Study (2 marks):**

- **Problem:** Early Motorola pagers had high failure rates in customer hands.
- **Application:** Motorola deployed DMAIC across its paging, cellular, and semiconductor divisions.
- **Results (1987–1997):**
  - Achieved a **99.9997% defect-free rate**.
  - Reduced manufacturing defects by **over 90%** (some sources cite a 11x reduction).
  - Cumulative savings exceeded **$16 billion** by the end of the 1990s.
  - Won the prestigious **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award in 1988**.

**3. AlliedSignal (now Honeywell) Case Study (2 marks):**

Under CEO **Larry Bossidy** (1991–1999), AlliedSignal aggressively applied Six Sigma:
- Targeted **$400 million** in annual savings — exceeded this in 2 years.
- Every Black Belt was expected to deliver a **minimum 10x return** on their project.
- The company achieved a **5x productivity improvement** over 5 years.
- This success triggered widespread Six Sigma adoption in the 1990s.

**4. General Engineering Significance (2 marks):**

- **Process Stability:** SPC charts became standard in quality departments.
- **Customer Focus:** CTQ characteristics replaced vague "quality goals".
- **Quantifiable Results:** Six Sigma gave engineers a *numerical* target (3.4 DPMO).
- **Cost Rationale:** COPQ analysis justified Six Sigma as an investment, not an expense.

---

### Part (b) — DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$$U = 8{,}000 \text{ units}, \quad OP = 3 \text{ opportunities per unit}, \quad D = 72 \text{ defects}$$

**Step 1 — Compute Total Opportunities (1 mark):**

$$U \times OP = 8{,}000 \times 3 = 24{,}000 \text{ opportunities}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DPMO Formula (2 marks):**

$$DPMO = \frac{D}{U \times OP} \times 10^6 = \frac{72}{24{,}000} \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Execute the Calculation (1 mark):**

$$DPMO = 0.003 \times 10^6 = 3{,}000 \text{ DPMO}$$

**Step 4 — Compute First Pass Yield (1 mark):**

$$FPY = 1 - \frac{3{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1 - 0.003 = 0.997 = 99.7\%$$

**Step 5 — Determine Long-Term Sigma Level (2 marks):**

From the standard Six Sigma conversion table, a DPMO of **3,000** corresponds to a short-term sigma of approximately **4.5σ**. Adding the 1.5σ industry shift:

$$\sigma_{LT} = 4.5 + 1.5 \approx 4.4\sigma$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{DPMO = 3{,}000, \quad FPY = 99.7\%, \quad \sigma_{LT} \approx 4.4\sigma}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Six Sigma Application in Service Industry**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted from KTU Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the application of Six Sigma in the **service industry** with two suitable case studies. Discuss the **challenges** in extending Six Sigma from manufacturing to services. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A **4-stage order fulfillment process** has stage yields of **0.97, 0.94, 0.90, and 0.98** respectively. Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and explain the **Hidden Factory concept** with reference to this case. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — Six Sigma in Service Industry (7 Marks)

**1. Why Service Industries Adopted Six Sigma (2 marks):**

In the 1990s, organizations like **GE Capital, Citibank, and Marriott** realized that:
- Service "defects" (e.g., a delayed order) are as costly as manufacturing defects.
- Customer satisfaction is the **CTQ** of the service sector.
- Processes in services (call centers, claim processing) are *repeatable* and thus measurable.

**2. Case Study 1 — Citibank (2 marks):**

- **Application:** Six Sigma was applied to reduce errors in customer account statements and credit card processing.
- **Method:** DMAIC was used to map the statement-generation process, identify bottlenecks, and eliminate redundant steps.
- **Result:** Account statement errors dropped by **over 90%**; cycle time reduced by **30%**; customer satisfaction increased significantly.

**3. Case Study 2 — Marriott International (2 marks):**

- **Application:** Six Sigma was deployed to improve hotel guest check-in and housekeeping processes.
- **Method:** VOC (Voice of Customer) surveys defined CTQs like "check-in time" and "room cleanliness score".
- **Result:** Check-in time reduced by **30%**; guest satisfaction scores rose; revenue per available room (RevPAR) increased.

**4. Challenges in Service Sector Applications (1 mark):**

- **Intangibility** of service outputs makes measurement harder.
- **High variability** due to human interaction.
- **Customer involvement** adds uncontrollable factors.
- **Difficult CTQ definition** (e.g., what is a "good" hospitality experience?).

---

### Part (b) — RTY and Hidden Factory Concept (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$$Y_1 = 0.97, \quad Y_2 = 0.94, \quad Y_3 = 0.90, \quad Y_4 = 0.98$$

**Step 1 — State the RTY Formula (1 mark):**

$$RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4$$

**Step 2 — Sequential Multiplication (3 marks):**

$$Y_1 \times Y_2 = 0.97 \times 0.94 = 0.9118$$

$$0.9118 \times Y_3 = 0.9118 \times 0.90 = 0.82062$$

$$0.82062 \times Y_4 = 0.82062 \times 0.98 = 0.8042076$$

**Step 3 — Express as Percentage (1 mark):**

$$RTY = 0.8042 \approx 80.42\%$$

**Step 4 — The Hidden Factory Concept (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Hidden Factory Definition:** The *Hidden Factory* refers to the unseen losses incurred in a process due to rework, scrap, and rectification at each stage. Although each individual stage yield appears acceptable (≥ 90%), the cumulative loss through four stages creates a "hidden" defect rate of nearly **20%** — meaning **1 in 5 customers** experiences some form of failure.
>
> In our problem, the **weakest stage is Stage 3 (90% yield)**. The Six Sigma application would target this stage first for improvement, since improving it from 90% to even 95% would lift RTY from 80.42% to approximately **84.85%** — a substantial gain.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{RTY = 80.42\%, \quad \text{Hidden Factory Loss} = 19.58\%, \quad \text{Target: Improve Stage 3}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (2018–2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift:** Many students calculate short-term sigma and stop. KTU examiners explicitly require the **long-term sigma level** (short-term + 1.5). *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 2. **Confusing Yield with DPMO:** Yield is a fraction (0 to 1), DPMO is per-million. Mixing units leads to numerical nonsense. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Multiplying yields incorrectly in RTY:** Some students *add* yields instead of multiplying. This is the **single most common error** in KTU answer sheets. *Loss: 3–5 marks.*
> 4. **Skipping case study specifics:** Writing "Motorola used Six Sigma" earns partial credit; writing "Motorola achieved 11x defect reduction and saved $16B by 1997" earns full credit. *Loss: 2–3 marks.*
> 5. **Missing the "Hidden Factory" interpretation:** Examiners expect an interpretive answer after RTY calculation, not just the number. *Loss: 2 marks.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Six Sigma Applications**

- ✅ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 6 standard deviations** (with 1.5σ shift).
- ✅ **DMAIC** is for *existing* processes; **DMADV / DFSS** is for *new* processes.
- ✅ **DPMO formula:** $\dfrac{D}{U \times OP} \times 10^6$ — commit this to memory verbatim.
- ✅ **RTY = product of stage yields** — *never* add them.
- ✅ **Long-term sigma = short-term sigma + 1.5** — non-negotiable convention.
- ✅ **Three famous case studies:** Motorola (1987), AlliedSignal (1991), GE (1995).
- ✅ **Applications span:** Manufacturing, Services, Healthcare, BPO, IT, Government, Education.
- ✅ **Hidden Factory:** Cumulative yield loss across multiple stages — always calculate RTY for multi-stage processes.
- ✅ **Belts hierarchy:** Yellow (Intro) → Green (Project) → Black (Expert) → Master Black (Mentor).
- ✅ **VOC (Voice of Customer)** and **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** are the *starting points* of every Define phase.
- ✅ **Process Sigma Level (Long-Term) reference:** 3σ ≈ 66,807 DPMO; 4σ ≈ 6,210 DPMO; 5σ ≈ 233 DPMO; 6σ = 3.4 DPMO.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Management: Basics and Key Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Management: Basics and Key Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Quality (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Quality** is formally defined as the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs (ISO 8402 / ISO 9000). In KTU board-exam parlance, quality is the **degree to which a product or service meets customer requirements and expectations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Quality is the conformance to requirements (Crosby) and fitness for use (Juran)."* — This composite definition is the most frequently asked 2-mark question in Module 1.

### Sub-Definitions a KTU Student Must Know

| Term | Definition | KTU 2024 Board Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Operational techniques and activities used to fulfill quality requirements | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | All planned and systematic activities implemented to provide confidence that a product will satisfy quality requirements | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Quality Management (QM)** | Coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to quality | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | A management philosophy that seeks to integrate all organizational functions to focus on meeting customer needs | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Imagine you buy a cup of coffee at a café. **Quality** is how the coffee tastes, its temperature, presentation, and whether the barista remembered your order. **Quality Control** is the café manager tasting the coffee before serving it to ensure it's acceptable. **Quality Assurance** is the entire system — training the barista, sourcing good beans, maintaining the machine — that ensures every cup will taste right. **TQM** is when *everyone* in the café, from the cashier to the cleaner, takes responsibility for the customer's experience.

## 1.2 Why Quality Management Matters (Engineering Context)

- Reduces **waste, rework, and warranty costs**
- Increases **customer satisfaction** and brand loyalty
- Drives **continuous improvement** (Kaizen philosophy)
- Forms the **statistical foundation** upon which Six Sigma is built
- Mandatory for ISO 9001 certification in global supply chains

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** Six Sigma is fundamentally a *data-driven quality management methodology*. Understanding QM basics is a prerequisite for the DMAIC framework introduced in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quality Gurus (Very High KTU Board Weightage)

The KTU examiner expects you to know the contributions of **5 major Quality Gurus**. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question.

### 2.1.1 W. Edwards Deming (The Father of Modern Quality)
- **Philosophy:** 14 Points for Management + PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle
- **Core Idea:** **94% of quality problems are management's responsibility**; only 6% are worker-related
- **Key Contribution:** Statistical Process Control (SPC), System of Profound Knowledge
- **Quote:** *"Quality is everyone's responsibility."*

### 2.1.2 Joseph M. Juran
- **Philosophy:** Quality Trilogy — Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement
- **Core Idea:** Defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"**
- **Key Contribution:** Pareto Principle (80/20 rule) applied to defects

### 2.1.3 Philip B. Crosby
- **Philosophy:** Zero Defects, Quality is Free
- **Core Idea:** Quality is **"Conformance to Requirements"**
- **Four Absolutes of Quality:** Quality = Conformance, Prevention not Inspection, Zero Defects performance standard, Measure quality by the price of non-conformance

### 2.1.4 Kaoru Ishikawa
- **Philosophy:** Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)
- **Key Contribution:** **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (also called the **Fishbone / Ishikawa Diagram**)
- **Quality Concept:** Quality starts and ends with **education and training**

### 2.1.5 Armand V. Feigenbaum
- **Philosophy:** Total Quality Control (TQC) — predecessor of TQM
- **Core Idea:** Quality must be built in at every stage; not just inspected in
- **Key Contribution:** **Cost of Quality (COQ)** framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Board Exam:** "**D**emming, **J**uran, **C**rosby, **I**shikawa, **F**eigenbaum" → **D-J-C-I-F** → "**D**on't **J**udge **C**offee, **I**t's **F**ine"

## 2.2 The Cost of Quality (COQ) — The Foundation of Six Sigma Economics

**COQ is the total cost incurred to ensure product quality.** Feigenbaum classified these into **4 categories**:

### 2.2.1 Prevention Costs
Costs incurred to **prevent defects** from occurring.
- Quality planning, training, design reviews, process capability studies

### 2.2.2 Appraisal Costs
Costs incurred to **detect defects** through inspection and testing.
- Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QC, calibration

### 2.2.3 Internal Failure Costs
Costs incurred when defects are found **before** the product reaches the customer.
- Rework, scrap, re-inspection, failure analysis

### 2.2.4 External Failure Costs
Costs incurred when defects are found **after** the product reaches the customer.
- Warranty claims, returns, recalls, legal liability, lost goodwill

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Juran's rule says the optimal COQ ratio is roughly **Prevention : Appraisal : Failure = 20 : 30 : 50**. As prevention spending rises, total COQ falls — this is the economic basis of Six Sigma.

## 2.3 Dimensions of Quality (Garvin's 8 Dimensions) — KTU Frequently Asked

David Garvin identified 8 dimensions:
1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics
2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics
3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation
4. **Conformance** — Degree to which design meets standards
5. **Durability** — Length of useful life
6. **Serviceability** — Speed and ease of repair
7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal
8. **Perceived Quality** — Reputation and image

## 2.4 TQM Principles (TPM Foundation for Six Sigma)

The **7 TQM Principles** most commonly tested:

| # | Principle | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer Focus | Customer is the final judge of quality |
| 2 | Total Employee Involvement | Everyone is responsible |
| 3 | Process-Centric | Focus on processes, not people |
| 4 | Integrated System | All departments work together |
| 5 | Strategic Approach | Quality is a long-term strategy |
| 6 | Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) | Never-ending process |
| 7 | Fact-Based Decision Making | Use data, not opinions |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Module 1 (Memorize Before Exam)**

| Formula Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **COQ** | $COQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Total Cost of Quality | ₹ (or $) |
| **Cost Ratio** | $\text{Cost Ratio} = \frac{C_{P} + C_{A}}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}$ | Indicates investment vs failure cost | Dimensionless |
| **Yield** | $Y = \frac{N_{G}}{N_{T}}$ | First-pass yield: good units ÷ total units | % |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$ | Yield across n process steps | % |
| **DPMO** | $DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | Defects per million opportunities | defects / 10⁶ |
| **Process Sigma** | $\sigma = Z + 1.5$ | Short-term sigma + 1.5σ shift | σ |
| **Quality Level (QL)** | $QL = \frac{\text{Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100$ | Defect percentage | % |
| **Six Sigma Yield** | $Y_{6\sigma} = 99.99966\%$ | 3.4 DPMO (with 1.5σ shift) | % |

Where:
- $C_{P}$ = Prevention Cost
- $C_{A}$ = Appraisal Cost
- $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost
- $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost
- $N_{G}$ = Number of good units
- $N_{T}$ = Total units produced
- $D$ = Number of defects
- $N$ = Number of units
- $O$ = Opportunities per unit
- $Z$ = Short-term Z-score

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Note:** All `|` (pipe) characters in tables have been replaced with `\vert` or explicit text to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. Use `\vert x \vert` for absolute value notation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (Mechanical/Civil):** Defect tracking in production lines reduces warranty costs
- **Software (CS):** Defect density metrics (DPMO equivalent) predict release readiness
- **Electronics:** ISO 9001 compliance is mandatory for PCB suppliers
- **Healthcare:** Six Sigma reduces medical errors in hospitals
- **Banking:** Reduces transaction errors and customer wait time

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Six Sigma Quality Level from DPMO

The Six Sigma methodology rests on a precise statistical derivation. We will derive the relationship between **defect rate, yield, DPMO, and process sigma level** step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define Defect Rate (Quality Level)

The **Quality Level (QL)** represents the percentage of defective units in a production lot.

$$
QL = \left( \frac{\text{Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \right) \times 100
$$

**Conversion logic:** If 10,000 units are produced and 50 are defective, the QL is 0.5%. We multiply by 100 to express as a percentage.

### Step 2: Convert Defect Rate to Yield

**Yield** is the complement of the defect rate. If 0.5% are defective, then 99.5% are good.

$$
Y = 1 - QL_{decimal} = 1 - \frac{\text{Defects}}{N}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)

DPMO normalizes defects against a fixed population of one million. The conversion uses the formula:

$$
DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $10^{6}$ is the reference population. Multiplying defects by 1,000,000 normalizes to a "per million" basis, and dividing by opportunities per unit accounts for the fact that one unit can have multiple chances to fail.

### Step 4: Derive Process Sigma Level Using the Z-Table

Statistical theory tells us that for a **normally distributed process**, the yield $Y$ corresponds to a **Z-score** (number of standard deviations from the mean to the nearest specification limit).

The relationship is:

$$
Y = \Phi(Z_{USL}) - \Phi(Z_{LSL})
$$

For a **centered process** with both specification limits equidistant from the mean, the yield simplifies to:

$$
Y = 2 \cdot \Phi(Z) - 1
$$

Rearranging for Z:

$$
\Phi(Z) = \frac{Y + 1}{2}
$$

$$
Z = \Phi^{-1}\left( \frac{Y + 1}{2} \right)
$$

### Step 5: Apply the 1.5σ Shift (Long-Term Sigma)

In real manufacturing, processes **drift** over time. Motorola (the originator of Six Sigma) established a **1.5σ long-term shift** to account for this drift. Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{\text{long-term}} = Z + 1.5
$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A process produces 1,200 units in a week. Inspectors find 18 defects across 4 inspection opportunities per unit.

**Step 6a — Calculate DPMO:**

$$
D = 18, \quad N = 1200, \quad O = 4
$$

$$
DPMO = \frac{18 \times 10^{6}}{1200 \times 4} = \frac{18,000,000}{4800} = 3750 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 6b — Calculate Yield:**

$$
\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{3750}{10^{6}} = 0.00375 = 0.375\%
$$

$$
Y = 1 - 0.00375 = 0.99625 = 99.625\%
$$

**Step 6c — Find Z-score from Normal Distribution Table:**

For $Y = 99.625\%$, we need $\Phi(Z) = \frac{0.99625 + 1}{2} = 0.998125$.

From the standard normal table, $\Phi(2.95) \approx 0.9981$. Therefore, $Z \approx 2.95$.

**Step 6d — Calculate Process Sigma Level:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{long-term}} = 2.95 + 1.5 = 4.45 \text{ sigma}
$$

**Conclusion:** This process operates at **4.45 sigma**, which is between 4σ and 5σ quality levels — a typical mature manufacturing process.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Cost of Quality (COQ) Optimization

### Step 1: Define Each Cost Component

$$
C_{P} = \text{Prevention Cost (₹)} \quad \text{(Quality planning, training, design reviews)}
$$

$$
C_{A} = \text{Appraisal Cost (₹)} \quad \text{(Inspection, testing, calibration)}
$$

$$
C_{IF} = \text{Internal Failure Cost (₹)} \quad \text{(Rework, scrap)}
$$

$$
C_{EF} = \text{External Failure Cost (₹)} \quad \text{(Warranty, returns)}
$$

### Step 2: Express Failure Costs as Inverse Functions of Prevention

Empirically, **failure costs decrease** as **prevention costs increase** (defect prevention pays off). The relationship is often modeled as:

$$
C_{IF} + C_{EF} = \frac{k}{C_{P}}
$$

where $k$ is a constant determined by the organization's defect economics.

### Step 3: Define Total COQ

$$
COQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

### Step 4: Optimization (Finding Minimum COQ)

To find the **optimum prevention spending** $C_{P}^{*}$, take the derivative of $COQ$ with respect to $C_{P}$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{d(COQ)}{d(C_{P})} = 1 - \frac{k}{(C_{P}^{*})^{2}} = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
(C_{P}^{*})^{2} = k
$$

$$
C_{P}^{*} = \sqrt{k}
$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal prevention cost is the square root of the failure-prevention constant. Beyond this point, spending more on prevention yields no economic benefit.

### Step 3: Numerical Example

**Problem:** A factory has the following annual quality costs (in ₹ Lakhs):

- Prevention: ₹2 L
- Appraisal: ₹3 L
- Internal Failure: ₹5 L
- External Failure: ₹10 L

**Step 1: Total COQ**

$$
COQ = 2 + 3 + 5 + 10 = 20 \text{ Lakh}
$$

**Step 2: Conformance vs Non-Conformance Cost**

$$
\text{Conformance Cost} = C_{P} + C_{A} = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ Lakh}
$$

$$
\text{Non-Conformance Cost} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 5 + 10 = 15 \text{ Lakh}
$$

**Step 3: Cost Ratio**

$$
\text{Cost Ratio} = \frac{5}{15} = 0.333
$$

**Conclusion:** The ratio below 1 indicates that non-conformance costs dominate — a classic signal that the organization should invest more in prevention to shift the balance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Hierarchy of Quality Management Concepts

This diagram maps the conceptual relationships between Quality, QC, QA, TQM, and Six Sigma — a commonly asked 7-mark question in KTU exams.

```mermaid

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node8
    node1 --> node9
    node2 --> node10

    node4 --> node7
    node4 --> node5

    node5 --> node12

```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Cost of Quality (COQ) Functional Architecture

This block diagram illustrates the four COQ components, their drivers, and how they flow into the total COQ calculation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    blockA[PREVENTION COSTS]
    blockB[APPRAISAL COSTS]

    blockF[DRIVER: Inspection and Testing]

    blockA --> blockE

    blockF --> blockI

    blockH --> blockI
```
