```mermaid
    blockB --> blockJ
    blockC --> blockK
    blockD --> blockK
    blockJ --> blockL
    blockK --> blockL
    blockI --> blockL
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Deming's PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)

This is a fundamental diagram tested in every KTU exam. It represents the continuous improvement cycle underlying Six Sigma DMAIC.

```mermaid
graph LR
    pdca1[PLAN Identify Problem and Plan Change]
    pdca2[DO Implement the Plan]
    pdca3[CHECK Measure Results and Compare]
    pdca4[ACT Standardize or Adjust]
    pdca5[Continuous Improvement Loop]

    pdca1 --> pdca2
    pdca2 --> pdca3
    pdca3 --> pdca4
    pdca4 --> pdca5
    pdca5 --> pdca1
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Six Sigma Quality Levels vs DPMO (Block Functional View)

This block diagram shows the mapping of sigma levels to defect rates, providing the foundation for Six Sigma classification.

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[1 Sigma = 690000 DPMO = 30.85 Percent Defects]
    s2[2 Sigma = 308000 DPMO = 6.68 Percent]
    s3[3 Sigma = 66800 DPMO = 1.35 Percent]
    s4[4 Sigma = 6210 DPMO = 0.27 Percent]
    s5[5 Sigma = 233 DPMO = 0.023 Percent]
    s6[6 Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 0.00034 Percent]
    sW[World Class Benchmark]
    sI[Industry Average 3 to 4 Sigma]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6
    sI --> s4
    sW --> s6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Quality Assurance and Quality Control. Distinguish between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Quality Assurance (QA):** QA refers to all the planned and systematic activities implemented within the quality system that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. It is **process-oriented** and focuses on preventing defects by improving the process.

**Quality Control (QC):** QC refers to the operational techniques and activities used to fulfill quality requirements. It is **product-oriented** and focuses on identifying and correcting defects in the finished product through inspection.

**Key Distinction:**

| Aspect | QA | QC |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Process | Product |
| Approach | Preventive | Detective |
| Responsibility | Everyone | QC Department |
| Timing | Before production | During/After production |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Use the phrase *"QA is process-oriented; QC is product-oriented"* — this single line fetches full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**Explain the concept of "Cost of Quality" with its four categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Cost of Quality (COQ), introduced by Armand Feigenbaum, is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product quality. It comprises four categories:

1. **Prevention Costs:** Costs of activities to prevent defects (e.g., quality planning, training).
2. **Appraisal Costs:** Costs of detecting defects (e.g., inspection, testing).
3. **Internal Failure Costs:** Costs of defects found before delivery (e.g., rework, scrap).
4. **External Failure Costs:** Costs of defects found after delivery (e.g., warranty, returns).

The total COQ is the sum: $COQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$. As prevention spending increases, failure costs decrease, leading to lower total COQ.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (A or B). Both questions test CO levels **Apply, Analyze, and Evaluate** (Bloom's Levels 3, 4, 5).

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the contributions of any **three Quality Gurus** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three Quality Gurus whose contributions form the foundation of modern quality management are:

**1. W. Edwards Deming (7 marks structure):**
Deming is regarded as the father of modern quality management. His key contributions include:
- **14 Points for Management:** A philosophy for transforming management practices, emphasizing constancy of purpose, adopting the new philosophy, and ceasing dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
- **PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act):** A four-step iterative cycle for continuous improvement.
- **System of Profound Knowledge:** A holistic system combining appreciation of a system, knowledge of variation, theory of knowledge, and psychology.
- Deming famously stated that **94% of quality problems are management's responsibility**, while only 6% are attributable to workers.

**2. Joseph M. Juran:**
- Defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"** — meaning the product must serve the customer's needs.
- Proposed the **Juran Trilogy**: Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement.
- Applied the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** to quality problems — 80% of defects arise from 20% of causes.

**3. Philip B. Crosby:**
- Defined quality as **"Conformance to Requirements"**.
- Advocated the **Zero Defects** philosophy — defects should not be acceptable under any circumstances.
- Authored **"Quality is Free"**, arguing that the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure.
- Proposed **Four Absolutes of Quality**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Valuation Tip:** Naming the Guru + giving one key contribution each = 4 marks. Elaboration with example or quote = remaining 3 marks.

---

**(b)** A manufacturing company produces **5,000 units per day**. Inspection reveals **125 defects** per day, and each unit has **3 opportunities** for a defect. Calculate: **(i) DPMO, (ii) Defect Rate, (iii) Yield, and (iv) Process Sigma Level.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 5000$ units/day
- $D = 125$ defects/day
- $O = 3$ opportunities/unit

**Step 1: Calculate DPMO**

$$
DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O} = \frac{125 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{5000 \times 3}
$$

$$
DPMO = \frac{125{,}000{,}000}{15{,}000} = 8333.33 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Defect Rate**

$$
\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{125}{5000} = 0.025 = 2.5\%
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Yield**

$$
Y = 1 - 0.025 = 0.975 = 97.5\%
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Process Sigma Level**

Find Z-score for $Y = 97.5\%$:

$$
\Phi(Z) = \frac{Y + 1}{2} = \frac{1.975}{2} = 0.9875
$$

From the standard normal table, $Z \approx 2.24$.

Apply the 1.5σ long-term shift:

$$
\sigma_{\text{long-term}} = 2.24 + 1.5 = 3.74 \text{ sigma}
$$

**Conclusion:** The process operates at **3.74 sigma**, which is below the world-class benchmark of 6σ but typical of an average industry process.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Where Students Lose 3 Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to divide by **opportunities ($O$)** in DPMO — always include this.
> 2. Failing to apply the **1.5σ shift** — without it, you cannot call it "Process Sigma".
> 3. Rounding Z incorrectly — use the normal distribution table, not memorization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Define **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. Explain any **five TQM principles** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** TQM is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members of the organization — from top management to shop-floor workers — to meet or exceed customer expectations.

**Five TQM Principles:**

**1. Customer Focus:** The customer is the ultimate judge of product/service quality. The organization must understand current and future customer needs, meet their requirements, and strive to exceed expectations. Example: Toyota's customer-first recall policy.

**2. Total Employee Involvement:** Every employee, from CEO to line worker, is responsible for quality. Empowerment, training, and recognition are essential. Example: Honda's team-based production system.

**3. Process-Centric Approach:** Quality is built into the process, not inspected in. Focus on improving processes to improve outcomes. Example: Toyota Production System (TPS).

**4. Integrated System:** All departments (production, marketing, finance, HR) work as a unified system with shared quality goals. Example: ISO 9001-certified integrated management systems.

**5. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Quality improvement is a never-ending journey. Small, incremental improvements compound over time. Example: Toyota's continuous kaizen culture.

**Bonus Principle (commonly tested):** Fact-based decision making using statistical tools and data analysis.

---

**(b)** A company has the following annual quality costs: **Prevention = ₹4 Lakh, Appraisal = ₹5 Lakh, Internal Failure = ₹8 Lakh, External Failure = ₹12 Lakh.** Calculate: **(i) Total COQ, (ii) Conformance Cost, (iii) Non-Conformance Cost, (iv) Cost Ratio.** Suggest **two strategies** to optimize COQ. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C_{P} = ₹4$ Lakh
- $C_{A} = ₹5$ Lakh
- $C_{IF} = ₹8$ Lakh
- $C_{EF} = ₹12$ Lakh

**Step 1: Total COQ**

$$
COQ = 4 + 5 + 8 + 12 = 29 \text{ Lakh}
$$

**Step 2: Conformance Cost**

$$
\text{Conformance Cost} = C_{P} + C_{A} = 4 + 5 = 9 \text{ Lakh}
$$

**Step 3: Non-Conformance Cost**

$$
\text{Non-Conformance Cost} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 8 + 12 = 20 \text{ Lakh}
$$

**Step 4: Cost Ratio**

$$
\text{Cost Ratio} = \frac{C_{P} + C_{A}}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}} = \frac{9}{20} = 0.45
$$

**Step 5: Strategies to Optimize COQ (2 marks)**

1. **Increase Prevention Spending:** Invest in employee training, statistical process control (SPC), and design reviews. This will reduce internal and external failure costs in the long term.

2. **Implement Six Sigma DMAIC:** Use the Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control framework to systematically eliminate root causes of defects, reducing both rework and warranty claims.

**Conclusion:** The cost ratio of 0.45 (well below 1) indicates that failure costs are dominant. Shifting spending toward prevention will lower total COQ and improve profitability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse COQ components:** Students often misclassify "rework" as appraisal cost — it is an **Internal Failure** cost. Inspect = Appraisal; Reject after production = Internal Failure; Customer complaint = External Failure.
> 2. **Always show units (₹ Lakhs/Crores)** — losing units in calculation costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Cost Ratio < 1** = bad (failure costs dominate). The goal is to invert it to > 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 1 Revision — Memorize Before Exam**

### Core Definitions (Verbatim for 2-Mark Questions)
- **Quality (Juran):** Fitness for Use
- **Quality (Crosby):** Conformance to Requirements
- **Quality (ISO 8402):** Totality of characteristics that satisfy stated and implied needs
- **TQM:** Customer-focused, organization-wide continuous improvement
- **Six Sigma:** 3.4 DPMO; 99.99966% yield goal

### The 5 Quality Gurus (Mnemonic: D-J-C-I-F)
- **D**eming → PDCA + 94% management responsibility
- **J**uran → Fitness for Use + Trilogy
- **C**rosby → Conformance + Zero Defects
- **I**shikawa → Fishbone Diagram + CWQC
- **F**eigenbaum → COQ + TQC

### The 4 COQ Categories (Mnemonic: PAIN)
- **P**revention (avoid defects)
- **A**ppraisal (detect defects)
- **I**nternal Failure (defects before delivery)
- **N**on-conformance = I + E together

### Garvin's 8 Dimensions (Mnemonic: "Please Remember, Car Drivers Find Service Areas Pretty")
- **P**erformance, **R**eliability, **C**onformance, **D**urability (Mechanical)
- **F**eatures, **S**erviceability, **A**esthetics, **P**erceived Quality (Aesthetic)

### Critical Formulas to Memorize
1. $COQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$
2. $DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$
3. $\sigma_{\text{long-term}} = Z + 1.5$
4. $RTY = \prod Y_{i}$ (product of step yields)
5. Six Sigma Goal: **3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield**

### One-Line Exam Confidences
- "QA is process-oriented; QC is product-oriented" — **use this line to score 3 marks in 1 sentence**
- "Six Sigma was pioneered by Motorola in 1986" — history question
- "DMAIC = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control" — Module 2 preview
- "TQM = ISO 9000 foundation" — frequently asked
- "Pareto (80/20) separates vital few from trivial many defects"

### Conversion Table — Sigma vs DPMO (Memorize 3σ, 4σ, 5σ, 6σ)
- 3σ = 66,800 DPMO (Industry Average)
- 4σ = 6,210 DPMO
- 5σ = 233 DPMO
- 6σ = 3.4 DPMO (World Class)

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Exam Strategy for KTU Module 1:**
> 1. Always cite the **Quality Guru's name** when giving a principle.
> 2. Always **show the 1.5σ shift** in sigma calculations — examiners check this.
> 3. For COQ problems, **classify each cost item** before adding them up.
> 4. Draw the **Fishbone/Ishikawa diagram** in 2 minutes for 7-mark quality tool questions.
> 5. Use **boxed final answers** — KTU examiners reward clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Total Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Total Quality Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Quality

**Quality** is formally defined as the **totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs** (ISO 8402 / ISO 9000). In KTU examination context, quality is the conformance to requirements, fitness for use, and the degree to which a product or service meets customer expectations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Quality is a customer determination based on the customer's experience with the product or service, measured against his/her requirements – stated or unstated, conscious or merely sensed, as well as against competing offerings.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant" View of Quality

Imagine you walk into a restaurant. The food tastes good, it arrives on time, the bill is correct, the ambience is clean, and the waiter smiles. None of these things are "the food itself" — yet they all determine whether you return. **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is exactly this philosophy applied to engineering and manufacturing: quality is not just the product, but the **entire experience chain** — design, supply, production, delivery, service, and feedback.

### 1.3 Definition of Total Quality Management (TQM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *TQM is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members of the organization — from top management to front-line workers — to meet or exceed customer expectations.*

**Three Pillars of TQM** (must be memorised for KTU exams):

| Pillar | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Customer Focus** | Quality = Customer satisfaction (internal & external) |
| **Total Involvement** | Every employee, every department, every level |
| **Continuous Improvement** | Kaizen philosophy — never-ending improvement |

### 1.4 Evolution of Quality Thought (Historical Timeline)

```
Inspection Era (1900s)        → Quality Control (1930s–50s)
Quality Assurance (1960s)     → Total Quality Management (1980s onwards)
Strategic Quality Management (2000s) → Six Sigma / Lean Six Sigma (Present)
```

**Key takeaway:** TQM evolved from a reactive inspection mindset to a proactive, system-wide strategic discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Principles of TQM (ISO 9000 Based)

These are **direct KTU high-yield points** — almost every Part A question fetches from here:

1. **Customer-Focused Organization** — Customer is the ultimate judge of quality.
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Involvement of People** — People at all levels are the essence of an organization.
4. **Process Approach** — Desired results are achieved more efficiently when activities are managed as processes.
5. **System Approach to Management** — Identifying, understanding, and managing interrelated processes.
6. **Continual Improvement** — Should be a permanent objective of the organization.
7. **Factual Approach to Decision Making** — Decisions based on data and information analysis.
8. **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships** — Creates value for both parties.

### 2.2 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Analytical Framework

The **Cost of Quality** is a critical TQM metric. It is the sum of all costs incurred to ensure quality plus the costs incurred due to lack of quality.

$$
COQ \;=\; COQ_{Prevention} \;+\; COQ_{Appraisal} \;+\; COQ_{Internal\;Failure} \;+\; COQ_{External\;Failure}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Cost of Quality (COQ) | $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | Total quality-related expenditure |
| Prevention Cost ($P$) | Sum of training, planning, quality system design | Money spent to *prevent* defects |
| Appraisal Cost ($A$) | Inspection, testing, calibration costs | Money spent to *detect* defects |
| Internal Failure Cost ($IF$) | Rework, scrap, re-inspection | Cost of defects found *before* delivery |
| External Failure Cost ($EF$) | Warranty, returns, recalls, loss of goodwill | Cost of defects found *after* delivery |
| Customer Satisfaction Index | $CSI = \frac{Expectations\;Met}{Total\;Expectations} \times 100\%$ | Measures customer perception |
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $FPY = \frac{Units\;Passing\;First\;Time}{Total\;Units\;Produced} \times 100\%$ | Process capability indicator |
| Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Yield across $n$ sequential processes |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | Six Sigma level measurement |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the DPMO formula, $D$ = number of defects, $U$ = number of units, $O$ = opportunities per unit. This is the bridge between TQM fundamentals and the Six Sigma core of this PEPET742 course.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In manufacturing industries (automotive, semiconductor, aerospace), TQM frameworks drive:
- Reduction of rework cycles (lowers production time by 15–30% in typical TQM-adopted firms).
- Improvement of supplier integration (just-in-time quality).
- Reduction of warranty claims (external failure cost).
- Foundation for advanced frameworks like **Six Sigma, Lean, ISO 9001, CMMI**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Quality Gurus and Their Contributions

This matrix is **the most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU TQM modules. Master this thoroughly.

| Guru | Key Contribution | Framework / Phrase |
|---|---|---|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management; Statistical Process Control | **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Planning, Control, Improvement | **Juran Trilogy** (QPI Spiral) |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Quality is Free; Zero Defects | **Quality Vaccine**; **4 Absolutes of Quality** |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control concept | Originated the term **"Total Quality Control"** |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Cause-and-Effect Analysis | **Fishbone / Ishikawa Diagram** |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss Function | **Taguchi Loss Function** |
| **Walter Shewhart** | Statistical Quality Control foundations | **Control Charts** (the original SPC) |

### 3.2 Derivation: Deming's PDCA Cycle (Mathematical/Logical Flow)

The PDCA cycle is a four-step iterative management method used for the continuous improvement of processes and products.

$$
\text{PDCA} \;=\; f(P,\;D,\;C,\;A)
$$

Where each function represents:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &: \text{Plan} \longrightarrow \text{Identify the problem, analyse, develop a plan} \\
D &: \text{Do} \longrightarrow \text{Implement the plan on a small scale (pilot)} \\
C &: \text{Check} \longrightarrow \text{Measure results, compare against expected outcomes} \\
A &: \text{Act} \longrightarrow \text{Standardise if successful, or restart cycle if not}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Derivation of PDCA as a Continuous Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quality Level at cycle } (n+1) &= Q_n + \Delta Q \\
\text{where } \Delta Q &= \eta \cdot (Q_{\text{target}} - Q_n) \\
\eta &\in (0, 1) \quad \text{(learning efficiency factor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows PDCA produces **geometric-like convergence** toward the target quality level — each cycle moves a fraction of the gap toward perfection.

### 3.3 Derivation: Juran's Trilogy (QPI Spiral)

Juran defined three universal processes of quality management:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quality Planning} &: \text{Identify customers, determine needs, develop product} \\
\text{Quality Control} &: \text{Evaluate actual performance vs goals, act on difference} \\
\text{Quality Improvement} &: \text{Establish infrastructure, identify projects, establish teams}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Closed-loop formulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{out}} &= Q_{\text{in}} + \Delta Q_{Plan} + \Delta Q_{Control} + \Delta Q_{Improve} \\
\text{At equilibrium: } Q_{\text{out}} &\geq Q_{\text{in}} \quad \text{(non-decreasing quality)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Crosby's Cost of Quality Equation

Crosby proposed that **quality is free** because the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost of Quality} &= \text{Price of Conformance} + \text{Price of Non-Conformance} \\
TCoQ &= P_c + P_{nc} \\
\text{Optimal Quality Level: } \frac{d(TCoQ)}{dQ} &= 0 \\
\text{At optimum: } \frac{dP_c}{dQ} &= -\frac{dP_{nc}}{dQ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This optimisation principle proves that beyond a certain quality investment, marginal cost of prevention exceeds marginal cost of failure — giving the classic **U-shaped COQ curve**.

### 3.5 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality (Memorise)

1. **Quality = Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness").
2. **Prevention, not inspection**, is the system of quality.
3. **Zero defects** performance standard.
4. **Measure quality by the price of non-conformance**.

### 3.6 Analytical Worked Example — DPMO Calculation

**Problem:** A manufacturing line produces 5,000 units/day. Each unit has 8 opportunities for defects. In one day, 120 defects are recorded. Calculate DPMO and identify the corresponding Six Sigma level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 120 \\
U &= 5000 \\
O &= 8 \\
DPMO &= \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} \\
&= \frac{120 \times 10^{6}}{5000 \times 8} \\
&= \frac{120{,}000{,}000}{40{,}000} \\
&= 3000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sigma Level (approximation):** $\sigma \approx 0.8406 + 29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(DPMO)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &\approx 0.8406 + 29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(3000) \\
&\approx 0.8406 + 29.37 - 2.221 \times 8.006 \\
&\approx 30.21 - 17.78 \\
&\approx 4.43 \; \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A DPMO of 3000 corresponds to roughly **4.4 sigma**, which is below the Six Sigma benchmark (3.4 DPMO).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TQM Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Customer Needs and Expectations] --> A1[Top Management Commitment]
    A1 --> A2[Strategic Quality Planning]
    A2 --> A3[Process Design and Documentation]
    A3 --> A4[Employee Training and Empowerment]
    A4 --> A5[Process Execution and SPC]
    A5 --> A6[Measure and Analyze Performance]
    A6 --> A7{Defects Found?}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Root Cause Analysis Ishikawa]
    A8 --> A9[Corrective and Preventive Action]
    A9 --> A5
    A7 -- No --> A10[Standardize Best Practice]
    A10 --> A11[Continuous Improvement PDCA Loop]
    A11 --> A2
    A6 --> A12[Customer Feedback Loop]
    A12 --> A0
```

### 4.2 Deming PDCA Cycle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PLAN Identify Problem and Set Objectives] --> D1[DO Implement on Pilot Scale]
    D1 --> C1[CHECK Measure Results vs Plan]
    C1 --> A1[ACT Standardize or Revise]
    A1 --> P1
    A1 -.Continual Improvement.-> P1
```

### 4.3 Juran Trilogy QPI Spiral

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    QP[Quality Planning Establish Goals and Methods] --> QC[Quality Control Evaluate Performance vs Goals]
    QC --> QI[Quality Improvement Establish Infrastructure and Teams]
    QI -.Upward Spiral.-> QP
```

### 4.4 Cost of Quality (COQ) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    COQ[Total Cost of Quality COQ]
    COQ --> P[Prevention Cost]
    COQ --> AP[Appraisal Cost]
    COQ --> IF[Internal Failure Cost]
    COQ --> EF[External Failure Cost]
    P --> P1[Training]
    P --> P2[Quality System Design]
    P --> P3[Process Capability Studies]
    AP --> AP1[Inspection]
    AP --> AP2[Testing]
    AP --> AP3[Calibration]
    IF --> IF1[Rework]
    IF --> IF2[Scrap]
    IF --> IF3[Reinspection]
    EF --> EF1[Warranty Claims]
    EF --> EF2[Returns and Recalls]
    EF --> EF3[Loss of Goodwill]
```

### 4.5 Quality Gurus Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SH[Walter Shewhart SPC Originator] --> DM[W Edwards Deming PDCA 14 Points]
    SH --> JR[Joseph Juran QPI Trilogy]
    DM --> ISH[Kaoru Ishikawa Fishbone]
    DM --> CR[Philip Crosby Zero Defects]
    JR --> TG[Genichi Taguchi Loss Function]
    SH --> FG[Armand Feigenbaum Total Quality Control]
    DM --> SS[Six Sigma DMAIC]
    CR --> QM[Quality is Free Doctrine]
    FG --> TQM[Total Quality Management]
    ISH --> TQM
    TG --> TQM
    JR --> TQM
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four principles of TQM.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):*
TQM is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy focused on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members of the organization to meet or exceed customer expectations.

*Four Principles (1.5 marks — any four):*
1. Customer-focused organization
2. Leadership and unity of direction
3. Involvement of people at all levels
4. Process approach to management
5. Continual improvement as a permanent objective
6. Factual approach to decision-making

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
What is meant by "Cost of Quality"? Mention its components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cost of Quality (1 mark):
COQ is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product/service quality, including the costs of preventing defects, appraising quality, and costs arising from internal and external failures.

Components (2 marks — 0.5 each):
1. **Prevention Cost** — training, quality planning, system design.
2. **Appraisal Cost** — inspection, testing, calibration.
3. **Internal Failure Cost** — rework, scrap, reinspection.
4. **External Failure Cost** — warranty, returns, recalls, loss of goodwill.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the Deming Wheel (PDCA Cycle) in detail with a suitable diagram. How does it support continuous improvement? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the contributions of Joseph Juran to quality management. Explain the Juran Trilogy with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Deming PDCA Cycle (7 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):*
The PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle, also called the Deming Wheel or Shewhart Cycle, is a four-step iterative method for continuous process improvement.

*Step-by-Step Explanation (4 marks):*
- **Plan:** Identify the problem or opportunity, analyse current state using data, set measurable objectives, and develop an action plan.
- **Do:** Implement the plan on a small pilot scale to test effectiveness and gather data.
- **Check:** Evaluate the results by comparing actual performance against expected outcomes using statistical tools.
- **Act:** If successful, standardise the change across the organisation; if not, identify causes and restart the cycle.

*Continuous Improvement Linkage (1 mark):*
The cycle is iterative — each completion raises the baseline quality level, producing a spiral of never-ending improvement.

*Diagram (1 mark):* PDCA cycle diagram (refer Section 4.2).

**(b) Juran's Contributions and Trilogy (7 marks):**

*Contributions (2 marks):*
- Quality Planning Handbook (Universal Process Sequence).
- Definition of quality as "fitness for use."
- Emphasis on the Pareto Principle (80/20 rule) for quality problems.
- Juran Trilogy framework.

*Juran Trilogy — QPI (4 marks):*
- **Quality Planning:** Identify customers, determine their needs, develop product features that meet needs, establish quality goals, and plan processes.
- **Quality Control:** Evaluate actual quality performance, compare against quality goals, and act on the difference.
- **Quality Improvement:** Establish infrastructure for improvement, identify specific improvement projects, and establish project teams.

*Diagram (1 mark):* QPI spiral (refer Section 4.3).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain Philip Crosby's contribution to quality management. State and explain his "Four Absolutes of Quality" and "Quality Vaccine." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process produces 8,000 units/day. Each unit has 10 defect opportunities. In one shift, 240 defects are recorded. Calculate DPMO and the corresponding Six Sigma level. Discuss its significance in a TQM framework. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Philip Crosby (7 marks):**

*Background (1 mark):*
Crosby authored *"Quality is Free"* (1979) and pioneered the concept of Zero Defects and prevention-based quality systems.

*Four Absolutes of Quality (3 marks — 0.75 each):*
1. **Quality = Conformance to requirements** — not elegance or goodness.
2. **Prevention, not inspection** — build quality in; do not inspect it in.
3. **Zero defects** performance standard — the only acceptable target.
4. **Measure quality by price of non-conformance** — quantify failure costs.

*Quality Vaccine (3 marks — three ingredients):*
1. **Determination** — top management commitment to quality.
2. **Education** — training every employee on quality concepts.
3. **Implementation** — translating commitment into daily practice through standards and corrective action.

**(b) DPMO Calculation (7 marks):**

*Formula and substitution (3 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} \\
&= \frac{240 \times 10^{6}}{8000 \times 10} \\
&= \frac{240{,}000{,}000}{80{,}000} \\
&= 3000 \; \text{DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Sigma level calculation (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &\approx 0.8406 + 29.37 - 2.221 \times \ln(3000) \\
&\approx 0.8406 + 29.37 - 2.221 \times 8.006 \\
&\approx 30.21 - 17.78 \\
&\approx 4.43 \; \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

*Significance in TQM (2 marks):*
A DPMO of 3000 (≈ 4.4 σ) is significantly below the Six Sigma benchmark of 3.4 DPMO (6 σ). In a TQM framework, this signals the need for focused improvement projects using DMAIC, increased prevention investment, employee training, and stronger process control to reach world-class quality levels.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **Deming's PDCA** with **Juran's QPI Trilogy** — both are continuous improvement cycles, but PDCA is a single-loop project method while QPI is a managerial framework. Mixing them loses marks.
> 2. In numerical problems, **forgetting to multiply by $10^6$** in DPMO formula is the most common arithmetic error. Always re-check unit consistency.
> 3. Writing "Quality = Good product" loses 1 mark instantly. Always use the formal definition — *"Quality = Conformance to requirements / Fitness for use / Customer satisfaction."*
> 4. Do **not** omit the **diagram** in 14-mark questions — at least 1–2 marks are reserved for it.
> 5. Crosby's Quality Vaccine must list all **three ingredients** (Determination, Education, Implementation) — partial answers attract only partial marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quality = Conformance to requirements + Customer satisfaction.**
- **TQM = Total involvement + Customer focus + Continuous improvement.**
- **PDCA Cycle (Deming):** Plan → Do → Check → Act — iterative, never-ending.
- **Juran Trilogy:** Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement — managerial QPI spiral.
- **Crosby:** Quality is Free, Zero Defects, Prevention not Inspection, Price of Non-Conformance.
- **Feigenbaum:** Coined the term "Total Quality Control."
- **Ishikawa:** Fishbone diagram for root cause analysis.
- **Taguchi:** Loss Function — quality loss begins the moment a product deviates from target.
- **Shewhart:** Father of Statistical Quality Control; originated control charts.
- **Cost of Quality (COQ) = P + A + IF + EF.**
- **DPMO formula:** $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ — the bridge to Six Sigma.
- **Six Sigma benchmark:** 3.4 DPMO = 6σ level.
- **8 TQM Principles:** Customer focus, Leadership, People involvement, Process approach, System management, Continual improvement, Factual decisions, Mutually beneficial supplier relations.
- **Customer satisfaction = perceived performance ÷ expectations.**
- **Quality Gurus Table is the highest-weightage 14-marker** — memorise contributions and frameworks.
- **Always draw a labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers — it secures 1–2 marks even if your text is weak.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Cost of Quality (COQ) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition Box**
> **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is defined as the total economic expenditure incurred by an organization to ensure that products and services conform to specified quality standards. It is a comprehensive financial measurement framework that aggregates all costs associated with **prevention, appraisal, and failure (both internal and external)**, providing a quantitative basis for quality management decision-making in Six Sigma methodologies.

The American Society for Quality (ASQ) classically defines COQ as *"a means to quantify the total cost of quality-related efforts and deficiencies throughout an organization."*

In **Six Sigma philosophy**, COQ is treated as a **strategic lever** — minimizing it directly translates to improved **process capability**, reduced **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**, and enhanced **bottom-line profitability**.

## 1.2 The Two Major Cost Categories

| Category | Symbol | Meaning | Who Bears It? |
|----------|--------|---------|---------------|
| **Cost of Good Quality** | **COGQ** | Money spent to *build* quality (prevention + appraisal) | The organization (proactive) |
| **Cost of Poor Quality** | **COPQ** | Money lost because of *poor* quality (internal + external failure) | The organization + customer (reactive) |

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for KTU Exams**
> $$\text{Total COQ} = \text{COGQ} + \text{COPQ}$$
> A mature Six Sigma enterprise aims to **shift the cost mix** from COPQ (reactive) toward COGQ (proactive), since prevention is exponentially cheaper than correction.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏥

> [!TIP] **The Hospital Analogy**
> Imagine you own a hospital. You have two ways to spend money on patient health:
>
> 1. **Vaccines, regular check-ups, hygiene protocols, staff training** → You spend money *before* people get sick. (This is **COGQ** — Prevention + Appraisal).
> 2. **Emergency surgeries, lawsuits from malpractice, compensation to affected families, reputation damage** → You spend money *after* people get sick. (This is **COPQ** — Failure costs).
>
> **The Paradox**: Many hospitals *cut* vaccine budgets to "save money" — but this *massively increases* emergency surgery costs. A Six Sigma hospital invests MORE in prevention, because every **₹1 spent on prevention saves ₹10 in failures**. This is the **1-10-100 Rule** (also called the **Rule of Ten** in quality economics).

## 1.4 Why COQ Matters in Six Sigma (KTU Context)

- **Quantifies the invisible**: Most managers see only *failure* costs; COQ reveals the *total* picture.
- **Drives DMAIC projects**: COQ analysis identifies the highest-leverage cost categories for improvement.
- **Justifies investment**: Helps in computing **ROI on quality programs** to senior leadership.
- **Tracks sigma-level progress**: As sigma level rises from 3σ → 6σ, COPQ drops dramatically.

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 6σ Miracle**
> Operating at **3σ** costs a company roughly **25–35% of total revenue** in quality-related expenses. Operating at **6σ** reduces this to **less than 1%**. This is why Motorola, GE, and Honeywell built empires on Six Sigma — the COQ savings are astronomical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PAF Model (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure Framework)

The **PAF Model**, originally proposed by Joseph Juran and refined by Armand Feigenbaum, is the **canonical classification** of quality costs used universally in KTU Six Sigma examinations.

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs (Category I)

> Costs incurred to **prevent defects from occurring** in the first place.

**Examples (memorize for KTU 2-mark questions):**
- Quality planning and **SOP (Standard Operating Procedure)** development
- **New product / process design reviews**
- **Supplier capability audits** and rating
- **Employee training and certification** in quality tools (SPC, FMEA, 5S)
- **Quality Improvement (QI) team** operating costs
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** software and infrastructure
- Process capability studies (Cp, Cpk calculations)
- **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) device design and installation

> [!NOTE] These are **proactive** investments. Increasing prevention costs *reduces* failure costs by an even greater magnitude — this is called the **"Multiplication Effect"** of prevention.

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs (Category II)

> Costs incurred to **detect defects** through inspection, testing, and measurement *before* the product reaches the customer.

**Examples:**
- **Incoming raw material inspection**
- In-process and **final product inspection**
- **Calibration** of measuring instruments and gauges
- **Acceptance sampling** plans (MIL-STD, ANSI/ASQ Z1.4)
- **Testing laboratories** and equipment depreciation
- Cost of inspectors, QC engineers, and CMM operators
- **Field testing** of prototypes

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs (Category III)

> Costs incurred when defects are detected **BEFORE** the product reaches the customer (i.e., within the factory).

**Examples:**
- **Scrap** (raw material + labor wasted on defective units)
- **Rework** and repair labor
- **Re-inspection** of reworked items
- **Downgrade** — selling premium product at a lower grade
- **Failure Mode Analysis** (root cause investigation)
- **Production line stoppages** due to defects
- **Material review board (MRB)** activities

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs (Category IV)

> Costs incurred when defects are detected **AFTER** the product reaches the customer — these are the **most expensive** of all.

**Examples:**
- **Warranty claims** and repairs
- **Product recalls** (catastrophic examples: Toyota, Samsung Note 7)
- **Customer complaints handling** and field service
- **Legal liability** and litigation costs
- **Concessions and refunds** to dissatisfied customers
- **Loss of goodwill** and brand damage
- **Return shipping** and logistics
- **Lost future sales** due to reputation damage

## 2.2 Traditional View vs. Modern (Six Sigma) View of COQ

| Aspect | Traditional View (Pre-1980s) | Modern Six Sigma View |
|--------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Optimum Quality Level** | An "optimum" point exists where total cost is minimum | **Zero defects** is the target; every defect has a cost |
| **Failure cost trend** | Considered unavoidable beyond a point | Considered **completely eliminable** through DMAIC |
| **Appraisal cost** | Viewed as a fixed overhead | Viewed as a **temporary investment** to be reduced via automation |
| **Prevention cost** | Often minimized in cost-cutting | Treated as **highest leverage** investment |
| **Curve shape** | U-shaped total cost curve | Continuously declining curve toward zero |

> [!IMPORTANT] **For KTU Long Answers**
> The modern view is the **correct** KTU answer. Always argue that prevention is the **highest-leverage** activity and that the *true optimum* lies in driving failure costs to near-zero through Six Sigma's DMAIC methodology.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total COQ | $\text{COQ}_{total} = P + A + IF + EF$ | ₹/₹-lakhs/USD | Sum of all four cost categories |
| 2 | Cost of Good Quality | $\text{COGQ} = P + A$ | Currency | Prevention + Appraisal |
| 3 | Cost of Poor Quality | $\text{COPQ} = IF + EF$ | Currency | Internal + External Failure |
| 4 | COQ as % of Sales | $\text{COQ\%} = \dfrac{\text{Total COQ}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Benchmarking across industries |
| 5 | COQ per Unit | $\text{COQ/unit} = \dfrac{\text{Total COQ}}{\text{Units Produced}}$ | ₹/unit | Cost reduction tracking |
| 6 | COPQ Savings Ratio | $\text{Savings Ratio} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{COPQ}}{\Delta \text{COGQ}}$ | Ratio | ROI of prevention investment |
| 7 | DPMO Conversion | $\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{D}{O}$ | Decimal | Links sigma level to COPQ |
| 8 | Sigma Level → COPQ link | $\text{COPQ} \propto 1/\sigma^2$ (approximate) | — | Quality cost vs sigma level |
| 9 | Prevention Leverage | $L_p = \dfrac{\Delta \text{IF} + \Delta \text{EF}}{\Delta P}$ | Ratio | Each ₹1 in P saves ₹Lₚ in failures |
| 10 | Quality Cost Index | $\text{QCI} = \dfrac{\text{COPQ}}{\text{COGQ}}$ | Ratio | Optimal target: QCI < 1 |

> [!NOTE] **Critical KTU Tip**: When computing COQ problems in exams, **always show all four categories separately** in a table before summation. Examiners award partial credit for structured working.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry**: Toyota's COQ analysis led to the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and Lean Six Sigma integration.
- **Software Industry**: COQ is applied to bug rates, code reviews (prevention), QA testing (appraisal), post-release patches (internal failure), and customer support (external failure).
- **Healthcare**: Used to track **hospital-acquired infections**, surgical errors, and readmission rates.
- **Manufacturing (KTU Relevance)**: Used in foundries, PCB assembly lines, CNC machining cells to optimize process capability.
- **Service Sector**: Banks use it to measure transaction error costs, customer churn, and complaint handling expenses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linking Sigma Level to COPQ

**Step 1 — Define DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects Found}}{\text{Number of Units Produced} \times \text{Number of Defect Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$$

**Step 2 — Convert DPMO to Process Yield**

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \left(\frac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Convert Yield to Sigma Level (Z-score)**

For a Six Sigma process with 1.5σ shift correction (industry standard):

$$Z_{bench} = Z_{st\ yield} + 1.5$$

where $Z_{st\ yield}$ is the standard normal inverse of the process yield.

**Step 4 — Link Sigma Level to COPQ (Empirical Model)**

Empirical studies (Harry & Schroeder, Motorola internal data) show:

$$\text{COPQ}_{relative} \approx \frac{K}{Z^2}$$

where $K$ is an industry-specific constant. This shows COPQ drops with the **square** of sigma level.

**Interpretation:**

| Sigma Level | DPMO | COPQ (relative to 3σ = 100%) |
|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | ~225% |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 100% (baseline) |
| 4σ | 6,210 | ~25% |
| 5σ | 233 | ~6% |
| 6σ | 3.4 | ~0.4% |

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — COQ Computation (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem**: A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality costs:
> - Quality training of operators: **₹2,00,000**
> - Incoming material inspection: **₹1,50,000**
> - Scrap and rework: **₹5,00,000**
> - Warranty claims: **₹6,00,000**
> - Supplier audits: **₹1,00,000**
> - Product design review: **₹2,50,000**
> - Final QC testing: **₹1,00,000**
> - Customer returns handling: **₹2,50,000**
>
> Total annual sales = **₹80,00,000**; Units produced = **20,000**.
>
> **Calculate**: (i) Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure, (ii) Total COQ, (iii) COQ as % of sales, (iv) COQ per unit, (v) COPQ, COGQ, and QCI.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Classify each cost into the PAF category**

| Cost Item | Amount (₹) | Category |
|---|---|---|
| Quality training of operators | 2,00,000 | **Prevention (P)** |
| Incoming material inspection | 1,50,000 | **Appraisal (A)** |
| Scrap and rework | 5,00,000 | **Internal Failure (IF)** |
| Warranty claims | 6,00,000 | **External Failure (EF)** |
| Supplier audits | 1,00,000 | **Prevention (P)** |
| Product design review | 2,50,000 | **Prevention (P)** |
| Final QC testing | 1,00,000 | **Appraisal (A)** |
| Customer returns handling | 2,50,000 | **External Failure (EF)** |

**Step 2 — Sum each category**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\
A &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\
IF &= 5{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\
EF &= 6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 8{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total COQ**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ}_{total} &= P + A + IF + EF \\
&= 5{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}50{,}000 \\
&= 21{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute COGQ and COPQ**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COGQ} &= P + A = 5{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\
\text{COPQ} &= IF + EF = 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}50{,}000 = 13{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — COQ as percentage of sales**

$$\text{COQ\%} = \frac{21{,}50{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 26.875\%$$

> [!WARNING] **Industry benchmark**: World-class manufacturers (Six Sigma firms) operate at COQ < 5% of sales. **26.875%** is a *crisis level* — the firm urgently needs DMAIC intervention.

**Step 6 — COQ per unit**

$$\text{COQ/unit} = \frac{21{,}50{,}000}{20{,}000} = 107.5 \;\text{₹/unit}$$

**Step 7 — Quality Cost Index (QCI)**

$$\text{QCI} = \frac{\text{COPQ}}{\text{COGQ}} = \frac{13{,}50{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 1.6875$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Interpretation**: QCI > 1 means the firm is spending **more on failure than on prevention**. The ideal target is **QCI < 1**. This firm needs to *shift* investment from failure (reactive) to prevention (proactive).

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Prevention Leverage Calculation

> **Problem**: A company invested an additional **₹1,00,000** in prevention (employee training and process FMEA) for one year. As a result, internal failure cost dropped by **₹3,50,000** and external failure cost dropped by **₹5,50,000**. Compute the prevention leverage ratio.

### Solution

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 1{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\
\Delta IF &= 3{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\
\Delta EF &= 5{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\
\text{Total COPQ saved} &= \Delta IF + \Delta EF = 9{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Prevention Leverage } (L_p) = \frac{\Delta IF + \Delta EF}{\Delta P} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 9$$

> [!TIP] **KTU Interpretation**: Each **₹1 invested in prevention saved ₹9 in failure costs**. This is a **9× ROI** on quality investment — strongly justifies increasing the prevention budget. This empirically validates the **1-10-100 Rule** mentioned in Section 1.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the "Cost of Iceberg"

The **Iceberg Model** of quality costs is a classic KTU concept. Only a small fraction of quality costs are **visible**; the majority are **hidden**.

| Layer | Type | Examples | Visibility | % of Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tip (Above Water)** | Direct, measurable | Scrap, warranty, rework | Fully visible | ~30% |
| **Just Below Water** | Indirect, measurable | Inspection, training, audits | Visible but aggregated | ~25% |
| **Deep Below Water** | Hidden, opportunity costs | Lost customer goodwill, lost sales, employee morale, rework of supplier materials, design rework | Invisible / unmeasured | ~45% |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Golden Point**: Any COQ analysis that ignores **hidden costs** is fundamentally flawed. A complete Six Sigma project uncovers the *entire* iceberg using tools like **Pareto Analysis**, **Activity-Based Costing (ABC)**, and **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    nodeCOPQ["COPQ: COST OF POOR QUALITY"]:::poor

    nodeA --> nodeA3["Instrument calibration"]:::leaf
    nodeA --> nodeA4["Acceptance sampling MIL STD"]:::leaf

    nodeIF --> nodeIF1["Scrap and rework"]:::leaf
    nodeIF --> nodeIF2["Re inspection cost"]:::leaf
    nodeIF --> nodeIF3["Production line stoppage"]:::leaf
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define **Cost of Quality (COQ)**. List and briefly explain its **four major categories** under the PAF model.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total financial expenditure associated with ensuring and assuring product quality, encompassing all prevention, appraisal, and failure-related expenses throughout the value chain.
>
> The four categories under the **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) model** are:
> 1. **Prevention Costs (P)** — Costs to *prevent* defects (e.g., training, design review, supplier audits).
> 2. **Appraisal Costs (A)** — Costs to *detect* defects via inspection and testing (e.g., incoming inspection, calibration).
> 3. **Internal Failure Costs (IF)** — Costs of defects caught *before* delivery (e.g., scrap, rework).
> 4. **External Failure Costs (EF)** — Costs of defects caught *after* delivery (e.g., warranty, recalls, lawsuits).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **Cost of Good Quality (COGQ)** and **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** with suitable examples.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | COGQ | COPQ |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Costs of building quality *into* the product | Costs resulting from *lack* of quality |
> | **Components** | Prevention + Appraisal | Internal Failure + External Failure |
> | **Nature** | Proactive investment | Reactive / failure-driven |
> | **Examples** | Employee training, process FMEA, supplier audits | Scrap, rework, warranty claims, product recalls |
> | **Six Sigma Stance** | Should be *increased* strategically | Should be *driven to zero* |
> | **Formula** | $\text{COGQ} = P + A$ | $\text{COPQ} = IF + EF$ |
>
> **Example**: Spending ₹2 lakhs on Six Sigma Black Belt training is COGQ. Spending ₹20 lakhs to recall defective cars is COPQ.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Question]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — COQ Computation & Interpretation**
>
> **Q.A.(a)** A precision machining company has recorded the following quality-related expenses for the year ending March 2024:
>
> | Cost Item | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
> |---|---|
> | New product design review | 3.5 |
> | Incoming raw material inspection | 2.0 |
> | Scrap and waste disposal | 6.5 |
> | Warranty repairs | 9.0 |
> | Quality circle team operating cost | 1.5 |
> | Final product testing and certification | 3.0 |
> | Customer complaint handling and refunds | 4.5 |
> | Calibration of CMM machines | 1.0 |
> | Process FMEA studies | 2.0 |
> | Re-inspection of reworked parts | 2.5 |
> | Supplier rating and audit | 1.5 |
> | Field service engineer salaries | 3.0 |
>
> The company produced **50,000 units** with total annual revenue of **₹250 Lakhs**.
>
> Compute:
> (i) Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure costs. [4 marks]
> (ii) Total COQ and COQ as % of sales. [2 marks]
> (iii) COQ per unit. [1 mark]
> (iv) Quality Cost Index (QCI) and comment. [2 marks]
>
> **Q.A.(b)** Explain the **"Iceberg Model" of quality costs** with a neat sketch. Why are hidden costs dangerous, and how does Six Sigma help uncover them? [5 marks]

#### **Model Solution for Q.A.(a)**

**Step 1 — Classification of each cost into PAF category**

| Cost Item | Amount (₹ L) | Category |
|---|---|---|
| New product design review | 3.5 | **P** |
| Incoming raw material inspection | 2.0 | **A** |
| Scrap and waste disposal | 6.5 | **IF** |
| Warranty repairs | 9.0 | **EF** |
| Quality circle team | 1.5 | **P** |
| Final product testing | 3.0 | **A** |
| Customer complaint handling | 4.5 | **EF** |
| Calibration of CMM | 1.0 | **A** |
| Process FMEA studies | 2.0 | **P** |
| Re-inspection of reworked parts | 2.5 | **IF** |
| Supplier rating and audit | 1.5 | **P** |
| Field service engineer salaries | 3.0 | **EF** |

**Step 2 — Category Totals**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 3.5 + 1.5 + 2.0 + 1.5 = 8.5 \;\text{₹ Lakhs} \\
A &= 2.0 + 3.0 + 1.0 = 6.0 \;\text{₹ Lakhs} \\
IF &= 6.5 + 2.5 = 9.0 \;\text{₹ Lakhs} \\
EF &= 9.0 + 4.5 + 3.0 = 16.5 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total COQ and % of Sales**

$$\text{COQ}_{total} = 8.5 + 6.0 + 9.0 + 16.5 = 40.0 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{COQ\%} = \frac{40.0}{250} \times 100 = 16.0\%$$

**Step 4 — COQ per Unit**

$$\text{COQ/unit} = \frac{40.0 \times 1{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = 80 \;\text{₹/unit}$$

**Step 5 — Quality Cost Index**

$$\text{COGQ} = P + A = 8.5 + 6.0 = 14.5 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{COPQ} = IF + EF = 9.0 + 16.5 = 25.5 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{QCI} = \frac{\text{COPQ}}{\text{COGQ}} = \frac{25.5}{14.5} \approx 1.76$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Interpretation (for 2 marks of QCI)**: QCI = 1.76 > 1, indicating the company spends **₹1.76 on failure for every ₹1 spent on prevention**. This is a *reactive* cost profile. World-class Six Sigma firms target **QCI < 0.5**. The firm urgently needs DMAIC projects focused on **EF reduction** (warranty + field service) — these are the largest single bucket (₹16.5 L).

#### **Model Solution for Q.A.(b) — Iceberg Model [5 marks]**

The **Iceberg Model** of quality costs, popularized by **Harrington (1987)**, visualizes quality costs as an iceberg floating in the ocean:

- **Tip (Above Water)**: Visible, easily measured costs such as scrap, warranty claims, rework labor, and rejected lots. These represent only **~30%** of total quality cost.
- **Just Below the Surface**: Measurable but often aggregated costs like inspection labor, equipment depreciation, training, and audit expenses (~25%).
- **Deep Below Surface (Hidden)**: Opportunity costs that are *not* captured in financial ledgers — lost customer goodwill, lost future sales, employee demotivation, supplier-side rework, design iteration loops, and underutilized capacity. These represent **~45%** of true quality cost.

**Why hidden costs are dangerous:**
1. They escape the income statement entirely.
2. They accumulate silently over years, eroding market share.
3. They create *false comfort* — management believes COQ is low.

**How Six Sigma uncovers them:**
- **DMAIC Define phase** maps the VOC (Voice of Customer) → reveals lost goodwill.
- **Measure phase** uses **Activity-Based Costing (ABC)** and **Pareto Analysis** → quantifies hidden waste.
- **Analyze phase** uses **Fishbone / 5-Why** → finds root causes of opportunity losses.
- **Control phase** tracks leading indicators → prevents recurrence of hidden losses.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style Question]**
>
> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Modern View, Prevention Leverage & DMAIC**
>
> **Q.B.(a)** Compare the **Traditional View** of quality cost with the **Modern (Six Sigma) View**. Use a neat diagram and explain why the modern view is preferred. [7 marks]
>
> **Q.B.(b)** A Six Sigma project team invested an additional **₹5,00,000** in prevention (design FMEA, employee certification, and SPC infrastructure). As a result, internal failure costs reduced by **₹18,00,000** and external failure costs reduced by **₹27,00,000** over the year. Calculate the **Prevention Leverage Ratio** and discuss its strategic significance. [7 marks]

#### **Model Solution for Q.B.(a) [7 marks]**

**1. Traditional View (Pre-1980s, also called the "Economic View"):**
- Assumes an **"Optimum Quality Level"** exists — a point where total cost is minimum.
- Beyond this point, additional prevention/appraisal cost exceeds the savings in failure cost.
- The total cost curve is **U-shaped**.
- Encourages accepting some level of defects as "economically optimal."

**2. Modern (Six Sigma) View:**
- The total cost curve is **continuously declining** — there is *no* optimum below zero defects.
- Prevention is the **most leveraged** cost category; appraisal costs decrease as **automated inspection** replaces manual checks.
- Failure costs are **completely eliminable** through DMAIC and design-for-six-sigma (DFSS) methodologies.
- The "optimum" is at **zero defects (6σ)**, not at some U-curve minimum.

**3. Diagrammatic Comparison:**

```
TRADITIONAL VIEW:                 MODERN (SIX SIGMA) VIEW:
Total COQ                         Total COQ
   |    U-curve                      |\ 
   |   /    \                         | \____  Continuously falling
   |  /      \                        |  \____\______
   | /        \                       |   \__________\____
   |/          \                      |    \______________\___
   +------ Quality level              +------ Quality level
                                          (rising sigma)
```

**4. Why Modern View is Preferred:**
- Empirical data (Motorola, GE, Honeywell) shows COPQ drops to **<1% of sales** at 6σ.
- The Traditional view is a **rationalization for mediocrity**; it justifies accepting defects.
- Six Sigma's modern view **aligns with customer expectations** of zero-defect delivery.
- It supports **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** philosophy, not static optimization.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B.(b) [7 marks]**

**Given Data:**
$$\Delta P = 5{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}, \quad \Delta IF = 18{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}, \quad \Delta EF = 27{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}$$

**Step 1 — Total COPQ savings**

$$\Delta \text{COPQ} = \Delta IF + \Delta EF = 18{,}00{,}000 + 27{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}$$

**Step 2 — Prevention Leverage Ratio**

$$L_p = \frac{\Delta \text{COPQ}}{\Delta P} = \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 9$$

**Step 3 — Net Savings (Bonus)**

$$\text{Net Benefit} = \Delta \text{COPQ} - \Delta P = 45{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}$$

**Step 4 — Return on Investment (ROI)**

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\Delta P} \times 100 = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 800\%$$

> [!TIP] **Strategic Significance (to be elaborated in 3–4 lines for full 7 marks):**
> - The prevention leverage of **9:1** means every ₹1 spent on prevention saved ₹9 in failure costs. This empirically validates Six Sigma's central thesis.
> - The **net benefit of ₹40 lakhs** can be reinvested in further Six Sigma projects, training, or R&D — creating a virtuous cycle of quality improvement.
> - The **800% ROI** is a powerful tool to convince top management to expand quality budgets. Senior leadership is far more likely to fund projects with quantified financial returns.
> - This case also illustrates the **Multiplication Effect** of prevention — failure costs decrease *disproportionately* more than prevention cost increases.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in COQ Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Mis-classifying costs**: Students often put "supplier audits" under Appraisal (it's **Prevention**). Memorize: *Audits, training, FMEA, design review → P; Inspection, testing, calibration → A; Scrap, rework, re-inspection → IF; Warranty, recalls, lawsuits, customer complaints → EF.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting units**: Always state **₹ / ₹ Lakhs / %** with every numerical answer. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
>
> 3. **Only computing Total COQ**: The question almost always asks for **P, A, IF, EF separately**. Showing only the total loses 50% of the marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping interpretation**: After every COQ calculation, you MUST comment on whether the result is good/bad, what the **QCI** indicates, and what action is needed. Pure arithmetic without interpretation fetches only partial credit.
>
> 5. **Confusing COGQ and COPQ**: COGQ = P + A; COPQ = IF + EF. Mixing them up corrupts the entire solution.
>
> 6. **Ignoring hidden costs**: When asked about "complete" COQ, mention the **Iceberg Model** and hidden opportunity costs — this is a frequently tested concept worth 2–3 marks.
>
> 7. **No diagram in long answers**: For 7+ mark questions, **always include a labeled diagram** (PAF structure, Iceberg model, or U-curve). It earns at least 1–2 easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **COQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure**.
- ✅ **COGQ = P + A** (money *spent* on quality); **COPQ = IF + EF** (money *lost* due to lack of quality).
- ✅ **Total COQ = COGQ + COPQ**.
- ✅ The **PAF Model** is the universal classification framework (Juran-Feigenbaum).
- ✅ **Prevention costs** are the *most leveraged* investment — they yield 5× to 10× returns in failure cost reduction.
- ✅ **External Failure (EF)** is the *most expensive* category — it includes warranty, recalls, lawsuits, and brand damage.
- ✅ **Modern (Six Sigma) view**: total COQ continuously decreases as quality improves — no U-curve optimum.
- ✅ **Quality Cost Index (QCI) = COPQ / COGQ**; target is **QCI < 1**, world-class is **QCI < 0.5**.
- ✅ **Prevention Leverage Ratio** $L_p = \Delta \text{COPQ} / \Delta P$ — quantifies the ROI on prevention spending.
- ✅ **Rule of Ten (1-10-100 Rule)**: cost of fixing a defect grows 10× at every stage — design → production → customer.
- ✅ **Iceberg Model**: 30% of quality costs are *visible*, 25% are *measurable but hidden*, 45% are *opportunity costs* (lost goodwill, lost sales, employee morale).
- ✅ **COQ as % of Sales**: World-class is **<5%**; 15–25% indicates serious quality problems demanding immediate DMAIC intervention.
- ✅ **DPMO** at 6σ is **3.4** defects per million opportunities; at 3σ it is **66,807** — a **20,000× difference**.
- ✅ **DMAIC's role in COQ**: Define (identify high-cost buckets via Pareto), Measure (quantify baseline), Analyze (root cause), Improve (eliminate root cause), Control (sustain savings).
- ✅ **Six Sigma is not free** — it requires investment in *prevention* (training, tools, design reviews) — but the failure-cost savings dwarf the prevention-cost investment.
- ✅ **Benchmarking COQ** across industries: Service 5–10%, Manufacturing 15–25%, Software 20–35%, Healthcare 30–40% (lowest maturity, highest opportunity).

> [!NOTE] **Final KTU Exam Tip**: Always structure your COQ answer as **(1) Tabulate** the costs by category, **(2) Compute** the four PAF totals, **(3) Sum** to get Total COQ, **(4) Compute** derived metrics (QCI, %, per-unit), **(5) Interpret** each result. This 5-step structure is the **gold standard** answer format used by toppers and approved by KTU model answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voice of Customer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voice of Customer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voice of Customer (VOC)** is a market research technique used in Six Sigma (specifically within the **DMAIC – Define Phase** and **DMADV – Define Phase**) that captures the customer's expectations, preferences, requirements, and dislikes about a product or service. It is the process of gathering and analyzing customer feedback to understand what matters most to them, so that those insights can be translated into measurable **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristics and engineering specifications.

In formal Six Sigma (George, 2005) terminology, VOC is defined as:

> *"The Voice of the Customer is the term used to describe the stated and unstated needs or requirements of the customer. It is the customer's perception of what is important, what creates value, and what must be achieved to delight the customer."*

The VOC framework is built on three core pillars:
1. **Customer Requirements (CRs)** – The raw, often unstructured, language used by customers.
2. **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** – Customer requirements translated into measurable, internal engineering metrics.
3. **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** – The resultant metric that closes the feedback loop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Chef**
> Imagine a chef (Six Sigma Team) preparing a dish. Before cooking, the chef walks to the dining hall and *listens* to the customers. Some say "I want it less spicy," some say "I want more cheese," and some say "I want it ready in 10 minutes." The chef does NOT simply start cooking — instead, the chef writes down every comment (raw VOC), groups similar comments together (e.g., "spice level," "cheese quantity," "delivery time"), and then converts them into kitchen-measurable terms like "cayenne = 2 grams per plate," "mozzarella = 50 grams," "plating time ≤ 10 min" (CTQ conversion). Finally, the chef cooks, serves, and re-checks with the customer if the dish is now perfect. **VOC is exactly this listening → grouping → translating → measuring → re-checking cycle applied to any engineering, manufacturing, or service process.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in VOC (KTU High-Yield Terms)

> [!TIP]
> **Bold-highlighted standard VOC metrics** you must memorize for KTU exams:
> - **CSAT (Customer Satisfaction Score)** – Usually on a 1–5 or 1–10 scale.
> - **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** – Ranges from **-100 to +100**.
> - **CES (Customer Effort Score)** – Measures ease of interaction.
> - **VOC Sample Size (n)** – Calculated using the standard sampling formula.
> - **CTQ Trees** – Hierarchical decomposition of customer needs.

### 1.4 Why VOC is the "First" Step in Six Sigma

Six Sigma methodology (DMAIC) begins with **Define**, and within Define, the very first sub-step is **VOC Capture**. Without VOC, a Six Sigma project is solving a problem that no customer cares about — a fatal waste of organizational resources. The mantra in Six Sigma is:

> *"If you cannot measure what the customer wants, you cannot improve what the customer experiences."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Framework of VOC (Step-by-Step Logic)

The VOC process is **sequential and iterative**. Below is the structured logic flow used by every Six Sigma Black Belt and Green Belt project:

1. **Identify Customer Segments** – Stratify the market into homogeneous groups (e.g., B2B vs B2C, age cohorts, geographic regions).
2. **Choose VOC Collection Method** – Select from surveys, interviews, focus groups, observations, complaint logs, social listening.
3. **Collect Raw Customer Data** – Capture the "voice" using open-ended questions (avoid leading questions).
4. **Translate & Clean the Data** – Convert colloquial customer language into standard terms (affinity mapping).
5. **Group into Affinity Clusters** – Use **Affinity Diagrams** to organize similar requirements.
6. **Prioritize Requirements** – Apply **Pareto Analysis (80/20 rule)** to identify the vital few CRs.
7. **Build CTQ Tree** – Translate top customer needs into measurable, internal CTQ variables.
8. **Validate with Customers** – Close the loop to ensure the translation is faithful.
9. **Feed into QFD / House of Quality** – Move CTQs to the design / improvement phase.

### 2.2 VOC Data Collection Methods — KTU Reference Matrix

| Method | Type | Best For | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Surveys / Questionnaires** | Quantitative | Large sample sizes | Statistically valid, easy to analyze | May miss deep emotional drivers |
| **One-on-One Interviews** | Qualitative | In-depth insights | Rich, contextual data | Time-consuming, small sample |
| **Focus Groups** | Qualitative | Idea generation | Group synergy, brainstorming | Dominant personalities may bias |
| **Observation (Gemba Walks)** | Qualitative | Service processes | Captures "unstated" needs (work-as-done vs work-as-imagined) | Hawthorne effect |
| **Complaint / Warranty Data** | Quantitative | Reactive issues | Real, unfiltered problems | Only captures dissatisfied customers |
| **Social Media / Reviews** | Quantitative | Modern digital VOC | High volume, real-time | Requires NLP/Text mining skills |
| **Customer Journey Mapping** | Qualitative | End-to-end experience | Identifies pain points at each touchpoint | Subjective interpretation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"List any four VOC methods,"* always state at least the first four (Surveys, Interviews, Focus Groups, Observation). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists these in the module descriptor.

### 2.3 The KANO Model — Classifying Customer Needs

The KANO Model (developed by Professor Noriaki Kano, 1984) is the most frequently tested VOC classification tool in KTU exams. It categorizes customer requirements into:

- **Must-Be (Basic) Requirements** – The minimum expected; absence causes extreme dissatisfaction, but presence does NOT increase satisfaction. (e.g., car brakes working, electricity in a hotel room).
- **One-Dimensional (Performance) Requirements** – Linear relationship; more is better. (e.g., fuel efficiency, processing speed).
- **Attractive (Delighter) Requirements** – Unexpected features that create excitement. (e.g., free Wi-Fi on a flight in 2003, heated seats).
- **Indifferent Requirements** – Customer does not care either way.
- **Reverse Requirements** – More of this feature actually decreases satisfaction.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** In a KANO questionnaire, every customer requirement is asked as a **positive question** ("If the product HAS this feature, how do you feel?") and a **negative question** ("If the product LACKS this feature, how do you feel?"). The combination of answers (Functional vs Dysfunctional form) places the requirement in one of the five KANO categories using a standard evaluation table.

### 2.4 CTQ Tree — Translation Hierarchy

A **CTQ Tree** (Critical-to-Quality Tree) is the Six Sigma tool that converts raw VOC statements into measurable, internal specifications. The hierarchy is:

- **Level 1: Customer Need (VOC)** – Stated in customer language.
- **Level 2: Customer Requirement (CR)** – Generalized statement of the need.
- **Level 3: CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** – Translated into a measurable, internal metric with a target and unit.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Tool | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **VOC Sample Size (Finite)** | $n = \dfrac{N \cdot z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2(N-1) + z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}$ | Calculate statistically valid survey size where $N$ = population, $z$ = confidence, $p$ = proportion, $e$ = margin of error. |
| 2 | **VOC Sample Size (Infinite)** | $n = \dfrac{z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | For large/unknown populations. |
| 3 | **CSAT Score (%)** | $\text{CSAT} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Satisfied Customers (4 or 5)}}{\text{Total Respondents}} \times 100$ | Measures % of happy customers. |
| 4 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $\text{NPS} = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Single-metric loyalty gauge. Range **-100 to +100**. |
| 5 | **Pareto Cumulative %** | $\text{Cum \%}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i} \times 100$ | Identifies the "vital few" complaints (typically the first 20% of categories causing 80% of issues). |
| 6 | **Kano Categorization** | Functional $\times$ Dysfunctional cross-tabulation | Maps requirements into Must-Be / One-Dimensional / Attractive / Indifferent / Reverse. |
| 7 | **CTQ Specification Limit** | $\text{USL} = \mu + 3\sigma \quad ; \quad \text{LSL} = \mu - 3\sigma$ | Translates VOC target into Six-Sigma capability limits. |
| 8 | **Affinity Grouping** | Qualitative clustering of $\geq 30$ raw VOC statements | Reduces noise and identifies dominant themes. |
| 9 | **Voice of Business (VOB) $\cap$ VOC** | Intersection matrix (QFD House of Quality) | Aligns customer needs with business strategy. |
| 10 | **VOC-to-CTQ Conversion Ratio** | Qualitative engineering judgment + QFD roof matrix | Bridges "what customer says" to "what engineer designs." |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Notice how absolute value is written as $\vert x \vert$ (using `\vert`) in all equations above to **avoid breaking** the markdown table syntax. KTU students should replicate this in their answer sheets when using pipe symbols.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

VOC is the foundation of **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)**, **Quality Function Deployment (QFD / House of Quality)**, **Lean Startup validation**, and **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.2 (Requirements for products and services)**. In modern Industry 4.0 environments, VOC is increasingly automated using **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **sentiment analysis**, and **Text Mining** on social media streams, call center transcripts, and online reviews — feeding directly into AI-driven CTQ prediction models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — VOC Sample Size Formula (Infinite Population Case)

The most commonly tested quantitative derivation in KTU exams for Module 1.

**Step 1: Start with the standard error of a proportion.**

The standard error of an estimated proportion $\hat{p}$ from a sample is given by:

$$
SE(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}
$$

**Step 2: Define the desired precision (margin of error, $e$).**

We want the sample estimate $\hat{p}$ to lie within $\pm e$ of the true population proportion $p$ with a confidence level corresponding to a Z-score.

$$
e = z \cdot SE(\hat{p}) = z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $n$ algebraically.**

Square both sides of the equation to remove the square root:

$$
e^2 = z^2 \cdot \dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}
$$

Multiply both sides by $n$:

$$
n \cdot e^2 = z^2 \cdot p(1-p)
$$

Divide both sides by $e^2$:

$$
n = \dfrac{z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}
$$

**Final derived formula (Infinite Population VOC Sample Size):**

$$
\boxed{n = \dfrac{z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}}
$$

> This is the *conservative* maximum sample size, achieved when $p = 0.5$ (giving $p(1-p) = 0.25$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Finite Population Correction (FPC) Applied to VOC

When the population $N$ is known and small, we apply the **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** factor.

**Step 1: Write the FPC factor.**

$$
\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}
$$

**Step 2: Multiply the infinite-population $n$ by the FPC factor.**

$$
n_{\text{finite}} = n_{\text{infinite}} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}
$$

**Step 3: Algebraic manipulation to the standard closed-form.**

Square both sides:

$$
n_{\text{finite}}^2 = n_{\text{infinite}}^2 \cdot \dfrac{N - n_{\text{finite}}}{N-1}
$$

Substitute $n_{\text{infinite}}^2 = \dfrac{z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$:

$$
n_{\text{finite}}^2 = \dfrac{z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2} \cdot \dfrac{N - n_{\text{finite}}}{N-1}
$$

Multiply both sides by $e^2 (N-1)$:

$$
n_{\text{finite}}^2 \cdot e^2 (N-1) = z^2 \cdot p(1-p) \cdot (N - n_{\text{finite}})
$$

Expand the right side:

$$
n_{\text{finite}}^2 \cdot e^2 (N-1) = z^2 \cdot p(1-p) \cdot N - z^2 \cdot p(1-p) \cdot n_{\text{finite}}
$$

Rearrange as a quadratic in $n_{\text{finite}}$:

$$
e^2 (N-1) \cdot n_{\text{finite}}^2 + z^2 \cdot p(1-p) \cdot n_{\text{finite}} - z^2 \cdot p(1-p) \cdot N = 0
$$

Apply the quadratic formula $n = \dfrac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$
\boxed{n = \dfrac{-z^2 p(1-p) + \sqrt{[z^2 p(1-p)]^2 + 4 \cdot e^2 (N-1) \cdot z^2 p(1-p) \cdot N}}{2 \cdot e^2 (N-1)}}
$$

In the KTU exam, students are only expected to write the **standard textbook form** (without showing this full quadratic expansion):

$$
\boxed{n = \dfrac{N \cdot z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2(N-1) + z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** A Six Sigma Green Belt is conducting VOC for a college canteen. The student population is $N = 5000$. The team wants $95\%$ confidence ($z = 1.96$), expected proportion $p = 0.5$ (most conservative), and margin of error $e = 0.05$. Calculate the required sample size.

**Step 1: Substitute the values into the infinite-population formula first (sanity check).**

$$
n = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1-0.5)}{(0.05)^2}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{3.8416 \cdot 0.25}{0.0025}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{0.9604}{0.0025} = 384.16 \approx 385
$$

**Step 2: Apply Finite Population Correction.**

$$
n = \dfrac{5000 \cdot 1.96^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{0.05^2 (5000-1) + 1.96^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{5000 \cdot 0.9604}{0.0025 \cdot 4999 + 0.9604}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{4802}{12.4975 + 0.9604}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{4802}{13.4579} = 356.8 \approx 357
$$

**Conclusion:** The Green Belt team should distribute **357 surveys** to obtain statistically valid VOC data with the desired precision.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — VOC Tools vs KTU Cognitive Levels

| KTU Bloom's Level | Typical Question | Tool Tested |
|---|---|---|
| **Remember (L1)** | Define VOC | Definition |
| **Understand (L2)** | Explain KANO categories | KANO Model |
| **Apply (L3)** | Calculate sample size for a survey | $n = \dfrac{z^2 p(1-p)}{e^2}$ |
| **Analyze (L4)** | Compare VOC methods, build CTQ tree | Affinity Diagram, CTQ Tree |
| **Evaluate (L5)** | Critique VOC data for bias | Survey Design Principles |
| **Create (L6)** | Design a VOC plan for a new product | Full VOC Project Plan |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions (14 marks) typically target **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** levels. A safe strategy is to combine a calculation (sample size) with a qualitative tool (CTQ tree / KANO) in a single answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — End-to-End VOC Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Business Problem] --> B[Identify Customer Segments]
    B --> C[Select VOC Collection Method]
    C --> D1[Surveys]
    C --> D2[Interviews]
    C --> D3[Focus Groups]
    C --> D4[Observation / Gemba]
    C --> D5[Complaint Data Mining]
    D1 --> E[Raw VOC Data Capture]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Data Cleaning and Translation]
```
