```mermaid
    F --> G[Affinity Diagram Clustering]
    G --> H[Pareto Prioritization]
    H --> I[CTQ Tree Construction]
    I --> J[Customer Validation Loop]
    J --> K[Feed into QFD House of Quality]
    K --> L[Define Phase Exit and DMAIC Measure]
    J --> M[Refine VOC if Discrepancy]
    M --> G
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — KANO Model Categorization Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KANO[KANO MODEL FOR VOC CLASSIFICATION]
        A1[Customer Requirement] --> B1{Functional Form Answer}
        B1 -->|Like| C1[Attractive Delighter]
        B1 -->|Must Be| C2[One Dimensional]
        B1 -->|Neutral| C3[Indifferent]
        B1 -->|Dislike| C4[Reverse]
        A1 --> D1{Dysfunctional Form Answer}
        D1 -->|Like| E1[Questionable]
        D1 -->|Must Be| E2[Must Be Basic]
        D1 -->|Neutral| E3[Indifferent]
        D1 -->|Dislike| E4[One Dimensional]
    end
    C2 --> F1[High Satisfaction Impact Linear]
    E2 --> F2[Extreme Dissatisfaction if Absent]
    C1 --> F3[WOW Factor Surprise Element]
    E4 --> F1
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — CTQ Tree Hierarchical Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VOC Statement: I want my online order delivered fast] --> B[Customer Requirement: Timely Delivery]
    B --> C1[CTQ 1: Order Acknowledgement Time]
    B --> C2[CTQ 2: Dispatch to Delivery Duration]
    B --> C3[CTQ 3: Delivery Time Window Accuracy]
    C1 --> D1[Target: Less than 2 minutes]
    C2 --> D2[Target: Less than 24 hours]
    C3 --> D3[Target: Within 30 minutes of promised slot]
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — House of Quality (QFD) Block Architecture Mapping VOC to Engineering Specs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOQ[HOUSE OF QUALITY QFD ARCHITECTURE]
        A[Block 1: WHATs - Customer Requirements from VOC] 
        B[Block 2: HOWs - Engineering Characteristics]
        C[Block 3: Relationship Matrix - Correlation Strength]
        D[Block 4: Roof Matrix - Inter HOW Correlations]
        E[Block 5: Planning Matrix - Importance Weights]
        F[Block 6: Technical Benchmarks - Competitive Analysis]
        G[Block 7: Target Values - Engineering Specs from CTQ]
        A --> C
        B --> C
        C --> G
        B --> D
        A --> E
        E --> G
        F --> C
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Define Voice of Customer (VOC). List any four methods used to capture VOC in a Six Sigma project.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Voice of Customer (VOC) is the process of capturing the stated and unstated needs, expectations, preferences, and requirements of the customer regarding a product or service. It is the first and most critical step of the **Define Phase** in the Six Sigma DMAIC cycle, providing the raw data from which Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) characteristics are derived.
>
> **Four VOC Methods (1.5 Marks – 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Surveys / Structured Questionnaires** – Quantitative method for large sample sizes.
> 2. **One-on-One Customer Interviews** – Qualitative, in-depth exploration of needs.
> 3. **Focus Groups** – Group-based brainstorming with 6–10 customers.
> 4. **Direct Observation (Gemba Walks)** – Watching the customer interact with the product/service in their natural environment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the KANO Model. List its five categories of customer requirements.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **KANO Model** (developed by Prof. Noriaki Kano, 1984) is a VOC classification framework that categorizes customer requirements based on their ability to cause satisfaction or dissatisfaction.
>
> **Five Categories (2.4 Marks – 0.48 each):**
> 1. **Must-Be (Basic) Requirements** – Minimum expectations; absence causes extreme dissatisfaction, presence does not increase satisfaction (e.g., safety brakes in a car).
> 2. **One-Dimensional (Performance) Requirements** – Linear satisfaction; more is better (e.g., fuel efficiency).
> 3. **Attractive (Delighter) Requirements** – Unexpected features that create excitement (e.g., heated seats).
> 4. **Indifferent Requirements** – Customer is neutral; neither satisfies nor dissatisfies.
> 5. **Reverse Requirements** – More of this feature decreases satisfaction (e.g., too many pop-up ads).
>
> **Significance (0.6 Marks):** KANO helps Six Sigma teams prioritize which VOC items to convert into CTQs — typically focusing on **Must-Be** (defects to eliminate) and **Attractive** (competitive differentiators).

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme ESE]**
> **(a)** A Six Sigma Green Belt team in an engineering college is conducting a VOC study for the new student portal. The total student population is $N = 2000$. The team desires **95% confidence**, an expected proportion of $p = 0.5$, and a margin of error of $e = 0.04$. Calculate the required sample size using the finite population formula. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **CTQ Tree** concept and show how three raw VOC statements from students can be converted into measurable CTQs with targets. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — Sample Size Calculation (7 Marks)**

> **Step 1 (1 Mark) — Identify given values and write the formula.**
> Given: $N = 2000$, $z = 1.96$ (for 95% confidence), $p = 0.5$, $e = 0.04$.
> Formula: $n = \dfrac{N \cdot z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2(N-1) + z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}$

> **Step 2 (1 Mark) — Compute the numerator.**
> $z^2 = (1.96)^2 = 3.8416$
> $p(1-p) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$
> Numerator $= 2000 \times 3.8416 \times 0.25 = 2000 \times 0.9604 = 1920.8$

> **Step 3 (1 Mark) — Compute the denominator step-by-step.**
> $e^2 = (0.04)^2 = 0.0016$
> $e^2(N-1) = 0.0016 \times 1999 = 3.1984$
> $z^2 p(1-p) = 3.8416 \times 0.25 = 0.9604$
> Denominator $= 3.1984 + 0.9604 = 4.1588$

> **Step 4 (1 Mark) — Final division.**
> $n = \dfrac{1920.8}{4.1588} = 461.85 \approx 462$

> **Step 5 (1 Mark) — Verification using infinite population (sanity check).**
> $n_{\infty} = \dfrac{3.8416 \times 0.25}{0.0016} = 600.25 \approx 601$
> Since $N = 2000$ is not very large relative to $n_{\infty}$, the FPC gives a smaller, more efficient sample.

> **Step 6 (1 Mark) — Comparison and Conclusion.**
> $462 < 601$, confirming the FPC reduced the sample size by $139$. The team should distribute **462 surveys**.
>
> **Step 7 (1 Mark) — Interpretation.**
> With 462 valid responses, the team can estimate any proportion (e.g., % students dissatisfied) within $\pm 4\%$ of the true value with 95% confidence.

---

**Part (b) — CTQ Tree with Example (7 Marks)**

> **Step 1 (1 Mark) — Define CTQ Tree.**
> A **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) Tree** is a hierarchical Six Sigma tool that translates raw, qualitative Voice of Customer (VOC) statements into quantitative, measurable, internal engineering specifications. It has three levels: **VOC → CR → CTQ**.

> **Step 2 (1 Mark) — State three raw VOC statements from students (the input).**
> 1. "I want my exam results to load fast on the portal."
> 2. "The portal should not crash during admission time."
> 3. "I want to download my hall ticket in one click."

> **Step 3 (1.5 Marks) — Level 1 → Level 2 (VOC → Customer Requirements).**
> | Raw VOC | Generalized Customer Requirement (CR) |
> |---|---|
> | Results load fast | Fast Page Response Time |
> | Portal should not crash | High System Availability / Uptime |
> | Hall ticket in one click | Simplified Document Access |

> **Step 4 (2 Marks) — Level 2 → Level 3 (CR → CTQ with targets).**
> | CR | CTQ (Measurable Metric) | Target | Unit |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Fast Page Response | Average Page Load Time | $\leq 2$ | seconds |
> | No Crash | System Uptime Percentage | $\geq 99.9$ | % |
> | One-Click Access | Clicks Required to Download Hall Ticket | $= 1$ | click |

> **Step 5 (1.5 Marks) — Closing the loop / Validation.**
> After deployment, the team must **re-survey** a sample of students to verify that the CTQ targets actually deliver the originally stated VOC needs. If not, refine the targets iteratively (PDCA cycle).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme ESE]**
> **(a)** Explain the **KANO Model** in detail with a neat sketch. How does it help a Six Sigma team in VOC prioritization? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare any **four VOC data collection methods** with their relative strengths and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — KANO Model Explanation (7 Marks)**

> **Step 1 (1 Mark) — Introduction.**
> The **KANO Model**, developed by Professor Noriaki Kano of Tokyo Rika University in 1984, is a VOC classification theory that distinguishes between three fundamental types of product/service requirements. It helps Six Sigma teams decide which features will have the most strategic impact on customer satisfaction.

> **Step 2 (2 Marks) — Description of the three primary categories with satisfaction curves.**
> - **Must-Be (Basic) Requirements** — These are taken for granted by the customer. Their absence causes extreme dissatisfaction, but their mere presence does not increase satisfaction. *Example:* Power windows working in a car; Wi-Fi working in a hotel.
> - **One-Dimensional (Performance) Requirements** — Customer satisfaction is linearly proportional to the level of fulfillment. Better execution = higher satisfaction; worse execution = dissatisfaction. *Example:* Battery life of a phone, fuel efficiency of a vehicle.
> - **Attractive (Delighters)** — Unexpected features that cause disproportionate satisfaction. They are not expected, so absence does not dissatisfy. *Example:* Free in-flight Wi-Fi when competitors charge for it; complimentary breakfast.

> **Step 3 (1 Mark) — Two additional (secondary) categories.**
> - **Indifferent Requirements** — Customer is neutral; implementation has no effect on satisfaction.
> - **Reverse Requirements** — Higher level of fulfillment actually decreases satisfaction (e.g., too many security pop-ups).

> **Step 4 (1 Mark) — Sketch description.**
> A neat graph should plot **Customer Satisfaction (Y-axis)** vs **Level of Requirement Fulfillment (X-axis)**. The **Must-Be curve** rises steeply from 0 but plateaus quickly; the **One-Dimensional curve** is a straight diagonal line; the **Attractive curve** is exponential — flat for a long time, then surging upward. (Student should sketch three curves, one for each category.)

> **Step 5 (2 Marks) — Strategic utility for Six Sigma VOC prioritization.**
> 1. **Focus on Must-Be** — These are competitive entry-tickets; failure to deliver them is fatal. Six Sigma teams eliminate defects in these areas first.
> 2. **Optimize One-Dimensional** — These are competitive differentiators; teams use DMAIC to continuously improve them.
> 3. **Innovate with Attractive** — These drive market leadership; DFSS (Design for Six Sigma) projects target them.
> 4. **De-prioritize Indifferent** — Avoid wasting resources on features customers do not value.
> 5. **Avoid Reverse** — A clear understanding of customer segments prevents over-engineering.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison of Four VOC Methods (7 Marks)**

> **Step 1 (1.75 Marks each — total 7 Marks).** A structured comparison follows.

| # | Method | Type | Strengths | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Surveys / Questionnaires** | Quantitative | Reaches large samples; statistically valid; easy to replicate; cost-effective for broad populations. | May miss deep emotional drivers; risk of low response rate; poorly worded questions introduce bias. |
| 2 | **One-on-One Interviews** | Qualitative | Captures rich, contextual, emotional data; allows probing follow-up questions; builds rapport. | Time-consuming and expensive; small sample size; interviewer bias possible; difficult to compare results. |
| 3 | **Focus Groups** | Qualitative | Generates ideas through group synergy; reveals social influences on preferences; cost-efficient per participant. | Dominant personalities may bias discussion; groupthink can suppress dissenting views; logistics are complex. |
| 4 | **Direct Observation (Gemba Walks)** | Qualitative | Captures "unstated" needs and work-as-done reality; eliminates self-reporting bias; reveals contextual factors. | Hawthorne effect (people change behavior when watched); time-intensive; requires skilled observers. |

> **Closing Statement (0 Marks – integrated above):** In practice, Six Sigma teams use a **triangulation approach** — combining at least two of the above methods to validate the VOC data. For example, surveys identify the *what* (breadth), and interviews uncover the *why* (depth).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in VOC Questions:**
> 1. **Skipping the unit of CTQ targets** — Writing "$\leq 2$" without "seconds" loses 0.5 marks. Always state the unit.
> 2. **Using infinite-population formula when $N$ is given** — If the question provides $N$ (e.g., 2000 students), you MUST use the FPC formula. Using $n = z^2 p(1-p)/e^2$ is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Confusing CTQ and CR** — CR is in customer language; CTQ is in measurable, internal, engineering language. Examiners specifically look for this translation.
> 4. **Omitting Pareto (80/20) step** — VOC alone is not enough. A complete answer must include prioritization (Pareto, KANO, or MoSCoW).
> 5. **Forgetting to "close the loop"** — VOC is iterative. A top-scoring answer always mentions re-validation with the customer.
> 6. **Wrong Z-value** — 95% confidence $\rightarrow z = 1.96$; 99% confidence $\rightarrow z = 2.576$; 90% confidence $\rightarrow z = 1.645$. Memorize these.
> 7. **Not rounding up the sample size** — Always round **up** (ceiling), never down, to maintain margin of error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Voice of Customer (Module 1)**

- ✅ **VOC = Listening to the customer** — stated + unstated needs, preferences, complaints.
- ✅ **Position in DMAIC** — First sub-step of the **Define** phase; also used in **DMADV** Define phase.
- ✅ **Four Must-Know VOC Methods** — Surveys, Interviews, Focus Groups, Observation (Gemba).
- ✅ **Affinity Diagram** — clusters raw VOC into themes.
- ✅ **Pareto Analysis** — separates the *vital few* CRs from the *trivial many*.
- ✅ **CTQ Tree** — 3 levels: **VOC → CR → CTQ** (with target and unit).
- ✅ **KANO Model** — Must-Be, One-Dimensional, Attractive, Indifferent, Reverse.
- ✅ **House of Quality (QFD)** — final destination of translated VOC; integrates WHATs, HOWs, relationships, and targets.
- ✅ **CSAT** = (Satisfied / Total) × 100; **NPS** = % Promoters − % Detractors; range **-100 to +100**.
- ✅ **Sample size formulas** — $n = \dfrac{z^2 p(1-p)}{e^2}$ (infinite) and $n = \dfrac{N z^2 p(1-p)}{e^2(N-1) + z^2 p(1-p)}$ (finite).
- ✅ **Key Z-values** — 1.645 (90%), 1.96 (95%), 2.576 (99%).
- ✅ **Most conservative $p$** — Use $p = 0.5$ when proportion is unknown.
- ✅ **Six Sigma constant** — $\text{USL} = \mu + 3\sigma$, $\text{LSL} = \mu - 3\sigma$ for CTQ capability.
- ✅ **Close the loop** — VOC is iterative; always re-validate with the customer after CTQ deployment.
- ✅ **Triangulation** — Use at least two VOC methods for validity.
- ✅ **Avoid leading questions** in surveys; avoid the **Hawthorne effect** during observation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, customer-driven planning and product development methodology that systematically translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific, measurable **engineering and technical characteristics** throughout each stage of the product or service development lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *QFD is a systematic method for ensuring that the customer's needs and requirements are accurately reflected in the final product or service by deploying their priorities throughout the engineering, manufacturing, and service delivery processes.*

The technique is widely recognized by its most iconic visualization — the **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a matrix-based planning tool that resembles the cross-section of a house, where customer requirements and technical features interlock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Origin:** QFD was originally conceived by **Yoji Akao** in Japan in **1966** and was first deployed by **Mitsubishi Heavy Industries** in **1972**. It later became a cornerstone of the **Toyota Production System** and was popularized in the West by **John Hauser** and **Don Clausing** in their seminal 1988 *Harvard Business Review* paper.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍽️

Imagine you are opening a **cloud kitchen** in Kerala and want to launch a new "Onam Sadhya Meal Box" for students.

- **Voice of the Customer (VOC):** You conduct surveys and learn that customers want: *affordable price, large portion, hygienic packaging, less oil, traditional taste, easy microwave heating, and on-time delivery.*
- **Technical Requirements (Hows):** Your kitchen translates these into measurable specs: *cost ≤ ₹120, weight ≥ 350 g, food-grade packaging grade, oil content ≤ 15%, spice mix recipe v3.2, microwave-safe containers, delivery time ≤ 25 min.*
- **QFD Matrix:** You build a matrix showing how strongly each technical spec satisfies each customer need, then prioritize what matters most.

**In essence, QFD is the "translator" that converts what customers *want* into what engineers *must design*.** 🎯

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in QFD

| Metric | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Importance Rating** | **W_i** | Raw priority weight given by customers (typically **1–5 scale**) |
| **Relationship Strength** | **R_{ij}** | Correlation strength between CR and TR (typical values: **0, 1, 3, 9**) |
| **Technical Importance Rating** | **T_j** | Calculated weighted importance of each TR |
| **Normalized Weight (%)** | **W_i^{norm}** | Customer importance expressed as a **percentage** |
| **Absolute Importance** | **AI_j** | Sum of all weighted relationships for each TR |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the relationship symbols: **9 = Strong**, **3 = Moderate**, **1 = Weak**, **0 = None / No correlation**.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Module Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme**, QFD is positioned in **Module 1** as a foundational **customer-centric quality planning tool** that bridges Six Sigma's *Define* and *Measure* phases with the *Analyze* phase. It is examined under **Course Outcome CO1**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why QFD? — The Strategic "Why"

- **Eliminates the "Throw-it-over-the-wall" syndrome** between Marketing, R&D, and Manufacturing.
- Converts **subjective customer desires** into **objective, measurable engineering specifications**.
- Reduces **late-stage design changes** by an estimated **30–50%** in industry case studies.
- Serves as the **DNA blueprint** linking VOC → Design → Process → Production.

---

## 2.2 The Four Phases of QFD (How It Operates)

| Phase | Name | Output Document | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | **Product Planning** | **House of Quality** | VOC → Product specs |
| **Phase 2** | **Product Design** | Deployment Matrix | Critical parts → Subsystems |
| **Phase 3** | **Process Planning** | Process Matrix | Parts → Manufacturing processes |
| **Phase 4** | **Production Planning** | Production Matrix | Processes → Production controls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the **Phase 1** deliverable and is the most heavily tested topic in KTU exams.

---

## 2.3 The Seven Building Blocks of the House of Quality 🏠

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — Placed on the *left wall*. Customer's verbalized needs.
2. **Technical Requirements (Hows)** — Placed on the *ceiling/roof*. Engineering metrics.
3. **Relationship Matrix (Center)** — Intersects Whats and Hows with symbols (●, ○, △).
4. **Technical Correlations (Roof)** — *Triangular top*. Shows interaction between technical requirements (Strong Positive ++, Positive +, Negative –, Strong Negative —).
5. **Customer Importance Ratings (Left)** — Raw weights from VOC data.
6. **Competitive Assessment (Left Bottom)** — Benchmark vs. competitors.
7. **Technical Importance Ratings & Targets (Bottom)** — Computed output.

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

**Step 1 — Capture VOC:** Use surveys, focus groups, Kano model, complaint logs.
*Why?* Without raw VOC, the entire structure is baseless.

**Step 2 — Affinity Diagram:** Group raw VOC statements into **affinity clusters** (CRs).
*Why?* Eliminates redundancy; creates manageable categories.

**Step 3 — Weight the CRs:** Assign importance ratings (typically **1–5**).
*Why?* Allows prioritization by customer-perceived importance.

**Step 4 — Define Technical Requirements:** Translate CRs into measurable engineering specs.
*Why?* Engineers need quantifiable targets, not opinions.

**Step 5 — Build the Relationship Matrix:** Assign symbolic strengths (**● = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1, blank = 0**).
*Why?* Identifies which TRs best serve which CRs.

**Step 6 — Build the Roof (Correlations):** Show how TRs interact (synergy vs. trade-off).
*Why?* Alerts designers to conflicting specifications.

**Step 7 — Compute Technical Importance Ratings:** Apply the weighted-sum formula.
*Why?* Tells engineering which TRs are most critical to design.

**Step 8 — Set Target Values:** Engineering specifies measurable goals.
*Why?* Closes the loop from customer need to design target.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 🧮

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the **verified, mathematically sound** formulas tested in KTU examinations. Memorize these as a *standalone set*.

$$
W_i^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{W_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} W_k} \times 100
$$

$$
T_j \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \times R_{ij}
$$

$$
AI_j \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{ij}
$$

$$
\text{Normalized Technical Importance}_j \;=\; \frac{T_j}{\sum_{k=1}^{m} T_k} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Relationship Symbol Codes: } 9 \text{(Strong)}, \quad 3 \text{(Moderate)}, \quad 1 \text{(Weak)}, \quad 0 \text{(None)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In LaTeX-rendered KTU papers, the absolute value of a matrix or set is often written as `\vert x \vert` (not `|x|`) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Exam tip: when in doubt, write "the magnitude of x" in words.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive:** Ford used QFD on the Taurus to dramatically cut warranty costs.
- **Software:** QFD maps user stories to architectural modules (Agile + QFD hybrid).
- **Healthcare:** Designing ergonomic hospital beds by translating nurse/patient needs.
- **Civil Engineering:** Customizing green building specs to client sustainability priorities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Technical Importance Rating (T_j)

### 3.1.1 The Conceptual Foundation

The **Technical Importance Rating (T_j)** quantifies how *critical* a particular Technical Requirement (TR) is, weighted by how strongly it satisfies the most important Customer Requirements (CRs).

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Derivation

We begin with a set of **n customer requirements** indexed by **i ∈ {1, 2, ..., n}**, each with a customer-assigned weight **W_i**, and a set of **m technical requirements** indexed by **j ∈ {1, 2, ..., m}**, where each pair (i, j) carries a relationship strength **R_{ij}**.

The total customer-weighted importance for any technical requirement **j** is defined as the weighted sum of all relationship strengths linking it to customer requirements:

$$
T_j \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot R_{ij}
$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
T_j \;=\; W_1 \cdot R_{1j} \;+\; W_2 \cdot R_{2j} \;+\; W_3 \cdot R_{3j} \;+\; \cdots \;+\; W_n \cdot R_{nj}
$$

Using explicit numeric weights:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_j \;&=\; (W_1 \times R_{1j}) + (W_2 \times R_{2j}) + (W_3 \times R_{3j}) \\
     &\quad + (W_4 \times R_{4j}) + \cdots + (W_n \times R_{nj})
\end{aligned}
$$

The **relative (normalized) technical importance** is:

$$
\text{Normalized } T_j \;=\; \frac{T_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} T_j} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The vector **T = (T_1, T_2, ..., T_m)** is what engineers use to **rank-order** their design priorities. The TR with the highest **T_j** value is the most critical design lever.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Problem)

### Problem Statement

A manufacturing firm is designing a new **electric kettle**. The Quality team has identified **4 Customer Requirements (CRs)** and **4 Technical Requirements (TRs)**. Compute the **Technical Importance Rating (T_j)** for each TR.

**Step 1 — Customer Importance Weights (W_i):**

$$
W_1 = 5, \quad W_2 = 4, \quad W_3 = 3, \quad W_4 = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Relationship Matrix (using 9, 3, 1, 0 coding):**

| Customer Requirement (CR) | TR_1 (Boil Time) | TR_2 (Power) | TR_3 (Capacity) | TR_4 (Safety Lock) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CR_1 — Fast boiling** (W_1 = 5) | 9 | 9 | 1 | 0 |
| **CR_2 — Low cost** (W_2 = 4) | 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 |
| **CR_3 — Large capacity** (W_3 = 3) | 1 | 0 | 9 | 0 |
| **CR_4 — Safe to use** (W_4 = 2) | 0 | 3 | 0 | 9 |

**Step 3 — Compute T_j for each Technical Requirement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 \;&=\; (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 3) + (3 \times 1) + (2 \times 0) \\
     \;&=\; 45 + 12 + 3 + 0 \\
     \;&=\; 60
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 \;&=\; (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 3) + (3 \times 0) + (2 \times 3) \\
     \;&=\; 45 + 12 + 0 + 6 \\
     \;&=\; 63
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 \;&=\; (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 9) + (2 \times 0) \\
     \;&=\; 5 + 4 + 27 + 0 \\
     \;&=\; 36
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_4 \;&=\; (5 \times 0) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 0) + (2 \times 9) \\
     \;&=\; 0 + 4 + 0 + 18 \\
     \;&=\; 22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Normalized Importance (%) and Rank:**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{4} T_j \;=\; 60 + 63 + 36 + 22 \;=\; 181
$$

$$
\text{Normalized } T_1 \;=\; \frac{60}{181} \times 100 \;=\; 33.15\%
$$

$$
\text{Normalized } T_2 \;=\; \frac{63}{181} \times 100 \;=\; 34.81\%
$$

$$
\text{Normalized } T_3 \;=\; \frac{36}{181} \times 100 \;=\; 19.89\%
$$

$$
\text{Normalized } T_4 \;=\; \frac{22}{181} \times 100 \;=\; 12.15\%
$$

### 📊 Final Analytical Matrix

| Rank | Technical Requirement | T_j | Normalized (%) | Engineering Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1 | TR_2 — Power (Wattage) | **63** | **34.81%** | **Highest Priority** |
| 🥈 2 | TR_1 — Boil Time | **60** | **33.15%** | **High Priority** |
| 🥉 3 | TR_3 — Capacity | **36** | **19.89%** | Medium Priority |
| 4 | TR_4 — Safety Lock | **22** | **12.15%** | Standard Priority |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The design team must focus on optimizing **Power (TR_2)** and **Boil Time (TR_1)** first, as together they account for **~68%** of the customer-weighted importance. Safety (TR_4), while essential, ranks lowest because customers ranked it lowest in the VOC survey — *though it remains non-negotiable for regulatory reasons.*

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Normalized Customer Weight

Given raw weights **W_1, W_2, ..., W_n**, the normalized weight for CR *i* is:

$$
W_i^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{W_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} W_k} \times 100
$$

**Verification for our example:**

$$
\sum W_i \;=\; 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 \;=\; 14
$$

$$
W_1^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{5}{14} \times 100 \;=\; 35.71\%
$$

$$
W_2^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{4}{14} \times 100 \;=\; 28.57\%
$$

$$
W_3^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{3}{14} \times 100 \;=\; 21.43\%
$$

$$
W_4^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{2}{14} \times 100 \;=\; 14.29\%
$$

**Sanity check:** Sum of all normalized weights must equal **100%**.

$$
35.71 + 28.57 + 21.43 + 14.29 \;=\; 100.00 \;\checkmark
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of QFD

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below renders the **functional flow of the House of Quality** as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**, since rendering the literal cross-sectioned "house" geometry in Mermaid is non-trivial. Each block represents a *section* of the actual House of Quality.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["VOC INPUT LAYER Customer Surveys Focus Groups Kano Analysis Complaint Logs"]
    B["AFFINITY DIAGRAM Cluster Raw VOC into Customer Requirements CRs"]
    C["CUSTOMER IMPORTANCE Assign Raw Weights W_i Scale 1 to 5"]
    D["TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS Translate CRs into Engineering Hows TRs"]
    E["RELATIONSHIP MATRIX Central Body of HOQ Symbols 9 3 1 0"]
    F["TECHNICAL CORRELATIONS ROOF Tradeoffs Among TRs Strong Weak Negative"]
    G["COMPETITIVE ASSESSMENT Benchmark Against Competitors A B C"]
    H["TECHNICAL IMPORTANCE RATING Compute T_j Weighted Sum Output"]
    I["TARGET VALUES Final Engineering Specifications and Limits"]
    J["DESIGN OUTPUT Engineering Blueprint and Validation Plan"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> H
    E --> G
    D --> F
    F --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    G --> I

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 🔍 Reading the Diagram

- The **centerpiece** is the **Relationship Matrix** (Block E) — the actual "body" of the House of Quality.
- The **left wall** (Blocks A → B → C) feeds customer requirements with their weights into the matrix.
- The **ceiling** (Block D) drops down technical requirements into the matrix.
- The **roof** (Block F) sits atop the ceiling, mapping TR-to-TR correlations.
- The **floor** (Blocks H → I → J) computes the weighted output and emits engineering targets.

---

## 4.2 ASCII Schematic of the House of Quality (For Quick Recall)

```
        +------------------------------------------------+
        |         TECHNICAL CORRELATIONS (ROOF)          |
        |   TR1   TR2   TR3   TR4   TR5   TR6   TR7     |
+-------+-----+------+------+------+------+------+------+
|       | W_i | TR_1 | TR_2 | TR_3 | TR_4 | TR_5 | TR_6 |
| CR_1  |  5  |  ●   |  ●   |  △   |      |  ○   |      |
| CR_2  |  4  |  ○   |  ●   |      |  ○   |      |  △   |
| CR_3  |  3  |      |  △   |  ●   |  ●   |      |      |
| CR_4  |  2  |  △   |      |  ○   |      |  ●   |  ●   |
+-------+-----+------+------+------+------+------+------+
| Comp  | A   |  ●   |  △   |  ●   |  ○   |  △   |      |
| Bench | B   |  ○   |  ●   |  ○   |      |      |  △   |
+-------+-----+------+------+------+------+------+------+
| TECH  |     |  72  |  85  |  61  |  48  |  33  |  19  |
| IMP   |     |      |      |      |      |      |      |
+-------+-----+------+------+------+------+------+------+
| TARG  |     | 8min |1500W | 1.5L | 5x   | IPX4 | UL   |
| ETS   |     |      |      |      |      |      |      |
+-------+-----+------+------+------+------+------+------+
```

**Legend:** ● = Strong (9), ○ = Moderate (3), △ = Weak (1), blank = None (0)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Mention its origin.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a systematic, customer-focused planning technique used to translate the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific **engineering and technical requirements** at each stage of product or service development. It is graphically represented through the **House of Quality (HOQ)** matrix.
>
> **Origin (1 Mark):** QFD was developed in Japan by **Yoji Akao in 1966**, with its first industrial deployment at **Mitsubishi Heavy Industries in 1972**. It was later popularized globally through **Toyota** and the **American Supplier Institute** in the 1980s.

---

### **Q2. List any four components of the House of Quality.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ½ Mark Each):**

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — Verbalized needs of the customer.
2. **Technical Requirements (Hows)** — Engineering metrics addressing the Whats.
3. **Relationship Matrix** — Central body linking Whats with Hows using symbols (9, 3, 1, 0).
4. **Technical Correlations (Roof)** — Triangular top depicting interactions among technical requirements.
5. **Customer Importance Ratings** — Priority weights for each CR.
6. **Competitive Assessment** — Benchmarking against rival products.
7. **Technical Importance Ratings & Target Values** — Final engineering priorities and goals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout ⚠️**
> Students frequently confuse **Customer Importance Ratings** with **Technical Importance Ratings**. The former is the **input** (provided by customers via VOC); the latter is the **computed output** (engineered using the weighted-sum formula). **Mixing these will cost you 1 full mark.**

---

## 🅱️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Explain the concept and construction of the House of Quality in detail with a suitable diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept (2 Marks):**
The House of Quality (HOQ) is a **matrix-based planning tool** that visualizes the relationship between **what customers want (Whats)** and **how the company will deliver those wants (Hows)**. It is the **Phase 1 deliverable** of QFD and serves as the foundation for all subsequent product, process, and production planning.

**2. Construction Steps (4 Marks):**

| Step | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify Customer Requirements (CRs) from VOC | List of Whats |
| 2 | Determine Technical Requirements (TRs) | List of Hows |
| 3 | Assign customer importance weights W_i | Left wall weights |
| 4 | Build relationship matrix using ● (9), ○ (3), △ (1) | Central matrix |
| 5 | Construct technical correlations roof | Synergies/trade-offs |
| 6 | Perform competitive assessment | Bottom-left benchmarks |
| 7 | Compute Technical Importance T_j | Bottom-row ratings |
| 8 | Set target values for each TR | Engineering targets |

**3. Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** and the ASCII schematic in **Section 4.2** for the layout.

---

#### **(b) For a manufacturing firm producing washing machines, four customer requirements and four technical requirements are identified. The customer importance weights and the relationship matrix are given below. Calculate the Technical Importance Ratings and rank the technical requirements.** (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

**Customer Importance Weights:**

$$
W_1 = 4, \quad W_2 = 5, \quad W_3 = 3, \quad W_4 = 2
$$

**Relationship Matrix:**

| Customer Requirement | TR_1 (Motor Speed) | TR_2 (Drum Capacity) | TR_3 (Water Usage) | TR_4 (Noise Level) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR_1 — Low noise (W_1 = 4) | 9 | 1 | 0 | 9 |
| CR_2 — Large capacity (W_2 = 5) | 0 | 9 | 3 | 1 |
| CR_3 — Low water use (W_3 = 3) | 1 | 1 | 9 | 0 |
| CR_4 — Low cost (W_4 = 2) | 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute T_j using the weighted-sum formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 \;&=\; (4 \times 9) + (5 \times 0) + (3 \times 1) + (2 \times 3) \\
     \;&=\; 36 + 0 + 3 + 6 \;=\; 45
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 \;&=\; (4 \times 1) + (5 \times 9) + (3 \times 1) + (2 \times 3) \\
     \;&=\; 4 + 45 + 3 + 6 \;=\; 58
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 \;&=\; (4 \times 0) + (5 \times 3) + (3 \times 9) + (2 \times 1) \\
     \;&=\; 0 + 15 + 27 + 2 \;=\; 44
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_4 \;&=\; (4 \times 9) + (5 \times 1) + (3 \times 0) + (2 \times 1) \\
     \;&=\; 36 + 5 + 0 + 2 \;=\; 43
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rank the Technical Requirements:**

| Rank | Technical Requirement | T_j | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1 | **TR_2 — Drum Capacity** | **58** | Highest design priority |
| 🥈 2 | **TR_1 — Motor Speed** | **45** | High priority |
| 🥉 3 | **TR_3 — Water Usage** | **44** | Medium priority |
| 4 | TR_4 — Noise Level | 43 | Standard priority |

**Step 3 — Engineering Recommendation (1 Mark):**
The design team must prioritize **Drum Capacity** since it has the highest weighted importance. Noise level, although critical for customer satisfaction, ranks lower because customers weighted CR_1 (low noise) at only 4 — but it must still meet regulatory decibel limits.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain the four phases of QFD. Discuss how QFD supports Six Sigma's DMAIC framework. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Four Phases of QFD (4 Marks):**

- **Phase 1 — Product Planning (House of Quality):** Captures VOC and translates it into product-level technical specifications. *Output: HOQ matrix.*
- **Phase 2 — Product Design:** Deploys critical product specs into subsystems and components. *Output: Deployment Matrix.*
- **Phase 3 — Process Planning:** Translates part-level requirements into specific manufacturing process parameters. *Output: Process Matrix.*
- **Phase 4 — Production Planning:** Maps process requirements into day-to-day production controls, work instructions, and quality checks. *Output: Production Matrix.*

**2. QFD's Linkage with Six Sigma DMAIC (3 Marks):**

| DMAIC Phase | QFD Contribution |
|---|---|
| **Define** | QFD identifies the **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** characteristics from VOC. |
| **Measure** | QFD's technical importance ratings inform **KPI selection and measurement system design**. |
| **Analyze** | QFD's roof matrix highlights **conflicting technical requirements** for root-cause analysis. |
| **Improve** | QFD target values drive **design of experiments (DOE)** and optimization. |
| **Control** | QFD's target values become **SPC control limits** and quality checkpoints. |

---

#### **(b) Discuss the benefits and limitations of QFD. Provide two real-world examples where QFD delivered measurable improvements.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Benefits of QFD (3 Marks):**

- **Customer Focus:** Ensures the final product truly reflects market needs.
- **Reduced Design Changes:** Early identification of trade-offs reduces downstream rework.
- **Improved Cross-Functional Communication:** Aligns Marketing, R&D, Manufacturing, and Quality.
- **Shorter Development Cycle:** Parallel rather than sequential engineering.
- **Documentation:** Creates a single, structured reference document (HOQ).

**2. Limitations of QFD (2 Marks):**

- **Time-intensive:** Construction of the full matrix requires significant effort.
- **Customer data dependency:** Garbage-in, garbage-out — biased VOC yields poor results.
- **Difficulty in quantifying relationships:** Subjective 9/3/1 ratings can vary between teams.
- **Diminishing returns in highly innovative/disruptive markets** where customer preferences evolve rapidly.

**3. Real-World Examples (2 Marks):**

- **Ford Taurus (USA, 1985):** Used QFD to integrate customer feedback; resulted in a top-selling sedan with markedly reduced warranty claims.
- **Toyota Lexus (Japan, 1989):** QFD applied across all four phases to engineer a luxury vehicle competing with Mercedes-Benz, focusing on **noise, vibration, and harshness (NVH)** as top-priority CTQs.
- **Hospital Bed Design (India, 2019):** A Coimbatore-based medical equipment firm used QFD to incorporate nurse and patient feedback, reducing pressure-ulcer incidents by **22%**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 QFD**

- ✅ QFD = **Voice of Customer → Engineering Specs** (translation framework).
- ✅ Originator: **Yoji Akao (Japan, 1966)**; First Industry: **Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (1972)**.
- ✅ Total **4 phases** of QFD: *Product Planning → Product Design → Process Planning → Production Planning*.
- ✅ The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is Phase 1 and the most exam-relevant.
- ✅ HOQ has **7 building blocks**: CRs, TRs, Relationship Matrix, Roof, Importance Weights, Competitive Assessment, Targets.
- ✅ Relationship symbols: **● = 9 (Strong), ○ = 3 (Moderate), △ = 1 (Weak), blank = 0 (None)**.
- ✅ Roof correlations: **++ (Strong Positive), + (Positive), − (Negative), − − (Strong Negative)**.
- ✅ Master formula (must memorize): **T_j = Σ (W_i × R_{ij})**.
- ✅ Normalization formula: **W_i^norm = (W_i / Σ W_k) × 100**.
- ✅ Normalized weights must always **sum to 100%** — use this as a sanity check.
- ✅ QFD complements DMAIC by feeding **CTQs** into the **Define** phase.
- ✅ In KTU answers, **always show every arithmetic step** — partial calculations lose 1–2 marks.
- ✅ Common student error: **forgetting to weight** the relationship strengths.
- ✅ Always **rank-order TRs by T_j value** — engineering priorities flow from this ranking.
- ✅ QFD is a **planning tool**, not a statistical one — no hypothesis testing required.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** Draw the **ASCII House of Quality** in your answer sheet early. Examiners award **partial marks for correct diagram structure** even if numerical calculations are imperfect. Spend **3 minutes on the diagram** to secure at least **2 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma Project Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma Project Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Six Sigma Project Identification** is the disciplined, data-driven *front-end* of the DMAIC (Define–Measure–Analyze–Improve–Control) lifecycle in which an organization systematically scans its internal and external environment, translates customer and business needs into measurable performance gaps, and selects a finite set of high-leverage improvement projects that are most worthy of Black Belt / Green Belt resources.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the activity encompasses:

1. **Voice of Customer (VOC)** capture,
2. **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** flowdown,
3. **Process mapping & SIPOC** at the macro level,
4. **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** estimation,
5. **Project scoring & prioritization**, and
6. **Project Charter** drafting and management sign-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – PEPET742):**
> *"Project Identification – VOC, CTQ, COPQ, project selection criteria, project charter, prioritization matrices, financial justification (NPV, ROI, Payback Period)."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏥

Imagine a **senior cardiologist in a multi-specialty hospital**. Before prescribing treatment, the doctor does not operate on every patient who walks in. Instead, the doctor:

| Doctor's Step | Six Sigma Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Listens to patient complaints (chest pain, breathlessness) | **VOC** – Voice of Customer |
| Orders blood test, ECG, echo | **CTQ** – Critical-to-Quality metrics |
| Identifies blocked artery (the *real* problem) | **Project Identification** |
| Decides if surgery is feasible and worth the cost | **Project Selection & Prioritization** |
| Writes a treatment plan (medication, schedule, risks) | **Project Charter** |

So **Six Sigma Project Identification = the diagnosis phase of organizational healing**. A wrong project choice is like operating on the wrong organ — costly, painful, and reputation-damaging.

---

## 1.3 The Three "Truths" of Project Identification

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Truths (Pyzdek & Keller, Six Sigma Handbook):**
> 1. *Not every problem is a Six Sigma project.* (Some are Kaizen events, some are Just-Do-Its.)
> 2. *Not every important problem is worth a project.* (Cost of solving > benefit = reject.)
> 3. *The biggest gains come from the fewest, sharpest projects.* (Pareto thinking – 20% of projects deliver 80% of impact.)

---

## 1.4 Core Metrics Used During Identification

| Acronym | Full Form | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|
| **VOC** | Voice of Customer | Qualitative + Quantitative |
| **CTQ** | Critical-to-Quality | Measurable specification |
| **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Numeric (0 to 1,000,000) |
| **σ (Sigma Level)** | Process Capability Index | 1σ to 6σ |
| **RTY** | Rolled Throughput Yield | 0 % to 100 % |
| **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality | Currency |
| **NPV** | Net Present Value | Currency |
| **ROI** | Return on Investment | Percentage |
| **IRR** | Internal Rate of Return | Percentage |
| **PP** | Payback Period | Years / Months |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Very Clever Doctors Run Costly Numerical Operations"* → **V**OC, **C**TQ, **D**PMO, **R**TY, **C**OPQ, **N**PV, **O** (ROI), **O** (Operations).

---

## 1.5 Why Project Identification Matters

- **Resource Scarcity:** A typical Fortune 500 firm has 50+ improvement ideas but only 3–5 Black Belts. Selection is *forced* by capacity.
- **Strategic Alignment:** A project that improves a non-strategic process wastes organizational capital.
- **Customer Centricity:** Modern Six Sigma (post-2000) starts with VOC, not with internal metrics, to avoid *local optimum* traps.
- **Financial Discipline:** Without ROI/NPV, Six Sigma is reduced to a "quality flavor of the month."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Project Identification Pipeline

### Stage 1 – *Capture the Voice of the Customer (VOC)*

VOC is the **structured expression of customer needs, expectations, and pain points**, gathered through:

- **Surveys** (Likert-scale, Net Promoter Score, Customer Satisfaction Score)
- **Focus Groups** (8–12 customers, moderated, 90 min)
- **Interviews** (1-on-1 with key accounts)
- **Complaint Logs & CRM tickets**
- **Field Observation / Gemba walks**
- **Social Media & Review Mining**
- **Kano Model** analysis (to classify needs as Basic, Performance, Excitement)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kano Reminder:**
> *Basic* needs → absence causes dissatisfaction, presence does NOT delight.
> *Performance* needs → more is better (linear).
> *Excitement* needs → unexpected delighters.

### Stage 2 – *Translate VOC to CTQ (CTQ Flowdown Tree)*

A **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** is a *quantifiable* specification that a customer uses to judge quality. The translation is done via a **CTQ Tree / KANO Tree**:

```
Customer Need (qualitative)
   ├── Driver 1 (mid-level)
   │     └── CTQ-1: measurable (units, target, tolerance)
   └── Driver 2
         └── CTQ-2: measurable
```

**Example:**

| Customer Need | Driver | CTQ (Measurable) | Unit | Target | USL / LSL |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| "Fast delivery" | Order processing speed | Order-to-dispatch time | Hours | 24 h | USL = 36 h |
| "Reliable product" | Functional reliability | Failures per 1000 hours | Failures | ≤ 1 | USL = 1 |
| "Affordable price" | Cost to customer | Total billed amount | ₹ | 50,000 | ± 5% |

### Stage 3 – *Quantify the Gap (Current vs. Desired Performance)*

For each CTQ, compute:

- **Current Performance (Cp):** measured baseline.
- **Desired Performance (Tp):** target / customer expectation.
- **Gap = Tp − Cp.**

If the gap is statistically significant and operationally meaningful, the CTQ becomes a **project candidate**.

### Stage 4 – *Prioritize & Select (Project Selection Matrix)*

Use a **weighted scoring matrix**:

$$
\text{Project Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight of criterion $i$ (sum of all $w_i$ = 1)
- $s_i$ = score of project on criterion $i$ (typically 1–10)

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | Project A ($s_i$) | Project B ($s_i$) | Project C ($s_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic Alignment | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Financial Impact | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 6 |
| Customer Impact | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Feasibility | 0.15 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Time to Complete | 0.10 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| **Weighted Total** | **1.00** | **7.75** | **7.70** | **7.75** |

Tie-breaker: use **Strategic Alignment** score → Project A wins (lower cost) over Project C.

### Stage 5 – *Draft the Project Charter*

A **Project Charter** is a *one-page executive document* that defines:

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| **Problem Statement** | What is wrong, by how much, since when? |
| **Goal Statement** | SMART target (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) |
| **Scope (In / Out)** | Boundaries of the project |
| **Business Case** | COPQ, projected savings, NPV, ROI |
| **Milestones** | Phase-gate timeline |
| **Team** | Sponsor, Champion, Black Belt, Green Belt, SMEs |
| **Risks & Assumptions** | Top 3 risks, key assumptions |
| **Sign-off** | Sponsor signature & date |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU mistake: writing the *Problem Statement* as a *Solution Statement* (e.g., "Implement ERP" instead of "Order-to-dispatch time is 48 h, target is 24 h"). Always state the **gap**, not the fix.

---

## 2.2 Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) — The Financial Engine

COPQ is the **total cost an organization bears because quality is imperfect**, broken into four categories (Feigenbaum, later refined by Juran):

$$
\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}
$$

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Prevention** | Training, SOP writing, FMEA workshops, design reviews |
| **Appraisal** | Incoming inspection, in-process checks, audits |
| **Internal Failure** | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, downtime |
| **External Failure** | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The ratio **External Failure : Internal Failure : Appraisal : Prevention** in a healthy Six Sigma firm is roughly **30 : 25 : 25 : 20** (i.e., Prevention + Appraisal ≈ External Failure). If P + A is much less than E, the firm is *firefighting*.

---

## 2.3 Financial Justification Metrics

### 2.3.1 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$
\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Cash flow at time $t$ (negative for outflow, positive for inflow)
- $r$ = Discount rate (hurdle rate, often the WACC)
- $T$ = Project life in years
- $t = 0$ is the initial investment.

**Decision rule:** Accept if NPV > 0.

### 2.3.2 Return on Investment (ROI)

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Net Gain} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

(Equivalent to Internal Rate of Return when time value of money is ignored.)

### 2.3.3 Payback Period (PP)

$$
\text{PP} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}
$$

**Decision rule:** Shorter PP → lower risk. Typical Six Sigma target: PP ≤ 24 months.

### 2.3.4 Benefit-to-Cost Ratio (BCR)

$$
\text{BCR} = \frac{\text{PV of Benefits}}{\text{PV of Costs}}
$$

**Decision rule:** BCR > 1 → Accept.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Decision Rule | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$ | Lower is better | Defects / 10⁶ opps |
| 2 | **Yield (Y)** | $Y = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}$ | Higher is better | Fraction (0–1) |
| 3 | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5$ | ≥ 4.5σ = World-class | σ units |
| 4 | **RTY** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Higher is better | Fraction |
| 5 | **COPQ** | $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Track quarterly | ₹ / $ |
| 6 | **NPV** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | NPV > 0 → Accept | Currency |
| 7 | **ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Gain} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$ | ROI > Hurdle | % |
| 8 | **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{I_0}{\text{Annual CF}}$ | PP ≤ 2 yrs preferred | Years |
| 9 | **Project Score** | $S = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$ | Max score wins | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **First-Time Yield** | $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{\text{Good units (no rework)}}{\text{Total units in}}$ | Higher is better | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $D$ = Number of defects observed
> - $U$ = Number of units produced
> - $O$ = Number of defect opportunities per unit
> - $\Phi^{-1}$ = Inverse standard normal CDF
> - $+1.5$ = Motorola's long-term drift shift
> - $C_P, C_A, C_{IF}, C_{EF}$ = Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure costs
> - $I_0$ = Initial investment
> - $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = score on criterion $i$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical Project Identification Outcome |
|---|---|
| **Banking (Loan Processing)** | Reduce loan sanction cycle from 14 days → 5 days (CTQ: TAT) |
| **Hospital (ER Wait Time)** | Reduce door-to-doctor time from 90 min → 30 min (CTQ: triage-to-treatment) |
| **Automobile (Assembly)** | Reduce first-time defect rate on torque from 1,500 DPMO to 200 DPMO |
| **Software (E-commerce)** | Reduce checkout abandonment from 38% → 20% (CTQ: cart-to-payment success) |
| **Telecom (Call Drops)** | Reduce dropped-call rate from 0.8% to 0.2% per 100 calls |

In every case, the project began with **VOC capture**, translated into **CTQ**, measured as **DPMO / σ**, justified using **NPV / ROI / PP**, and authorized through a **Project Charter**.

# 📒 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked examples below mirror the exact 14-mark KTU numerical style. Show all steps explicitly — this is how the Board examiner awards partial marks.**

---

## 3.1 Example 1: DPMO → Sigma Level Conversion (Full Derivation)

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A hospital admission process handles **9,000 patients per month**. Each admission has **12 opportunities** for an error (data entry, insurance verification, bed assignment, etc.). During the last month, **324 defects** were recorded. Calculate the **DPMO**, **Yield**, and the **process Sigma Level**.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Data

- Units processed: $U = 9{,}000$ patients/month
- Opportunities per unit: $O = 12$
- Defects observed: $D = 324$

### Step 2 — Compute DPMO

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{324}{9{,}000 \times 12} \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{324}{108{,}000} \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= 0.003 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}000 \; \text{DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Process Yield (Y)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \frac{3{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.003 \\[4pt]
&= 0.997 \quad \text{(i.e., 99.7\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Yield to Short-Term Sigma (Z_st)

Using the standard normal table, we need the $z$-value for which $\Phi(z) = 0.997$:

$$
z_{st} = \Phi^{-1}(0.997) \approx 2.748
$$

### Step 5 — Add the 1.5σ Drift (Long-Term Sigma)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{LT} &= z_{st} + 1.5 \\[4pt]
&= 2.748 + 1.5 \\[4pt]
&= 4.248 \;\; \sigma \;\; \approx 4.25\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A 4.25σ process yields ~99.7% defect-free admissions. The hospital is operating **above world-class (≥ 4.5σ)** for patient-facing processes only marginally. Each sigma improvement will reduce defects by an order of magnitude — strong justification for a DMAIC project to push it to 5σ.

---

## 3.2 Example 2: NPV and Payback Period for a Six Sigma Project

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A Six Sigma project at a manufacturing firm requires an **initial investment of ₹60,00,000 (₹60 lakhs)**. It is expected to generate **annual cash savings of ₹20,00,000** for **5 years**. The firm's hurdle (discount) rate is **12 % p.a.**

Calculate:
(a) **Payback Period**
(b) **Net Present Value (NPV)**
(c) Comment on the project's acceptability.

### Step 1 — Payback Period

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PP} &= \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 3.0 \;\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Discount Factors

$$
\text{DF}_t = \frac{1}{(1+r)^t}, \quad r = 0.12
$$

| Year ($t$) | Cash Flow (₹) | Discount Factor $\dfrac{1}{1.12^t}$ | PV of CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −60,00,000 | 1.0000 | −60,00,000 |
| 1 | +20,00,000 | 0.8929 | 17,85,800 |
| 2 | +20,00,000 | 0.7972 | 15,94,400 |
| 3 | +20,00,000 | 0.7118 | 14,23,600 |
| 4 | +20,00,000 | 0.6355 | 12,71,000 |
| 5 | +20,00,000 | 0.5674 | 11,34,800 |
| | | **Total PV of Inflows** | **72,09,600** |

### Step 3 — Compute NPV

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \sum_{t=0}^{5} \frac{CF_t}{(1.12)^t} \\[4pt]
&= 72{,}09{,}600 - 60{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{+12{,}09{,}600 \; (\text{₹ 12.10 lakhs})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule Applied:**
> - **Payback Period = 3.0 years** → Acceptable (industry norm ≤ 3 yrs for manufacturing).
> - **NPV = +₹12.10 lakhs > 0** → **Project ACCEPTED.**

---

## 3.3 Example 3: Project Selection Matrix — Full Weighted Scoring

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A firm has shortlisted three candidate projects (A, B, C). Use the following criteria and weights to recommend the best project.

| Criterion | Weight |
|---|---|
| Strategic Alignment | 0.30 |
| Financial Impact | 0.25 |
| Customer Impact | 0.20 |
| Feasibility | 0.15 |
| Time-to-Complete | 0.10 |

**Raw scores (out of 10):**

| Criterion | Weight | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic Alignment | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Financial Impact | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 6 |
| Customer Impact | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Feasibility | 0.15 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Time-to-Complete | 0.10 | 8 | 6 | 9 |

### Step 1 — Project A Weighted Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 0.80 \\
&= \mathbf{7.75}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Project B Weighted Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 2.10 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.20 + 0.60 \\
&= \mathbf{7.70}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Project C Weighted Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.50 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.90 \\
&= \mathbf{7.75}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Tie-Breaker

A and C tie at **7.75**. Break the tie using the **Strategic Alignment** score (the highest-weight criterion):

- Project A: Strategic Alignment = 8
- Project C: Strategic Alignment = 9

> [!TIP]
> **Recommendation:** **Project C** is selected because it scores higher on the most heavily weighted criterion (Strategic Alignment = 0.30). If the firm values short execution time, A becomes the alternate choice.

---

## 3.4 Example 4: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for Multi-Stage Process

**Problem Statement:**
A four-stage process has first-time yields of $Y_1 = 0.98$, $Y_2 = 0.95$, $Y_3 = 0.92$, $Y_4 = 0.90$. Calculate RTY and interpret.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\
&= 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90 \\
&= 0.7706 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 77.06\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Hidden-Factory Insight:** Even though every individual stage is ≥ 90 % first-pass, the rolled yield drops to **77 %**. This is the *silent killer* of complex processes — identification-stage RTY computation exposes stages that *appear* healthy in isolation but are catastrophic in series.

---

## 3.5 Example 5: COPQ Calculation for a Plant

A plant reports the following annual quality costs:

| Category | Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Prevention (training, FMEA) | 8 |
| Appraisal (inspection, audits) | 12 |
| Internal Failure (rework, scrap) | 25 |
| External Failure (warranty, returns) | 55 |

Compute COPQ and the **P+A : IF : EF** ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ} &= 8 + 12 + 25 + 55 = 100 \; \text{₹ lakhs} \\[4pt]
P + A &= 8 + 12 = 20 \\[4pt]
IF &= 25 \\[4pt]
EF &= 55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio** $P + A : IF : EF = 20 : 25 : 55 = 4 : 5 : 11$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnosis:** EF (₹55L) is more than *2.7×* the prevention spend (₹8L). This is a classic **firefighting** profile. A Six Sigma identification effort should target the **₹55L external failure pool** — every rupee invested in prevention can plausibly save 4–6 rupees in EF.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define VOC and explain its role in identifying Six Sigma projects. List any four VOC collection techniques. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**
**Voice of Customer (VOC)** is the structured process of capturing customer requirements, expectations, and pain points in their own language, then translating them into measurable internal specifications.

**Role in Six Sigma Project Identification:**
- Anchors the project in *external value* rather than internal convenience.
- Prevents *local optimum* — improving the wrong metric.
- Forms the input for the **CTQ flowdown tree**.
- Enables prioritization of projects that customers *actually* care about.

**Four VOC Collection Techniques:**
1. Customer Surveys (CSAT, NPS)
2. Focus Group Discussions
3. One-on-one Customer Interviews
4. CRM Complaint & Returns Log Analysis
*(Acceptable: Gemba visits, social media mining, Kano questionnaires.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Project Charter? Mention any four elements it must contain. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**
A **Project Charter** is a one-page executive document that formally authorizes a Six Sigma project, defines its boundaries, and aligns stakeholders on goals and metrics.

**Four Essential Elements (any four of the following):**
1. **Problem Statement** (the gap, not the solution)
2. **SMART Goal Statement**
3. **Scope (In / Out)**
4. **Business Case** (NPV, ROI, COPQ, Payback Period)
5. **Team Structure** (Sponsor, Champion, Black Belt, Green Belt, SMEs)
6. **Milestones / Phase Gates**
7. **Top 3 Risks & Assumptions**
8. **Sponsor Sign-off with Date**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Style Instruction:** *Answer any ONE full question from this module. Each full question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.*

---

### 🔷 **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Project Identification Process + Numerical DPMO/σ*

#### **Q.A (a)** *Explain the Six Sigma project identification process in detail. Highlight the role of VOC, CTQ, COPQ, and the project selection matrix. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — VOC Capture (1.5 Marks):**
The Voice of Customer is captured via surveys, interviews, focus groups, complaint logs, Kano analysis, and field observation. Output: a list of *qualitative* customer needs prioritized by pain index.

**Step 2 — CTQ Flowdown (1.5 Marks):**
Each VOC need is decomposed into a driver tree and finally into *measurable* Critical-to-Quality characteristics (e.g., "fast delivery" → "order-to-dispatch TAT ≤ 24 h").

**Step 3 — COPQ Quantification (1 Mark):**
For each CTQ gap, the Cost of Poor Quality (Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure) is estimated. This converts "customer pain" into "rupee pain" that finance will fund.

**Step 4 — Project Selection Matrix (2 Marks):**
A weighted scoring matrix is applied across criteria — *Strategic Alignment, Financial Impact, Customer Impact, Feasibility, Time-to-Complete* — with weights summing to 1.0. Scores yield a ranked list of candidate projects.

**Step 5 — Project Charter (1 Mark):**
The winning project is documented in a charter with problem statement, SMART goal, scope, business case, milestones, team, risks, and sign-off.

> [!TIP]
> **Synthesis line (for the last 0.5 mark):** *"Together, these five steps convert unstructured customer pain into a financially justified, strategically aligned, and operationally feasible Six Sigma project."*

#### **Q.A (b)** *An e-commerce order-fulfilment process handles 6,000 orders per day. Each order has 8 opportunities for error (pricing, address, payment, shipping label, etc.). In a single day, 240 defects are recorded. Calculate the DPMO, the process Yield, and the long-term Sigma Level. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Units processed: $U = 6{,}000$ orders
- Opportunities per unit: $O = 8$
- Defects: $D = 240$

**Step 1 — DPMO (3 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6 \\
&= \frac{240}{6{,}000 \times 8} \times 10^6 \\
&= \frac{240}{48{,}000} \times 10^6 \\
&= 0.005 \times 10^6 \\
&= 5{,}000 \;\text{DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Process Yield (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} \\
&= 1 - \frac{5{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\
&= 1 - 0.005 \\
&= 0.995 \;\; \text{(i.e., 99.5\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Long-Term Sigma Level (2 Marks):**
From the standard normal table, $z_{st} = \Phi^{-1}(0.995) \approx 2.576$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{LT} &= z_{st} + 1.5 \\
&= 2.576 + 1.5 \\
&= \mathbf{4.076 \; \sigma \;\; \approx 4.08\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** A 4.08σ process in e-commerce is *just below* world-class. A DMAIC project is **justified** to push it to ≥ 4.5σ, where DPMO would drop below 1,000.

---

### 🔷 **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Sources of Projects + NPV/Payback Numerical*

#### **Q.B (a)** *Discuss the major sources from which Six Sigma projects are typically identified in a manufacturing organization. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Source | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Voice of Customer (VOC)** | Customer complaints, returns, NPS drops — projects to fix recurring pain points. |
| 2 | **Voice of Business (VOB)** | Strategic goals: revenue growth, market share, cost reduction — projects aligned to P&L. |
| 3 | **Voice of Process (VOP)** | Internal KPI dashboards showing yield < target, downtime spikes, OEE gaps. |
| 4 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) analysis** | Categories with the largest cost (usually External Failure) become project targets. |
| 5 | **Warranty & Field-Failure Data** | Pareto of failure modes (e.g., "30% of warranty cost from sensor X") → project on sensor X. |
| 6 | **Employee Suggestions / Gemba Walks** | Operator-observed waste, rework, and bottlenecks. |
| 7 | **Benchmarking & Competitive Intelligence** | Competitor capability gaps signal potential Six Sigma projects. |
| 8 | **Regulatory & Compliance Pressure** | New statutory norms trigger dedicated projects. |
| 9 | **Brainstorming & CTQ Tree Workshops** | Cross-functional teams decompose VOC into actionable projects. |
| 10 | **Strategic Deployment (Hoshin Kanri)** | Top-down cascading of annual goals into deployable projects. |

> [!TIP]
> **Synthesis (last 0.5 mark):** *A robust identification system uses both push (top-down strategy) and pull (bottom-up VOC/VOP) sources, screened through a weighted matrix.*

#### **Q.B (b)** *A Six Sigma project requires an initial investment of ₹80 lakhs and is expected to generate annual savings of ₹25 lakhs for 5 years. The firm uses a discount rate of 10% p.a. Compute the Payback Period, the NPV, and comment on acceptability. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Initial investment: $I_0 = 80$ lakhs
- Annual cash inflow: $CF = 25$ lakhs
- Project life: $T = 5$ years
- Discount rate: $r = 10\% = 0.10$

**Step 1 — Payback Period (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PP} &= \frac{I_0}{\text{Annual CF}} = \frac{80}{25} = \mathbf{3.2 \;\text{years}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Discount Factors and PV of Cash Flows (3 Marks):**

| Year $t$ | Cash Flow (₹ Lakhs) | DF $\dfrac{1}{1.10^t}$ | PV (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −80.00 | 1.0000 | −80.0000 |
| 1 | +25.00 | 0.9091 | 22.7275 |
| 2 | +25.00 | 0.8264 | 20.6610 |
| 3 | +25.00 | 0.7513 | 18.7825 |
| 4 | +25.00 | 0.6830 | 17.0750 |
| 5 | +25.00 | 0.6209 | 15.5225 |
| | | **Σ PV inflows** | **94.7685** |

**Step 3 — NPV (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \sum_{t=0}^{5} \frac{CF_t}{(1.10)^t} \\
&= 94.7685 - 80.0000 \\
&= \mathbf{+14.77 \; \text{lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision (0.5 Mark, but counts!):**
- **NPV > 0** → **ACCEPT.**
- **PP = 3.2 years** → Acceptable (within the 3–4 year band typical for capex-heavy manufacturing projects).

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion (to round off the answer):** The project is financially viable and should be approved for the Define phase of DMAIC.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Project Identification Questions:**
> 1. **DPMO units:** Students forget to multiply by $10^6$ and write 0.005 instead of 5,000. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 2. **Sigma Level:** Confusing *short-term* and *long-term* sigma. Always add 1.5 for long-term. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **NPV sign:** Forgetting to subtract initial investment at $t=0$. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 4. **Project Charter:** Writing *solutions* instead of *problems* in the Problem Statement section. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **CTQ Tree:** Stopping at qualitative drivers and *not* reaching measurable CTQs with units and targets. **−1 mark.**
> 6. **Selection Matrix:** Forgetting to verify that $\sum w_i = 1.0$. **−0.5 mark.**
> 7. **COPQ:** Mixing Prevention/Appraisal (cost of conformance) and Internal/External Failure (cost of non-conformance) into one bag. **−0.5 mark.**
> 8. **Payback vs. Discounted Payback:** If the question says *"use discount rate,"* use discounted PP, not simple PP. **−1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Six Sigma Project Identification (Module 2):**

- [x] **Project Identification = the *front-end diagnosis* of DMAIC.** Wrong choice = wasted Black Belt time.
- [x] **Five-step pipeline:** VOC → CTQ → Gap Quantification → Selection Matrix → Project Charter.
- [x] **VOC sources:** Surveys, Interviews, Focus Groups, CRM tickets, Kano, Gemba, Social Media.
- [x] **CTQ Tree:** Always end at *measurable* specifications with **unit, target, USL/LSL**.
- [x] **Kano Model:** *Basic* (must-have), *Performance* (more is better), *Excitement* (delighter).
- [x] **COPQ = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$**; EF dominance = firefighting.
- [x] **DPMO = $\dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$**; lower is better.
- [x] **Yield $Y = 1 - \text{DPMO}/10^6$**.
- [x] **Sigma Level (long-term) = $\Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5$**; 6σ = 3.4 DPMO.
- [x] **RTY = $\prod Y_i$**; exposes *hidden factory*.
- [x] **Project Selection Matrix:** $S = \sum w_i s_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- [x] **NPV > 0 → Accept**; use $r$ = hurdle rate (WACC).
- [x] **ROI = (Gain − Cost)/Cost × 100%.**
- [x] **Payback Period = Initial Investment / Annual CF**; target ≤ 2 years.
- [x] **Project Charter must contain:** Problem (not solution), SMART Goal, Scope, Business Case, Team, Milestones, Risks, Sign-off.
- [x] **Project sources:** VOC, VOB, VOP, COPQ analysis, warranty data, Gemba, benchmarking, regulation, Hoshin Kanri.
- [x] **Three Truths of Identification:** Not every problem is a project; not every important problem is worth a project; fewest, sharpest projects = biggest gains.
- [x] **Mnemonic for project sources:** *"CVWEC GBR H"* → Customer, Voice of Business, Warranty, Employee, COPQ, Gemba, Benchmarking, Regulation, Hoshin.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Six Sigma Project Identification.**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme PEPET742 — NEP 2020 Aligned.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection and Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selection and Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Six Sigma DMAIC methodology** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control), the **"Selection and Definition"** phase is the *foundational first pillar* of the framework. It encompasses two tightly coupled activities:

1. **Project Selection** — The structured, data-driven process of identifying, screening, prioritizing, and formally approving a business problem that is worthy of Six Sigma intervention.
2. **Project Definition** — The rigorous articulation of the approved problem into a clear, measurable, and bounded scope, typically captured in a **Project Charter**, supported by **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** translation into **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristics, and bounded by a high-level **SIPOC** (Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers) map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Selection and Definition* is the first step of the **DMAIC roadmap**. It answers the two most fundamental questions of any Six Sigma initiative: **(i) Which problem should we solve?** and **(ii) Exactly what does solving it mean?**

The KTU syllabus treats this as the conceptual gateway that prevents organizations from "solving the wrong problem perfectly" — a common cause of project failure documented by Harry and Schroeder (2000) and Snee (2004) in Six Sigma literature.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital ER Triage**
> Imagine a hospital Emergency Room receiving 50 patients at once. Before any doctor begins treatment, a **triage nurse** performs two jobs:
> 1. **Selection:** Decides *which* patients need immediate, intensive care (heart attack vs. common cold) based on severity, survivability, and resource availability.
> 2. **Definition:** For each selected patient, writes a precise chart: *Chief Complaint, Vital Signs, Allergies, Treatment Boundaries.*
> 
> Six Sigma's **Selection and Definition** phase is the **triage nurse of an organization**. Without it, expensive Black Belts waste months "treating" low-impact problems or treating the right problem with the wrong scope.

**The Three Golden Questions of Selection & Definition:**

| # | Question | Selection or Definition? |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | *Why this project and not another?* | **Selection** |
| 2 | *What exactly is the problem, and where does it start/end?* | **Definition** |
| 3 | *How will we know we have succeeded?* | **Definition (CTQ)** |

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Emphasis)

The following terms are **mandatory KTU glossary items** — students must be able to define each in two lines:

- **Project Charter** — A formal, one-page document authorizing the Six Sigma project and defining its scope, goals, timeline, and team.
- **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — The actual needs, expectations, and preferences of the *end customer*, captured through structured listening.
- **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** — The measurable characteristics of a product, process, or service that the customer explicitly values.
- **SIPOC** — A high-level process map showing **S**uppliers, **I**nputs, **P**rocess, **O**utputs, and **C**ustomers.
- **Kano Model** — A prioritization tool classifying customer requirements into *Must-Be*, *One-Dimensional*, and *Attractive* categories.
- **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** — The financial quantification of waste due to defects, rework, warranty, and lost goodwill — the prime driver of project selection.
- **Project Y** — The measurable outcome metric (the *dependent variable*) the project aims to improve.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars in Detail

### 2.1.1 Pillar A — Project Selection (Choosing the *Right* Problem)

A Six Sigma project is *not* an ordinary improvement task. It must clear **four sequential filters**:

**Filter 1 — Strategic Alignment**
The problem must be linked to a strategic business goal (revenue, cost, customer retention, quality). Selection without strategic alignment is **rejected** by the Steering Committee.

**Filter 2 — Financial Justification (COPQ Lens)**
The Cost of Poor Quality must be quantified to demonstrate ROI. Projects that recover > **10× the project cost** are typically auto-approved.

**Filter 3 — Feasibility Check**
The problem must be **measurable** (data exists or can be collected), **controllable** (the team has process authority), and **bounded** (3–6 month horizon).

**Filter 4 — Y Identification**
A single, measurable **Project Y** must exist. If you cannot name *one* metric, the scope is too vague.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "SMART-UP"** for project selection criteria: **S**trategic, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound, **U**nique (not overlapping with another project), **P**rofitable.

### 2.1.2 Pillar B — Project Definition (Defining the *Right* Way)

Once selected, the problem is defined using **four artifacts**:

1. **Project Charter** — Who, What, When, Why, How-much.
2. **VOC → CTQ Tree** — Translates customer *needs* into *measurable specifications*.
3. **SIPOC Diagram** — Defines the *boundaries* of the process being improved.
4. **Problem Statement** — A one-paragraph, neutral, fact-based description (no blame, no solution).

## 2.2 The VOC → CTQ Translation Logic (Critical for KTU)

The translation follows a **3-level drill-down**:

$$\text{Level 1: VOC (Customer Need in their language)}$$

$$\text{Level 2: Customer Requirement (Engineered translation)}$$

$$\text{Level 3: CTQ (Measurable specification with unit \& target)}$$

**Illustrative Example — A Banking Six Sigma Project:**

| Level | Element | Statement |
|-------|---------|-----------|
| VOC | Customer Voice | *"I want my loan to be approved faster."* |
| Requirement | Engineered | *Loan approval time must be reduced.* |
| CTQ | Measurable | *Mean loan approval cycle time ≤ **48 hours** with σ ≤ **6 hours**.* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every CTQ **must** have three components: **(i) a metric, (ii) a unit, (iii) a target value**. A CTQ without a target is a *wish*, not a specification.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Construct | Symbol | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Project ROI | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual COPQ Savings}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100$ | Expressed in **%**; threshold ≥ **500%** |
| 2 | Defect Rate (DPMO input) | $\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | In **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) |
| 3 | Process Sigma Level Lookup | $\sigma = f(\text{DPMO})$ via Standard Normal CDF | Unitless; 3.4 DPMO = **6σ** |
| 4 | CTQ Target Value | $Y_{\text{target}} = \mu \pm k\sigma$ | Where $k$ = customer tolerance multiplier |
| 5 | Project Y Baseline | $Y_{\text{baseline}} = \bar{Y}_{\text{current}}$ | Computed in the **Measure** phase, but *defined* here |
| 6 | Kano Satisfaction Function (qualitative) | $S = f(\text{Investment in CTQ})$ | Discontinuous for *Attractive* and *Must-Be* needs |
| 7 | COPQ Categorization | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{internal}} + C_{\text{external}}$ | Internal: rework, scrap. External: warranty, returns |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Selection & Definition phase is universally applied in:
