- **Manufacturing** — Selecting a defect-reduction project on a semiconductor line.
- **Healthcare** — Defining a CTQ for "Door-to-Balloon Time" in cardiac care.
- **IT/Software** — Choosing a Six Sigma project to reduce *Mean Time to Recovery* (MTTR).
- **Service Industry** — Defining "First-Call Resolution" as a CTQ in a BPO.

In every case, the **discipline of selection before definition before measurement** prevents the classic failure mode: *low-impact projects consuming Black Belt talent.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Project Selection

Below is the **exhaustive decision logic** a Six Sigma Steering Committee follows. Each branch must be justified in writing.

### Step 1 — Strategic Filter

$$
\text{Project Passes Strategic Filter} \iff \text{Project} \in \{\text{Initiatives on the Hoshin Kanri Matrix}\}
$$

If the project is **not** on the strategic deployment matrix, it is **rejected** regardless of its technical merit.

### Step 2 — COPQ Quantification

The COPQ is decomposed into four categories following the **Feigenbaum classification**:

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}
$$

**Numerical Illustration:**

A manufacturing firm observes:
- Internal failure cost (rework + scrap) = **₹ 40,00,000 / year**
- External failure cost (warranty + returns) = **₹ 25,00,000 / year**

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}} = 40{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 65{,}00{,}000 \text{ / year}
$$

### Step 3 — Feasibility Triad (The "MCC" Test)

A feasible project must satisfy all **three** constraints simultaneously:

$$
\text{Feasible} \iff \big[ \text{Measurable}(Y) \big] \land \big[ \text{Controllable}(\text{Process}) \big] \land \big[ \text{Bounded}(\text{S}) \big]
$$

where $\text{Bounded}$ means the project scope $S$ fits within a **3–6 month** Black Belt calendar window.

### Step 4 — Y Definition

The Project Y must satisfy **the Y-formula identity**:

$$
Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)
$$

At this stage, **only $Y$ is identified**. The $X_i$'s (process inputs) are discovered in the **Analyze** phase.

**Worked Example — Step-by-Step Project Selection:**

A hospital records the following candidate project proposals:

| Proposal | Y (Metric) | Strategic? | COPQ (₹ Lakh) | Measurable? | Controllable? | Time ≤ 6 mo? | Selected? |
|----------|------------|-----------|---------------|-------------|---------------|--------------|-----------|
| P1: ER Wait Time | 47 min | Yes (Patient Sat.) | 85 | Yes | Yes | Yes | ✅ |
| P2: Cafeteria Menu Variety | 12 items | No (Non-core) | 5 | Yes | No | Yes | ❌ |
| P3: MRI Downtime | 14 hrs/mo | Yes (Revenue) | 62 | Yes | Yes (Vendor) | Yes | ✅ |
| P4: Parking Space Availability | 38 % | No | 3 | Yes | No | No | ❌ |

> **Conclusion:** Two projects (P1 and P3) clear all filters and proceed to **Definition**.

## 3.2 CTQ Derivation — Voice-to-Specification

The CTQ tree is derived in **three deterministic steps**:

**Step A — Capture VOC using structured tools:**

$$
\text{VOC} = \{\text{Focus Groups}\} \cup \{\text{Surveys}\} \cup \{\text{Complaint Logs}\} \cup \{\text{Focus Interviews}\}
$$

**Step B — Convert VOC to CTQ using the 5W2H + metrics filter:**

$$
\text{CTQ}_i = \text{Filter}_{measurable}(\text{VOC}_i) \;\;\; \text{where} \;\;\; \text{Filter}_{measurable}: V \mapsto (Y_i, U_i, T_i)
$$

Here, $(Y_i, U_i, T_i)$ = (Metric, Unit, Target).

**Step C — Validate CTQ using the *Operational Definition* test:**

A CTQ is valid **iff** two independent observers, given the same data, would compute the **identical** value. If they disagree, the CTQ is **operationally ill-defined** and must be reworked.

$$
\text{CTQ}_{\text{valid}} \iff \text{Inter-Rater Reliability} \rightarrow 1
$$

## 3.3 SIPOC Construction — Boundary Logic

The SIPOC defines the **process boundaries** $B$:

$$
B = [X_{\text{start}}, X_{\text{end}}]
$$

A SIPOC has exactly **five columns** and typically **4–7 process steps** at the highest level of abstraction.

**Construction Algorithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Name the Outputs (CTQs)} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; O \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Identify Customers of those Outputs} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; C \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Identify Inputs needed for Outputs} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; I \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Identify Suppliers of those Inputs} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; S \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Fill the Process column with 4–7 high-level steps} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; P
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SIPOC is built **right-to-left** (Outputs first, then Customers, then Inputs, then Suppliers), then Process is added. This order prevents the common error of including steps that produce *no* output the customer cares about.

## 3.4 Project Charter — Canonical Structure

The Project Charter is the *contract* between the team and the sponsor. It contains exactly **nine elements**:

$$
\text{Charter} = \{ \text{Problem}, \text{Goal}, \text{Y}, \text{Scope}, \text{Team}, \text{Sponsor}, \text{Timeline}, \text{Milestones}, \text{Constraints} \}
$$

**Empty Charter Template (KTU-ready):**

| Element | Content |
|---------|---------|
| Problem Statement | *One paragraph, neutral, fact-based.* |
| Goal Statement | *Quantified target for Y.* |
| Project Y | *The single dependent metric.* |
| In-Scope | *Process steps covered.* |
| Out-of-Scope | *Process steps excluded.* |
| Team | *BB, GB, Champion, Process Owner, Members.* |
| Sponsor / Champion | *Senior leader name.* |
| Timeline | *Gantt-style milestones.* |
| Constraints | *Budget, regulatory, technology.* |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Selection & Definition Phase Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Business Strategy Hoshin Kanri] --> B[Identify Candidate Problems]
    B --> C[Strategic Alignment Check]
    C --> D{Strategic?}
    D -- No --> Z1[Reject Project]
    D -- Yes --> E[COPQ Quantification]
    E --> F[Feasibility Triad MCC]
    F --> G{Measurable Controllable Bounded?}
    G -- No --> Z1
    G -- Yes --> H[Define Project Y Metric]
    H --> I[Capture VOC]
    I --> J[VOC to CTQ Translation Tree]
    J --> K[Build SIPOC Map]
    K --> L[Draft Project Charter]
    L --> M[Steering Committee Approval]
    M --> N[Project Authorized Move to Measure Phase]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — VOC → CTQ Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Customer Verbal Statement] --> V2[Translate to Engineering Requirement]
    V2 --> V3[Decompose into Measurable CTQs]
    V3 --> V4[Attach Unit and Target Value]
    V4 --> V5[Validate Operational Definition]
    V5 -- Valid --> V6[Approved CTQ Pool]
    V5 -- Invalid --> V2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — SIPOC High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Suppliers
        S1[Supplier A Raw Material]
        S2[Supplier B Information]
        S3[Supplier C Equipment]
    end
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Material X]
        I2[Data D]
        I3[Machine M]
    end
    subgraph Process
        P1[Step 1 Receive]
        P2[Step 2 Process]
        P3[Step 3 Inspect]
        P4[Step 4 Deliver]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Output 1 CTQ1]
        O2[Output 2 CTQ2]
    end
    subgraph Customers
        C1[End Customer External]
        C2[Internal Customer Next Stage]
    end
    S1 --> I1
    S2 --> I2
    S3 --> I3
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    O1 --> C1
    O2 --> C2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Project Charter Elements Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Project Charter))
    Problem
      Neutral fact based
      No blame
      No solution
    Goal
      Quantified target
      Linked to Y
    Scope
      In scope
      Out of scope
    Team
      Black Belt
      Green Belt
      Champion
      Sponsor
    Timeline
      Start date
      End date
      Milestones
    CTQ
      Metric
      Unit
      Target
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Project Charter" as used in Six Sigma. List any four elements of a Project Charter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Project Charter** is a formal, single-page document that authorizes a Six Sigma project, defines its boundaries, identifies the team, and records the sponsor's expectations. It is the **official contract** between the project team and the Steering Committee.

The **four mandatory elements** are:
1. **Problem Statement** — A neutral, one-paragraph description of the current state.
2. **Goal Statement** — A quantified target for the Project Y metric.
3. **Scope** — Explicit *In-Scope* and *Out-of-Scope* boundaries.
4. **Team Composition** — Black Belt, Green Belt, Champion, Sponsor, and Process Owner names.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any four correct elements.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Distinguish between VOC and CTQ with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | VOC (Voice of Customer) | CTQ (Critical to Quality) |
|--------|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| Nature | Customer's *expressed need* in their language | Engineered, *measurable specification* |
| Quantification | Qualitative (often verbal) | Quantitative (number + unit + target) |
| Example: *"I want faster delivery"* | The verbal complaint/survey response | *Delivery cycle time ≤ 48 hours* |

The relationship is **one-way and transformational**:

$$
\text{VOC} \xrightarrow{\text{Engineer \& Measure}} \text{CTQ}
$$

*(Award 1 mark each for distinction and 1 mark for the example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Project Selection Process** in Six Sigma. Discuss the **four filters** a candidate project must pass before approval, with an illustrative example.

**Model Solution:**

The **Project Selection Process** ensures that organizational resources (Black Belts, Green Belts, capital) are deployed only on problems that are **strategically vital, financially justified, technically feasible, and measurable**. It is governed by the Steering Committee (Champion + Finance + Process Owners) and operates through **four sequential filters**.

**Filter 1 — Strategic Alignment (2 marks)**
The project must be traceable to a strategic business objective on the *Hoshin Kanri* matrix. If the proposed problem does not contribute to at least one of the top-3 strategic goals (e.g., customer retention, cost reduction, regulatory compliance), the project is rejected irrespective of its technical merit.

**Filter 2 — Financial Justification via COPQ (2 marks)**
The **Cost of Poor Quality** is computed:

$$
\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}
$$

If the annual COPQ savings divided by project cost yields ROI ≥ **500 %**, the project is financially justified.

**Filter 3 — Feasibility (MCC Triad) (2 marks)**
The project must be **Measurable** (data available), **Controllable** (team has authority), and **Bounded** (3–6 month scope). If any of the three fails, the project is rejected.

**Filter 4 — Project Y Identification (1 mark)**
A single, quantifiable **Project Y** is named. Without Y, no DMAIC project can proceed.

**Illustrative Example:** A hospital identifies that ER wait time averages **47 minutes**, against a target of **20 minutes**. The COPQ (lost patients, complaints) is **₹ 85 lakh/year**. The metric (Y = wait time) is measurable, the ER team is the controllable owner, and the scope fits 4 months. The project clears all four filters and is approved.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **SIPOC Diagram** in detail. With a suitable example, explain its **five columns** and the **right-to-left construction logic**.

**Model Solution:**

**SIPOC** is a high-level process-mapping tool used during the **Definition** phase to bound the project scope. It has exactly **five columns**:

| Column | Meaning | Example (Loan Approval) |
|--------|---------|--------------------------|
| **S**uppliers | Who provides the inputs | Customer, Credit Bureau, Branch |
| **I**nputs | What is needed to start the process | Application form, ID proof, Credit score |
| **P**rocess | 4–7 high-level steps | Receive → Verify → Assess → Approve/Decline |
| **O**utputs | What is delivered to customer | Approval letter, Disbursed amount |
| **C**ustomers | Who receives the output | Applicant, Internal Loan Disbursement Unit |

**Right-to-Left Construction Logic (3 marks):**
The SIPOC is built in the order **O → C → I → S → P** (Outputs first), because starting from the customer's needs prevents inclusion of irrelevant process steps:

1. **Start with Outputs (CTQs):** Name what the customer receives.
2. **Identify Customers:** Internal + External consumers of each output.
3. **Identify Inputs:** What materials/data are required to create each output.
4. **Identify Suppliers:** Who provides each input.
5. **Define Process (4–7 steps):** The minimum set of steps that transform inputs into outputs.

This disciplined order ensures the SIPOC is **customer-anchored** and **scope-bounded**, a frequent KTU evaluation point.

---

### **Question B** — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **VOC to CTQ translation process**. With an example, demonstrate the **3-level drill-down** and state the **three mandatory components** of a valid CTQ.

**Model Solution:**

The **VOC → CTQ translation** is the engineering step that converts unstructured customer language into measurable specifications. It operates in **three levels**:

**Level 1 — VOC (Customer's Voice):** Captured from surveys, complaint logs, focus groups, interviews. Example: *"My online order is delivered late."*

**Level 2 — Customer Requirement (Engineered):** The verbal statement is rewritten as a process-oriented requirement. Example: *Order delivery cycle time must be reduced.*

**Level 3 — CTQ (Measurable Specification):** The requirement is quantified with a metric, unit, and target. Example: *Delivery time ≤ 24 hours in 95 % of cases.*

**Three Mandatory Components of a Valid CTQ (3 marks):**

1. **Metric (Y)** — A measurable variable, e.g., "delivery time."
2. **Unit** — e.g., "hours."
3. **Target Value** — e.g., "≤ 24 hours."

**Operational Definition Test:** Two independent observers must compute the same value from the same data. If not, the CTQ is invalid and must be redefined.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Project Charter** in Six Sigma. List all **nine elements** and explain any **five** in detail.

**Model Solution:**

The **Project Charter** is the formal authorization document for a Six Sigma project. It serves as the *contract* between the project team and the sponsor/Champion. The charter must contain **nine elements**:

1. **Problem Statement**
2. **Goal Statement**
3. **Project Y**
4. **Scope (In/Out)**
5. **Team (BB, GB, Champion, Process Owner)**
6. **Sponsor / Champion**
7. **Timeline**
8. **Milestones**
9. **Constraints**

**Detailed Explanation of Five Elements (5 × 1 = 5 marks + 2 for listing):**

- **Problem Statement:** A *neutral*, *fact-based*, one-paragraph description of the current undesirable state. It must **not** assign blame or propose a solution.
- **Goal Statement:** A *quantified* target for Y (e.g., "Reduce ER wait time from 47 min to 20 min within 4 months").
- **Scope:** Explicit declaration of *In-Scope* (e.g., ER triage process) and *Out-of-Scope* (e.g., Inpatient admission). Scope discipline prevents project creep.
- **Team:** Identifies the Black Belt (project leader), Green Belt (part-time support), Champion (senior sponsor), and Process Owner (the line manager whose process is being changed).
- **Constraints:** Lists restrictions — budget caps, regulatory limits, technology freeze periods, or organizational policy boundaries.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in "Selection and Definition" Answers:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing VOC with CTQ** — VOC is qualitative *need*; CTQ is quantitative *specification*. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if no clear distinction is shown.
> 2. **SIPOC built left-to-right** — Always build right-to-left (**O → C → I → S → P**). Reversing the order is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Missing the target value in a CTQ** — A CTQ without a target is incomplete. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Project Charter written with blame or solution** — Problem Statements must be neutral. Examiners flag this immediately.
> 5. **Confusing Project Y with Project Goal** — Y is the *metric*; Goal is the *desired numerical value* of Y. They are not synonymous.
> 6. **Omitting the COPQ calculation** — In Project Selection, simply stating "financially viable" without the COPQ/ROI math costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Selection and Definition"**

- ☐ **Two Pillars:** Project Selection + Project Definition (both mandatory).
- ☐ **Four Filters of Selection:** Strategic Alignment → COPQ → Feasibility (MCC) → Y Identification.
- ☐ **COPQ Formula:** $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{internal}} + C_{\text{external}}$.
- ☐ **ROI Threshold:** ≥ **500 %** for auto-approval in most firms.
- ☐ **VOC** is qualitative; **CTQ** is quantitative — always convert VOC → CTQ.
- ☐ **CTQ has 3 components:** Metric, Unit, Target Value.
- ☐ **Operational Definition Test:** Two independent observers must agree on the same value.
- ☐ **SIPOC Order:** Build **right-to-left** — Outputs first, Customers, Inputs, Suppliers, then Process.
- ☐ **SIPOC Process Steps:** Exactly **4–7** high-level steps.
- ☐ **Project Charter = 9 elements:** Problem, Goal, Y, Scope, Team, Sponsor, Timeline, Milestones, Constraints.
- ☐ **Problem Statement:** Neutral, fact-based, **no blame, no solution**.
- ☐ **Feasibility Triad:** **M**easurable, **C**ontrollable, **B**ounded (≤ 6 months).
- ☐ **Project Y Formula:** $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ — but $X_i$'s are *unknown* until the Analyze phase.
- ☐ **Kano Model** classifies needs as Must-Be, One-Dimensional, or Attractive.
- ☐ **Selection & Definition is the gatekeeper** — bad selection = months of wasted Black Belt effort downstream.
- ☐ **Mnemonic:** **SMART-UP** for selection; **PGT-YST-C** for charter elements (Problem, Goal, Team, Y, Scope, Timeline, Constraints).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Characteristics and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Characteristics and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Process and Process Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Process** is a set of inter-related working activities that transforms **Inputs** (raw materials, information, human effort, machines, methods) into **Outputs** (products, services, or information) of greater value to a customer. A **Process Characteristic** is any measurable, quantifiable property of a process that describes its behavior, performance, or output quality. Process Characteristics form the bridge between the input variables (\(x\)) and the output response (\(Y\)).

Mathematically, the process is modelled by the **Six Sigma transfer function**:

$$
Y = f(x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n)
$$

where every \(x_i\) is an **input variable** (controllable or noise) and \(Y\) is the **output response** (the Critical-to-Quality characteristic).

### 1.2 Classification of Process Characteristics

Process characteristics are broadly classified into three hierarchical levels used throughout the **DMAIC** framework:

| Level | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** | Direct customer-visible outputs | Cycle time of a bank loan |
| **Level 2** | **Process Output (\(Y\))** | Internal measurable outcomes | Average page-load latency |
| **Level 3** | **Process Inputs (\(x\))** | Variables driving the output | Server CPU load, query length |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Line:** *"CTQs are the Voice of the Customer translated into measurable engineering specifications."*

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **South Indian Coffee Filter Process** ☕:

- **Inputs (\(x\))**: Coffee powder mass, water temperature, water quantity, filter cloth porosity, steeping time.
- **Process**: Brewing (diffusion + extraction of solubles).
- **Outputs (\(Y\))**: Volume of decoction, colour intensity (CTQ), bitterness index.
- **Process Characteristics**: The measurable properties you monitor (extraction temperature, flow rate through the filter, total dissolved solids).

If the **water temperature** (an input) fluctuates, the **decoction concentration** (the output CTQ) drifts. A Six Sigma analyst identifies *which* \(x\) is causing the variation in \(Y\) — this is the essence of **Process Characteristics and Analysis**.

### 1.4 The Three Voices (VOC, VOP, VOB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Voices Triad**
> - **VOC — Voice of the Customer:** What the customer *wants* (translated into CTQs).
> - **VOP — Voice of the Process:** What the process *is capable of* delivering (capability indices).
> - **VOB — Voice of the Business:** What the business *needs* to remain profitable (cost, throughput).

A Six Sigma project succeeds only when **VOC ≈ VOP**, while satisfying **VOB**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Process — The \(Y = f(x)\) Model

The Six Sigma philosophy treats every business problem as a function relating output to inputs. The **transfer function** \(Y = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)\) is rarely known exactly, so we approximate it statistically.

**Step-wise breakdown of the analytical approach:**

1. **Identify \(Y\)** — The CTQ that the customer cares about. Must be quantifiable (defects per million, cycle time, NPS score, etc.).
2. **Map the Process** — Document every step from supplier to customer (use SIPOC — covered below).
3. **Identify all candidate \(x\)'s** — Brainstorm using Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa / Fishbone) diagrams.
4. **Quantify Variation of each \(x\)** — Measure mean and standard deviation.
5. **Quantify Variation of \(Y\)** — Establish baseline \(\sigma_Y\).
6. **Statistically Link \(x\) to \(Y\)** — Using correlation, regression, ANOVA, or DOE.
7. **Reduce Variation in Critical \(x\)'s** — Implement controls (SPC charts, poka-yoke).
8. **Re-verify \(Y\) Stability** — Confirm CTQ has shifted in the desired direction.

### 2.2 SIPOC — The Foundational Process Map

**SIPOC** is a top-down process-mapping tool used at the *Measure* phase of DMAIC.

| Element | Acronym Meaning | Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Suppliers | Who provides the inputs? |
| **I** | Inputs | What do we need? |
| **P** | Process | What are the high-level steps? |
| **O** | Outputs | What is produced? |
| **C** | Customers | Who receives the output? |

### 2.3 Process Capability Indices (The Heart of Process Analysis)

These indices quantify how well a process meets specification limits. The standard Six Sigma metrics are **Cp**, **Cpk**, **Pp**, and **Ppk**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** *C* stands for *Capability* (short-term / within-subgroup), *P* stands for *Performance* (long-term / overall). The extra **k** stands for *centering* (accounts for mean shift).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning / Use | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Sigma Level | \(Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}\) | Distance from mean to spec in sigma units | One-sided |
| Two-sided Sigma | \(Z_t = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{2\sigma}\) | Centre-distance metric | Bench Z = 6 |
| Cp | \(C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}\) | Process potential (assuming centred) | \(C_p \geq 1.0\) acceptable |
| Cpk (upper) | \(C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}\) | Centring-corrected capability (upper) | Use min of both |
| Cpk (lower) | \(C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma}\) | Centring-corrected capability (lower) | Use min of both |
| Cpk (final) | \(C_{pk} = \min(C_{pk,\text{upper}}, C_{pk,\text{lower}})\) | Reported capability index | \(C_{pk} \geq 1.33\) preferred |
| Pp | \(P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{\text{overall}}}\) | Overall (long-term) performance | Uses total \(\sigma\) |
| Ppk | \(P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma_{\text{overall}}}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma_{\text{overall}}}\right)\) | Overall centring-corrected perf. | Lower than Cpk normally |
| DPMO | \(\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6\) | Defects per million opportunities | Basis of Sigma Level |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | \(RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\) | Geometric product of step yields | Always \(\leq\) each \(Y_i\) |
| Process Cycle Time | \(t_{cycle} = t_{value} + t_{waste}\) | Value + non-value time | Lean metric |
| Process Efficiency | \(\eta = \dfrac{t_{value}}{t_{cycle}} \times 100\%\) | % value-added time | Lean metric |
| Six-Sigma Yield | \(Y_{6\sigma} = 99.99966\%\) | 3.4 DPMO | Target benchmark |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When writing capability formulas in KTU answer scripts, **always** state whether you are using **within-subgroup standard deviation** (\(\bar{R}/d_2\) or \(s/\hat{c}_4\)) for Cp/Cpk, or the **overall standard deviation** (\(s_{\text{long-term}}\)) for Pp/Ppk. Examiners deduct marks when students mix the two.

### 2.5 Variation — The Universal Language of Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variation is the enemy of quality.** Every process exhibits two types of variation:
> - **Common-Cause Variation (Noise):** Inherent to the process; predictable; requires system-level change.
> - **Special-Cause Variation (Signals):** External shocks; unpredictable; requires local investigation.

A process is said to be **In Statistical Control** when only common-cause variation is present (no points outside control-chart limits, no patterns). A process is **Capable** when its natural spread fits within specification limits.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Process Characteristics & Analysis is the backbone of:

- **Semiconductor Manufacturing** — Tracking wafer-defect density (CTQ) against lithography dose (input \(x\)).
- **Automotive Assembly** — Monitoring torque on wheel nuts (CTQ).
- **BPO / Service Sector** — Tracking average call-handling time (AHT) against agent training hours (input).
- **Healthcare** — Reducing surgery turnaround time (CTQ) by analysing pre-op preparation variables.

Every Continuous Improvement initiative, ISO 9001 QMS audit, and Lean Six Sigma certification draws from this foundational body of knowledge.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Capability Index \(C_p\)

We begin from the engineering specification statement:

A process is **acceptable** if its natural six-sigma spread \([\mu - 3\sigma,\; \mu + 3\sigma]\) is comfortably inside the customer's specification window \([LSL,\; USL]\).

The process spread equals:

$$
\text{Spread}_{\text{process}} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma
$$

The specification window equals:

$$
\text{Spread}_{\text{spec}} = USL - LSL
$$

For the process to fit the spec window, the ratio is defined as:

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Spread}_{\text{spec}}}{\text{Spread}_{\text{process}}}
$$

Substituting the two expressions:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

This is the **process capability** assuming the mean is *perfectly centred*. **Industry rule of thumb:** \(C_p \geq 1.33\) indicates an *excellent* capable process.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Centring Index \(C_{pk}\)

The original \(C_p\) is blind to off-centring. To incorporate the distance from the mean to the *nearest* specification limit, we compute two one-sided distances:

**Upper distance** (in sigma units):

$$
Z_{\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}
$$

**Lower distance** (in sigma units):

$$
Z_{\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

The reported capability index uses the **minimum** of the two (the binding constraint):

$$
C_{pk} = \min(Z_{\text{upper}}, Z_{\text{lower}})
$$

Written out explicitly:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Identity:** When \(\mu = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}\) (perfect centring), \(Z_{\text{upper}} = Z_{\text{lower}}\) and therefore \(C_{pk} = C_p\). This is why \(C_p \geq C_{pk}\) is an algebraic certainty.

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement (typical 14-mark sub-part):**
A CNC machining process produces shafts with diameter specification \(LSL = 24.90 \text{ mm}\), \(USL = 25.10 \text{ mm}\). A sample of 25 shafts yields \(\bar{x} = 25.02 \text{ mm}\) and sample standard deviation \(s = 0.018 \text{ mm}\). Using a subgroup of size \(n=5\) with mean range \(\bar{R} = 0.045 \text{ mm}\), compute **\(C_p\)**, **\(C_{pk}\)**, **Ppk**, the **process sigma level**, and the **DPMO** if 4 defects were found in 12,000 shafts, each having 3 defect opportunities.

**Step 1 — Compute the within-subgroup standard deviation** using \(d_2\) for \(n = 5\). From the standard constants table, \(d_2(n=5) = 2.326\).

$$
\sigma_{\text{within}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.045}{2.326} = 0.01935 \text{ mm}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The average range divided by the bias-correction constant \(d_2\) yields an unbiased estimate of within-subgroup variability, used for Cp/Cpk.

**Step 2 — Compute Cp**:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{\text{within}}} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.01935}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{0.20}{0.1161} = 1.722
$$

*Conversion logic:* Numerator is the spec window (0.20 mm); denominator is six times the within-subgroup sigma. A value above 1.33 means the process *potential* is excellent.

**Step 3 — Compute Cpk**:

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma_{\text{within}}} = \frac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.01935} = \frac{0.08}{0.05805} = 1.378
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma_{\text{within}}} = \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{0.05805} = \frac{0.12}{0.05805} = 2.067
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.378,\; 2.067) = 1.378
$$

*Conversion logic:* The upper specification limit is closer to the mean, so it is the binding constraint. Cpk = 1.378 confirms the process is well-capable even when accounting for the 0.02 mm centring drift.

**Step 4 — Compute Pp and Ppk using overall standard deviation** (\(s = 0.018 \text{ mm}\)):

$$
P_p = \frac{0.20}{6 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.20}{0.108} = 1.852
$$

$$
P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{0.08}{0.054},\; \frac{0.12}{0.054}\right) = \min(1.481,\; 2.222) = 1.481
$$

*Conversion logic:* Since \(P_p > C_p\), the long-term variability is slightly smaller than short-term (no special-cause shocks).

**Step 5 — Compute Process Sigma Level** (using \(Z_{\text{upper}}\) since it is smaller):

$$
Z_{\text{process}} = \frac{0.08}{0.018} = 4.44
$$

*Conversion logic:* This process is operating at approximately a **4.4-sigma** level (well below the Six-Sigma goal of 6, but quite good).

**Step 6 — Compute DPMO**:

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{4}{12000 \times 3} \times 10^6 = \frac{4}{36000} \times 10^6 = 111.11 \approx 111 \text{ DPMO}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Total opportunities = 12,000 units × 3 opportunities per unit = 36,000. The 4 defects divided by 36,000 and scaled to per-million yield 111 DPMO, equivalent to roughly a **5.7-sigma** performance.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Use What)

| Scenario | Use | Formula | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process is centred, you want *potential* | \(C_p\) | \((USL-LSL)/(6\sigma_{\text{within}})\) | Best-case capability |
| Process has drifted, you want *reality* | \(C_{pk}\) | \(\min(\cdot, \cdot)\) | Includes centring penalty |
| Long-term performance review | \(P_p\) | \((USL-LSL)/(6\sigma_{\text{overall}})\) | Uses total variance |
| Customer-facing reporting | \(P_{pk}\) | \(\min(\cdot, \cdot)\) | Total + centring |
| Quick sigma benchmark | DPMO | \(D/(U \times O) \times 10^6\) | Standardised across industries |
| Customer-driven cost-benefit | RTY | \(\prod Y_i\) | Quantifies rework cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The \(Y = f(x)\) Process-Characteristic Transfer Block

The following Mermaid block illustrates the canonical Six Sigma flow from inputs (\(x\)) through a process block to the output (\(Y\)), with feedback loops for analysis and control.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUTS X1 X2 Xn] --> B[PROCESS BLOCK F]
    B --> C[OUTPUT Y CTQ]
    C --> D{CAPABLE?}
    D -- YES --> E[IN CONTROL SPC CHARTS]
    D -- NO --> F[ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS]
    F --> G[FISHBONE 5M1E]
    G --> H[DOE FMEA]
    H --> B
    E --> I[STANDARDIZE CONTROL PLAN]
    I --> A
```

*Reading the diagram:* Raw inputs enter the process; outputs are measured; if the process is not capable, root-cause tools (Fishbone, FMEA, DOE) drive corrective action back into the process, while capable processes are locked down via Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts and a Control Plan.

---

### 4.2 Variation Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PROCESS VARIATION] --> B{TYPE OF VARIATION}
    B --> C[COMMON CAUSE NOISE]
    B --> D[SPECIAL CAUSE SIGNAL]
    C --> E[INHERENT TO PROCESS]
    C --> F[FIX THE SYSTEM]
    D --> G[EXTERNAL SHOCK]
    D --> H[FIX THE POINT]
    E --> I[SPC CHART STABLE]
    F --> J[KAIZEN DMAIC]
    G --> K[INVESTIGATE ROOT CAUSE]
    H --> L[LOCAL CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    I --> M[CAPABLE PROCESS]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

*Reading the diagram:* Every fluctuation in a process belongs to one of two categories. Common-cause variation is reduced by system redesign; special-cause variation is eliminated by targeted local fixes. Both paths converge on a capable, stable process.

---

### 4.3 SIPOC Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[SUPPLIERS] --> A2[INPUTS] --> A3[PROCESS STEPS] --> A4[OUTPUTS] --> A5[CUSTOMERS]
    A1 -- WHO PROVIDES --> A2
    A2 -- WHAT WE NEED --> A3
    A3 -- HOW WE DO IT --> A4
    A4 -- WHAT WE PRODUCE --> A5
    A5 -- WHO RECEIVES --> A1
```

*Reading the diagram:* A SIPOC diagram flows linearly left-to-right, but the customer feedback loop closes back to suppliers, embodying the closed-loop nature of process analysis.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Process Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[DEFINE PHASE] --> P2[MEASURE PHASE]
    P2 --> P3[ANALYZE PHASE]
    P3 --> P4[IMPROVE PHASE]
    P4 --> P5[CONTROL PHASE]
    P2 -. BASELINE CTQ .-> S1[CAPABILITY CP CPK]
    P2 -. BASELINE CTQ .-> S2[DPMO SIGMA LEVEL]
    P3 -. CAUSE X IDENTIFICATION .-> S3[FISHBONE REGRESSION]
    P5 -. LONG TERM .-> S4[SPC CONTROL CHARTS]
    P5 -. LONG TERM .-> S5[STANDARDIZE CONTROL PLAN]
    S1 --> P3
    S2 --> P3
    S3 --> P4
    S4 --> P1
    S5 --> P1
```

*Reading the diagram:* DMAIC is a sequential five-phase project structure. Process Characteristics and Analysis feeds the Measure and Analyze phases with capability indices and cause-identification tools, while the Control phase locks in gains via SPC and Standard Work. Each output loops back to re-trigger Define for the next project cycle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`**

> Define the term **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) characteristic**. How does it differ from a generic process characteristic? Provide **two** examples of CTQs in a banking process.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristic is a measurable, customer-driven specification of a product or service that directly influences customer satisfaction and is therefore prioritised for Six Sigma analysis (1 mark).

Unlike a generic process characteristic (which may be an internal metric of little customer interest), a CTQ is *translated directly from the Voice of the Customer (VOC)* and tied to specification limits (USL/LSL) (1 mark).

**Examples in a banking process (1 mark):**
- **Loan disbursement turnaround time** in working days (target ≤ 5 days).
- **First-contact resolution rate** of customer complaints (target ≥ 90%).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`**

> Distinguish between **Common-Cause** and **Special-Cause variation** with one engineering example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Common-Cause Variation | Special-Cause Variation |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Inherent to the process | External to the process |
| Predictability | Predictable, stable | Unpredictable, episodic |
| Magnitude | Small, distributed | Large, isolated |
| Action | System redesign | Local corrective action |

**Engineering example (1 mark):**
- *Common cause:* The gradual, expected wear of a CNC cutting tool over 10,000 cycles causes small, regular drift in shaft diameter.
- *Special cause:* A sudden power surge that stops the spindle, producing a single batch of undersized shafts.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **\(Y = f(x)\)** model used in Six Sigma. List the steps involved in process analysis. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A bottling plant fills 500-mL bottles. The specification is \(USL = 505 \text{ mL}\), \(LSL = 495 \text{ mL}\). From 25 subgroups of size 5, the mean range is \(\bar{R} = 1.8 \text{ mL}\) and the grand average is \(\bar{\bar{X}} = 501.2 \text{ mL}\). Compute **Cp**, **Cpk**, and comment on whether the process is capable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a):**

The **\(Y = f(x)\)** transfer function (1 mark) treats every business output as a function of multiple inputs:

- **\(Y\)** = the CTQ (output response) — e.g., bottle volume.
- **\(x_i\)** = controllable and noise input variables — e.g., fill-nozzle pressure, viscosity, line speed, ambient temperature.
- **\(f(\cdot)\)** = the unknown (often non-linear) transfer function approximated via regression or DOE.

Refer to the **block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes for the schematic (2 marks).

**Steps involved in Process Analysis (4 marks):**

1. **Identify the CTQ (\(Y\))** — Translate VOC into measurable specifications.
2. **Map the process** using SIPOC.
3. **List candidate \(x\)'s** via brainstorming, Fishbone, Pareto.
4. **Measure baseline** variation of \(Y\) (mean, sigma, DPMO).
5. **Quantify \(x\) variation** with capability indices.
6. **Statistically link** \(x \to Y\) using regression / DOE.
7. **Reduce variation** in critical \(x\)'s.
8. **Verify** improvement and standardize.

---

**Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Within-subgroup standard deviation** for \(n = 5\), \(d_2 = 2.326\):

$$
\sigma_{\text{within}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{1.8}{2.326} = 0.7739 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cp**:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{505 - 495}{6 \times 0.7739} = \frac{10}{4.6434} = 2.153
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cpk**:

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{505 - 501.2}{3 \times 0.7739} = \frac{3.8}{2.3217} = 1.637
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{501.2 - 495}{2.3217} = \frac{6.2}{2.3217} = 2.671
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.637, 2.671) = 1.637
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation** (1 mark): Since \(C_p = 2.153 > 1.33\) and \(C_{pk} = 1.637 > 1.33\), the process is **highly capable** (i.e., excellent) even with the 1.2 mL centring shift toward the upper specification limit. No immediate process improvement is mandated; however, periodic SPC monitoring is recommended to maintain the centring.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the **SIPOC** framework with a worked example for a college-admission process. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define **DPMO** and **Process Sigma Level**. A process has 18 defects observed in 9,000 units, with each unit having 4 defect opportunities. Compute the **DPMO**, **Yield**, and the corresponding **Sigma Level** using the standard conversion table. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a):**

**SIPOC framework definition (2 marks):** SIPOC is a top-down process-mapping tool that answers five questions — *Who supplies? What do we need? What do we do? What do we produce? Who receives?* — and is applied during the *Measure* phase of DMAIC.

**Worked example — College admission process (5 marks):**

| Element | Example |
|---|---|
| **Suppliers** | Higher-secondary schools, entrance-testing agency (e.g., NTC), scholarship sponsors |
| **Inputs** | Student applications, mark lists, entrance scores, transfer certificates |
| **Process** | Application receipt → Document verification → Entrance test → Merit list generation → Counselling → Seat allotment |
| **Outputs** | Admitted student list, fee receipts, ID cards, joining reports |
| **Customers** | Students, parents, recruiting companies, accreditation bodies |

The framework ensures every step has a *supplier* and a *customer*, making accountability explicit (1 mark).

---

**Solution to B(b):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities):** Total number of defects multiplied by one million, divided by total number of opportunities for defect.
- **Process Sigma Level:** The number of standard deviations between the process mean and the nearest specification limit, assuming a 1.5-sigma shift for long-term performance.

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities**:

$$
U \times O = 9000 \times 4 = 36{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — DPMO**:

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{18}{36{,}000} \times 10^6 = 500 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 3 — Yield**:

$$
Y = \left(1 - \frac{500}{10^6}\right) \times 100\% = 99.95\%
$$

**Step 4 — Sigma Level** (1 mark): Using the standard conversion table, 500 DPMO corresponds to a **Process Sigma Level ≈ 4.91** (or about 4.9-sigma with the 1.5-sigma long-term shift allowance).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Process Characteristics & Analysis questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Cp with Cpk** — Many students compute only one and forget the centring correction.
> 2. **Wrong choice of σ** — Using the *sample* standard deviation \(s\) for Cp/Cpk instead of the *within-subgroup* \(\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2\). Examiners *do* deduct marks here.
> 3. **Forgetting the min() function** — Reporting both \(C_{pk,upper}\) and \(C_{pk,lower}\) without selecting the minimum.
> 4. **Missing units** — Always write mm, mL, seconds etc. after every numerical value.
> 5. **No 1.5-sigma shift** — Sigma Level conversion must apply the 1.5-sigma shift unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **process** converts inputs (\(x\)) into outputs (\(Y\)), described by the transfer function \(Y = f(x)\).
- **CTQs** are the customer-driven, measurable output specifications.
- **VOC, VOP, VOB** must align for a Six Sigma project to succeed.
- **SIPOC** is the canonical process-mapping tool used in the *Measure* phase.
- **Cp** measures *potential* capability; **Cpk** measures *actual* capability with centring correction.
- **Pp** and **Ppk** are the long-term (overall) counterparts; they typically use \(\sigma_{\text{overall}}\) instead of \(\sigma_{\text{within}}\).
- **DPMO** standardises defect rates across industries and converts into the **Process Sigma Level** using the 1.5-sigma-shift table.
- **Common-cause** variation → system redesign; **Special-cause** variation → local corrective action.
- Always use **min()** when reporting Cpk and Ppk.
- Always report **units** and state which σ you used.
- A process is **capable** if \(C_{pk} \geq 1.33\); **excellent** if \(C_{pk} \geq 1.67\); **Six-Sigma class** if \(C_{pk} \geq 2.00\).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Mapping (SIPOC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Mapping (SIPOC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SIPOC** is a high-level **process mapping tool** used in the **Define (D)** phase of the **DMAIC** methodology of Six Sigma. The acronym **SIPOC** stands for:

- **S** – **Suppliers** (the entities that provide the inputs)
- **I** – **Inputs** (the raw materials, data, or information required to execute the process)
- **P** – **Process** (the series of steps that transform inputs into outputs — typically 4 to 6 high-level steps)
- **O** – **Outputs** (the products, services, or information produced by the process)
- **C** – **Customers** (the recipients of the outputs, both internal and external)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SIPOC is recognized by KTU as a foundational **Project Charter** supplement. It is universally taught as the *first* diagrammatic step before constructing a detailed **Process Flow Chart**, **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram**, or **Voice of Customer (VOC)** matrix. It provides a "30,000-foot view" of a process before deeper analytical tools are deployed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of SIPOC like ordering a **pizza from a restaurant**:

- **Suppliers** → The farm (provides cheese, flour, tomatoes) and the dairy.
- **Inputs** → Raw dough, mozzarella, sauce, toppings, and your *order instructions*.
- **Process** → (1) Receive order → (2) Prepare dough → (3) Add toppings → (4) Bake → (5) Deliver.
- **Outputs** → A hot, boxed pizza and a printed bill/receipt.
- **Customers** → You (the diner) and your family members (internal recipients) who eat the slices.

In one glance, the restaurant manager understands the **entire ecosystem** of producing a pizza — exactly what SIPOC does for an engineer or Six Sigma Black Belt analyzing a manufacturing or service workflow.

## 1.3 Standard Six Sigma Metrics Referenced

- **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)**: The measurable characteristics of the output that the customer cares about.
- **VOC (Voice of Customer)**: The explicit/implicit needs, expectations, and feedback of the customer.
- **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)**: The financial penalty incurred due to defects, rework, or warranty claims — used to justify Six Sigma projects.
- **COPQ Components**: **Prevention Cost + Appraisal Cost + Internal Failure Cost + External Failure Cost**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Process** in Six Sigma is defined as a *set of causes and conditions that repeatedly come together to transform inputs into outputs*. A process is *capable* when its natural variation falls within the customer’s specification limits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of SIPOC Construction

The SIPOC diagram is *not* built sequentially from S to C. It is built **in reverse order**, starting from the **Customer**, because the Six Sigma philosophy is **customer-centric**.

The recommended order of construction is:

1. **Identify the Customers (C)** — both internal and external.
2. **Define the Outputs (O)** — what the customers receive; link each output to a CTQ.
3. **Outline the Process (P)** — 4 to 6 high-level steps. Never exceed 7 steps at this level.
4. **List the Inputs (I)** — what is required to produce each output.
5. **Identify the Suppliers (S)** — who provides each input.

## 2.2 Element-by-Element Breakdown

| Element | Definition | Typical Sources of Information | Example (Hospital Admission) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S – Suppliers** | Entities (internal or external) that provide inputs | Procurement records, vendor lists, inter-departmental SLAs | Patient, Referring Doctor, Insurance Company |
| **I – Inputs** | Materials, data, instructions that trigger the process | Bill of Materials, data dictionaries, training manuals | Patient records, ID proof, admission form |
| **P – Process** | 4–6 high-level transformation steps | Existing SOPs, work instructions | Register → Verify → Allocate Bed → Inform Ward |
| **O – Outputs** | Tangible/intangible deliverables | Service catalog, delivery records | Admitted patient, case file, billing record |
| **C – Customers** | Recipients of outputs | VOC surveys, complaint logs | Patient, Attending Physician, Billing Dept. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** SIPOC is a *boundary-setting* tool. It deliberately **omits** low-level details (like decision diamonds, sub-loops, or rework paths) to maintain a panoramic view. It is typically placed as **Exhibit 1** in a DMAIC project report.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Expression | Units / Boundary | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Six Sigma Quality Level | $3.4 \text{ DPMO}$ | Defects per Million Opportunities | World-class benchmark |
| Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Dimensionless; $\geq 1.33$ acceptable, $\geq 1.67$ capable | Measures centeredness |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $Y_{rt} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{N_i - D_i}{N_i}\right)$ | $0 \leq Y_{rt} \leq 1$ | Measures end-to-end yield |
| DPMO Calculation | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times N}$ | $D$ = defects, $U$ = units, $N$ = opportunities per unit | Defect rate metric |
| Sigma Level Conversion | $\text{Defect Rate} = \Phi\left(k - \dfrac{3}{k}\right) \times 10^{6}$ | $k$ = sigma level | Process performance |
| Cost of Poor Quality | $\text{COPQ} = C_{prev} + C_{app} + C_{intF} + C_{extF}$ | Currency (INR/USD) | Financial justification |
| Total Process Steps (SIPOC limit) | $4 \leq P \leq 6$ | Steps | Maintains panoramic view |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Warning on LaTeX:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value bars inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table layout (e.g., write `\vert \mu \vert` instead of `\vert \mu \vert`).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing:** SIPOC a CNC machining line to identify which supplier of bar stock causes dimensional variation.
- **Software/Tech:** SIPOC the *bug-resolution* workflow to identify the test engineer (supplier) of defect tickets (input).
- **Healthcare:** SIPOC the *patient discharge* process to reduce average Length-of-Stay (LOS).
- **Civil Engineering:** SIPOC a *concrete pouring* sub-process to identify formwork vendors, curing inputs, and quality inspectors.
- **MBA / Industrial Engineering:** SIPOC is the *gateway artifact* before a **Value Stream Map (VSM)** is constructed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Construction of SIPOC

The following is a **rigorous, exhaustive, step-by-step procedure** for building a SIPOC diagram as taught in Six Sigma Green Belt / Black Belt curricula and tested in KTU examinations.

## 3.1 Stage 1 — Define the Process Boundaries

**Step 1.1:** Write the **Process Name** and **Process Owner**.
**Step 1.2:** Define the **Start Point (SIP)** and **End Point (EOP)** of the process.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process Scope} &= \{x \mid \text{SIP} \leq x \leq \text{EOP}\} \\[4pt]
\text{where } x &= \text{any activity, decision, or handoff within the boundary}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1.3:** Identify the **Process Charter Number** (typically issued by the Project Champion) for traceability.

## 3.2 Stage 2 — Reverse-Engineer the SIPOC

**Step 2.1 (Customers → C):** List all customers. Classify them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{external} \cup C_{internal} \\[4pt]
C_{external} &= \{\text{end users, paying clients, regulators}\} \\[4pt]
C_{internal} &= \{\text{downstream departments, management, support teams}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.2 (Outputs → O):** For each customer, list the deliverable they receive.

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_i &= f(C_i, \text{CTQ}_i) \\[4pt]
\text{where } f &= \text{mapping from customer need to deliverable output}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.3 (Process → P):** Construct the 4–6 high-level steps. A common pattern:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \{p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4, p_5\}, \quad \text{where typically } p_1 &= \text{Receive Trigger} \\
p_2 &= \text{Acquire Inputs} \\
p_3 &= \text{Transform} \\
p_4 &= \text{Verify} \\
p_5 &= \text{Deliver}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.4 (Inputs → I):** For each process step, list the required inputs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= \bigcup_{j=1}^{5} I_{p_j} \\[4pt]
I_{p_j} &= \{\text{material}_j, \text{data}_j, \text{instruction}_j, \text{resource}_j\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.5 (Suppliers → S):** Map each input to its supplier.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S: I &\rightarrow \text{Supplier} \\[4pt]
s(i_k) &= \text{the entity providing } i_k
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Stage 3 — Validate with VOC and CTQ

Cross-reference each output with at least one CTQ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CTQ}_i \in \mathbb{R}^+ \quad &\text{with metric unit} \\[4pt]
\text{CTQ Target} &= \text{Specification Limit from VOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Rule:** Every SIPOC must satisfy the **closed-loop condition**:
>
> $$\sum \text{Inputs} \xrightarrow{\text{Process}} \sum \text{Outputs} \xrightarrow{\text{Customers}}$$
>
> If any output has *no customer*, or any input has *no supplier*, the SIPOC is **incomplete**.

## 3.4 Stage 4 — Publish and Freeze the SIPOC

- Add **Date of Issue** and **Revision Number**.
- Obtain **sign-off** from the Process Owner and the Project Champion.
- Attach the SIPOC to the **Project Charter** as **Annexure A**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standard SIPOC Layout (Block-Level Flow)

The diagram below shows the canonical **SIPOC architecture** with the *inverse-construction logic* highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["S - SUPPLIERS"]
        sA["Vendor A"]
        sB["Internal Dept B"]
        sC["Customer Itself"]
        sD["Regulatory Body"]
    end

    subgraph B["I - INPUTS"]
        iA["Raw Materials"]
        iB["Specifications"]
        iC["Order Request"]
        iD["Compliance Data"]
    end

    subgraph C["P - PROCESS 4 to 6 Steps"]
        p1["P1 Receive Trigger"]
        p2["P2 Acquire Inputs"]
        p3["P3 Transform"]
        p4["P4 Verify Quality"]
        p5["P5 Deliver Output"]
    end

    subgraph D["O - OUTPUTS"]
        oA["Product or Service"]
        oB["Documentation"]
        oC["Invoice or Report"]
    end

    subgraph E["C - CUSTOMERS"]
        cA["External Customer"]
        cB["Internal Customer"]
        cC["Regulator"]
    end

    sA --> iA
    sB --> iB
    sC --> iC
    sD --> iD
    iA --> p1
    iB --> p1
    iC --> p1
    iD --> p1
    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> oA
    p5 --> oB
    p5 --> oC
    oA --> cA
    oB --> cB
    oC --> cC
```

## 4.2 Inverse Construction Flow (How to Build SIPOC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Start DMAIC Define Phase"]) --> step1["Step 1 Identify Customers C"]
    step1 --> step2["Step 2 Define Outputs O and CTQs"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3 Outline 4 to 6 Process Steps P"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4 List Inputs I"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5 Map Suppliers S to Inputs"]
    step5 --> check{"All Outputs have Customers and Inputs have Suppliers?"}
    check -- No --> fix["Revise SIPOC and Fill Gaps"]
    fix --> step1
    check -- Yes --> signoff["Obtain Sign off from Champion"]
    signoff --> done(["SIPOC Attached to Project Charter"])
```

## 4.3 Worked Example — SIPOC for a University Course Registration Process

| Column | Content |
|---|---|
| **Suppliers** | Student, Registrar Office, Finance Department, UGC, ERP Vendor |
| **Inputs** | Student application form, fee receipt, academic transcripts, ERP credentials, course catalog |
| **Process (5 Steps)** | (1) Receive Application → (2) Verify Eligibility → (3) Collect Fee → (4) Assign Roll Number → (5) Confirm Registration |
| **Outputs** | Registered student record, ID card, timetable, fee receipt |
| **Customers** | Student (external), Department (internal), Controller of Examinations (internal), UGC (regulatory) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip:** In KTU answer sheets, always draw the SIPOC as a **single-row table** with **five columns** labeled S, I, P, O, C. This is the universally accepted board-exam format.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define SIPOC. Mention the full form and state at which DMAIC phase it is used.**

**Model Answer:**

**SIPOC** stands for **Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, and Customers**. It is a high-level process mapping tool used in the **Define (D) phase** of the **DMAIC** methodology to provide a panoramic view of a process before detailed analysis. It helps identify process boundaries, stakeholders, and the customer–supplier chain at a glance. SIPOC is typically the first visual artifact created in any Six Sigma project and is attached to the **Project Charter** as an annexure. *(3 marks)*

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Why is SIPOC constructed in the reverse order (C → O → P → I → S) and not in the natural S → I → P → O → C order?**

**Model Answer:**

SIPOC is built starting from the **Customer (C)** because Six Sigma is a *customer-centric* philosophy — every process exists to deliver value to a customer. By identifying the customer first, the team ensures that the outputs defined are *relevant* and tied to actual **CTQs (Critical-to-Quality)**. If built in the S → I → P → O → C sequence, the team risks designing a process around *available inputs* rather than *customer needs*. The reverse order enforces the principle: *"Begin with the end in mind."* This reverse construction also reduces rework during project execution. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **exceed 7 process steps** when drawing SIPOC — the examiner deducts up to 2 marks because SIPOC must be **high-level**, not a detailed process flow.
> - Students often **omit internal vs external customers** — full 1 mark lost.
> - Drawing SIPOC without **CTQ linkage** to outputs is a major deduction trigger.
> - **Do NOT** confuse SIPOC with **COPIS** (the reverse form used occasionally in Design for Six Sigma — DFSS).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the five elements of SIPOC with a suitable example from a manufacturing or service industry. Draw a neat SIPOC diagram.

**Model Answer:**

SIPOC consists of five elements as follows:

1. **Suppliers (S):** Entities (vendors, internal departments, customers themselves) that provide the required inputs to the process. Example: For a *car assembly line*, suppliers include the *steel vendor*, *tyre manufacturer*, and *electronics supplier*.

2. **Inputs (I):** The raw materials, information, or specifications supplied to the process. Example: Steel sheets, engine blocks, seat assemblies, and the *Bill of Materials (BOM)*.

3. **Process (P):** A high-level sequence of 4–6 transformation steps. Example: *(1) Receive parts → (2) Assemble chassis → (3) Mount engine → (4) Paint and finish → (5) Quality check and dispatch*.

4. **Outputs (O):** The tangible deliverables (products, services, or information) produced by the process. Example: A finished car, warranty card, and invoice.

5. **Customers (C):** The recipients of the outputs — both *external* (end buyer, dealer) and *internal* (sales department, accounts team).

**Neat SIPOC Diagram:**

| S | I | P | O | C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steel Vendor | Steel sheets | P1 Receive parts | Finished car | Dealer |
| Tyre Manufacturer | Wheels, tyres | P2 Assemble chassis | Warranty card | End customer |
| Electronics Supplier | ECU, wiring harness | P3 Mount engine | Invoice | Accounts Dept. |
| Paint Vendor | Paint, thinner | P4 Paint and finish | QC certificate | Service Centre |
| Internal Stores | Bolts, fasteners | P5 QC and dispatch | Delivery note | Logistics |

*(7 marks: 4 for description + 3 for diagram)*

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the role of SIPOC in the Define phase of DMAIC. How does it differ from a detailed Process Flow Chart?

**Model Answer:**

In the **Define phase** of DMAIC, SIPOC plays four critical roles:

1. **Scope Definition:** SIPOC sets the *boundaries* of the project — what is in scope and what is out of scope. This prevents scope creep.
2. **Stakeholder Identification:** All suppliers and customers are listed, enabling stakeholder analysis and RACI matrix construction.
3. **CTQ Linkage:** Each output is mapped to a CTQ, providing the foundation for the Voice of Customer (VOC) matrix and the Quality Function Deployment (QFD) matrix.
4. **Communication:** SIPOC serves as a **single-page communication artifact** for the project team, sponsor, and management to align on the process.

**Difference from a Process Flow Chart:**

| Aspect | SIPOC | Process Flow Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Level of Detail** | High-level (4–6 steps) | Granular (every decision, rework, sub-process) |
| **Purpose** | Scope and stakeholder identification | Step-by-step process analysis |
| **Decision Points** | None | Yes (diamond symbols) |
| **When Created** | First, in Define phase | Later, in Measure/Analyze phase |
| **Audience** | Sponsor, team, management | Process engineers, analysts |

*(7 marks: 4 for DMAIC role + 3 for comparison)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Construct a SIPOC diagram for the **process of issuing a KTU Hall Ticket** to a B.Tech student. Clearly list at least 3 suppliers, 4 inputs, 5 process steps, 3 outputs, and 3 customers.

**Model Answer:**

| Element | Content |
|---|---|
| **Suppliers (S)** | (1) University ERP Server Team, (2) Office of the Controller of Examinations (COE), (3) Student (self), (4) Affiliated College Principal |
| **Inputs (I)** | (1) Student registration data, (2) Fee payment confirmation, (3) Exam registration form, (4) Photograph and signature upload, (5) Attendance eligibility certificate |
| **Process (P)** | (1) Receive exam registration → (2) Verify fee payment → (3) Check attendance eligibility (75% rule) → (4) Generate hall ticket in ERP → (5) Approve and dispatch digitally |
| **Outputs (O)** | (1) Digital hall ticket (PDF), (2) SMS confirmation, (3) Attendance compliance report |
| **Customers (C)** | (1) Student (external), (2) Exam Invigilator (internal), (3) COE Office (internal) |

*(7 marks: 5 for table + 2 for explanation of CTQ linkage)*

**CTQ Linkage Examples:**
- *Output 1 (Hall Ticket)* → CTQ: *Accuracy of name, register number, and exam center* — measured as **0 defects** (Six Sigma target).
- *Output 2 (SMS)* → CTQ: *Delivery within 24 hours of generation* — measured in **hours**.

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)**. How are CTQs derived from SIPOC outputs, and how do they lead to the **SIPOC-to-CTQ Tree**?

**Model Answer:**

A **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** is a measurable characteristic of a product or service that the customer considers critical to *perceived quality* and *satisfaction*. CTQs translate *qualitative* customer needs into *quantitative* specifications.

**Derivation of CTQs from SIPOC Outputs:**

For each output $O_i$ listed in SIPOC, the project team performs a **VOC → CTQ translation** using the phrase:

$$
\text{“The customer wants the } \underbrace{O_i}_{\text{Output}} \text{ to be } \underbrace{X}_{\text{CTQ attribute}} \text{ within } \underbrace{L}_{\text{Tolerance}}”}
$$

**Example:**
- VOC: *"I want my pizza to be hot when delivered."*
- Output (from SIPOC): Pizza.
- CTQ: *Temperature at delivery* $\geq 60^{\circ}\text{C}$ within 30 minutes of baking.

**SIPOC-to-CTQ Tree Structure:**

```
Output (from SIPOC)
  └── CTQ (measurable characteristic)
        └── Specification Limit (SL)
              └── Measurement System
                    └── Defect Definition
```

**Mathematical Expression of CTQ Compliance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield}_{CTQ} &= \dfrac{N_{\text{units meeting CTQ}}}{N_{\text{total units}}} \\[4pt]
\text{DPMO}_{CTQ} &= \left(1 - \text{Yield}_{CTQ}\right) \times 10^{6} \\[4pt]
\sigma\text{ Level} &= \Phi^{-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

The CTQ tree provides the *bridge* from a high-level SIPOC to the statistical analysis performed in the **Measure and Analyze** phases of DMAIC.

*(7 marks: 3 for CTQ definition + 2 for derivation + 2 for tree and formulas)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SIPOC** = **S**uppliers, **I**nputs, **P**rocess, **O**utputs, **C**ustomers.
- Always constructed in the **reverse order**: C → O → P → I → S.
- Process column must contain **4 to 6 steps only** — never more.
- Used in the **Define phase** of **DMAIC**.
- SIPOC is a **panoramic (high-level)** tool — *not* a detailed process flow chart.
- Every output must be linked to a **CTQ**; every input must be linked to a **supplier**.
- Internal and external customers must be **distinguished**.
- SIPOC is **Exhibit 1** in a Six Sigma project report, attached to the **Project Charter**.
- Formula to remember: $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ and $Y_{rt} = \prod Y_i$.
- **Don't confuse** SIPOC with **COPIS** (reverse form used in DFSS).
- Common CTQs: defect rate, cycle time, on-time delivery, accuracy, customer satisfaction score (CSAT).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measure Phase: Data Collection and Summarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measure Phase: Data Collection and Summarization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Measure Phase** is the second stage of the **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) roadmap used in Six Sigma projects. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEPET742, this phase is formally defined as:

> The structured process of quantifying the current performance of a process by establishing reliable data collection systems, defining operational metrics, summarizing baseline data using descriptive statistics, and validating the measurement system itself. Its primary objective is to objectively establish a **factual baseline** against which future improvements can be benchmarked.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** In ESE (End Semester Examination), the Measure Phase is *never* a stand-alone descriptive question. It is *always* tested in combination with statistical summarization (mean, standard deviation, Cp, Cpk). Always pair the conceptual answer with a numerical example.

The two pillars of this phase are:
1. **Data Collection** — gathering accurate, repeatable, and operationally-defined data.
2. **Data Summarization** — converting raw data points into meaningful statistical descriptors.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor trying to treat a patient with high fever. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor does three things:
1. Asks what symptoms the patient has (**Define** — what's the problem?).
2. Measures the temperature, blood pressure, weight, and runs blood tests (**Measure** — how bad is it right now?).
3. Records these readings on a chart to compare with the healthy range (**Summarize** — what does the data say?).

The **Measure Phase** is exactly this doctor's diagnostic step. Without it, the doctor (and the Six Sigma team) would be "guessing" the disease (problem). The "readings" become the **baseline**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering analogy:** In a CNC machining cell producing aluminum brackets, the Measure Phase involves:
> - Defining "defect" as any bracket outside ±0.05 mm tolerance.
> - Sampling 125 brackets over 5 days.
> - Recording the diameter of each bracket on a data sheet.
> - Computing the mean and standard deviation of those 125 readings.
> - Plotting a histogram to see the natural variation pattern.
> 
> Only *after* this baseline is established can the team conclude whether the process is "capable" and quantify how much improvement is needed.

---

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary (High-Frequency KTU Terms)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Baseline** | The current quantified state of the process *before* improvement. |
| **Operational Definition** | A precise, unambiguous description of *how* a characteristic will be measured. |
| **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** | The output characteristic that the customer cares about most. |
| **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities — the core Six Sigma metric. |
| **Yield** | The proportion of defect-free units produced. |
| **SPC** | Statistical Process Control — continuous monitoring using control charts. |
| **VOC / VOB / VOE** | Voice of Customer / Business / Employee — sources of CTQ derivation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** Six Sigma quality corresponds to **3.4 DPMO**, which translates to a process yield of **99.99966%** — i.e., only 3.4 defects allowed per million opportunities. This is the **golden benchmark** examiners love to quote.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Flow of the Measure Phase

The Measure Phase is executed in **five sequential logic steps**. Each step has a clear "Why" and "How":

**Step 1 — Process Mapping (As-Is)**
- *Why:* To understand exactly which subprocesses generate data.
- *How:* Using SIPOC (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) and detailed flowcharts. The **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality) is mapped onto each subprocess.

**Step 2 — Operational Definitions & Data Collection Plan (DCP)**
- *Why:* Two engineers measuring the same part must arrive at the same number. Ambiguity = data unreliability.
- *How:* The DCP table specifies: What to measure, how to measure, when, by whom, with what instrument, sample size, and recording format.

**Step 3 — Measurement System Analysis (MSA)**
- *Why:* A gauge that reads "1 kg" when the actual weight is "1.3 kg" destroys every downstream statistic.
- *How:* Two MSA tools are critical:
  - **Gage R&R** (Repeatability & Reproducibility) — measures gauge variation.
  - **Attribute Agreement Analysis** — for pass/fail or go/no-go data.

**Step 4 — Data Collection**
- *Why:* Without raw data, statistics are fiction.
- *How:* Sampling strategies:
  - **Random sampling** — every item has equal chance.
  - **Stratified sampling** — sub-group population first, then random within strata.
  - **Systematic sampling** — every $k$-th item (e.g., every 10th).
  - **Rational sub-grouping** — items produced under nearly identical conditions.

**Step 5 — Data Summarization (Descriptive Statistics + Visualization)**
- *Why:* 10,000 raw numbers are unreadable; 5 statistical descriptors are decision-ready.
- *How:* Compute central tendency, dispersion, shape, and visualize via histogram, Pareto, box plot, scatter plot.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Convention:** $n$ = sample size, $N$ = population size, $x_i$ = individual data point, $\bar{x}$ = sample mean, $\mu$ = population mean, $s$ = sample standard deviation, $\sigma$ = population standard deviation.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Arithmetic Mean (Sample)** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | Same as the data unit |
| 2 | **Median** | Middle value of sorted $x$ (or avg of two middles) | Position $= \frac{n+1}{2}$ |
| 3 | **Mode** | Most frequently occurring value | Useful for categorical |
| 4 | **Range** | $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Sensitive to outliers |
| 5 | **Sample Variance** | $s^{2} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{n-1}$ | Square of data unit |
| 6 | **Sample Std. Deviation** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{n-1}}$ | Same as data unit |
| 7 | **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless % |
| 8 | **Standard Error of Mean** | $SE = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Used in confidence intervals |
| 9 | **Process Capability ($C_p$)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability (within) |
| 10 | **Process Capability ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Actual capability (centred) |
| 11 | **Process Performance ($P_p$)** | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Overall capability (long-term) |
| 12 | **Process Performance ($P_{pk}$)** | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Centred long-term |
| 13 | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | Defects Per Million Opps |
| 14 | **Yield** | $Y = 1 - \text{DPO}$, where $\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{U \cdot O}$ | Decimal or % |
| 15 | **Sigma Level ($Z$)** | $Z = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Used to read DPMO table |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Pitfall:** $C_p$ assumes the process is *perfectly centered* between LSL and USL. In reality, processes drift, so **$C_{pk}$ is the more meaningful industrial index**. A process with $C_p = 2$ but $C_{pk} = 0.8$ is a *centered problem*, not a *variation problem*.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (CNC, casting, forging):** $C_{pk}$ values guide acceptance of new equipment.
- **Healthcare (Six Sigma hospitals):** Wait-time variability is summarized using standard deviation; $C_{pk}$ benchmarks ER performance.
- **Software Engineering (IT Six Sigma):** Defect density (bugs per KLOC) is treated as a continuous CTQ.
- **Banking/Service:** First-call resolution rate becomes the attribute data summarized via DPMO.

The Measure Phase produces the **only legitimate evidence** that improvement has occurred. A team that skips it is "improving in the dark."
