## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Why Sample Variance Uses $(n-1)$

**Goal:** Show mathematically why $s^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ and **not** $n$.

**Starting point:** We want an estimator $s^2$ that is **unbiased**, i.e., $E[s^2] = \sigma^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } Q &= \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu + \mu - \bar{x})^2 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n}[(x_i - \mu) - (\bar{x} - \mu)]^2 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 \;-\; 2(\bar{x} - \mu)\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) \;+\; n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice that $\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = n(\bar{x} - \mu)$. So the middle term becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= -2(\bar{x} - \mu) \cdot n(\bar{x} - \mu) \;+\; n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2 \\
&= -2n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2 \;+\; n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2 \\
&= -n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 - n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectation on both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[Q] &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[(x_i - \mu)^2] - n\,E[(\bar{x} - \mu)^2] \\
&= n\sigma^2 - n \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \\
&= n\sigma^2 - \sigma^2 \\
&= (n-1)\sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^2 = \frac{Q}{n-1} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E[s^2] = \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Dividing by $(n-1)$, called **Bessel's correction**, restores unbiasedness. The "$1$" lost in the denominator is the **degree of freedom consumed** by using $\bar{x}$ as a proxy for $\mu$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If a question specifies "population data" (all units are measured, no sampling), then divide by $N$, not $N-1$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this distinction is ignored.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full Measure Phase Computation

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A CNC lathe produces shafts with a target diameter of 25.00 mm. Specification limits are $LSL = 24.90$ mm and $USL = 25.10$ mm. A random sample of $n = 10$ shafts is taken and diameters (in mm) are:

$$
25.02,\; 24.97,\; 25.05,\; 24.99,\; 25.01,\; 24.95,\; 25.03,\; 25.00,\; 24.98,\; 25.02
$$

**Compute:** (a) Mean and standard deviation. (b) Range and CV. (c) $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$. (d) Comment on process capability.

#### (a) Mean and Standard Deviation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{25.02 + 24.97 + 25.05 + 24.99 + 25.01 + 24.95 + 25.03 + 25.00 + 24.98 + 25.02}{10} \\
&= \frac{250.02}{10} = 25.002 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 &= (0.018)^2 + (-0.032)^2 + (0.048)^2 + (-0.012)^2 + (0.008)^2 \\
&\quad + (-0.052)^2 + (0.028)^2 + (-0.002)^2 + (-0.022)^2 + (0.018)^2 \\
&= 0.000324 + 0.001024 + 0.002304 + 0.000144 + 0.000064 \\
&\quad + 0.002704 + 0.000784 + 0.000004 + 0.000484 + 0.000324 \\
&= 0.00816 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^2 &= \frac{0.00816}{10 - 1} = \frac{0.00816}{9} = 0.000907 \text{ mm}^2 \\
s &= \sqrt{0.000907} = 0.0301 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Range and CV

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= x_{\max} - x_{\min} = 25.05 - 24.95 = 0.10 \text{ mm} \\
CV &= \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.0301}{25.002} \times 100\% = 0.1204\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) Capability and Performance Indices

For a stable process, we use $\sigma = s = 0.0301$ mm (assumed).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.0301} = \frac{0.20}{0.1806} = 1.107 \\
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{25.10 - 25.002}{3 \times 0.0301},\; \frac{25.002 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.0301}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{0.098}{0.0903},\; \frac{0.102}{0.0903}\right) \\
&= \min(1.085,\; 1.130) = 1.085
\end{aligned}
$$

Since we have only sample data, $P_p = P_{pk} = C_p = C_{pk}$ in this small-sample case:

$$
P_p = 1.107,\qquad P_{pk} = 1.085
$$

#### (d) Engineering Interpretation

> **Result:** $C_{pk} = 1.085$ is **below the industrial benchmark of 1.33** for an existing process, and far below **1.67** for a new process. The shaft-turning process is **marginally capable** but not centered optimally — note that $\bar{x} = 25.002$ is slightly above the target $25.00$, pulling the upper margin tighter than the lower margin. The team should aim to (i) reduce variation (since $C_p$ is also low) and (ii) re-center the spindle offset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Benchmarks (must memorize for KTU):**
> - $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → Six Sigma / World-class
> - $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ → Capable (existing process minimum)
> - $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → Marginally capable
> - $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → **Not capable**, defects are statistically inevitable.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Data Type Selection and Tool Mapping

| CTQ Type | Example | Sample Size Guidance | Summarization Tools | Visualization |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Variable** | Diameter, weight, voltage | $n \geq 30$ for normality | Mean, $s$, $C_{pk}$ | Histogram, Box plot, Run chart |
| **Attribute (Defective)** | Pass/Fail unit | $n \geq 50$ | $p$-chart, DPMO | Pareto chart |
| **Attribute (Defects)** | Scratches per panel | $n \geq 25$ sub-groups | $c$-chart, $u$-chart | Pareto + control chart |
| **Ordinal / Likert** | Customer rating 1–5 | $n \geq 100$ | Median, mode, % top-2 | Bar chart, stacked bar |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Measure Phase Workflow Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional architecture** of the Measure Phase, mapping the interaction between process mapping, data collection, MSA, and summarization activities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Charter from Define Phase] --> B[Step 1: SIPOC and Process Mapping]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify CTQs and KPIs]
    C --> D[Step 3: Build Operational Definitions]
    D --> E[Step 4: Draft Data Collection Plan DCP]
    E --> F{Step 5: MSA - Gage R and R Approved?}
    F -- No --> G[Calibrate Gauge / Train Operators]
    G --> F
    F -- Yes --> H[Step 6: Collect Raw Data]
    H --> I[Step 7: Stratify and Clean Data]
    I --> J[Step 8: Compute Descriptive Statistics]
    J --> K[Step 9: Build Visualizations]
    K --> L[Step 10: Compute Capability Indices Cp Cpk Pp Ppk]
    L --> M{Meets Capability Target?}
    M -- No --> N[Return to Define / Analyze for Root Cause]
    M -- Yes --> O[Baseline Frozen - Hand off to Analyze Phase]
    O --> P((Analyze Phase DMAIC Step 3))
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Tip:** Notice the **feedback loop** from Step 5 (MSA failure) back to calibration. This visually emphasizes that no data is reliable until the gauge is proven. The final frozen baseline at Step 10 is the *contract* between Measure and Analyze.

---

### 4.2 Data Type Decision Tree for CTQ Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CTQ Identified] --> B{Is it measurable on a number scale?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Continuous Variable Type]
    C --> D[Use Mean, Standard Deviation, Cp, Cpk]
    C --> E[Visualize: Histogram, Box Plot, Run Chart]
    B -- No --> F{Does the item either pass or fail?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Attribute Defective Type]
    G --> H[Use p-chart, DPMO, Yield]
    G --> I[Visualize: Pareto Chart]
    F -- No --> J[Attribute Defects Count Type]
    J --> K[Use c-chart or u-chart]
    J --> L[Visualize: Pareto plus Control Chart]
```

---

### 4.3 Sampling Strategy Selection Matrix (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PopulationLayer[Population Universe]
        PO[All Units N]
    end
    subgraph StrategyLayer[Sampling Strategy Choices]
        S1[Simple Random Sampling]
        S2[Stratified Sampling]
        S3[Systematic Sampling]
        S4[Rational Subgroup Sampling]
    end
    subgraph DecisionLayer[Selection Logic]
        D1{Homogeneous batches?}
        D2{Within batch high homogeneity?}
    end
    PO --> D1
    D1 -- Yes --> S1
    D1 -- No --> S2
    S2 --> D2
    D2 -- Yes --> S3
    D2 -- No --> S4
    S1 --> OUT[Sample n Units]
    S3 --> OUT
    S4 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Map to Exam:** Examiners frequently present a process with three machines producing the same part and ask "which sampling strategy?". The correct answer is **stratified sampling** because machine-to-machine variation is a known factor.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*What is the Measure Phase in Six Sigma? List any two objectives of this phase.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Measure Phase is the second stage of the DMAIC methodology. Its primary purpose is to **establish a quantified, fact-based baseline** of the current process performance so that future improvements can be objectively verified.

**Two objectives (any two):**
1. To establish **operational definitions** for each Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) characteristic so that all team members measure the same feature in the same way.
2. To validate the **Measurement System** through Gage R&R studies, ensuring that observed variation is due to the process and not the gauge.
3. To collect and statistically summarize baseline data using **descriptive statistics** (mean, standard deviation, range) and compute **process capability indices** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two objectives = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

*Differentiate between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ with a one-line interpretation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Index | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $C_p$ | $\dfrac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma}$ | **Potential** capability — what the process *could* achieve if perfectly centered. |
| $C_{pk}$ | $\min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\bar{x}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x}-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | **Actual** capability — what the process *is* achieving given its current centering. |

If $C_p = C_{pk}$, the process is perfectly centered. If $C_p > C_{pk}$, the process has a **centering problem** and must be re-aligned before variation is reduced.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five sequential steps** of the Measure Phase with a clear engineering example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing process has specification limits $LSL = 12.00$ mm and $USL = 12.20$ mm. A sample of $n = 8$ measurements yields:

$$
12.08,\; 12.12,\; 12.05,\; 12.10,\; 12.15,\; 12.09,\; 12.11,\; 12.14
$$

Calculate the mean, standard deviation, and the indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. State whether the process is capable according to industrial benchmarks. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to A(a):**

The five steps of the Measure Phase are:

1. **Process Mapping** — Build a SIPOC diagram of the as-is process. Example: In a packaging line (Suppliers: raw material vendors; Inputs: bottles, caps, labels; Process: filling, capping, sealing, labeling; Outputs: packed product; Customers: distributors and end users).

2. **Identify CTQs and Operational Definitions** — The team picks "fill volume" as a CTQ and operationally defines it as "the volume of liquid in mL measured by a calibrated volumetric dispenser at the discharge nozzle, recorded to two decimal places."

3. **Build Data Collection Plan (DCP)** — A table specifying: Characteristic = fill volume; Instrument = 100 mL graduated cylinder; Operator = Quality inspector A; Sample size = 50 bottles per shift; Frequency = every 2 hours.

4. **Validate Measurement System (MSA)** — Perform Gage R&R on the cylinder. Ensure that Gauge R&R contribution to total variation < **30%** (industry threshold) before approving the gauge.

5. **Collect Data and Summarize** — Record the 50 readings, compute $\bar{x}$ and $s$, plot a histogram, and calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$. These constitute the **baseline**.

*(1 mark for naming the 5 steps correctly + 1 mark for context-specific example + remaining 5 marks distributed for clarity, sequential logic, and engineering appropriateness = 7 marks)*

---

**Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{12.08 + 12.12 + 12.05 + 12.10 + 12.15 + 12.09 + 12.11 + 12.14}{8} \\
&= \frac{96.84}{8} = 12.105 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Deviations and Squared Deviations:**

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 12.08 | $-0.025$ | $0.000625$ |
| 12.12 | $+0.015$ | $0.000225$ |
| 12.05 | $-0.055$ | $0.003025$ |
| 12.10 | $-0.005$ | $0.000025$ |
| 12.15 | $+0.045$ | $0.002025$ |
| 12.09 | $-0.015$ | $0.000225$ |
| 12.11 | $+0.005$ | $0.000025$ |
| 12.14 | $+0.035$ | $0.001225$ |
| **Sum** | — | **$0.00740$** |

**Step 3 — Compute Standard Deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.00740}{7}} = \sqrt{0.001057} \\
&\approx 0.0325 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{12.20 - 12.00}{6 \times 0.0325} = \frac{0.20}{0.195} \approx 1.026
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{12.20 - 12.105}{3 \times 0.0325},\; \frac{12.105 - 12.00}{3 \times 0.0325}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{0.095}{0.0975},\; \frac{0.105}{0.0975}\right) \\
&= \min(0.974,\; 1.077) = 0.974
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

> $C_p \approx 1.026$ and $C_{pk} \approx 0.974$ are both **below 1.00**, indicating that the process is **NOT capable**. The process is producing defects relative to the specification limits. Further, $C_p > C_{pk}$ reveals a mild **centering issue** (mean is biased upward). The team should (i) reduce common-cause variation and (ii) re-center the process to the target value of 12.10 mm.

*(Mark distribution: Mean = 1, SD = 2, Cp = 1.5, Cpk = 1.5, Interpretation = 1 = total 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Operational Definitions** and **MSA (Gage R&R)** in the Measure Phase. Why is "calibrated gauge" not sufficient by itself? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A call centre records the following **defects per shift** over 10 shifts: 4, 6, 3, 5, 7, 4, 6, 5, 8, 5. The process has 3 call types, each representing an opportunity for defect. Compute the **DPMO** and the **process sigma level** (assume $C_{pk} = 1.25$ for sigma lookup from standard tables). *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to B(a):**

**Operational Definitions** are unambiguous, agreed-upon statements of *how* a CTQ will be measured. Example: "Customer complaint" is not enough; "any incoming call where the caller expresses dissatisfaction using the words 'unsatisfied', 'disappointed', or 'angry' before hanging up" is an operational definition.

Their role:
1. Eliminate operator-to-operator variation in subjective judgments.
2. Ensure data collected on Day 1 and Day 30 are directly comparable.
3. Form the basis of every downstream statistical test.

**Measurement System Analysis (MSA) — Gage R&R:**
Even with a calibrated gauge, two issues remain:
- **Repeatability** — same operator, same part, same gauge gives different readings (equipment variation).
- **Reproducibility** — different operators give different readings on the same part (operator variation).

Gage R&R quantifies both. The industry rule is:
- Gage R&R < **10%** of total variation → Excellent.
- **10–30%** → Acceptable.
- **> 30%** → Unacceptable; gauge must be replaced or operators retrained.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write "calibrated gauge = good data" in the exam and lose 2 marks. A calibrated gauge only ensures **accuracy at one reference point**; Gage R&R ensures accuracy and precision **across the entire measurement range used in production**. Both are mandatory.

---

**Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Total Defects and Total Units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total defects } D &= 4 + 6 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 4 + 6 + 5 + 8 + 5 = 53 \\
\text{Total units } U &= 10 \text{ shifts} \\
\text{Opportunities per unit } O &= 3 \text{ (call types)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — DPMO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O} = \frac{53 \times 10^6}{10 \times 3} = \frac{53 \times 10^6}{30} \\
&= 1.767 \times 10^6 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **far above** the Six Sigma target of **3.4 DPMO**; the call centre is operating at a very low sigma level (around 2.0 sigma, well below world-class).

**Step 3 — Process Sigma Level from $C_{pk}$:**

For a normally distributed, two-sided specification, the relationship between sigma level $Z$ (in sigma-shift convention where $Z = C_{pk}$ when shift = 0) and $C_{pk}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{short-term}} &\approx C_{pk} + 1.5 \text{ (long-term shift allowance)} \\
&= 1.25 + 1.5 = 2.75 \text{ sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

Looking up the standard Six Sigma DPMO table:

| Sigma Level | DPMO |
|---|---|
| 2.0 | 308,537 |
| 2.5 | 66,807 |
| **2.75** | **≈ 30,000** |
| 3.0 | 6,210 |
| 4.0 | 63 |

The call centre's computed DPMO of **1,766,667** indicates a **process sigma level of approximately 1.5**, vastly below the assumed $C_{pk}$ of 1.25. The mismatch is because the call centre's "defects" are not normally distributed — they are Poisson-distributed count data, requiring a different capability framework.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Warning:** Students often quote $Z = 1.25$ as the final answer without applying the **1.5-sigma shift**. The KTU board always tests whether you know this industry convention. Always write: "Short-term $C_{pk}$ corresponds to short-term sigma; adding 1.5 gives the long-term sigma level reported on the Six Sigma DPMO chart."

*(Mark distribution: DPMO formula = 2, substitution = 1, answer = 1, sigma-shift = 2, lookup + interpretation = 1 = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Measure Phase**

- ✅ The Measure Phase **quantifies** the problem; it does **not** solve it.
- ✅ **Five sequential steps:** Process Map → CTQ + Operational Def. → DCP → MSA → Collect & Summarize.
- ✅ **Operational Definitions** eliminate measurement ambiguity between operators.
- ✅ **Gage R&R < 30%** is the industry acceptance threshold for measurement systems.
- ✅ **Sample variance uses $(n-1)$** (Bessel's correction); **population variance uses $N$**.
- ✅ $C_p$ measures *potential* capability (centered), $C_{pk}$ measures *actual* capability.
- ✅ $C_p > C_{pk}$ ⇒ centering problem; $C_p = C_{pk}$ ⇒ perfectly centered.
- ✅ Industrial $C_{pk}$ benchmarks: **<1.0** Not capable, **1.33** Capable, **1.67** Six Sigma.
- ✅ Six Sigma benchmark = **3.4 DPMO** = **99.99966% yield**.
- ✅ Always add the **1.5-sigma long-term shift** when reporting the "process sigma level."
- ✅ Choose **stratified sampling** when there are known sub-populations (machines, shifts, suppliers).
- ✅ Histogram + $C_{pk}$ is the **mandatory visual deliverable** at the end of Measure Phase.
- ✅ Never proceed to Analyze with un-calibrated gauge data; the **baseline must be valid**.
- ✅ For attribute data (defects), use **DPMO + Pareto chart**, not mean/std deviation.
- ✅ KTU 14-mark questions on this topic **always pair a 7-mark concept** with a **7-mark numerical**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement Systems Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement Systems Fundamentals # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Measurement System A **Measurement System** is a collection of instruments, standards, procedures, and software used to assign a numerical value to a physical, chemical, geometric, or functional characteristic of an object or process. In the **DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)** framework of Six Sigma, the *Measure* phase relies entirely on a robust measurement system, because every subsequent statistical inference about a process is only as good as the data captured by this system. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A measurement system comprises all the operations, personnel, equipment, software, environment, and procedures involved in obtaining a measured value. It transforms an input stimulus (the true characteristic) into an output indication (the recorded value). A *Measurement System Analysis (MSA)* is the formal study used to evaluate the contribution of this system to the total observed variation in a process. The **AIAG MSA Reference Manual (4th Edition)** is the global industry standard referenced implicitly across KTU coursework. --- ## 1.2 The Five Functional Elements of a Measurement System Every measurement system — from a simple ruler to a Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) — can be decomposed into five cascading functional blocks: | # | Element | Function | Real-World Example | |---|---------|----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Primary Sensing Element** (Sensor) | Detects the physical stimulus | Thermocouple junction, Strain gauge, LVDT core | | 2 | **Variable Conversion Element** (Transducer) | Converts the sensed signal into a usable form (usually electrical) | Piezoelectric crystal, RTD | | 3 | **Variable Manipulation Element** | Amplifies, filters, integrates, or linearizes the signal | Op-amp, ADC, Cold-junction compensator | | 4 | **Data Transmission Element** | Carries the conditioned signal to the display | Shielded cable, Wireless telemetry | | 5 | **Data Presentation Element** | Indicates the reading for human interpretation | Digital display, Computer SCADA, Dial gauge | --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Doctor's Diagnostic Kit > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — Reading this once will cement the concept forever:** > Imagine a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. The patient's *true* body temperature is unknown. The doctor uses a *thermometer* (sensor), interprets the *mercury level* (transducer/converter), records the value in a *chart* (data transmission), and announces "**98.6 °F**" (display). If the thermometer is poorly calibrated, the doctor will misdiagnose a fever. **In Six Sigma, the "patient" is the process, and the "thermometer" is your measurement system.** If your Gage is broken, every Pareto chart, control chart, and hypothesis test you build is a lie. A second, sharper analogy: a **ruler** that has been stretched by 2 mm will make every engineering drawing, every tolerance check, and every Cp/Cpk calculation wrong by exactly 2 mm. The Six Sigma Black Belt must therefore *prove* the ruler is trustworthy before trusting any conclusion drawn from it. --- ## 1.4 The Core "Truth-Telling" Metrics of a Measurement System The Six Sigma body of knowledge recognizes **eight** canonical properties. Memorize these — they appear every single semester: 1. **Accuracy (Bias)** — How *close* the average of measurements is to the true value. 2. **Precision (Variance)** — How *repeatable* the measurements are among themselves. 3. **Sensitivity** — Smallest change in stimulus that produces a detectable change in response. 4. **Linearity** — Constancy of bias across the entire operating range. 5. **Stability (Drift)** — Consistency of bias over time. 6. **Repeatability (EV)** — Variation observed when *one* operator measures *one* part *multiple times* with the *same* gage. 7. **Reproducibility (AV)** — Variation observed when *different* operators measure the *same* part with the *same* gage. 8. **Resolution / Discrimination** — Fineness of the smallest readable increment. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark question is *"Differentiate between Accuracy and Precision."* Memorize the difference: **Accuracy = Closeness to True Value (Bias)**, **Precision = Closeness to Repeated Values (Variance)**. A dartboard analogy clinches it: a tight cluster in the bullseye = accurate *and* precise; a tight cluster in the outer ring = precise but not accurate; a scattered pattern in the bullseye = accurate on average but not precise. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Measurement Systems Measurement systems are classified along **three orthogonal axes** that frequently appear in KTU theory questions. ### 2.1.1 Axis 1 — By Output Nature | Type | Description | Engineering Example | |------|-------------|---------------------| | **Analog** | Continuous output proportional to input | Mercury thermometer, Bourdon pressure gauge | | **Digital** | Discrete, quantized output | Digital caliper, CNC probe readout | | **Null-Type (Zero-Deflection)** | Measures by balancing the unknown against a known reference | Wheatstone bridge, Potentiometric balance | | **Deflection-Type** | Output is a deflection proportional to the stimulus | Analog voltmeter, Spring scale | ### 2.1.2 Axis 2 — By Mode of Operation | Mode | Description | When Used | |------|-------------|-----------| | **Active (Intrinsic)** | System extracts energy from the process under measurement | Thermocouple (Seebeck effect) | | **Passive (Modulating)** | System uses an external excitation source | RTD, Strain gauge (Wheatstone bridge) | | **Direct** | No calibration or comparison required | Tachometer | | **Indirect** | Requires calibration against a primary standard | Bourdon gauge | ### 2.1.3 Axis 3 — By Dynamic Response | Characteristic | Static Systems | Dynamic Systems | |----------------|----------------|-----------------| | **Input** | Constant / Slowly varying | Time-varying | | **Response** | Equilibrium value | Transient + Steady state | | **Parameters studied** | Accuracy, Linearity, Hysteresis | Rise time, Settling time, Damping ratio, Natural frequency | --- ## 2.2 The Two Families of System Characteristics ### 2.2.1 Static Characteristics (DC Behaviour) - **Accuracy** = $V_m - V_t$, where $V_m$ is the measured mean and $V_t$ is the true (reference) value. The associated statistical metric is **Bias**. - **Bias %** = $\dfrac{\bar{X} - X_{ref}}{X_{ref}} \times 100\%$ - **Precision** is quantified by standard deviation $\sigma$ or variance $\sigma^2$. High precision = low $\sigma$. - **Linearity** is the slope-of-bias plot. The bias $B$ at reference value $X_i$ is modeled as: $$B_i = a + b \cdot X_i$$ A perfectly linear system has $b = 0$ (bias is constant across the range). Linearity error = maximum deviation of $B_i$ from the best-fit line. - **Sensitivity (S)** = $\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x}$, the slope of the calibration curve at the operating point. - **Resolution (R)** = smallest measurable increment; for digital systems, R = LSB (Least Significant Bit). - **Threshold** = minimum input that produces a measurable output. - **Hysteresis** = difference in output when approached from increasing vs. decreasing input. - **Dead Zone** = range of input for which no output change is observed. - **Stability / Drift** = change in output with time at a constant input, often expressed in units/month. ### 2.2.2 Dynamic Characteristics (AC Behaviour) A linear measurement system is typically modeled as a **first-order** or **second-order** differential equation. - **First-Order System** (e.g., thermometer bulb): $\tau \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$ - $\tau$ = time constant, $K$ = static sensitivity. - Step response: $y(t) = K \cdot x_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ - **Second-Order System** (e.g., accelerometer, seismic mass): $$m\ddot{y} + c\dot{y} + k y = k x(t)$$ - Damping ratio $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$, natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$. - Underdamped, critically damped, overdamped regimes govern rise time, overshoot, and settling time. --- ## 2.3 Measurement System Analysis (MSA) — The Six Sigma Way The **AIAG MSA framework** decomposes the observed *total* variation in measurements into the following orthogonal sources: $$\sigma^2_{Total} = \sigma^2_{Part\text{-}to\text{-}Part} + \sigma^2_{Repeatability} + \sigma^2_{Reproducibility}$$ or in the more common variance-additive form: $$TV^2 = (PV)^2 + (R\&R)^2$$ where: - $TV$ = Total Variation in measurements - $PV$ = Part-to-Part Variation (real product differences — *desired* signal) - $R\&R$ = Repeatability + Reproducibility (measurement *noise* — *undesired*) The decision criteria established by the **AIAG MSA Manual** (and referenced in KTU 2024 scheme questions) are: | %R&R | Decision | Interpretation | |:----:|----------|----------------| | **< 10 %** | ✅ Excellent | Measurement system is world-class; the gage can reliably detect process shifts. | | **10 % – 30 %** | ⚠️ Marginal | May be acceptable for some applications; investigate root causes; seek improvement. | | **> 30 %** | ❌ Unacceptable | Measurement system is unfit for purpose; the process is being controlled by noise, not signal. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **%R&R is the single most-tested quantity in KTU Six Sigma exams.** If you remember nothing else, remember the three thresholds: **10 %** and **30 %**. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates *every* equation you need for Module 2 numerical questions. Memorize the column order. | # | Metric | Formula | Unit / Note | |---|--------|---------|-------------| | 1 | Bias (Accuracy) | $B = \bar{X} - X_{ref}$ | Same as measured variable | | 2 | % Bias | $\%B = \dfrac{\bar{X} - X_{ref}}{X_{ref}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | | 3 | Repeatability (EV) | $EV = \bar{R} \times K_1$ | $K_1 = 0.8862$ for $n=2$ trials | | 4 | Reproducibility (AV) | $AV = \sqrt{(X_{DIFF} \times K_2)^2 - \dfrac{EV^2}{n \cdot r}}$ | $K_2 = 0.5231$ for 3 operators | | 5 | Gage R&R | $R\&R = \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2$ | $6\sigma$ spread | | 6 | Part Variation (PV) | $PV = R_{part} \times K_3$ | $K_3 = 0.4030$ for 3 parts | | 7 | Total Variation (TV) | $TV = \sqrt{R\&R^2 + PV^2$ | $6\sigma$ spread | | 8 | % R&R | $\%R\&R = \dfrac{R\&R}{TV} \times 100$ | Decision metric | | 9 | Number of Distinct Categories (ndc) | $ndc = 1.41 \times \dfrac{PV}{R\&R}$ | Effective resolution | | 10 | Accuracy Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{\sigma_{ms}}{T/6}$ | $T$ = tolerance | | 11 | Precision-to-Tolerance (P/T) | $P/T = \dfrac{6\sigma_{ms}}{T} \times 100$ | Replaces %R&R for attribute gages | | 12 | Linearity Bias | $B_i = a + b X_i$ | $b \to 0$ ideal | | 13 | Stability | $\dfrac{B(t_2) - B(t_1)}{t_2 - t_1}$ | Units / month | | 14 | First-Order Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ (thermal) or $L/R$ (electrical) | seconds | | 15 | Second-Order Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}$ | rad/s | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-symbol trap in Markdown:** Equations above were written with `\vert` and `\sqrt{...}` to avoid breaking the table renderer. Do **not** write `|x|` or `R&R^2 + PV^2` literally inside a table cell — use the LaTeX form. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (Turbine blade inspection):** CMM Gage R&R must be < 10 % to certify blade profile tolerances of ±0.025 mm. - **Pharmaceutical (Tablet weight):** %R&R determines whether 100 % automatic in-line checkweighers or statistical sampling is acceptable under **FDA Process Validation Guidance (2011)**. - **Automotive (Engine block machining):** %R&R is a mandatory line item in every **PPAP (Production Part Approval Process)** submission. - **Semiconductor (Wafer thickness):** At 5 nm nodes, ndc must exceed 8; otherwise the gage cannot distinguish process drift from measurement noise. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Gage R&R Constants (K1, K2, K3) The constants $K_1$, $K_2$, and $K_3$ are not arbitrary — they are derived from the **range-to-standard-deviation conversion factor** $d_2$, which connects the mean range $\bar{R}$ of subgroups of size $n$ to the underlying process standard deviation $\sigma$. ### Step 1 — Range-to-Sigma Conversion For a subgroup of size $n$ drawn from a normal distribution, the expected value of the range is: $$E[R] = d_2 \cdot \sigma$$ Therefore, an unbiased estimator of $\sigma$ is: $$\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$ The constant $d_2$ is tabulated against subgroup size $n$. For $n = 2$ (the case for two repeated measurements), $d_2 = 1.128$, so: $$\dfrac{1}{d_2} = \dfrac{1}{1.128} = 0.8862 = K_1$$ This gives the **first key equation** of Gage R&R: $$EV = \bar{R} \times K_1$$ where $\bar{R}$ is the **average range** across all operator × part combinations. ### Step 2 — Reproducibility Constant K2 For $m$ operators, the range of operator means is $X_{MAX} - X_{MIN} = X_{DIFF}$. The standard deviation of operator means is approximated by: $$\sigma_{operator} \approx \dfrac{X_{DIFF}}{d_2^{(m)}}$$ For $m = 3$ operators, $d_2 = 1.910$, so: $$\dfrac{1}{d_2} = \dfrac{1}{1.910} = 0.5231 = K_2$$ The variance contributed by operators (Reproducibility) is: $$AV^2 = (X_{DIFF} \times K_2)^2$$ But because each operator's mean itself is estimated from $n \cdot r$ measurements, we must remove the *within-operator* variance (Repeatability) embedded in the operator means: $$AV = \sqrt{(X_{DIFF} \times K_2)^2 - \dfrac{EV^2}{n \cdot r}}$$ ### Step 3 — Part Variation Constant K3 For $p$ parts, the range of part means is $R_{part} = X_{part,max} - X_{part,min}$. Using $d_2$ for $p = 3$ parts: $$\dfrac{1}{d_2^{(3)}} = \dfrac{1}{2.482} = 0.4030 = K_3$$ $$PV = R_{part} \times K_3$$ ### Step 4 — Final Gage R&R and %R&R Putting it all together: $$\begin{aligned} R\&R &= \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2} \\ TV &= \sqrt{R\&R^2 + PV^2} \\ \%R\&R &= \dfrac{R\&R}{TV} \times 100 \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Full Gage R&R Calculation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement (typical 14-mark KTU style):** > A manufacturing cell in Kochi produces precision-machined flanges. A Gage R&R study uses **3 operators (A, B, C)**, **3 parts (1, 2, 3)**, and **2 trials** per operator-part combination. The measurements (in mm, target = 50.00) are tabulated below. Compute EV, AV, R&R, PV, TV, %R&R, and ndc, and state the decision. ### Step 1 — Raw Data Matrix | Part \ Operator | A (Trial 1) | A (Trial 2) | B (Trial 1) | B (Trial 2) | C (Trial 1) | C (Trial 2) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 50.02 | 49.98 | 50.05 | 50.01 | 49.95 | 49.99 | | 2 | 49.90 | 49.94 | 49.85 | 49.89 | 49.92 | 49.88 | | 3 | 50.10 | 50.06 | 50.12 | 50.08 | 50.05 | 50.09 | ### Step 2 — Compute the Range for Each Operator × Part Cell For Part 1, Operator A: Range = $\vert 50.02 - 49.98 \vert = 0.04$ | Part \ Operator | A Range | B Range | C Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.04 | 0.04 | 0.04 | | 2 | 0.04 | 0.04 | 0.04 | | 3 | 0.04 | 0.04 | 0.04 | The average of all 9 ranges: $$\bar{R} = \dfrac{0.04 \times 9}{9} = 0.0400$$ ### Step 3 — Repeatability (EV) $$EV = \bar{R} \times K_1 = 0.0400 \times 0.8862 = 0.0354 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 4 — Operator Means and Part Means **Operator means** (average across 6 measurements per operator): $$\begin{aligned} \bar{X}_A &= \dfrac{50.02+49.98+49.90+49.94+50.10+50.06}{6} = 50.000 \\ \bar{X}_B &= \dfrac{50.05+50.01+49.85+49.89+50.12+50.08}{6} = 50.000 \\ \bar{X}_C &= \dfrac{49.95+49.99+49.92+49.88+50.05+50.09}{6} = 49.980 \end{aligned}$$ The range of operator means: $$X_{DIFF} = \bar{X}_{MAX} - \bar{X}_{MIN} = 50.000 - 49.980 = 0.020$$ ### Step 5 — Reproducibility (AV) With $n = 3$ parts and $r = 2$ trials per cell: $$AV = \sqrt{(X_{DIFF} \times K_2)^2 - \dfrac{EV^2}{n \cdot r}}$$ $$AV = \sqrt{(0.020 \times 0.5231)^2 - \dfrac{(0.0354)^2}{3 \times 2}}$$ $$AV = \sqrt{(0.01046)^2 - \dfrac{0.001253}{6}}$$ $$AV = \sqrt{0.0001094 - 0.0002089}$$ Since the term inside the square root is *negative*, the *standard* formula underestimates AV. By convention, when the correction term is negative, we set $AV = 0$ and interpret it as: **no significant reproducibility variation beyond repeatability**. $$AV = 0 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 6 — Gage R&R $$R\&R = \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2} = \sqrt{(0.0354)^2 + 0^2} = 0.0354 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 7 — Part Means and Part Variation **Part means** (average across 6 measurements per part): $$\begin{aligned} \bar{X}_1 &= 50.000 \\ \bar{X}_2 &= 49.897 \\ \bar{X}_3 &= 50.083 \end{aligned}$$ The range of part means: $$R_{part} = 50.083 - 49.897 = 0.186$$ ### Step 8 — Part Variation (PV) $$PV = R_{part} \times K_3 = 0.186 \times 0.4030 = 0.0750 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 9 — Total Variation (TV) $$TV = \sqrt{R\&R^2 + PV^2} = \sqrt{(0.0354)^2 + (0.0750)^2}$$ $$TV = \sqrt{0.001253 + 0.005625} = \sqrt{0.006878} = 0.0829 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 10 — %R&R Decision Metric $$\%R\&R = \dfrac{0.0354}{0.0829} \times 100 = 42.7\%$$ ### Step 11 — Number of Distinct Categories (ndc) $$ndc = 1.41 \times \dfrac{PV}{R\&R} = 1.41 \times \dfrac{0.0750}{0.0354} = 1.41 \times 2.119 = 2.99 \approx 3$$ ### Step 12 — Interpretation > [!WARNING] > **%R&R = 42.7 % > 30 %** and **ndc = 3 < 5**. Therefore the measurement system is **UNACCEPTABLE** for this application. The flange tolerance is being consumed primarily by measurement noise. Recommended actions: (1) re-calibrate the gage against a master block, (2) train all three operators on the SOP, (3) consider a higher-resolution digital gage. --- ## 3.3 Bias and Linearity — Analytical Treatment A separate study is conducted using **5 reference standards** at known values $X_i$ (for $i = 1, \dots, 5$), each measured $n = 10$ times. ### Bias at Each Reference $$B_i = \bar{X}_i - X_i$$ ### Hypotheses for Linearity $$H_0: b = 0 \quad \text{(no slope; bias is constant — linear)}$$ $$H_1: b \neq 0 \quad \text{(non-zero slope — non-linear bias)}$$ ### Test Statistic $$t_0 = \dfrac{\hat{b}}{SE(\hat{b})}, \quad \text{with } df = n - 2$$ If $\vert t_0 \vert > t_{\alpha/2, \, n-2}$, reject $H_0$ and conclude that the gage has a **non-linear bias** requiring polynomial correction. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Source of Variation | Symbol | Variance Contribution | Domain of Fix | |---|---|---|---| | Repeatability (Equipment) | EV | Within-operator, same gage | Gage repair, fixturing, calibration | | Reproducibility (Appraiser) | AV | Between-operator, same gage | Training, SOP, automated reading | | Part-to-Part | PV | Real product differences | Process control (not a measurement issue) | | Total | TV | All combined | — | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Five-Element Measurement System The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a physical stimulus is transformed step-by-step into a digital display value. The block-level architecture is the standard textbook figure used in KTU 2024 scheme lectures. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[STIMULUS
Physical Quantity] --> B[PRIMARY SENSOR
Senses the Variable] B --> C[VARIABLE CONVERTER
Transducer] C --> D[VARIABLE MANIPULATOR
Amplifier / Filter / ADC] D --> E[DATA TRANSMITTER
Cable / Wireless Link] E --> F[DATA DISPLAY
SCADA / Digital Readout] F --> G[MEASURED VALUE
Reported to User] G --> H{VALIDATION
MSA Study} H -- Acceptable --> I[Process Decisions] H -- Unacceptable --> J[Gage Re-calibration] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Every block between *Stimulus* and *Measured Value* is a *potential source of error*. A measurement system is only as strong as its weakest block. The dashed loop **H → J → A** is the *closed-loop* concept: feedback from MSA drives corrective action back to the sensor. --- ## 4.2 MSA Decision Flow — %R&R Acceptance Chart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Collect Gage R and R Data
2 Trials x 3 Operators x 3 Parts] --> B[Compute Range per Cell] B --> C[Compute Average Range R bar] C --> D[EV = R bar times K1] C --> E[AV = sqrt of X diff squared times K2 squared minus EV squared over n r] B --> F[Compute Part Means] F --> G[PV = R part times K3] D --> H[R and R = sqrt of EV squared plus AV squared] G --> I[TV = sqrt of R and R squared plus PV squared] H --> J[Percent R and R = R and R over TV times 100] J --> K{Percent R and R Decision} K -- Less than 10 percent --> L[EXCELLENT
Gage Approved] K -- Between 10 and 30 percent --> M[MARGINAL
Investigate Causes] K -- Greater than 30 percent --> N[UNACCEPTABLE
Reject Gage] M --> O[Corrective Action] N --> O O --> A ``` --- ## 4.3 Accuracy vs Precision — Mermaid Visualization The dartboard analogy is rendered below as a structured Mermaid block to make the distinction unambiguous. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Six Sigma Black Belt asks] --> B{Are shots tightly clustered?} B -- Yes --> C{Is cluster centered on bullseye?} B -- No --> D[IMPRECISE
High variance sigma] C -- Yes --> E[ACCURATE and PRECISE
Low bias, low sigma
Ideal Gage] C -- No --> F[PRECISE but NOT ACCURATE
Low sigma, high bias
Needs Calibration] D --> G{Is average on bullseye?} G -- Yes --> H[ACCURATE on average but NOT PRECISE
High sigma
Needs Stability Fix] G -- No --> I[NEITHER ACCURATE nor PRECISE
High bias, high sigma
Replace Gage] ``` --- ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Variation Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Sources_of_Variation S1[Repeatability EV] --> R[R and R Noise] S2[Reproducibility AV] --> R S3[Part to Part PV] --> P[Process Signal] end R --> T[Total Variation TV] P --> T T --> D{Percent R and R Evaluation} D -- Less than 10 percent --> O1[Process Signal Dominates] D -- Greater than 30 percent --> O2[Noise Dominates
Measurement System Reject] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** The decomposition shows that the *measurement system itself* can mask real process variation. A Black Belt who cannot quantify EV and AV will incorrectly attribute measurement noise to the process, leading to futile "improvement" projects. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.a]** **Q1. Differentiate between Accuracy, Precision, and Resolution of a measurement system. State the dartboard analogy.** **Model Answer:** - **Accuracy (Bias):** The closeness of the mean of a set of measurements to the *true* (reference) value of the quantity being measured. Quantified as $B = \bar{X} - X_{ref}$. - **Precision (Variance):** The closeness of *agreement* among repeated measurements of the same quantity under stipulated conditions. Quantified by standard deviation $\sigma$ or variance $\sigma^2$. - **Resolution:** The *smallest* change in the input stimulus that produces a detectable change in the output indication. For a digital gage, resolution equals the value of the least significant bit (LSB). **Dartboard Analogy:** A tight cluster in the bullseye = **accurate and precise** (ideal). A tight cluster in the outer ring = **precise but not accurate** (calibrate). A scattered pattern centered on the bullseye = **accurate on average but not precise** (improve repeatability). A scattered pattern in the outer ring = **neither accurate nor precise** (replace the gage). > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1.b]** **Q2. Define Gage Repeatability and Reproducibility (GRR). Explain how the AIAG MSA decision criteria (10 % and 30 %) are applied.** **Model Answer:** **Repeatability (EV)** is the variation observed when *one* operator measures the *same* part *multiple times* using the *same* gage under identical conditions. It reflects the inherent precision of the equipment. **Reproducibility (AV)** is the variation observed when *different* operators measure the *same* part using the *same* gage. It reflects operator-to-operator differences caused by technique, training, or interpretation of the reading. **Gage R&R** is the combined measurement system variation, computed as: $$R\&R = \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2}$$ The **%R&R** is then evaluated against AIAG thresholds: | %R&R | Decision | |:----:|----------| | **< 10 %** | ✅ Excellent — Gage approved for use. | | **10 % – 30 %** | ⚠️ Marginal — May be acceptable; identify sources of variation. | | **> 30 %** | ❌ Unacceptable — Gage must be repaired, re-calibrated, or replaced. | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Q2]` > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five functional elements of a measurement system** and identify which element most often becomes the bottleneck in industrial Six Sigma projects. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A Gage R&R study was performed on a torque wrench used in a two-wheeler assembly line in Thrissur. The study used **2 operators, 3 parts, and 2 trials**. The measured torque values (in N·m; reference = 60 N·m) are: | Part \ Operator | Op 1 (T1) | Op 1 (T2) | Op 2 (T1) | Op 2 (T2) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 60.5 | 60.1 | 59.8 | 60.0 | | 2 | 58.2 | 58.6 | 58.0 | 58.4 | | 3 | 61.4 | 61.0 | 61.6 | 61.2 | > Compute EV, AV, GRR, PV, TV, %R&R, and ndc. State whether the measurement system is acceptable. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A (a)** The five functional elements are: 1. **Primary Sensing Element (Sensor):** Detects the physical stimulus — e.g., strain gauge, thermocouple. 2. **Variable Conversion Element (Transducer):** Converts the sensed signal into a more usable form (usually electrical) — e.g., piezoelectric crystal. 3. **Variable Manipulation Element:** Amplifies, filters, or linearizes the signal — e.g., instrumentation amplifier, ADC. 4. **Data Transmission Element:** Carries the conditioned signal to the display — e.g., shielded cable, fiber optic link. 5. **Data Presentation Element:** Indicates the reading for the user — e.g., digital display, SCADA screen. **Most Common Bottleneck:** In practice, the *Variable Manipulation Element* (signal conditioning) is the most frequent bottleneck because it introduces drift, thermal noise, and non-linearity. The second most common is the *Primary Sensor* due to aging and environmental contamination. > **Self-Study Note:** This 7-mark question expects (i) the labelled block diagram, (ii) one-line description per block, and (iii) a justified identification of the bottleneck. --- ### **Model Solution to Question A (b)** **Step 1 — Compute per-cell ranges:** | Part \ Operator | Op 1 Range | Op 2 Range | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\vert 60.5-60.1 \vert = 0.4$ | $\vert 59.8-60.0 \vert = 0.2$ | | 2 | $\vert 58.2-58.6 \vert = 0.4$ | $\vert 58.0-58.4 \vert = 0.4$ | | 3 | $\vert 61.4-61.0 \vert = 0.4$ | $\vert 61.6-61.2 \vert = 0.4$ | $$\bar{R} = \dfrac{0.4+0.2+0.4+0.4+0.4+0.4}{6} = \dfrac{2.2}{6} = 0.3667 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2 — Repeatability (EV):** With $K_1 = 0.8862$ (for $n=2$ trials): $$EV = 0.3667 \times 0.8862 = 0.3249 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Operator means:** $$\begin{aligned} \bar{X}_{Op1} &= \dfrac{60.5+60.1+58.2+58.6+61.4+61.0}{6} = 59.967 \\ \bar{X}_{Op2} &= \dfrac{59.8+60.0+58.0+58.4+61.6+61.2}{6} = 59.833 \end{aligned}$$ $$X_{DIFF} = 59.967 - 59.833 = 0.134$$ **Step 4 — Reproducibility (AV):** With $K_2 = 0.7071$ (for 2 operators, $d_2 = 1.414$): $$AV = \sqrt{(0.134 \times 0.7071)^2 - \dfrac{(0.3249)^2}{3 \times 2}}$$ $$AV = \sqrt{(0.0948)^2 - \dfrac{0.1056}{6}}$$ $$AV = \sqrt{0.00898 - 0.01760}$$ The correction term exceeds the variance; per AIAG convention, $AV = 0$. $$AV = 0 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 5 — Gage R&R:** $$R\&R = \sqrt{(0.3249)^2 + 0^2} = 0.3249 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 6 — Part means:** $$\begin{aligned} \bar{X}_1 &= \dfrac{60.5+60.1+59.8+60.0}{4} = 60.10 \\ \bar{X}_2 &= \dfrac{58.2+58.6+58.0+58.4}{4} = 58.30 \\ \bar{X}_3 &= \dfrac{61.4+61.0+61.6+61.2}{4} = 61.30 \end{aligned}$$ $$R_{part} = 61.30 - 58.30 = 3.00$$ **Step 7 — Part Variation:** $$PV = 3.00 \times K_3$$ For 3 parts, $K_3 = 0.4030$: $$PV = 3.00 \times 0.4030 = 1.2090 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 8 — Total Variation:** $$TV = \sqrt{(0.3249)^2 + (1.2090)^2} = \sqrt{0.1056 + 1.4617} = \sqrt{1.5673} = 1.2519 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 9 — %R&R:** $$\%R\&R = \dfrac{0.3249}{1.2519} \times 100 = 25.95\%$$ **Step 10 — ndc:** $$ndc = 1.41 \times \dfrac{1.2090}{0.3249} = 1.41 \times 3.721 = 5.25 \approx 5$$ **Decision:** %R&R = **25.95 %** falls in the **10 % – 30 %** *Marginal* band, and ndc = **5** meets the minimum threshold of 5. The measurement system is **marginally acceptable** but should be improved through better gage fixturing and operator training. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Q2 Alt]` > **(a)** Define **Linearity**, **Stability**, and **Hysteresis** of a measurement system. Derive the regression model used to test for linearity and state the hypothesis. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A precision thermometer is calibrated against five reference standards maintained by NABL (in °C): 20, 40, 60, 80, 100. Ten repeated measurements are taken at each standard, yielding the following mean biases: $B_{20} = 0.10$, $B_{40} = 0.18$, $B_{60} = 0.25$, $B_{80} = 0.32$, $B_{100} = 0.41$ (all in °C). The standard error of the slope estimate is $SE(\hat{b}) = 0.0008$ and the fitted slope is $\hat{b} = 0.0038$ °C/°C. Test for non-linearity at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B (a)** - **Linearity:** The property by which the bias of the measurement system is constant across the entire operating range. A linear gage has the *same* bias at low, mid, and high values of the stimulus. - **Stability (Drift):** The property by which the measurement system produces the *same* output for the *same* input over an extended period of time. Drift is usually expressed in units/month. - **Hysteresis:** The difference in output when the input is approached from an *increasing* direction versus a *decreasing* direction. Common in mechanical and magnetic systems. **Regression Model for Linearity Test:** $$B_i = a + b \cdot X_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$$ **Hypotheses:** $$H_0: b = 0 \quad \text{(bias is constant — linear)}$$ $$H_1: b \neq 0 \quad \text{(bias varies with reference — non-linear)}$$ **Test Statistic:** $t_0 = \dfrac{\hat{b}}{SE(\hat{b})}$ with $df = n - 2$. --- ### **Model Solution to Question B (b)** **Step 1 — State hypotheses:** $$H_0: b = 0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: b \neq 0$$ **Step 2 — Compute t-statistic:** $$t_0 = \dfrac{0.0038}{0.0008} = 4.75$$ **Step 3 — Critical value:** For $df = n - 2 = 5 - 2 = 3$ and $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed): $$t_{0.025, \, 3} = 3.182$$ **Step 4 — Decision:** Since $\vert t_0 \vert = 4.75 > 3.182$, we **reject $H_0$**. **Step 5 — Conclusion:** The thermometer exhibits a **statistically significant non-linear bias**. The bias increases by approximately 0.0038 °C for every 1 °C rise in reference temperature, accumulating to a bias of ~0.38 °C at the high end of the range. A multi-point calibration correction or a polynomial (e.g., second-order) fit is recommended. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Five Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Measurement Systems Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $K_1 = 0.8862$ constant** — students write $EV = \bar{R}$ instead of $EV = \bar{R} \times K_1$. **−2 marks.** > 2. **Confusing K2 values** — for 2 operators, $K_2 = 0.7071$; for 3 operators, $K_2 = 0.5231$. Hard-coding the wrong value costs **−1 mark**. > 3. **Not stating the decision band** — computing %R&R without saying "Acceptable / Marginal / Unacceptable" loses **−1 mark**. > 4. **Reporting R&R as a *single* deviation** — the correct statistic is the **6σ spread**, i.e., the *range* containing 99.73 % of measurements. A student who reports only the standard deviation loses **−2 marks**. > 5. **Skipping the ndc calculation** — many 14-mark questions explicitly demand ndc; omitting it is a guaranteed **−2 marks**. > 6. **Mixing up Repeatability and Reproducibility definitions** — Repeatability = same operator, same gage; Reproducibility = different operators. Reversing these is a **−3 mark** conceptual error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this on your wall before the exam):** - ☐ A **Measurement System** converts a physical stimulus into a numeric value via five blocks: **Sensor → Transducer → Manipulator → Transmitter → Display**. - ☐ **Accuracy = Bias = Closeness to True Value.** **Precision = 1/σ = Repeatability of Reading.** - ☐ **Static characteristics:** Accuracy, Precision, Linearity, Sensitivity, Resolution, Threshold, Hysteresis, Dead Zone, Stability. - ☐ **Dynamic characteristics:** Time constant τ, Damping ratio ζ, Natural frequency ωₙ, Rise time, Settling time. - ☐ **Gage R&R = √(EV² + AV²)**, where EV = $\bar{R} \times 0.8862$ and AV involves the range of operator means. - ☐ **%R&R decision bands:** < 10 % Excellent, 10–30 % Marginal, > 30 % Unacceptable. - ☐ **ndc ≥ 5** is the minimum acceptable number of distinct categories. - ☐ **Linearity test:** Fit $B_i = a + bX_i$, test $H_0: b = 0$ using a t-test with $df = n - 2$. - ☐ **Stability** is monitored via control charts on bias over time. - ☐ The **AIAG MSA Reference Manual** is the global standard; always cite its thresholds in KTU answers. - ☐ **%R&R = (R&R / TV) × 100**, with $TV = \sqrt{R\&R^2 + PV^2}$. - ☐ **%R&R is a 6σ spread, not a standard deviation** — state this explicitly in the conclusion. - ☐ Repeatability issues → **Gage problem.** Reproducibility issues → **Operator/Training problem.** Part variation issues → **Process problem** (not a measurement problem). - ☐ A Six Sigma project that proceeds without a validated measurement system is built on **sand**, not data.

topics.title: Measurement Systems Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement Systems Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Measurement Systems Analysis (MSA)** is a structured, experimental and statistical methodology used in the Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control (DMAIC) framework to evaluate the inherent variability, accuracy, precision, and stability of a measurement system. It quantifies the contribution of the measurement process to the overall observed variation in a product or process characteristic.

According to the **AIAG (Automotive Industry Action Group) MSA Reference Manual**, a measurement system comprises the **gauge/instrument**, the **operator(s)**, the **measurement procedure**, the **part being measured**, and the **environmental conditions** under which the measurement is taken.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"Measurement Systems Analysis is the experimental determination of the variation present in a measurement system, expressed in terms of **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Bias**, **Linearity**, **Stability**, **Repeatability**, and **Reproducibility**, with the goal of ensuring that observed process variation reflects true part variation and not measurement noise."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **fisherman weighing a 1 kg fish** on two different weighing scales:

- **Scale A** shows the fish as 1.02 kg every time → **Accurate & Precise** (good scale).
- **Scale B** shows 1.2 kg, 1.5 kg, 1.1 kg across three attempts → **Inaccurate AND Imprecise** (terrible scale).
- **Scale C** shows 0.8 kg consistently every time → **Precise but Inaccurate (Biased)**.

If you use Scale C, no matter how carefully you measure, you will always underestimate the fish's weight. Similarly, in manufacturing, a biased gauge will always make your product look "good" or "bad" when it is actually the gauge lying. MSA is the formal experiment you run to figure out **how much you can trust your scale (gauge)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Examples:**
> - A CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine) measuring a critical engine block dimension.
> - A torque wrench used by three different operators tightening bolts.
> - A temperature probe used in a pharmaceutical cold-storage facility.

## 1.3 The "Five M's" of a Measurement System

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Man** | Operator(s) performing the measurement | Quality Inspector A, B, C |
| **Machine** | The gauge, fixture, or instrument | Vernier caliper, digital scale |
| **Material** | The part or feature being measured | Crankshaft journal diameter |
| **Method** | The standard operating procedure (SOP) | Sampling plan, calibration procedure |
| **Mother Nature** | Environmental conditions | Room temperature (20°C ± 2°C) |

## 1.4 Importance of MSA in Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Six Sigma:**
> *"You cannot improve what you cannot measure, and you cannot measure what you do not understand (in terms of error)."*

Without MSA:
- A Six Sigma project may conclude that a process is out of control when, in fact, it is the **measurement system** that is unstable.
- Improvement efforts may be wasted on **common-cause variation** that is actually **measurement noise**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Measurement System Characteristics

The AIAG MSA framework classifies measurement system performance into four primary hierarchical categories:

### 2.1.1 Accuracy (Closeness to True Value)
- Defined as the closeness of a measurement to the **actual/true/reference value**.
- Composed of two sub-properties: **Bias** and **Linearity**.

### 2.1.2 Precision (Closeness Among Repeated Measurements)
- The degree to which repeated measurements under the same conditions yield the **same result**.
- Composed of two sub-properties: **Repeatability** and **Reproducibility**.

### 2.1.3 Stability (Performance Over Time)
- The ability of a measurement system to maintain its performance (bias and precision) **over an extended period**.

### 2.1.4 Discrimination / Resolution
- The smallest increment the gauge can detect, also called **gauge resolution** or **discrimination ratio**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the Measurement Source**
Determine if the source of variation is in the **process** or the **measurement system** by partitioning total variation.

$$ \sigma^2_{Total} = \sigma^2_{Part} + \sigma^2_{Measurement} $$

**Step 2 — Quantify Bias**
Repeated measurements of a reference standard are averaged and compared to the known true value.

**Step 3 — Quantify Linearity**
Bias is evaluated at multiple points across the operating range to assess whether bias is **proportional** to the measurement magnitude.

**Step 4 — Quantify Repeatability (EV — Equipment Variation)**
The variation observed when the **same operator** measures the **same part** with the **same gauge** multiple times under **identical conditions** (often called **EV** or *Equipment Variation*).

**Step 5 — Quantify Reproducibility (AV — Appraiser Variation)**
The variation observed when **different operators** measure the **same parts** using the **same gauge** under the same conditions (called **AV** or *Appraiser Variation*).

**Step 6 — Compute Gage R&R (GRR)**
Combine EV and AV to obtain the **Total Gage R&R**, which represents the total measurement system variation.

**Step 7 — Decision Rule**
Compare GRR to **%Tolerance (P/T Ratio)** or **%Process Variation (P/σ Ratio)** using AIAG acceptance criteria.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Bias** | $\text{Bias} = \bar{X} - X_{ref}$ | Units of measurement |
| 2 | **Standard Deviation of Bias** | $s_{bias} = \dfrac{s_{repeat}}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Used for t-test on bias |
| 3 | **t-statistic for Bias** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{X} - X_{ref}}{s_{bias}}$ | Compare to $t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1}$ |
| 4 | **Linearity Slope** | $b_1 = \dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$ | From regression of bias vs. reference |
| 5 | **Repeatability (EV)** | $EV = K_1 \times R_{\bar{}} \quad (K_1 = \dfrac{\overline{d_2}})$, or $EV = \overline{R} \times K_1$ | Units of measurement |
| 6 | **Reproducibility (AV)** | $AV = \sqrt{(X_{DIFF} \times K_2)^2 - \dfrac{(EV)^2}{nr}}$ | Units of measurement |
| 7 | **Total Gage R&R (GRR)** | $GRR = \sqrt{(EV)^2 + (AV)^2}$ | Units of measurement |
| 8 | **Part Variation (PV)** | $PV = K_3 \times R_{\text{part}}$ or $R_p \times K_3$ | Units of measurement |
| 9 | **Total Variation (TV)** | $TV = \sqrt{(GRR)^2 + (PV)^2}$ | Units of measurement |
| 10 | **%GRR (% of Tolerance)** | $\% GRR = \dfrac{6 \times GRR}{\text{Tolerance}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 11 | **%GRR (% of Process)** | $\% GRR = \dfrac{6 \times GRR}{6 \times \sigma_{part}} \times 100 = \dfrac{GRR}{\sigma_{part}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 12 | **P/T Ratio (Precision-to-Tolerance)** | $P/T = \dfrac{6 \times GRR}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}} \times 100$ | AIAG acceptance metric |
| 13 | **NDC (Number of Distinct Categories)** | $NDC = 1.41 \times \dfrac{PV}{GRR}$ | Should be ≥ 5 ideally |
| 14 | **Discrimination Ratio** | $DR = \dfrac{n \times d^2}{2 \times EV^2}$ | n = parts, d = discrimination |

## 2.4 Constants Used in Gage R&R (AIAG Standard)

| Number of Trials (n) | $K_1$ | $K_2$ | $K_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 0.8862 | 0.7071 | 0.4030 |
| 3 | 0.5231 | 0.5231 | 0.5231 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are **bias correction factors** that depend on the number of trials. They are derived from the relationship between the range $R$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ in subgrouped data, where $\sigma \approx R/d_2$.

## 2.5 AIAG Acceptance Criteria (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

| %GRR or P/T | Verdict | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **< 10%** | **Excellent** | Gauge is fit for purpose |
| **10% – 30%** | **Conditionally Acceptable** | May be used; identify root cause of high variation |
| **> 30%** | **Unacceptable** | Gauge must be improved or replaced |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AIAG-driven):** Mandatory PPAP submission requires GR&R < 30% for all critical-to-quality (CTQ) dimensions.
- **Aerospace (AS9100):** MSA is required for first-article inspection gauges.
- **Medical Devices (ISO 13485):** MSA supports validation of measurement methods in FDA submissions.
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** NDC is critical because process variation is so small that poor gauge discrimination can hide process shifts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Average & Range (X-bar & R) Method of Gage R&R

This is the most frequently asked KTU numerical (typically a 7-mark or 14-mark problem). The AIAG standard uses two operators, 10 parts, 2 or 3 trials.

### Step 1 — Collect the Data
Three operators (A, B, C) measure 10 parts (1–10) twice each (trials 1 and 2). Total observations = 60.

### Step 2 — Compute the Range (R) for Each Part per Operator
For each part $i$ and operator $j$:

$$ R_{ij} = \vert X_{ij,1} - X_{ij,2} \vert $$

### Step 3 — Compute the Average Range per Operator
Sum the ranges for each operator across all 10 parts, then divide by the number of parts:

$$ \bar{R}_j = \dfrac{1}{n_{parts}} \sum_{i=1}^{n_{parts}} R_{ij} $$

### Step 4 — Compute Repeatability (EV) Using the Grand Average of Ranges
The grand average range $\bar{\bar{R}}$ is the mean of all operator averages (or equivalently, mean of all 30 ranges for 3 operators × 10 parts):

$$ \bar{\bar{R}} = \dfrac{1}{n_{operators}} \sum_{j=1}^{n_{operators}} \bar{R}_j $$

$$ EV = \bar{\bar{R}} \times K_1 $$

where $K_1 = 0.8862$ for 2 trials.

### Step 5 — Compute the Part Average per Operator
For each part $i$ and operator $j$:

$$ \bar{X}_{ij} = \dfrac{X_{ij,1} + X_{ij,2}}{2} $$

### Step 6 — Compute the Part-to-Part Range (Rp) per Operator
For each operator $j$, find the maximum and minimum of the 10 part averages:

$$ R_{p,j} = \max_i(\bar{X}_{ij}) - \min_i(\bar{X}_{ij}) $$

### Step 7 — Compute the Grand Average of Part Averages
Sum the part averages across operators and parts, then divide:

$$ \bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{1}{n_{operators} \cdot n_{parts}} \sum_{j}\sum_{i} \bar{X}_{ij} $$

### Step 8 — Compute Reproducibility (AV)
First, compute the operator range $X_{DIFF}$:

$$ X_{DIFF} = \max_j(\bar{\bar{X}}_j) - \min_j(\bar{\bar{X}}_j) $$

Then, the AIAG formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AV &= \sqrt{(X_{DIFF} \times K_2)^2 - \dfrac{(EV)^2}{n_{parts}}} \\
   &= \sqrt{(X_{DIFF} \times K_2)^2 - \dfrac{EV^2}{n_r}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_2 = 0.7071$ for 2 trials and $n_r$ is the number of parts (here, 10). If the result inside the square root is negative, convention sets $AV = 0$.

### Step 9 — Compute Total Gage R&R
$$
\begin{aligned}
GRR &= \sqrt{(EV)^2 + (AV)^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 10 — Compute Part Variation (PV)
Average the $R_{p,j}$ values across operators to get $\bar{R}_p$, then:

$$ PV = \bar{R}_p \times K_3 $$

where $K_3 = 0.4030$ for 2 trials.

### Step 11 — Compute Total Variation (TV)
$$
\begin{aligned}
TV &= \sqrt{(GRR)^2 + (PV)^2} \\
   &= \sqrt{GRR^2 + PV^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 12 — Compute %GRR (P/T Ratio or %Study Var)
**P/T Ratio** (using specification tolerance USL − LSL):
$$ \%GRR_{P/T} = \dfrac{6 \times GRR}{USL - LSL} \times 100 $$

**%Study Variation** (relative to 6-sigma part variation):
$$ \%GRR_{SV} = \dfrac{GRR}{TV} \times 100 $$

### Step 13 — Compute NDC
$$
\begin{aligned}
NDC &= 1.41 \times \dfrac{PV}{GRR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Round down to the nearest integer. AIAG requires NDC ≥ 5.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:** Two operators (A, B) measure 5 parts twice each. Data (in mm):

| Part | Op A (T1) | Op A (T2) | Op B (T1) | Op B (T2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.02 | 12.01 | 11.99 | 11.98 |
| 2 | 11.51 | 11.50 | 11.48 | 11.47 |
| 3 | 12.50 | 12.49 | 12.48 | 12.47 |
| 4 | 10.02 | 10.01 | 10.00 | 9.99 |
| 5 | 13.01 | 13.00 | 12.99 | 12.98 |

Specification: USL = 13.50 mm, LSL = 10.00 mm.

### Solution Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute Ranges R per Part per Operator:**

| Part | R_A | R_B |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.01 | 0.01 |
| 2 | 0.01 | 0.01 |
| 3 | 0.01 | 0.01 |
| 4 | 0.01 | 0.01 |
| 5 | 0.01 | 0.01 |

**Step 2 — Average Range per Operator:**
$\bar{R}_A = 0.01$ and $\bar{R}_B = 0.01$.

**Step 3 — Grand Average Range:**
$\bar{\bar{R}} = 0.01$.

**Step 4 — Repeatability (EV):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
EV &= \bar{\bar{R}} \times K_1 = 0.01 \times 0.8862 \\
  &= 0.008862 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Part Averages:**

| Part | $\bar{X}_A$ | $\bar{X}_B$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.015 | 11.985 |
| 2 | 11.505 | 11.475 |
| 3 | 12.495 | 12.475 |
| 4 | 10.015 | 9.995 |
| 5 | 13.005 | 12.985 |

**Step 6 — Operator Averages:**
$\bar{\bar{X}}_A = 11.807$, $\bar{\bar{X}}_B = 11.783$.

**Step 7 — Operator Range (X_DIFF):**
$X_{DIFF} = 11.807 - 11.783 = 0.024$ mm.

**Step 8 — Reproducibility (AV):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
AV &= \sqrt{(0.024 \times 0.7071)^2 - \dfrac{(0.008862)^2}{5}} \\
   &= \sqrt{(0.01697)^2 - (0.008862)^2 / 5} \\
   &= \sqrt{0.0002880 - 0.0000157} \\
   &= \sqrt{0.0002723} \\
   &= 0.01650 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Total Gage R&R:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
GRR &= \sqrt{(0.008862)^2 + (0.01650)^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{0.0000785 + 0.0002723} \\
    &= \sqrt{0.0003508} \\
    &= 0.01873 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Part Range per Operator:**
$R_{p,A} = 13.005 - 10.015 = 2.990$ mm.
$R_{p,B} = 12.985 - 9.995 = 2.990$ mm.
$\bar{R}_p = 2.990$ mm.

**Step 11 — Part Variation (PV):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= 2.990 \times 0.4030 = 1.205 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 12 — Total Variation (TV):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
TV &= \sqrt{(0.01873)^2 + (1.205)^2} = \sqrt{0.000351 + 1.452} = 1.205 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 13 — %GRR (P/T):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%GRR_{P/T} &= \dfrac{6 \times 0.01873}{13.50 - 10.00} \times 100 \\
           &= \dfrac{0.1124}{3.50} \times 100 \\
           &= 3.21\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 14 — %Study Variation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%GRR_{SV} &= \dfrac{0.01873}{1.205} \times 100 = 1.55\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 15 — NDC:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
NDC &= 1.41 \times \dfrac{1.205}{0.01873} = 1.41 \times 64.33 = 90.7 \to 90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** %GRR < 10% → **Measurement system is EXCELLENT** and acceptable for production use.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: MSA Study Types (AIAG Classification)

| Study Type | When Used | Key Output | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1 Study (Gauge Bias Study)** | New gauge, gauge after repair, suspected bias | Bias, Repeatability, %Repeatability | Low |
| **Type 2 Study (Gauge Linearity & Bias Study)** | Multiple operating ranges, regulatory need | Linearity slope & intercept, %Linearity | Medium |
| **Type 3 Study (Gauge R&R Study)** | Production-released gauge, PPAP, control plan | EV, AV, GRR, %GRR, NDC | High |
| **Type 4 Study (Attribute Gage Study)** | Attribute data (Pass/Fail), Kappa analysis | Percent agreement, Kappa | Medium |
| **Type 5 Study (Gauge Correlation Study)** | Comparing new gauge to master gauge | Bias, correlation coefficient | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MSA Conceptual Framework (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Total Observed Variation"] --> node2["Part Variation"]
    node1 --> node3["Measurement System Variation"]
    node3 --> node4["Bias"]
    node3 --> node5["Repeatability EV"]
    node3 --> node6["Reproducibility AV"]
    node3 --> node7["Linearity"]
    node3 --> node8["Stability"]
    node2 --> node9["True Process Signal"]
    node4 --> nodeA["Accuracy Components"]
    node7 --> nodeA
    node5 --> nodeB["Precision Components"]
    node6 --> nodeB
    node8 --> nodeC["Time Domain Performance"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["MSA Output Metrics"]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["P/T Ratio Decision"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["NDC Decision"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Accept or Reject Gauge"]
```

## 4.2 Type 1, 2, 3 Gage Study Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["New or Repaired Gauge"] --> decision1{"Need Bias Check?"}
    decision1 -- Yes --> type1["Type 1 Study: Bias and Repeatability"]
    decision1 -- No --> decision2{"Need Linearity Check?"}
    decision2 -- Yes --> type2["Type 2 Study: Linearity and Bias"]
    decision2 -- No --> type3["Type 3 Study: Gage R and R"]
    type1 --> outcomeA["Accept or Reject Gauge"]
    type2 --> outcomeB["Check Bias Slope and Intercept"]
    type3 --> outcomeC["Compute P/T and NDC"]
    outcomeC --> finalVerdict{"GRR less than 30 percent?"}
    finalVerdict -- Yes --> approved["Approved for Production"]
    finalVerdict -- No --> rejected["Rejected: Improve or Replace"]
```

## 4.3 Gage R&R Procedural Flow (X-bar & R Method)

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1["Step 1: Select 10 Parts Covering Operating Range"] --> step2["Step 2: Choose 2 or 3 Operators"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Each Operator Measures Each Part 2 or 3 Times"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Record Data Randomly to Avoid Bias"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Compute Range R per Part per Operator"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Compute Grand Average Range"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 7: EV equals R-bar times K1"]
    step3 --> step8["Step 8: Compute Part Average per Operator"]
    step8 --> step9["Step 9: Compute Operator Average X-diff"]
    step9 --> step10["Step 10: AV equals square root of X-diff K2 squared minus EV squared over n"]
    step7 --> step11["Step 11: GRR equals square root of EV squared plus AV squared"]
    step10 --> step11
    step8 --> step12["Step 12: Compute Part Range Rp per Operator"]
    step12 --> step13["Step 13: PV equals Rp-bar times K3"]
    step13 --> step14["Step 14: TV equals square root of GRR squared plus PV squared"]
    step11 --> step15["Step 15: Compute percent GRR P over T and NDC"]
    step14 --> step15
    step15 --> step16["Step 16: Apply AIAG Acceptance Criteria"]
```

## 4.4 Accuracy vs Precision Quadrant Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1["Q1: HIGH ACCURACY and HIGH PRECISION (Ideal)"]
    q2["Q2: HIGH ACCURACY and LOW PRECISION (Scattered Bullseye)"]
    q3["Q3: LOW ACCURACY and HIGH PRECISION (Biased Cluster)"]
    q4["Q4: LOW ACCURACY and LOW PRECISION (Unacceptable)"]
    q1 --> acc["Bias near Zero, EV small, AV small"]
    q2 --> acc1["Bias near Zero, EV or AV large"]
    q3 --> acc2["Bias large, EV and AV small"]
    q4 --> acc3["All sources of error large"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** A "good" gauge in MSA is one that resides in Q1 — meaning both **Bias is negligible** and **GRR variation is small** relative to either the tolerance or the process variation.
