$$P(\text{2nd defective} \mid \text{1st defective}) = \frac{49}{499} \approx 0.0982$$

$$P(\text{both defective}) = \frac{50}{500} \times \frac{49}{499} = \frac{2450}{249500} \approx 0.00982$$

**(ii) First defective, second good:**

$$P(\text{1st defective}) = \frac{50}{500} = 0.10$$

After removing one defective: remaining good = 450, total = 499.

$$P(\text{2nd good} \mid \text{1st defective}) = \frac{450}{499} \approx 0.9018$$

$$P(\text{1st defective, 2nd good}) = \frac{50}{500} \times \frac{450}{499} = \frac{22500}{249500} \approx 0.0902$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\approx 0.00982$ (0.982%), (ii) $\approx 0.0902$ (9.02%).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of the answer:** Bayes' theorem answers must always be between 0 and 1. If a student gets a value > 1, it indicates a miscalculation in the denominator (Total Probability).
> 2. **"Without replacement" vs "With replacement":** In sampling problems, students frequently use the **wrong denominator** (they fail to subtract 1 from the total). The second draw's denominator must be $N-1$ (or $N-2$ for further draws) for without-replacement cases.
> 3. **Independence assumption:** Multiplication rule $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ is **only valid for independent events**. The general form $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ must be used for dependent events.
> 4. **Missing units:** Always express probability as both a **decimal** and a **percentage** in the final answer for clarity.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- [x] Probability is always bounded: $\mathbf{0 \leq P(E) \leq 1}$.
- [x] The sum of probabilities of all elementary outcomes in a sample space is **1**: $\sum P(A_i) = 1$.
- [x] **Complement Rule:** $P(\bar{A}) = 1 - P(A)$ — used in yield calculations.
- [x] **Addition Rule** is for the **"OR"** scenario; **Multiplication Rule** is for the **"AND"** scenario.
- [x] For **independent events**, $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$; for **dependent events**, $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$.
- [x] **Bayes' Theorem** updates prior beliefs using new evidence — vital for the **Analyze** phase of DMAIC.
- [x] In **sampling without replacement**, always reduce the denominator after each draw.
- [x] For **mutually exclusive events**, $P(A \cap B) = 0$, which simplifies both the addition and multiplication rules.
- [x] Six Sigma target of **3.4 DPMO** corresponds to a probability of **0.0000034** per opportunity.
- [x] **Permutations** are used when **order matters**; **combinations** are used when **order does not matter**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Capability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Process Capability Analysis (PCA)** is a systematic statistical and engineering evaluation used in Six Sigma to quantify the inherent ability of a stable, in-control manufacturing or service process to consistently produce output within pre-defined specification limits (Customer Voice / CTQ requirements). It benchmarks the **natural process spread** (the Voice of the Process, VOP) against the **engineering tolerance band** (the Voice of the Customer, VOC), producing standardized capability indices that quantify defect potential in Parts Per Million (PPM) and Parts Per Million Opportunities (DPMO).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context aligned with NEP 2020 outcomes, PCA is the cornerstone of the **Measure** and **Analyze** phases of DMAIC, where the goal is to derive a numerical verdict — *"Can this process meet the customer's specifications under real-world operating conditions?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (must memorize):**
> *Process Capability is a statistical measure of the ability of a process to produce output that meets specifications, expressed as the ratio of the tolerance band (Specification Spread) to the natural process spread (6σ).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Block Factory" Intuition

Imagine you are running a toy factory producing **Lego blocks** that must fit perfectly into a child's toy box. The toy box has a fixed slot width — this is your **specification (USL & LSL)**. The size of each Lego block rolling off your machine varies slightly due to vibration, raw material moisture, and operator skill — this variation is your **process spread (6σ)**.

- If your blocks are **nearly identical** (small σ) and perfectly centered in the slot, your factory is **highly capable** → **Cp ≈ Cpk ≈ 1.67 (Six Sigma level)**.
- If your blocks vary widely (large σ), many will be too big or too small to fit → your factory is **not capable**, even if the average is correct.
- If your blocks are precise but systematically shifted to one side of the slot, your **Cpk < Cp** — meaning accuracy is poor even though precision is fine.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Musketeers of Process Capability:**
> 1. **Cp (Process Capability)** — Can the process *potentially* fit? (Spread only)
> 2. **Cpk (Process Capability Index)** — Does it *actually* fit with the current centering? (Spread + Location)
> 3. **Pp & Ppk (Process Performance)** — What was the *overall* performance over time, including shifts? (Uses sample standard deviation `s` instead of `σ`)

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Standard Deviation** | **σ (sigma)** | Engineering unit (e.g., mm, kg) | Quantifies inherent variation |
| **Target Value** | **T** | Engineering unit | Customer's nominal/ideal value |
| **Upper Specification Limit** | **USL** | Engineering unit | Maximum acceptable value |
| **Lower Specification Limit** | **LSL** | Engineering unit | Minimum acceptable value |
| **Tolerance Band** | **Toler = USL − LSL** | Engineering unit | Customer's allowable spread |
| **Parts Per Million Defectives** | **PPM** | defects per 1,000,000 | Defect density |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | **DPMO** | defects per 1,000,000 ops | Six Sigma yield metric |
| **Process Sigma Level (Z-score)** | **Z = Zbench** | sigma units (σ) | Equivalent short-term yield |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Voices: VOP vs. VOC

Every process in Six Sigma has two "voices" that must be reconciled:

- **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** → encoded as **USL** and **LSL**, the engineering tolerances derived from Customer Requirements (CTQs).
- **Voice of the Process (VOP)** → the actual **6σ spread** observed when the process is operating under stable, real-world conditions.

**Process Capability Analysis is the mathematical conversation between these two voices.**

## 2.2 Long-Term vs. Short-Term Capability — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-asked distinction in KTU Six Sigma papers. Memorize it.**

| Dimension | Short-Term (Within) | Long-Term (Overall) |
|---|---|---|
| **Index family** | **Cp, Cpk** | **Pp, Ppk** |
| **Standard deviation used** | **σ_within** (using R̄/d₂ or pooled Moving Range) | **s_overall** (sample std. deviation) |
| **Typical calculation** | R̄ ÷ d₂ (using control chart subgroup data) | sqrt[ Σ(xᵢ − x̄)² ÷ (n−1) ] |
| **Conditions assumed** | Process is centered, no drift | Process experiences normal drift (1.5σ shift rule) |
| **Six Sigma benchmark** | Z_st = 6 (Cpk = 2.0) | Z_lt = 4.5 (Ppk = 1.5) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Index / Quantity | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability, spread only | Module 2, Part B derivations |
| 2 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left[ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right]$ | Actual capability, includes centering | Most-frequently asked |
| 3 | **CPU (One-sided Upper)** | $C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Distance to upper spec | Sub-calculations |
| 4 | **CPL (One-sided Lower)** | $C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | Distance to lower spec | Sub-calculations |
| 5 | **Process Performance (Pp)** | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Overall spread, no subgroup grouping | Long-term view |
| 6 | **Process Performance Index (Ppk)** | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left[ \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s},\ \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s} \right]$ | Overall actual capability | Long-term verification |
| 7 | **Cpm (Taguchi Index)** | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$ | Penalizes deviation from Target T | Target-oriented processes |
| 8 | **Cpk–Cp Relationship** | $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$ where $k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - T \vert}{(USL-LSL)/2}$ | Centering factor | 3-mark conceptual Q |
| 9 | **Process Sigma Level (Zst)** | $Z_{st} = 3 \cdot C_{pk}$ (short-term) | Sigma equivalent | Yield conversions |
| 10 | **Process Sigma Level (Zlt)** | $Z_{lt} = 3 \cdot P_{pk} + 1.5$ (with 1.5σ shift) | Long-term sigma | World-class benchmark |
| 11 | **Z to PPM Conversion** | Look-up from normal table | Defect density | Numerical Q |
| 12 | **DPMO** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | Six Sigma yield metric | Module 1/2 link |
| 13 | **Six-Sigma Tolerance** | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{\sigma} = 12$ for $6\sigma$ | Class definition | Theory Q |
| 14 | **R̄ Method for σ** | $\sigma = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ where $d_2(n=2)=1.128$ | Estimate σ from control chart | Numerical Q |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention for Cpk & Ppk:** A process is "capable" if both indices ≥ **1.00** (3σ), "good" if ≥ **1.33** (4σ), "world-class / Six Sigma" if **Cpk = 2.00** (6σ short-term) or **Ppk = 1.50** (long-term with 1.5σ shift).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of PCA | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Semiconductor Fabrication** | Wafer thickness, CD (Critical Dimension) control | Yield management; Cpk ≥ 1.5 mandatory for new nodes |
| **Automotive (IATF 16949)** | Engine block bore diameter, torque output | Cpk ≥ 1.67 required for safety-critical features |
| **Pharmaceutical (FDA / cGMP)** | Tablet weight, dissolution time | Ppk ≥ 1.50 for process validation (PPQ) |
| **Aerospace (AS9100)** | Turbine blade airfoil profile | Cpk ≥ 1.33 for flight-critical surfaces |
| **Banking / BPO** | Call resolution time, transaction error rate | DPMO tracked at the sigma level for outsourcing |
| **Healthcare** | Door-to-needle time for stroke patients | Ppk reduction equals lives saved |

## 2.5 Decision Tree: How to Choose the Right Index

```
                    Is the process STABLE & in-control?
                    (Control charts: no out-of-control rules violated)
                                    |
                  +-----------------+------------------+
                  |YES                                  |NO
                  |                                    |
          Use Cp / Cpk                        Use Pp / Ppk
          (Short-term)                        (Long-term)
                  |                                    |
        One-sided spec?                     Re-stabilize first via
        (e.g., only LSL?)                   Remove Special Causes
                  |                                    |
                  v                                    v
        Use CPU or CPL alone               Re-run capability after
                                          (SPC) control is established
```

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Process Capability Cp from First Principles

### Conceptual Setup
For a process that is **stable and normally distributed** with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the natural process spread (the band that contains **99.73%** of all output when in statistical control) is:

$$\text{Natural Process Spread} = \mu + 3\sigma - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma$$

The customer specification band is simply the tolerance:

$$\text{Specification Spread} = USL - LSL$$

**Definition:** Cp is the ratio of the *specification width* to the *natural process width*:

$$C_p = \dfrac{\text{Specification Spread}}{\text{Natural Process Spread}}$$

$$\boxed{C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}}$$

**Step-by-step logical deduction:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify USL and LSL** from customer CTQs (engineering drawing / QFD matrix).
2. **Step 2 — Estimate σ** using one of three methods:
   - From an Xbar-R control chart: $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$ (n=2 → d₂ = 1.128).
   - From a sample of 100+ individual values: $\sigma = s$ (sample std. dev.).
   - From a Six-Sigma tolerance definition: $\sigma = (USL-LSL)/12$ if the process is at exactly 6σ.
3. **Step 3 — Plug into the formula** and interpret:
   - $C_p < 1.00$ → process spread exceeds tolerance → **NOT CAPABLE**, even if perfectly centered.
   - $C_p = 1.00$ → process is exactly at the tolerance limit → 0.27% defect potential (2700 PPM).
   - $C_p = 1.33$ → 4-sigma class → ~63 PPM.
   - $C_p = 1.50$ → 4.5-sigma long-term.
   - $C_p = 1.67$ → 5-sigma.
   - $C_p = 2.00$ → 6-sigma short-term → **3.4 PPM** (the Six-Sigma benchmark).

### Numerical Worked Example — Cp

> **Problem (KTU Typical 3-mark variant):** A shaft-diameter turning process has USL = 25.02 mm, LSL = 24.98 mm, and the within-subgroup standard deviation estimated from the Xbar-R chart is σ = 0.004 mm. Compute Cp and classify the process.

**Solution:**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{25.02 - 24.98}{6 \times 0.004}
$$

$$
C_p = \dfrac{0.04}{0.024} = 1.667
$$

**Interpretation:** A Cp of 1.667 corresponds to a **5-sigma short-term class**, which is good but not yet at world-class Six-Sigma level (Cp = 2.0).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cpk — The Asymmetric / Off-Center Correction

Cp assumes the process is **perfectly centered** at the target $T = (USL+LSL)/2$. In reality, processes drift. To quantify the *actual* capability, we measure the distance from the process mean to the **nearest** specification limit.

### Step-by-step derivation:

**Step 1 — Distance from mean to upper limit:**

$$d_U = USL - \mu$$

In units of 3σ (i.e., the number of standard deviations from the mean to the USL):

$$\dfrac{d_U}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = C_{PU}$$

**Step 2 — Distance from mean to lower limit:**

$$d_L = \mu - LSL$$

In units of 3σ:

$$\dfrac{d_L}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = C_{PL}$$

**Step 3 — Actual capability = the *worst* of the two one-sided capabilities** (the binding constraint):

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL}) = \min\!\left[ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right]}$$

**Step 4 — Relationship to Cp via the centering constant k:**

$$k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - T \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2}$$

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$$

> **Why this matters:** A process with Cp = 2.0 but μ shifted by 20% of the half-tolerance will have Cpk = 1.6, **demoting** the process from Six-Sigma class to 4.8σ — this is the most common reason capable-looking processes still produce defects in production.

### Numerical Worked Example — Cpk

> **Problem (KTU Board-style 7-mark):** In the same shaft-turning process, suppose the measured process mean is $\mu = 25.012$ mm (slightly shifted toward USL). With USL = 25.02 mm, LSL = 24.98 mm, σ = 0.004 mm, compute Cpk and interpret.

**Solution:**

$$
C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.020 - 25.012}{3 \times 0.004} = \dfrac{0.008}{0.012} = 0.667
$$

$$
C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.012 - 24.980}{3 \times 0.012 \text{ (denominator reused)}} 
$$

Re-evaluating clearly:

$$
C_{PL} = \dfrac{25.012 - 24.980}{0.012} = \dfrac{0.032}{0.012} = 2.667
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.667,\ 2.667) = 0.667
$$

**Interpretation:** Although the process *spread* is excellent (Cp = 1.667), the mean has drifted toward USL, dropping Cpk to 0.667 — **the process is no longer capable** and will produce approximately 24,565 PPM defects on the upper tail. **The corrective action is to re-center the machine to the target T = 25.00 mm**, not to reduce variation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Process Performance Pp & Ppk (Long-Term)

The **Pp / Ppk** family uses the **overall sample standard deviation** $s$ (with $n-1$ in the denominator), computed from a large dataset of consecutive measurements across time. This captures the **1.5σ drift** that Cp/Cpk ignore.

$$s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$$

$$
P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}
$$

$$
P_{pk} = \min\!\left[ \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s},\ \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s} \right]
$$

**Six-Sigma equivalence table (long-term, with 1.5σ shift):**

| Ppk Value | Z_lt = 3·Ppk + 1.5 | PPM Defective (approx) | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.67 | 3.5σ | 46,560 | 3σ — Bare minimum |
| 1.00 | 4.5σ | 6,210 | 4σ — Industry average |
| 1.33 | 5.5σ | 233 | 5σ — World-class |
| 1.50 | 6.0σ | 3.4 | **Six-Sigma** |
| 2.00 | 7.5σ | ≈ 0 | Beyond Six-Sigma |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Process Status | Best Index | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| New process, never run | Unknown | **None valid** | Need ≥ 25 subgroups to estimate |
| Stable, just qualified | In-control | **Cp, Cpk** | Within-subgroup variation is meaningful |
| Stable for 6 months, want true yield | In-control, with drift history | **Pp, Ppk** | Captures real-world shift |
| Process has assignable cause present | Out-of-control | **None** — return to Step 1 of DMAIC | Capability is undefined |
| Customer specifies a *Target* (not just limits) | In-control | **Cpm** | Penalizes off-target |
| One-sided spec (e.g., strength ≥ limit) | In-control | **CPU or CPL alone** | No opposite limit exists |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Process Capability Block Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Raw Material and CTQ Data] --> B[Establish USL and LSL from VOC]
    B --> C[Collect Subgroup Data n ge 25 subgroups of size 2 to 5]
    C --> D[Build Xbar-R Control Chart]
    D --> E{Is Process In Statistical Control?}
    E -- No --> F[Remove Special Causes via SPC and 5-Why Analysis]
    F --> C
    E -- Yes --> G[Estimate Sigma within using Rbar divided by d2]
    G --> H[Compute Cp and Cpk]
    H --> I{Cpk ge 1.33}
    I -- No --> J[Project Sigma Level Zst equals 3 times Cpk]
    J --> K[Look Up PPM from Normal Distribution Table]
    K --> L[Recommend Process Improvement Reduce Sigma or Re-center]
    I -- Yes --> M[Classify as Capable 4 sigma class]
    M --> N[Compute Pp and Ppk Using Overall Sample Standard Deviation s]
    N --> O{Ppk ge 1.50 Long Term}
    O -- No --> P[Long Term Sigma Zlt equals 3 times Ppk plus 1.5]
    O -- Yes --> Q[Confirm Six Sigma World Class Process]
    P --> L
```

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Cp, Cpk and Centering Relationship (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Specification Window] --> A1[LSL on Left]
    A --> A2[USL on Right]
    B[Process Distribution Normal] --> B1[Mean mu]
    B --> B2[Six Sigma Spread]
    C[Centering Status] --> C1[mu equals Target T]
    C --> C2[mu shifted from T]
    C1 --> D1[Cpk equals Cp]
    C2 --> D2[Cpk less than Cp]
    D1 --> E1[Process is both Precise and Accurate]
    D2 --> E2[Process is Precise but NOT Accurate]
```

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Six-Sigma Tolerance Band Visualization (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SixSigmaBand
    direction LR
    Z1[LSL at minus 6 sigma] --> S1[Minus 5 sigma]
    S1 --> S2[Minus 4 sigma]
    S2 --> S3[Minus 3 sigma LSL Boundary]
    S3 --> M[Mean mu at Target T]
    M --> S4[Plus 3 sigma USL Boundary]
    S4 --> S5[Plus 4 sigma]
    S5 --> S6[Plus 5 sigma]
    S6 --> Z2[USL at plus 6 sigma]
    end
    SixSigmaBand --> OUT[Total 12 sigma between LSL and USL equals Tolerance Width]
    SixSigmaBand --> DEF[Defect Region: Beyond minus 6 sigma and beyond plus 6 sigma equals 3.4 PPM total]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The flow in Figure 1 is the canonical DMAIC sub-procedure for Capability Analysis. Figure 2 explains the geometric intuition for why a centered process (Cpk = Cp) outperforms a drifted one even with the same spread. Figure 3 is the geometric definition of a Six-Sigma class process — the total tolerance must equal **12σ** (12 sigma-units wide), allowing a 1.5σ drift on either side and still leaving 4.5σ margins.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Define Process Capability (Cp) and Process Capability Index (Cpk). State the relationship between them using the centering constant `k`.**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Capability (Cp):** It is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a process to produce output within specification limits, defined as the ratio of the tolerance band to the natural process spread (6σ).

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Process Capability Index (Cpk):** It is the *actual* capability including the effect of process centering, defined as the minimum of the one-sided upper and lower capability indices.

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left[ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right]$$

**Relationship via centering factor k:**

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k),\quad \text{where } k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - T \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2}$$

A perfectly centered process (μ = T) gives k = 0 and hence Cpk = Cp; a shifted process gives Cpk < Cp.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Differentiate between Cp/Cpk and Pp/Ppk. When is each used?**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Cp / Cpk | Pp / Ppk |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Short-term / Within-subgroup capability | Long-term / Overall performance |
| **Standard deviation** | σ_within = R̄ / d₂ (from control chart) | s_overall (sample std. dev. with n−1) |
| **Variation captured** | Only common-cause variation | Common-cause **plus** drift over time |
| **Used when** | Process is **stable and in-control** for short-term assessment | Want the **true long-term performance** including 1.5σ shift |
| **Six-Sigma benchmark** | Cpk ≥ 2.0 | Ppk ≥ 1.5 |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Model 14-mark question]**

> A pharmaceutical tablet-filling process has specification limits LSL = 480 mg and USL = 520 mg (target T = 500 mg). Twenty-five subgroups of size 5 are collected under stable conditions. The grand average of all 125 measurements is $\bar{\bar{x}} = 502$ mg, and the average subgroup range is $\bar{R} = 8.0$ mg. The overall sample standard deviation of all 125 readings is $s = 4.6$ mg.
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Compute Cp and Cpk using the within-subgroup standard deviation (use d₂ for n = 5). State the short-term sigma level and the corresponding PPM defect rate.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compute Pp and Ppk using the overall standard deviation. Calculate the long-term sigma level (with 1.5σ shift) and comment on whether the process qualifies as a Six-Sigma class process.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Short-Term Capability (Cp, Cpk)

**Step 1 — Estimate σ within using the R̄ method.** For subgroup size n = 5, the standard table gives $d_2 = 2.326$.

$$
\sigma_{within} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{8.0}{2.326} = 3.439 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cp.**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6 \sigma_{within}} = \dfrac{520 - 480}{6 \times 3.439} = \dfrac{40}{20.634} = 1.938
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cpk.**

$$
C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3 \sigma_{within}} = \dfrac{520 - 502}{3 \times 3.439} = \dfrac{18}{10.317} = 1.745
$$

$$
C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3 \sigma_{within}} = \dfrac{502 - 480}{10.317} = \dfrac{22}{10.317} = 2.133
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.745,\ 2.133) = 1.745
$$

**Step 4 — Short-term sigma level and PPM defect rate.**

$$
Z_{st} = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 1.745 = 5.235 \text{ sigma}
$$

From the standard normal table, a one-sided tail area at Z = 5.235 is approximately 0.00000082 per side, giving a total defect rate of roughly **1.64 PPM** (essentially 0.00164 ‰) on each tail, totalling about **3.3 PPM** overall — very close to Six-Sigma performance.

#### Part (b) — Long-Term Performance (Pp, Ppk)

**Step 5 — Compute Pp using overall s = 4.6 mg.**

$$
P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6 s} = \dfrac{40}{6 \times 4.6} = \dfrac{40}{27.6} = 1.449
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Ppk using sample mean $\bar{x} = \bar{\bar{x}} = 502$ mg.**

$$
P_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3 s} = \dfrac{520 - 502}{3 \times 4.6} = \dfrac{18}{13.8} = 1.304
$$

$$
P_{PL} = \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3 s} = \dfrac{502 - 480}{13.8} = \dfrac{22}{13.8} = 1.594
$$

$$
P_{pk} = \min(1.304,\ 1.594) = 1.304
$$

**Step 7 — Long-term sigma level (with 1.5σ shift) and classification.**

$$
Z_{lt} = 3 \times P_{pk} + 1.5 = 3 \times 1.304 + 1.5 = 3.912 + 1.5 = 5.412 \text{ sigma}
$$

**Comment:** A long-term sigma level of 5.412 places the process at the boundary of a **5-sigma class** (Ppk = 1.33 corresponds to 5.5σ long-term). To achieve a true Six-Sigma class process, the requirement is **Ppk ≥ 1.50** (i.e., Z_lt ≥ 6.0). The current Ppk of 1.304 is below this threshold; the gap is mainly due to **process drift** (σ_overall > σ_within) and slight off-centering. **Recommendations:**

1. Implement **SPC alarm rules** to detect shifts in real-time.
2. **Re-center** the process from 502 mg back to the target T = 500 mg.
3. Investigate sources of long-term drift (raw material lot variation, ambient temperature, machine wear).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Model 14-mark question]**

> A CNC milling operation produces a critical step on an aerospace bracket. The engineering specification is LSL = 9.95 mm and USL = 10.05 mm (target T = 10.00 mm). Under stable conditions, the process mean is μ = 10.02 mm, and the within-subgroup standard deviation is σ = 0.008 mm.
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate Cp and Cpk. State the centering constant k and interpret the result. Determine the short-term sigma level.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Suppose the management wishes to re-center the process to T = 10.00 mm without changing σ. Calculate the new Cpk. Also calculate the percentage reduction in defect rate (in PPM) achieved by re-centering.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Current Cp, Cpk and Centering

**Step 1 — Cp:**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{10.05 - 9.95}{6 \times 0.008} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.048} = 2.083
$$

**Step 2 — Centering constant k:**

Target T = 10.00 mm, μ = 10.02 mm, half-tolerance = 0.05 mm.

$$
k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - T \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2} = \dfrac{\vert 10.02 - 10.00 \vert}{0.05} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.05} = 0.40
$$

**Step 3 — Cpk:**

$$
C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k) = 2.083 \times (1 - 0.40) = 2.083 \times 0.60 = 1.250
$$

**Step 4 — Short-term sigma level:**

$$
Z_{st} = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 1.250 = 3.75 \text{ sigma}
$$

**Interpretation:** The process has excellent *potential* capability (Cp = 2.083 ≈ 6σ class) but is **40% off-center**. The actual capability Cpk = 1.25 corresponds only to a 3.75-sigma short-term level, dropping the process from a 6σ class to a 3.75σ class — a dramatic loss caused purely by **location error**, not by variation.

#### Part (b) — After Re-Centering

**Step 5 — New Cpk after re-centering (μ = T = 10.00 mm, σ unchanged):**

With μ = T, we have k = 0, and Cpk = Cp:

$$
C_{pk,\text{new}} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = C_p = 2.083
$$

**Step 6 — New short-term sigma level:**

$$
Z_{st,\text{new}} = 3 \times 2.083 = 6.25 \text{ sigma}
$$

**Step 7 — Defect rates before and after re-centering (per million).**

For the original Cpk = 1.250, the short-term Z = 3.75. The upper-tail area at Z = 3.75 (from the standard normal table) is approximately **88 PPM**, and the lower-tail area at Z = 1.25 (since μ is shifted up, the lower distance is 1.25σ) is approximately **105,600 PPM**. **Total original PPM ≈ 105,688.**

For the re-centered process, Z = 6.25 gives an upper-tail area of approximately **0.0000 PPM** (negligible) and the lower tail at Z = 6.25 is essentially **0 PPM**. **Total new PPM ≈ 0.034 PPM** (approximately 3.4 PPM equivalent).

**Step 8 — Percentage reduction:**

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = \dfrac{105{,}688 - 3.4}{105{,}688} \times 100 \approx 99.997\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A simple 0.02 mm re-centering of the machine eliminates virtually 100% of the defects. **Re-centering before reducing variation is a fundamental Six-Sigma principle** — it is virtually free, whereas reducing σ requires capital investment, training, or process redesign.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in Process Capability questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting d₂** — Students compute σ as $\bar{R}$ alone (i.e., 8.0 mg) instead of $\bar{R}/d_2$. This artificially shrinks the process spread, inflates Cp, and leads to a wrong defect-rate estimate. **Always write the d₂ value used explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Mixing σ_within and σ_overall** — Never substitute `s` (overall) into the Cp/Cpk formula; Cp/Cpk is *defined* in terms of σ_within. Conversely, Pp/Ppk must use `s` (overall), not σ_within.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the `min` in Cpk** — A common error is to *average* CPU and CPL. Cpk is the **worst-case** (binding) value, not the mean.
>
> 4. **Off-by-one in Cpm formula** — Cpm uses $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}$, not $\sqrt{\sigma^2} + (\mu - T)^2$. The squared shift term is **inside** the square root.
>
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (mg, mm, etc.).
>
> 6. **Wrong interpretation of "Six Sigma"** — Students often claim a process is "Six Sigma" based on Cpk = 2.0 (short-term only). The *Six-Sigma* class in industry-standard long-term terms requires **Ppk ≥ 1.50** (which gives Z_lt = 6.0 after the 1.5σ shift).
>
> 7. **No control-chart prerequisite** — A process with out-of-control points is **not eligible** for capability analysis. Always state *"process is in statistical control"* before quoting Cp/Cpk.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **Cp measures spread only**; **Cpk measures spread + location**.
- [x] **Pp uses `s` (overall)**, **Cp uses σ_within** = R̄/d₂.
- [x] **d₂ table values to remember:** n=2 → 1.128, n=3 → 1.693, n=4 → 2.059, **n=5 → 2.326**, n=6 → 2.534.
- [x] **Cpk = Cp (1 − k)** where k is the *centering constant*, $k \in [0, 1]$.
- [x] **One-sided specs** (e.g., strength ≥ limit) → use only **CPU** or **CPL**, never Cp.
- [x] **Six-Sigma class** = Cpk = 2.0 (short-term) **OR** Ppk = 1.5 (long-term) → both equal 3.4 PPM.
- [x] **Sigma → PPM conversion shortcuts:**
  - 3σ → 66,807 PPM (2-sided)
  - 4σ → 6,210 PPM
  - 5σ → 233 PPM
  - 6σ → 3.4 PPM
- [x] **Cpm** is used when **target value matters**, not just limits — penalizes deviation from T.
- [x] **Rule of thumb for KTU:** If Cpk < 1.0 → **not capable**; 1.0–1.33 → capable but improvable; 1.33–1.67 → good; ≥ 1.67 → world-class.
- [x] **Always verify in-control** (Xbar-R or I-MR chart) **before** computing capability indices.
- [x] **Re-centering is free; reducing σ is expensive** — always do the cheap fix first.
- [x] **DPMO formula** = (Total Defects ÷ (Units × Opportunities per Unit)) × 10⁶.
- [x] **Z_lt = 3·Ppk + 1.5** is the canonical long-term sigma (incorporates Motorola's 1.5σ shift).
- [x] **Cpk < Cp ⇒** process is off-center; **Cpk = Cp ⇒** process is perfectly centered.
- [x] **Cpk negative** is mathematically possible if μ lies *outside* [LSL, USL] — interpret as "completely incapable" and call for emergency corrective action.
- [x] **Module-2 KTU focus areas:** d₂-based σ estimation, Cpk with one-sided specs, conversion of Cpk to PPM, and the long-term vs short-term distinction.
- [x] **Watch the `k` symbol** — KTU sometimes uses `K` for the process shift constant; ensure you are not confusing it with Cp/Cpk.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures and Indices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measures and Indices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Six Sigma (PEPET742)**, the term **Measures and Indices** refers to the standardized quantitative tools used to objectively evaluate, monitor, and benchmark the performance and capability of a process.

- **Measures (Performance Metrics):** These are the *raw, descriptive statistics* collected from a process that quantify the magnitude of defects, opportunities, and yields. Common measures include **DPU, DPO, DPMO, FPY,** and **RTY.**
- **Indices (Normalized Ratios):** These are *dimensionless ratio-based parameters* that benchmark the inherent capability of a process against its engineering specification limits. Common indices include **Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk,** and the **Sigma Level (Z).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between "Measures" and "Indices" is a high-weightage conceptual question. Measures tell you *what happened* (descriptive), while Indices tell you *how good the process is* relative to specifications (evaluative).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor evaluating a patient's health**.

- The patient's **weight, blood pressure, and cholesterol level** are the *measures* (raw numbers, descriptive).
- The patient's **BMI (Body Mass Index)** and **Cardiac Risk Ratio** are the *indices* — they are normalized values that compare the patient's raw numbers to ideal standards, telling the doctor "how healthy" the patient is.

In the exact same way, Six Sigma uses **defect counts and yields as measures**, and converts them into **Cpk or Sigma Level as indices** to judge whether a process is "healthy" (i.e., capable of meeting customer specifications).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Six Sigma

The fundamental building blocks used to construct all measures and indices are:

- **USL** (Upper Specification Limit)
- **LSL** (Lower Specification Limit)
- **μ (Mu):** Process Mean
- **σ (Sigma):** Standard Deviation
- **O:** Number of Opportunities per Unit

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Opportunity:** An *opportunity* is any characteristic of a product/service that can be measured and verified as either conforming or non-conforming (i.e., defective). A single car has thousands of opportunities (paint, engine, brakes, electronics, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Six Sigma Measures

### Pillar 1: Defect Measures (Granular Counting)

- **DPU — Defects Per Unit:**  
  The average number of defects expected per single unit. A unit can have *multiple* defects.  
  *Logic:* Sum up all defects and divide by the total units produced.

- **DPO — Defects Per Opportunity:**  
  The fraction of opportunities that result in a defect. It is a probability (0 ≤ DPO ≤ 1).  
  *Logic:* Normalize the defects by the total possible opportunities (Units × Opportunities per unit).

- **DPMO — Defects Per Million Opportunities:**  
  The industrial benchmark metric. It scales DPO up to a "per million" basis for easy comparison.  
  *Logic:* DPO is rare (e.g., 0.0005), so multiplying by 1,000,000 gives a readable integer.

### Pillar 2: Yield Measures (Throughput Perspective)

- **FPY — First Pass Yield:**  
  The percentage of units that pass through a process step *without any rework or defect* on the first attempt.  
  *Logic:* Healthy output / Total input.

- **RTY — Rolled Throughput Yield:**  
  The cumulative yield of an entire multi-step process.  
  *Logic:* It is the *product* of individual FPYs at each step, not the sum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RTY is critical:** If 5 sequential process steps each have 90% FPY, naive expectation says 90% overall. But RTY = 0.9⁵ = **59%** — a massive hidden loss called the "*Law of Diminishing Returns*."

### Pillar 3: Capability Indices (Specification Perspective)

- **Cp — Process Capability Index:**  
  Compares the *spread allowed* (USL − LSL) to the *actual process spread* (6σ). It assumes the process is perfectly centered.  
  *Logic:* A process is "capable" if its natural variation (6σ) fits within the spec limits. Cp ≥ 1.0 is the minimum baseline; Cp ≥ 1.33 is industry standard; Cp = 2.0 is **Six Sigma world-class.**

- **Cpk — Process Capability Index (Centered):**  
  The realistic, "off-center" version of Cp. It accounts for the fact that real processes drift.  
  *Logic:* It is the *minimum* of the upper and lower capability — the worst-case side of the process.

- **Pp and Ppk — Process Performance Indices:**  
  These are the *long-term* counterparts of Cp and Cpk. They use **s** (sample standard deviation of *all* data) instead of σ (within-subgroup standard deviation).  
  *Logic:* Pp/Cpk = "what the process *can* do under ideal short-term control." Pp/Ppk = "what the process *actually* does over weeks/months."

- **Sigma Level (Z):**  
  The universal language of Six Sigma. Maps DPMO to a process sigma. The **1.5σ shift** is the famous convention Motorola introduced to account for long-term mean drift.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics are the *lingua franca* of quality engineering. An automotive OEM (e.g., Toyota) negotiates with suppliers requiring **Cpk ≥ 1.33**. A semiconductor fab tracks **DPMO in the single digits**. Pharma companies use **Ppk for FDA validation**. Without these measures and indices, quality is subjective; with them, it is a science.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Index | Formula | What it Tells You | Target / Interpretation |
|---|----------------|---------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **DPU** | $DPU = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | Average defects per unit | Lower is better |
| 2 | **DPO** | $DPO = \dfrac{D}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}$ | Probability of defect per chance | $0 \le DPO \le 1$ |
| 3 | **DPMO** | $DPMO = DPO \times 10^{6}$ | Defects per million chances | Industry benchmark |
| 4 | **FPY** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passing 1st time}}{\text{Total units}}$ | Step-level yield | $0 \le FPY \le 1$ |
| 5 | **RTY** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Total process yield | Hidden losses revealed |
| 6 | **Cp** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability (centered) | $\ge 1.33$ ideal |
| 7 | **Cpk** | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Actual capability (off-center) | $\ge 1.33$ ideal |
| 8 | **Pp** | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Long-term potential | $\le Cp$ typically |
| 9 | **Ppk** | $P_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3s}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3s}\right]$ | Long-term actual | $\le Cpk$ typically |
| 10 | **Sigma Level** | $\sigma_{Level} = Z_{score} - 1.5$ (or use DPMO table) | Universal quality scale | $6\sigma$ = world class |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Convention:** When only one specification exists (e.g., only USL for strength of a material), use $Cpk = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ — a one-sided index, also called **Cpu** or **Cpl.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Example Problem 1 — Measures (Defects and Yield)

> **Problem Statement:** A manufacturing line produces **500 mobile phones** in a shift. Each phone has **25 opportunities** for defects (screen, battery, camera, software, etc.). During inspection, **120 defects** are found across the batch. **75 phones** were found to have at least one defect. Compute DPU, DPO, DPMO, FPY, and assume the line has 4 sequential stations, each with the same FPY. Compute RTY.

### Step 1: Compute DPU (Defects Per Unit)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPU &= \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units}} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{120}{500} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.240 \text{ defects/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Meaning:** On average, every phone leaving the line carries **0.24 defects** — meaning roughly 1 in 4 phones has a defect.

### Step 2: Compute DPO (Defects Per Opportunity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPO &= \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{120}{500 \times 25} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{120}{12{,}500} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.0096
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Meaning:** The probability that *any single opportunity* on *any single phone* results in a defect is **0.96%**.

### Step 3: Compute DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= DPO \times 10^{6} \\[4pt]
     &= 0.0096 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
     &= 9{,}600 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Meaning:** For every million chances to make a defect, this process makes **9,600 of them**. Compared to the Six Sigma world-class benchmark of **3.4 DPMO**, this process is performing at approximately the **3.7σ level** (see conversion in Example 2).

### Step 4: Compute FPY (First Pass Yield)

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPY &= \frac{\text{Units passing on 1st attempt}}{\text{Total units}} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{500 - 75}{500} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{425}{500} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.85 = 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)

Since the 4 stations each have FPY = 0.85:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTY &= \prod_{i=1}^{4} FPY_i \\[4pt]
    &= (0.85)^{4} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.5220 = 52.20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Critical Insight:** Although each station seems healthy (85% pass), the *overall* yield drops to just **52.2%**. Almost **half the units require at least one rework** across the entire flow. This hidden loss is exactly why RTY was invented.

---

## 3.2 Example Problem 2 — Sigma Level Conversion

> **Problem Statement:** Convert **9,600 DPMO** to a Sigma Level using the standard Six Sigma conversion table.

### Step 1: Locate the DPMO in the Standard Table

Standard Six Sigma Table (with 1.5σ long-term shift convention):

| Sigma Level | DPMO |
|:-----------:|:----:|
| 6.0σ | 3.4 |
| 5.0σ | 233 |
| 4.5σ | 1,350 |
| 4.0σ | 6,210 |
| 3.5σ | 22,750 |
| 3.0σ | 66,807 |

Our value **9,600 DPMO** lies between **4.0σ (6,210)** and **3.5σ (22,750)**.

### Step 2: Linear Interpolation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fraction} &= \frac{22{,}750 - 9{,}600}{22{,}750 - 6{,}210} \\[4pt]
                &= \frac{13{,}150}{16{,}540} \\[4pt]
                &= 0.7952
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{Level} &= 3.5 + (0.7952 \times 0.5) \\[4pt]
               &= 3.5 + 0.3976 \\[4pt]
               &\approx 3.90\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Meaning:** The process is performing at approximately **3.9σ quality level** — better than average (3σ ≈ 93% yield), but still miles away from the **6σ world-class goal (3.4 DPMO)**.

---

## 3.3 Example Problem 3 — Process Capability Indices (Cp & Cpk)

> **Problem Statement:** A CNC lathe produces steel shafts. Specification: **USL = 25.10 mm, LSL = 24.90 mm.** A short-term study of 30 samples yields: **μ = 25.02 mm, σ = 0.03 mm.** Compute Cp and Cpk.

### Step 1: Compute Cp

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.03} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{0.20}{0.18} \\[4pt]
    &\approx 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Cp > 1.0 means the *spread* of the process fits inside the spec limits. Cp = 1.111 is acceptable but not excellent (target ≥ 1.33).

### Step 2: Compute Cpk (Upper Side)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \\[4pt]
                    &= \frac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.03} \\[4pt]
                    &= \frac{0.08}{0.09} \\[4pt]
                    &\approx 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Cpk (Lower Side)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \\[4pt]
                    &= \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.03} \\[4pt]
                    &= \frac{0.12}{0.09} \\[4pt]
                    &\approx 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Take the Minimum

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(0.889, \; 1.333) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Critical Insight:** Cp = 1.111 (good), but **Cpk = 0.889 (poor!)** The process is *off-center* — drifting toward the USL. The **upper side** is the bottleneck. This is why **Cpk is more honest than Cp** for real-world processes.

---

## 3.4 Example Problem 4 — Distinguishing Cp/Cpk vs Pp/Ppk

> **Conceptual Derivation:**  
> - **σ (sigma, lowercase in short-term context):** Average of within-subgroup standard deviations, $\bar{R}/d_2$ or pooled estimate. Reflects *instantaneous, common-cause variation.*
> - **s (sample standard deviation):** Computed using *all* data points as one big sample. Reflects *total variation over time,* including special-cause shifts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{within}} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \quad &\text{(short-term)} \\[4pt]
s_{\text{overall}} &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N-1}} \quad &\text{(long-term)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why Pp ≤ Cp typically:**  
> $s_{\text{overall}} \ge \sigma_{\text{within}}$ always (more variation captured when including time-related drift). Since σ is in the denominator of Cp, a *larger* denominator makes Cp *larger.* Therefore, **Pp is generally ≤ Cp**, exposing the real "weaker" long-term performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If **Pp ≈ Cp** and **Ppk ≈ Cpk**, your process is stable and in control. If **Pp < Cp**, your process has *special-cause variation* lurking that short-term studies missed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Measures to Indices Conversion Pipeline

The following Mermaid block maps the logical flow from raw shop-floor data all the way to the executive-level Sigma Level metric. This is the **"Six Sigma Calculation Pipeline"** that every Black Belt memorizes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Process Data] --> B[Count Defects and Units]
    B --> C[Measure: DPU]
    B --> D[Measure: DPO]
    C --> E[Measure: DPMO]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Sigma Conversion Table Lookup]
    F --> G[Index: Sigma Level]
    A --> H[Compute Sample Std Dev s]
    A --> I[Compute Within Subgroup Std Dev sigma]
    H --> J[Index: Pp]
    H --> K[Index: Ppk]
    I --> L[Index: Cp]
    I --> M[Index: Cpk]
    A --> N[Identify Defective Units]
    N --> O[Measure: FPY]
    O --> P[Measure: RTY]
    P --> Q[Benchmark Process Health]
    G --> Q
    J --> Q
    K --> Q
    L --> Q
    M --> Q
    Q --> R[Six Sigma Decision: Improve, Control, or Standardize]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Index

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Need to evaluate process?] --> B{Is process in\nstatistical control?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is it short-term\nor long-term study?}
    B -- No --> D[Use Pp and Ppk]
    C -- Short-term --> E[Use Cp and Cpk]
    C -- Long-term --> F[Use Pp and Ppk]
    E --> G{Centered process?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Report Cp only\nCp = Cpk]
    G -- No --> I[Report both Cp and Cpk]
    I --> J[Cpk lt Cp = Off-center drift]
    D --> K{Centered overall?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Report Pp only]
    K -- No --> M[Report both Pp and Ppk]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in KTU exams:** A common 7-mark question asks students to "explain with a flowchart how defect data becomes a Sigma Level." The pipeline above is your *model answer architecture*. Memorize the boxes, not the arrows.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

> **Q: Define DPMO. Why is it considered the most universally accepted defect metric in Six Sigma?**

**Model Answer:**

**DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** is defined as the number of defects observed in a process, normalized and scaled to a per-million-opportunities basis. Mathematically:

$$
DPMO = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}
$$

It is universally accepted because: (i) it is **dimensionless and normalized**, allowing comparison across different industries, products, and processes; (ii) it can be **directly converted to a Sigma Level** using the standard Six Sigma table; (iii) it accounts for the *complexity* of a product (more opportunities = harder to be defect-free). A car with 3,000 opportunities and a chip with 50 opportunities can now be fairly compared.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

> **Q: Differentiate between Cp and Cpk. Under what condition does Cp = Cpk?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cp | Cpk |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Compares spec width to process spread | Compares distance from mean to nearest spec |
| Assumes centering? | Yes, assumes process is perfectly centered | No, handles off-center processes |
| Formula | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $\min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ |
| Realistic? | The "best case" (potential) | The "actual case" (real) |

**Condition for Cp = Cpk:** The process mean must be **exactly at the midpoint** of USL and LSL, i.e., $\mu = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2}$. When the process is perfectly centered, the upper and lower capabilities are equal, making Cpk reduce to Cp.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

> **Q: An electronics assembly line produces 1,000 printed circuit boards (PCBs) per day. Each PCB has 80 opportunities for defects. Quality inspection found 240 defects in total, and 180 boards were rejected due to at least one defect.**
> **(a)** Calculate DPU, DPO, DPMO, and FPY for this process.  **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Convert the DPMO obtained to a Sigma Level using the standard conversion table. Also state what this means for the process.  **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1: DPU**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPU &= \frac{240}{1000} = 0.240 \text{ defects/board}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: DPO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPO &= \frac{240}{1000 \times 80} = \frac{240}{80{,}000} = 0.003
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: DPMO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= 0.003 \times 10^{6} = 3{,}000 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: FPY**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPY &= \frac{1000 - 180}{1000} = 0.820 = 82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(a) – 7 marks: 1.5 for formula, 1 for substitution, 0.5 for answer; repeated for each of the 4 sub-metrics.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Locate 3,000 DPMO on the standard Six Sigma table:**

| Sigma Level | DPMO |
|:-----------:|:----:|
| 4.5σ | 1,350 |
| 4.0σ | 6,210 |
| 3.5σ | 22,750 |

Our value **3,000 DPMO** lies between **4.5σ (1,350)** and **4.0σ (6,210)**.

**Step 2: Linear Interpolation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fraction} &= \frac{6{,}210 - 3{,}000}{6{,}210 - 1{,}350} = \frac{3{,}210}{4{,}860} = 0.6605 \\[4pt]
\sigma_{Level} &= 4.5 - (0.6605 \times 0.5) = 4.5 - 0.330 = 4.17\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Engineering Interpretation**

The process is operating at approximately **4.17σ quality level**. This is **better than the industry average (3σ)** but still falls **short of the 6σ world-class benchmark of 3.4 DPMO**. The yield implied is roughly **99.7%**, leaving room for improvement. Recommended actions: identify top defect types using **Pareto analysis** and pursue **DMAIC** improvement cycles.

*(b) – 7 marks: 2 for table lookup, 2 for interpolation, 2 for final answer, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

> **Q: A pharmaceutical tablet-coating process has specifications USL = 105 mg and LSL = 95 mg for the active ingredient per tablet.**
> **(a)** During a short-term capability study, the process mean μ = 102 mg and standard deviation σ = 1.5 mg. Calculate Cp, Cpk, and comment on whether the process is capable.  **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain with a clear comparison why Cp/Cpk differ from Pp/Ppk. A long-term study on the same process yielded an overall sample standard deviation s = 2.0 mg. Compute Pp and Ppk, and state which is more reliable.  **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Cp**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{105 - 95}{6 \times 1.5} = \frac{10}{9.0} \approx 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cpk — Upper Capability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{105 - 102}{3 \times 1.5} = \frac{3}{4.5} = 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Cpk — Lower Capability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{102 - 95}{3 \times 1.5} = \frac{7}{4.5} \approx 1.556
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final Cpk**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(0.667, \; 1.556) = 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Capability Comment**

Although Cp = 1.111 suggests the *spread* fits, **Cpk = 0.667 < 1.0** means the process is **not capable**. The mean (102) is shifted toward the **USL**, leaving only 0.667σ of headroom. The process is producing out-of-spec tablets on the upper side and **must be re-centered** before it can be considered acceptable. Pharmaceutical norms require **Cpk ≥ 1.33** for FDA validation.

*(a) – 7 marks: 1.5 for Cp, 1.5 for each Cpk side, 1 for final min, 1.5 for interpretation.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Conceptual Comparison Table**

| Aspect | Cp / Cpk | Pp / Ppk |
|---|---|---|
| Variation source | $\sigma$ — within-subgroup, short-term | $s$ — overall, long-term |
| Use case | Process is stable and in control | Process has special-cause variation |
| Reflects | *Potential* capability | *Actual* performance |
| Typical relation | $P_p \le C_p$, $P_{pk} \le C_{pk}$ | Smaller because long-term variation is bigger |
| Time horizon | Hours to one shift | Days to months |

**Step 2: Compute Pp**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \frac{10}{6 \times 2.0} = \frac{10}{12} \approx 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Ppk — Upper**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3s} = \frac{3}{3 \times 2.0} = \frac{3}{6.0} = 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Ppk — Lower**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3s} = \frac{7}{6.0} \approx 1.167
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Ppk**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pk} &= \min(0.500, \; 1.167) = 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Which is more reliable?**

**Ppk (0.500) is more reliable** for predicting real-world performance because it captures the *total* long-term variation (s = 2.0) including drifts, environmental changes, batch-to-batch differences, and operator effects. Cp/Cpk only used the short-term σ = 1.5, painting an *overly optimistic* picture. Since **Ppk = 0.5 < Cpk = 0.667**, the process is even *worse* in the long run than short-term studies suggested. **Conclusion: The process is failing badly and must be stabilized before any capability claims can be made.**

*(b) – 7 marks: 2 for comparison table, 1.5 for Pp, 1.5 for Ppk, 2 for justification of which is more reliable.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing σ with s:** Many students write Cp using *s* or Pp using *σ*. This *immediately* voids the marks. Memorize: **Cp/Cpk → σ (short-term)**, **Pp/Ppk → s (long-term).**
> 2. **Forgetting to take the MIN in Cpk/Ppk:** Full marks only if you compute *both* sides and explicitly write "min(...)".
> 3. **Wrong DPMO formula:** Writing $\frac{Defects}{Units} \times 10^6$ is a classic mistake. The correct denominator is *Units × Opportunities.*
> 4. **Sigma Level via interpolation:** Examiners deduct marks if you skip the linear interpolation step. Always show the fraction and the final calculation.
> 5. **RTY vs FPY:** Do not sum FPYs across steps — *multiply* them. This is the #1 conceptual trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Measures and Indices**

- **DPU** = Defects ÷ Units. Counts total defects, allows multiple per unit.
- **DPO** = Defects ÷ (Units × Opportunities). A probability, always between 0 and 1.
- **DPMO** = DPO × 1,000,000. The *gold-standard* defect metric in industry.
- **FPY** = (Good units on first pass) ÷ Total units. Step-level yield.
- **RTY** = Product of FPYs across all steps. Reveals hidden quality losses.
- **Cp** = (USL − LSL) / 6σ. Assumes perfect centering; only measures *spread*.
- **Cpk** = min[(USL−μ)/3σ, (μ−LSL)/3σ]. The *realistic, off-center* capability.
- **Cp = Cpk only if μ = (USL + LSL)/2** — perfect centering condition.
- **Pp** uses *s* (overall/long-term σ); **Cp** uses *σ* (short-term, within subgroup).
- **Ppk** is almost always ≤ Cpk; a large gap (Cp − Cpk) or (Pp − Ppk) signals centering problems.
- **Target values:** Cp, Cpk ≥ **1.33** (capable); ≥ **1.67** (excellent); ≥ **2.0** (Six Sigma world-class).
- **6σ quality** = 3.4 DPMO (with 1.5σ long-term shift convention).
- **Sigma Level** is found via the DPMO-to-Sigma table, with linear interpolation between tabulated values.
- **1.5σ shift** is the Motorola convention to account for long-term mean drift; it is an *engineering assumption*, not a physical constant.
- **For one-sided specs** (e.g., strength ≥ limit), use $Cpk = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ only.
- **High-yield exam tip:** Always show the *units* of DPMO (DPMO is dimensionless but conventionally written as "DPMO"), and always report Cpk to **3 decimal places.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-normal Process Capability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-normal Process Capability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Non-normal Process Capability Analysis (Non-Normal PCA)** is a branch of statistical quality control that quantifies the ability of a process to produce output within specification limits (USL/LSL) when the underlying quality characteristic does **not** follow a Gaussian (Normal) distribution. Standard capability indices such as **Cp**, **Cpk**, **Pp**, and **Ppk** rely on the assumption that data is normally distributed; therefore, when the process exhibits **skewness**, **kurtosis**, or **multi-modality**, dedicated non-parametric or transformation-based methods must be applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Capability analysis is valid only when data are **Independent, Identically Distributed (IID)** and drawn from a **Stable** process. For non-normal data, the classical **±3σ** spread (which corresponds to the **99.73%** of observations under normality) no longer represents the true process spread, requiring alternatives such as the **0.135th and 99.865th percentiles**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine you are measuring the **drying time of paint on a car door**. Most doors take about **45 minutes**, but a few stubborn doors with high humidity take **80 minutes**, while a small number of well-ventilated doors dry in just **20 minutes**. The histogram is *lopsided* — it has a long right tail. This is a **right-skewed (positively skewed)** distribution, not a bell curve.

If you naively apply the standard Cpk formula, you will assume the natural process spread is symmetric (μ ± 3σ), which **under-represents the true tail risk** on the slow-drying side. The KTU 2024 framework teaches you to handle this by either **transforming** the data into a normal shape, or by using **percentile-based** capability indices that respect the actual shape of the data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Key Statistical Benchmarks used throughout this topic:**
> - **Z-Score at 99.865%** = **3.00** (Upper benchmark for normal data)
> - **Z-Score at 0.135%** = **−3.00** (Lower benchmark for normal data)
> - **Benchmark Spread** (normal) = **6σ**
> - **Skewness** threshold for normality rejection = $|\text{Skew}| > 2$
> - **Kurtosis** threshold for normality rejection = $|\text{Kurt} - 3| > 2$

### 1.3 Why Processes Become Non-Normal

Processes deviate from normality for several engineering reasons that KTU examiners frequently test:

- **Tool Wear Effects** — gradual increase in mean → *right-skew*
- **Bimodal Tooling** — two machines producing same part → *bimodal*
- **Asymmetric Tolerances** — natural lower bound of zero (e.g., viscosity, roughness, concentration)
- **Mixing of Sub-populations** — supplier batches blended together
- **Fatigue and Failure** — exponential decay in strength over time

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Strategic Approaches

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the methodology into **two fundamental approaches**:

**Approach A — Transformation-Based (Transform → Analyze → Back-Transform)**
- **Box-Cox Transformation** — handles moderate skewness
- **Johnson Transformation** — handles severe skewness and kurtosis
- Calculate capability on the *transformed (normal)* data, then back-transform the indices.

**Approach B — Non-Parametric / Percentile-Based (Direct)**
- Bypasses normality assumption entirely
- Uses **empirical percentiles** of the data to define the process spread
- Capability indices are computed directly in the original units

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property — Invariance under Monotonic Transformation:** A capability index like **Cpk** is **mathematically invariant** under any monotonic (strictly increasing) transformation, because both the specification limits and the percentile structure are transformed identically. However, the **Cp** index **changes** under transformation. This is a high-yield exam point.

### 2.2 The Box-Cox Transformation

For a positive dataset $x_i > 0$, the Box-Cox transformation with parameter $\lambda$ is defined as:

$$
y_i(\lambda) = \frac{x_i^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}, \quad \lambda \neq 0
$$

And the special case when $\lambda = 0$:

$$
y_i(\lambda) = \ln(x_i)
$$

The optimal $\lambda$ is found by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** that minimizes:

$$
\ell(\lambda) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\left(y_i(\lambda) - \bar{y}(\lambda)\right)^{2}\right] + (\lambda - 1)\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln(x_i)
$$

### 2.3 The Johnson Transformation

Johnson's system fits data into one of **three families**, depending on the bounded/unbounded nature of the data:

- **SB** (Bounded) — for data with both natural lower and upper limits
- **SL** (Lognormal) — for data with only a natural lower limit
- **SU** (Unbounded) — for data with no natural limits (handles all skewness and kurtosis combinations)

The general transformation form is:

$$
z = \gamma + \eta \cdot f\!\left(\frac{x - \varepsilon}{\lambda}\right)
$$

where $z \sim N(0,1)$, $\gamma, \eta$ are shape parameters, $\varepsilon$ is the location, $\lambda$ is the scale, and $f$ is the appropriate family function.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Index / Method** | **Formula** | **When to Use** | **Key Property** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Box-Cox Transform ($y$) | $y = (x^{\lambda} - 1)/\lambda$ | Moderate skew, $x>0$ | Optimal $\lambda$ via MLE |
| Johnson SU (Unbounded) | $z = a + b \cdot \sinh^{-1}\!\left((x-c)/d\right)$ | Severe skew/kurtosis | Most flexible family |
| Johnson SB (Bounded) | $z = a + b \cdot \ln\!\left((x-c)/(d-x)\right)$ | Data with both bounds | $c < x < d$ |
| Johnson SL (Lognormal) | $z = a + b \cdot \ln(x-c)$ | Lognormal-like data | $x > c$ |
| Percentile Cp | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{P_{99.865} - P_{0.135}}$ | Non-normal, percentile method | Symmetric spec reference |
| Percentile Cpk (upper) | $C_{pk,u} = \dfrac{USL - P_{50}}{P_{50} - P_{0.135}}$ | Non-normal, upper spec concern | One-sided |
| Percentile Cpk (lower) | $C_{pk,l} = \dfrac{P_{50} - LSL}{P_{99.865} - P_{50}}$ | Non-normal, lower spec concern | One-sided |
| Cpk (Non-normal) | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pk,u}, \ C_{pk,l})$ | Overall non-normal capability | Asymmetric handling |
| Process Performance (non-normal) | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{P_{99.865} - P_{0.135}}$ (overall) | Long-term capability | Uses $\sigma_{\text{overall}}$ |
| Transformation Invariance | $C_{pk}(x) = C_{pk}(y)$ for $y = T(x)$ monotonic | Conceptual property | Cp is NOT invariant |
| Z-bench (USL) | $Z_{USL} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\# \text{ defects}}{\text{Total}}\right)$ | Yield-to-Capability bridge | Defect-based metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Non-normal capability analysis is the industry standard in **pharmaceutical tablet hardness**, **battery cycle life**, **surface roughness (Ra)**, **viscosity**, and **MTBF/Reliability** metrics. The Six Sigma Black Belt ASQ body of knowledge treats this as a *mandatory* topic — directly aligned with KTU 2024 PEPET742.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Standard Cp Fails for Non-Normal Data

The classical capability index for a normally distributed process is:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

This is derived from the fact that for $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$:

$$
P(X < \mu - 3\sigma) = 0.00135 = 0.135\%
$$

$$
P(X < \mu + 3\sigma) = 0.99865 = 99.865\%
$$

Therefore the *natural process spread* is $\mu + 3\sigma - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma$.

For a **non-normal** distribution, the probability mass between $(\mu - 3\sigma)$ and $(\mu + 3\sigma)$ is **not** $99.73\%$. For example, a **right-skewed** Weibull distribution with shape $k=2$ has only $\approx 94.5\%$ of its data within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$, meaning the true process spread is **underestimated** if we use $6\sigma$.

The percentile-based method **avoids this issue** entirely by replacing $6\sigma$ with the actual empirical spread $P_{99.865} - P_{0.135}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cpk for Non-Normal Data (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Define the median of the process $M = P_{50}$ (the 50th percentile).

**Step 2:** Define the upper-tail spread:

$$
S_{\text{upper}} = P_{99.865} - P_{50}
$$

**Step 3:** Define the lower-tail spread:

$$
S_{\text{lower}} = P_{50} - P_{0.135}
$$

**Step 4:** Build one-sided capability indices:

$$
C_{pk,u} = \frac{USL - P_{50}}{P_{50} - P_{0.135}} = \frac{USL - P_{50}}{S_{\text{lower}}}
$$

Wait — note the careful definition. By convention (and the Clements/Booth-Peterson approach used in KTU curriculum), the **Cpk upper** uses the **lower** half-spread as the reference. This ensures that a process that is well-centered (median = target) gives $C_{pk,u} = C_{pk,l}$ for the symmetric case, preserving continuity with the normal Cpk.

**Step 5:** Combine:

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pk,u}, \ C_{pk,l})
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A bottling line fills 500 ml bottles. The 50 measured fill volumes (in ml) yield the following percentiles:

$$
P_{0.135} = 488 \ \text{ml}, \quad P_{50} = 502 \ \text{ml}, \quad P_{99.865} = 518 \ \text{ml}
$$

Specifications: $LSL = 485 \ \text{ml}$, $USL = 515 \ \text{ml}$. The data is **right-skewed** and the Anderson-Darling test rejects normality at $\alpha = 0.05$. Compute the non-normal capability indices.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the lower half-spread:

$$
S_{\text{lower}} = P_{50} - P_{0.135} = 502 - 488 = 14 \ \text{ml}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the upper half-spread:

$$
S_{\text{upper}} = P_{99.865} - P_{50} = 518 - 502 = 16 \ \text{ml}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the overall spread:

$$
\text{Spread} = P_{99.865} - P_{0.135} = 518 - 488 = 30 \ \text{ml}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $C_{pk,u}$:

$$
C_{pk,u} = \frac{USL - P_{50}}{S_{\text{lower}}} = \frac{515 - 502}{14} = \frac{13}{14} = 0.929
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $C_{pk,l}$:

$$
C_{pk,l} = \frac{P_{50} - LSL}{S_{\text{upper}}} = \frac{502 - 485}{16} = \frac{17}{16} = 1.063
$$

**Step 6:** Final Cpk:

$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.929, \ 1.063) = 0.929
$$

**Step 7:** Compute Cp:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{\text{Spread}} = \frac{515 - 485}{30} = \frac{30}{30} = 1.000
$$

**Step 8:** Compute the $C_{pk} / C_p$ ratio to assess centering:

$$
\frac{C_{pk}}{C_p} = \frac{0.929}{1.000} = 0.929
$$

Since this ratio is below 1.0, the process is **off-center**, and the upper specification limit is the binding constraint. Improvement actions should focus on **lowering the median** by approximately $\frac{13 - 16}{2} = -1.5 \ \text{ml}$ to re-center the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When $C_p = 1.0$ but $C_{pk} = 0.929$, the process has *sufficient spread* but is *misaligned*. The KTU examiner awards marks to students who interpret $C_{pk}/C_p$ as a *centering indicator* and not just a number.

### 3.4 Back-Transformation: The Cp and Cpk Dilemma

Suppose we transform data using $y = \ln(x)$ (Box-Cox with $\lambda = 0$). The capability indices on $y$ are $C_p^{(y)}$ and $C_{pk}^{(y)}$.

- $C_{pk}^{(x)} = C_{pk}^{(y)}$ — **invariant**, no change needed.
- $C_p^{(x)} \neq C_p^{(y)}$ — **not invariant**, must be back-computed.

To recover $C_p^{(x)}$:

**Step 1:** Identify USL_y, LSL_y, and target_y on the *transformed* scale.

**Step 2:** Back-transform to original scale:

$$
USL_x = e^{USL_y}, \quad LSL_x = e^{LSL_y}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the empirical $P_{0.135}^{(x)}$ and $P_{99.865}^{(x)}$ in original data, then apply:

$$
C_p^{(x)} = \frac{USL_x - LSL_x}{P_{99.865}^{(x)} - P_{0.135}^{(x)}}
$$

This is the explicit, KTU-recommended procedure.

### 3.5 Capability-to-Yield Conversion (Z-bench)

For non-normal data, the standard Z-score is replaced by a **Z-bench** value derived from defects-per-million (DPM):

$$
Z_{\text{bench}} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \text{Defect Rate}\right)
$$

Where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the inverse standard normal CDF. For a Cpk of **1.50** under normality, the yield is **99.9930%** and $Z_{\text{bench}} = 4.5$. For non-normal processes, the same Cpk can correspond to *different* yield values, which is why direct yield-based monitoring is preferred for these processes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow for Selecting the Right Non-Normal Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect process data n >= 30] --> B{Test normality}
    B -->|Shapiro-Wilk / AD p > 0.05| C[Use standard normal PCA]
    B -->|p less than 0.05| D{Assess shape}
    D -->|Moderate skew only| E[Box-Cox transformation]
    D -->|Severe skew or kurtosis| F{Data bounds check}
    F -->|Has natural bounds| G[Johnson SB family]
    F -->|Lower bound only| H[Johnson SL family]
    F -->|No natural bounds| I[Johnson SU family]
    E --> J[Apply MLE for optimal lambda]
    G --> K[Estimate Johnson params]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> L[Transform data to normal]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Cp Cpk on transformed]
    M --> N{Back-transform required?}
    N -->|Cpk only| O[Report Cpk directly]
    N -->|Cp needed| P[Recover Cp via empirical percentiles in original units]
    O --> Q[Final capability report]
    P --> Q
```

### 4.2 Capability Calculation Workflow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        IN1[Raw data x with n obs]
        IN2[Spec limits USL LSL]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Processing Layer]
        P1[Normality test]
        P2[Percentile estimation P0.135 P50 P99.865]
        P3[Transformation if needed]
    end
    subgraph CALC[Calculation Layer]
        C1[Compute Cpk upper]
        C2[Compute Cpk lower]
        C3[Take minimum as Cpk]
        C4[Compute Cp using spread]
        C5[Compute Cpk over Cp ratio]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Final Cpk Cp ratio]
        O2[Yield estimate Z-bench]
        O3[Centering decision]
    end
    IN1 --> P1
    IN2 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> C1
    P2 --> C2
    P2 --> C4
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> O1
    C5 --> O2
    C5 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Capability Index Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Process Capability Family] --> B[Normal Data]
    A --> C[Non-Normal Data]
    B --> B1[Standard Cp Cpk Pp Ppk]
    B --> B2[6 sigma spread]
    C --> C1[Transformation Methods]
    C --> C2[Percentile Methods]
    C1 --> C1A[Box-Cox]
    C1 --> C1B[Johnson SB SL SU]
    C2 --> C2A[Direct Percentile Cpk]
    C2 --> C2B[Empirical Distribution]
    C1A --> D[Compute on transformed]
    C1B --> D
    D --> E[Back-transform Cp]
    C2A --> F[Report in original units]
    C2B --> F
    E --> G[Final Six Sigma Report]
    F --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
*Why is the standard Cpk formula inappropriate for non-normally distributed data? Suggest two remedial approaches.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The standard Cpk formula is derived under the assumption of normality, where the natural process spread is symmetrically defined as $\mu \pm 3\sigma$, covering **99.73%** of observations **(1 mark)**. For non-normal data (skewed or kurtotic), this $\pm 3\sigma$ range does **not** correspond to 99.73% of data, leading to incorrect capability estimates and poor defect-rate predictions **(1 mark)**. Two remedial approaches are: **(i) Transformation-based methods** such as Box-Cox or Johnson transformation that convert data to a normal shape before computing indices, and **(ii) Percentile-based methods** that use empirical $P_{0.135}$, $P_{50}$, and $P_{99.865}$ directly from the data, bypassing the normality assumption entirely **(1 mark)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the Box-Cox transformation formula. What is the role of the parameter $\lambda$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For $x_i > 0$ and $\lambda \neq 0$:

$$
y_i(\lambda) = \frac{x_i^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}
$$

For $\lambda = 0$, the formula reduces to $y_i = \ln(x_i)$ **(1 mark)**. The parameter $\lambda$ controls the *shape* of the transformation; its optimal value is found via **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** to make the transformed data $y_i$ as close to a normal distribution as possible **(1 mark)**. Common special cases include $\lambda = 1$ (no change, identity transform), $\lambda = 0$ (logarithmic), $\lambda = 0.5$ (square-root), and $\lambda = -1$ (inverse), each suitable for different degrees of skewness **(1 mark)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Board Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the methodology of **Box-Cox transformation** for non-normal process capability analysis. Discuss the role of MLE in finding the optimal $\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical tablet hardness test yields the following summary from 200 measurements:
- $P_{0.135} = 8.2 \ \text{kP}$, $P_{50} = 12.5 \ \text{kP}$, $P_{99.865} = 19.8 \ \text{kP}$
- Specifications: $LSL = 7.0 \ \text{kP}$, $USL = 22.0 \ \text{kP}$
- Anderson-Darling test rejects normality at $p = 0.012$

Compute the non-normal $C_p$, $C_{pk,u}$, $C_{pk,l}$, and the final $C_{pk}$. Comment on the centering of the process. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks**

The Box-Cox methodology follows these structured steps:

**Step 1 — Normality Verification (1 mark):** The data is tested using **Shapiro-Wilk** (small samples) or **Anderson-Darling** (general use) tests. If the null hypothesis of normality is rejected at $\alpha = 0.05$, proceed with transformation.

**Step 2 — Apply Box-Cox Family (1 mark):** Compute $y_i(\lambda) = (x_i^{\lambda} - 1)/\lambda$ for a range of $\lambda \in [-2, 2]$ (typical grid step 0.1 or finer).

**Step 3 — Find Optimal $\lambda$ via MLE (2 marks):** The log-likelihood function is:

$$
\ell(\lambda) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\left(y_i(\lambda) - \bar{y}(\lambda)\right)^{2}\right] + (\lambda - 1)\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln(x_i)
$$

The first term rewards small residual variance in the transformed data; the second term is the **Jacobian correction** for the change of variable. Maximize $\ell(\lambda)$ over $\lambda$ to obtain $\lambda^{*}$.

**Step 4 — Validate Transformation (1 mark):** Re-test the transformed $y_i(\lambda^{*})$ for normality. If accepted, proceed to compute capability indices on the *transformed* data.

**Step 5 — Compute & Back-Transform (1 mark):** Compute $C_{pk}$ on the transformed data (it is invariant and need not be back-transformed). Recover $C_p$ by back-transforming the specification limits to the original scale and using empirical percentiles.

**Step 6 — Decision (1 mark):** If transformation does not achieve normality, switch to the **Johnson transformation** family which handles a wider class of shapes including severe kurtosis.

---

**Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute half-spreads (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\text{lower}} = P_{50} - P_{0.135} = 12.5 - 8.2 = 4.3 \ \text{kP}
$$

$$
S_{\text{upper}} = P_{99.865} - P_{50} = 19.8 - 12.5 = 7.3 \ \text{kP}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute overall spread (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Spread} = 19.8 - 8.2 = 11.6 \ \text{kP}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cp (1 mark):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{\text{Spread}} = \frac{22.0 - 7.0}{11.6} = \frac{15.0}{11.6} = 1.293
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cpk,u (1 mark):**

$$
C_{pk,u} = \frac{USL - P_{50}}{S_{\text{lower}}} = \frac{22.0 - 12.5}{4.3} = \frac{9.5}{4.3} = 2.209
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cpk,l (1 mark):**

$$
C_{pk,l} = \frac{P_{50} - LSL}{S_{\text{upper}}} = \frac{12.5 - 7.0}{7.3} = \frac{5.5}{7.3} = 0.753
$$

**Step 6 — Final Cpk (1 mark):**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(2.209, \ 0.753) = 0.753
$$

**Step 7 — Centering Analysis (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{C_{pk}}{C_p} = \frac{0.753}{1.293} = 0.583
$$

Since $C_{pk}/C_p \ll 1$, the process is **significantly off-center**, with the **lower specification limit (7.0 kP)** being the binding constraint. The median (12.5 kP) is too close to the LSL. **Recommendation:** Re-center the process upward by approximately 2.0 kP to achieve a balanced $C_{pk}$ on both sides. The upper side has abundant margin ($C_{pk,u} = 2.209$), so relaxing the target is also an option.

---

**Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Modified Board Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Johnson transformation system** and its three families (SB, SL, SU). Under what conditions is each family selected? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-normal process has capability indices computed on the Box-Cox transformed data as $C_p^{(y)} = 1.42$ and $C_{pk}^{(y)} = 1.18$. The original specification limits are $LSL = 50$, $USL = 150$, and the box-cox optimal $\lambda = 0.5$. The empirical $P_{0.135}$ and $P_{99.865}$ in the original data are 62 and 142 respectively. Determine the non-normal $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ in the **original units**, and explain the invariance property. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks**

The Johnson transformation is a system of three parametric families that map any continuous data to a standard normal variable $z$ **(1 mark)**:

**Johnson SB (Bounded):** Used when data has both natural lower and upper bounds (e.g., percentage yield between 0–100%). The form is:

$$
z = \gamma + \eta \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{x - \varepsilon}{\lambda + \varepsilon - x}\right)
$$
