The numerical measure of correlation is the **Correlation Coefficient**, denoted by **r** for sample data and **ρ (rho)** for population data. For Karl Pearson's method:

$$r = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y}$$

where **Cov(X, Y)** is the **covariance** between X and Y, and **σ_X, σ_Y** are the **standard deviations**.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy 🍎

Imagine you are a Six Sigma Black Belt investigating the relationship between **the number of hours a machine is operated per day (X)** and **the number of defects produced per day (Y)**.

- If you plot daily data points on a graph, you will likely observe a **pattern**.
- Correlation tells you: *"Are machines running longer producing more defects in a predictable, consistent way?"*
- The correlation coefficient **r** is like a **strength-meter** for this pattern:
  - **r = +1** → Perfect, lock-step upward trend
  - **r = −1** → Perfect, lock-step downward trend
  - **r = 0** → No linear pattern at all (pure chaos)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correlation does NOT imply causation.** Two variables may move together perfectly (e.g., ice cream sales and drowning deaths) without one *causing* the other. A hidden third variable (summer heat) is the actual driver.

## 1.2 Regression Analysis — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Regression Analysis** is the statistical methodology used to *model* the functional relationship between a **dependent variable (Y)** and one or more **independent variables (X)**. It produces a **predictive equation** — the *regression equation* — used to estimate or forecast Y from known values of X.

In simple linear regression (one independent variable), the model is:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$$

where **β₀** is the **intercept**, **β₁** is the **slope (regression coefficient)**, and **ε** is the **random error term**.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy 🚗

Continuing the machine-defects example: Once you *know* a relationship exists (via correlation), you want a **rule** to **predict** tomorrow's defects from tomorrow's operating hours. Regression gives you that rule — a **best-fit straight line** through the scatter of data points. The line's slope tells you *"for every extra hour of operation, defects increase by k units."*

---

## 1.3 Correlation vs. Regression — Quick Distinction

| Aspect | Correlation | Regression |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Measures *strength* of relationship | Models the *functional form* of relationship |
| **Output** | A single number: **r** (between −1 and +1) | An **equation**: Y = a + bX |
| **Symmetry** | Symmetric (r_xy = r_yx) | Asymmetric (Y on X ≠ X on Y) |
| **Causality** | No causal claim | Implies Y *depends on* X |
| **Use** | Exploratory analysis | Prediction & forecasting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Distinguish between correlation and regression."* Memorise the table above — it appears almost every academic year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Correlation

### 2.1.1 By Direction

- **Positive Correlation** — Both variables move in the **same direction** (r > 0). Example: Hours studied ↔ Exam score.
- **Negative Correlation** — Variables move in **opposite directions** (r < 0). Example: Machine maintenance frequency ↔ Breakdown rate.
- **Zero Correlation** — **No linear relationship** (r ≈ 0).

### 2.1.2 By Magnitude

| Magnitude | Range of r |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Perfect** | r = ±1 |
| **Strong** | 0.7 ≤ \vert r \vert < 1 |
| **Moderate** | 0.4 ≤ \vert r \vert < 0.7 |
| **Weak** | 0.2 ≤ \vert r \vert < 0.4 |
| **Negligible** | \vert r \vert < 0.2 |

### 2.1.3 By Number of Variables

- **Simple Correlation** — Between **two** variables.
- **Multiple Correlation** — Among **three or more** variables.
- **Partial Correlation** — Measures correlation between two variables *holding a third constant*.

## 2.2 Methods of Measuring Correlation

1. **Scatter Diagram Method** — Visual/graphical inspection of plotted points.
2. **Karl Pearson's Coefficient of Correlation** — Most widely used, valid for *quantitative* data. Assumes linearity and roughly normal distribution.
3. **Spearman's Rank Correlation Coefficient (ρ or r_s)** — Used for *qualitative/ordinal* data, or when data is non-normal. Computed on ranks, not raw values.
4. **Kendall's Tau (τ)** — Another rank-based method; useful for small samples.

## 2.3 Karl Pearson's Correlation Coefficient — Two Computational Forms

### 2.3.1 Form 1: Deviation Form (Direct Means)

$$r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

### 2.3.2 Form 2: Raw-Score Formula (Ungrouped Data)

$$r = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{\sqrt{[n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2][n\sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2]}}$$

where **n** = number of paired observations.

### 2.3.3 Form 3: Change-of-Origin-and-Scale Form

If we apply **u = (x − A)/h** and **v = (y − B)/k**, then:

$$r = \frac{n\sum uv - \sum u \sum v}{\sqrt{[n\sum u^2 - (\sum u)^2][n\sum v^2 - (\sum v)^2]}}$$

This form is **exam-friendly** because you may choose convenient A, B, h, k to reduce arithmetic.

## 2.4 Spearman's Rank Correlation Coefficient

$$r_s = 1 - \frac{6 \sum D^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}$$

where **D = R_x − R_y** (difference of ranks for each observation) and **n** = number of observations. When ties exist, a **correction factor** must be applied:

$$r_s = \frac{\sum x^2 + \sum y^2 - \sum D^2}{2\sqrt{\sum x^2 \cdot \sum y^2}}$$

with **CF = (m³ − m)/12** for each tied group of size m added to **∑x²** and **∑y²**.

## 2.5 Lines of Regression

The two regression lines (in simple linear regression) are:

$$Y \text{ on } X: \quad Y = a_Y + b_{YX} \cdot X$$

$$X \text{ on } Y: \quad X = a_X + b_{XY} \cdot Y$$

## 2.6 Regression Coefficients

$$b_{YX} = r \cdot \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X} = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$$

$$b_{XY} = r \cdot \frac{\sigma_X}{\sigma_Y} = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2}$$

The intercepts are:

$$a_Y = \bar{Y} - b_{YX} \bar{X}$$

$$a_X = \bar{X} - b_{XY} \bar{Y}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important rapid-revision asset** for the KTU Board exam on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Range / Boundary | Key Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Pearson's r (raw-score) | $r = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{\sqrt{[n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2][n\sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2]}}$ | $-1 \le r \le 1$ | Most-used KTU formula |
| 2 | Pearson's r (mean-dev.) | $r = \dfrac{\sum(x-\bar x)(y-\bar y)}{\sqrt{\sum(x-\bar x)^2 \cdot \sum(y-\bar y)^2}}$ | $-1 \le r \le 1$ | Used when means are whole numbers |
| 3 | Spearman's rank ρ | $\rho = 1 - \dfrac{6\sum D^2}{n(n^2-1)}$ | $-1 \le \rho \le 1$ | Apply CF when ties exist |
| 4 | Regression coeff. $b_{YX}$ | $b_{YX} = r \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X}$ | All reals | Slope of Y-on-X line |
| 5 | Regression coeff. $b_{XY}$ | $b_{XY} = r \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_X}{\sigma_Y}$ | All reals | Slope of X-on-Y line |
| 6 | Product relation | $b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY} = r^2$ | $0 \le r^2 \le 1$ | Famous KTU identity |
| 7 | Standard error of estimate (Y on X) | $S_{YX} = \sigma_Y \sqrt{1 - r^2}$ | $S_{YX} \ge 0$ | Lower = better fit |
| 8 | Coefficient of determination | $R^2 = r^2$ | $0 \le R^2 \le 1$ | % of variance explained |
| 9 | Angle between two regression lines | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{1 - r^2}{r} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}{\sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2}$ | $0^\circ \le \theta \le 90^\circ$ | Lines coincide when r = ±1 |
| 10 | Mean of X | $\bar X = \dfrac{\sum x}{n}$ | — | Required for intercepts |

## 2.8 Properties of Correlation Coefficient

- **r** is a **pure, dimensionless number** (no units).
- **r** is **independent of change of origin and scale** of X and Y.
- **r** lies in the closed interval **[−1, +1]**.
- **r = +1 or −1** indicates a **perfect linear** relationship.
- **r** is a measure of **linear** association only — a zero r does not mean *no* relationship, just *no linear* one.
- The **geometric mean** of the two regression coefficients equals **\vert r \vert**: $\sqrt{b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY}} = \vert r \vert$.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Six Sigma DMAIC (Measure/Analyse):** Identify which process input (X) most strongly correlates with the output defect rate (Y).
- **Quality Control:** Correlate ambient temperature vs. product dimensional tolerance drift.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Build a regression model: *Vibration level → Bearing failure probability*.
- **Lean Manufacturing:** Quantify the relationship between cycle time and takt time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Covariance Definition to Karl Pearson's r

### Step 1 — Define covariance

The covariance of two random variables X and Y (with means $\bar X$ and $\bar Y$) is:

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar x)(y_i - \bar y)$$

*Conversion logic:* Covariance measures the **average joint deviation** of X and Y from their respective means. A positive value means they deviate in the same direction; a negative value means opposite.

### Step 2 — Define variances

$$\sigma_X^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar x)^2, \qquad \sigma_Y^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \bar y)^2$$

### Step 3 — Standardise covariance

The Pearson correlation coefficient is the **standardised** version of covariance — covariance normalised by the product of standard deviations:

$$r = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the definitions

Substituting the raw definitions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$r = \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar x)(y_i - \bar y)}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar x)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \bar y)^2}}$$

The factor $\frac{1}{n}$ in numerator and denominator cancels cleanly:

$$r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar x)(y_i - \bar y)}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar x)^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar y)^2}}$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the **deviation form** of Pearson's r — the form used when the means $\bar X$ and $\bar Y$ are convenient whole numbers.

### Step 5 — Convert to raw-score form

We expand the terms algebraically. Recall the **algebraic identities**:

$$\sum (x_i - \bar x)^2 = \sum x_i^2 - \frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}$$

$$\sum (y_i - \bar y)^2 = \sum y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n}$$

$$\sum (x_i - \bar x)(y_i - \bar y) = \sum x_i y_i - \frac{\sum x_i \sum y_i}{n}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by **n** to clear the fraction:

$$r = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right]\left[n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2\right]}}$$

This is the **raw-score form** of Pearson's r — the workhorse formula for KTU Board exam calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Slope of the Y-on-X Regression Line ($b_{YX}$)

### Step 1 — State the principle

The regression line of Y on X is the line that **minimises the sum of squared vertical deviations** of the observed Y values from the predicted Y values (the **Least Squares Method**).

$$\text{Minimise: } S = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum (y_i - a - b x_i)^2$$

### Step 2 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2 \sum (y_i - a - b x_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2 \sum x_i (y_i - a - b x_i) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the normal equations

From the first equation, dividing by 2 and rearranging:

$$\sum y_i = n a + b \sum x_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \bar y - b \bar x \tag{i}$$

From the second equation:

$$\sum x_i y_i = a \sum x_i + b \sum x_i^2 \tag{ii}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute (i) into (ii)

Substitute $a = \bar y - b \bar x$ into (ii):

$$\sum x_i y_i = (\bar y - b \bar x)\sum x_i + b \sum x_i^2$$

$$\sum x_i y_i = \bar y \sum x_i - b \bar x \sum x_i + b \sum x_i^2$$

### Step 5 — Solve for b

Move the b-terms to one side:

$$b\left(\sum x_i^2 - \bar x \sum x_i\right) = \sum x_i y_i - \bar y \sum x_i$$

$$b \cdot \sum x_i^2 - b \cdot \frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n} = \sum x_i y_i - \frac{\sum y_i \sum x_i}{n}$$

Multiplying both sides by **n**:

$$b_{YX} = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the **slope** of the Y-on-X line. Notice the numerator is **identical** to that of Pearson's r — the link between correlation and regression is now explicit.

### Step 6 — Express in r-form

Since $\sigma_X^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar x)^2$ and using the identity from §3.1:

$$b_{YX} = r \cdot \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The Identity $b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY} = r^2$

### Step 1 — Write both coefficients

From the derivation above, by symmetry (swap X and Y):

$$b_{YX} = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$$

$$b_{XY} = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2}$$

### Step 2 — Multiply them

$$b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY} = \frac{\left[n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y\right]^2}{\left[n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2\right] \left[n \sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2\right]}$$

### Step 3 — Recognise the expression

The right-hand side is **exactly** the square of Pearson's r (see §3.1, Step 5). Therefore:

$$b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY} = r^2$$

*Conversion logic:* This identity is **extremely important** in KTU Board problems — if you are given one regression coefficient and r, you can directly compute the other.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Question:** Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient and the two regression coefficients from the following data:

| X | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Y | 3 | 7 | 5 | 9 | 11 |

### Step 1 — Build the calculation table

| x | y | x² | y² | xy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | 3 | 4 | 9 | 6 |
| 4 | 7 | 16 | 49 | 28 |
| 6 | 5 | 36 | 25 | 30 |
| 8 | 9 | 64 | 81 | 72 |
| 10 | 11 | 100 | 121 | 110 |
| **Σ = 30** | **Σ = 35** | **Σ = 220** | **Σ = 285** | **Σ = 246** |

### Step 2 — Apply the raw-score formula for r

$$r = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{\sqrt{[n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2][n \sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2]}}$$

Numerator: $5 \times 246 - 30 \times 35 = 1230 - 1050 = 180$.

Denominator (X-part): $5 \times 220 - 30^2 = 1100 - 900 = 200$.

Denominator (Y-part): $5 \times 285 - 35^2 = 1425 - 1225 = 200$.

$$r = \frac{180}{\sqrt{200 \times 200}} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

*Conversion logic:* r = 0.90 indicates a **strong positive linear correlation** between X and Y.

### Step 3 — Compute $b_{YX}$ and $b_{XY}$

$$b_{YX} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90, \qquad b_{XY} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

Since $b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY} = 0.81 = r^2$, the **identity is verified** ✓

### Step 4 — Write the regression equations

Means: $\bar X = 6$, $\bar Y = 7$.

Line 1 (Y on X): $Y - 7 = 0.90(X - 6) \Rightarrow Y = 1.6 + 0.9X$.

Line 2 (X on Y): $X - 6 = 0.90(Y - 7) \Rightarrow X = -0.3 + 0.9Y$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Spearman's Rank Correlation with Correction Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Ties in ranks require a **correction factor (CF)**. Many students lose marks by forgetting this — see the Examiner's Warning in Section 5.

**Data:** Two judges rank 5 contestants as follows. Find Spearman's ρ with the correction factor.

| Contestant | A | B | C | D | E |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Judge 1 (R₁) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| Judge 2 (R₂) | 2 | 1 | 3 | 5 | 4 |

**Step 1 —** D = R₁ − R₂: (−1, 1, 0, −1, 1). D² = 1, 1, 0, 1, 1. **ΣD² = 4**.

**Step 2 —** No ties here, so CF = 0.

**Step 3 —** Apply formula:

$$\rho = 1 - \frac{6 \times 4}{5(25 - 1)} = 1 - \frac{24}{120} = 1 - 0.2 = 0.80$$

**Interpretation:** Strong agreement (positive correlation) between the two judges' rankings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map: Bivariate Statistical Analysis

The following diagram shows the logical flow from raw bivariate data to a usable predictive model — the *Six Sigma DMAIC* perspective.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Bivariate Data X and Y] --> B[Plot Scatter Diagram]
    B --> C{Linear Pattern Detected?}
    C -- No --> D[Use Non-Linear or Rank Methods]
    C -- Yes --> E[Compute Pearson r]
    E --> F{r magnitude}
    F -- r near 0 --> G[No Useful Linear Relation]
    F -- weak r --> H[Predictor is Weak Reject Model]
    F -- strong r --> I[Proceed to Regression]
    I --> J[Compute bYX and bXY]
    J --> K[Form Two Regression Lines]
    K --> L[Validate Standard Error Sxy]
    L --> M[Deploy for Forecasting]
    M --> N[Six Sigma DMAIC Predict and Control Phase]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Correlation-to-Regression Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputBlock[INPUT STAGE]
        X1[Variable X Independent]
        Y1[Variable Y Dependent]
    end
    subgraph corrBlock[CORRELATION STAGE]
        C1[Mean Xbar Ybar]
        C2[Variance SigX2 SigY2]
        C3[Covariance X Y]
        C4[Pearson Coefficient r]
    end
    subgraph regBlock[REGRESSION STAGE]
        R1[Slope bYX]
        R2[Slope bXY]
        R3[Intercept a]
        R4[Regression Line Equation]
    end
    subgraph validBlock[VALIDATION STAGE]
        V1[Standard Error Sxy]
        V2[Coefficient of Determination r2]
        V3[Forecast Confidence]
    end
    X1 --> C1
    Y1 --> C1
    X1 --> C2
    Y1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> R1
    C4 --> R2
    C2 --> R1
    C2 --> R2
    C1 --> R3
    C4 --> V1
    C4 --> V2
    R4 --> V3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Correlation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start What Type of Data] --> Q{Quantitative Continuous}
    Q -- Yes --> R{Linearity and Normality}
    Q -- No --> S[Ranks Ordinal or Tied Data]
    R -- Yes --> T[Karl Pearson r]
    R -- No --> S
    S --> T2{More than 10 percent Ties}
    T2 -- No --> U[Standard Spearman Formula]
    T2 -- Yes --> V[Spearman with Correction Factor]
    T --> X[Calculate and Interpret]
    U --> X
    V --> X
```

## 4.4 Geometry: Angle Between the Two Regression Lines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Both Lines Coincide] -->|r equals plus or minus 1| G2[Angle theta equals 0 degrees Perfect Fit]
    G3[r equals 0] --> G4[Lines are Perpendicular Angle equals 90 degrees]
    G5[Intermediate r] --> G6[Lines Intersect at an Acute Angle measured by tan theta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **acute angle θ** between the two regression lines shrinks as \vert r \vert → 1 and grows to **90°** as r → 0. This geometric property is a favourite KTU conceptual question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define correlation. State any four properties of the Karl Pearson's correlation coefficient.**

**Model Answer:**

Correlation is a statistical measure that expresses the **degree and direction** of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables. It is quantified by the **correlation coefficient r**, which is the standardised form of covariance:

$$r = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y}$$

**Four properties:**

1. **r is dimensionless** — it has no unit, because it is a ratio.
2. **r lies in the closed interval [−1, +1]** — a hard mathematical bound.
3. **r is independent of change of origin and scale** — multiplying X or Y by a positive constant does not change r.
4. **r = +1 or −1** corresponds to a **perfect linear** relationship between X and Y.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**What is the difference between correlation and regression? Why is regression said to be superior to correlation?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Correlation | Regression |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output | A single value r | An equation Y = a + bX |
| Purpose | Measures strength | Enables prediction |
| Symmetry | Symmetric | Asymmetric |

Regression is **superior** to correlation because:

- It provides a **functional equation**, allowing **quantitative prediction** of Y from X.
- It indicates the **rate of change** of Y with respect to X.
- It is **asymmetric** — it correctly reflects the cause–effect direction (Y depends on X).
- It supports **forecasting** and **process control**, which is central to Six Sigma.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, you must answer **one full question from a choice of two**. Each 14-mark question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) Compute Karl Pearson's correlation coefficient for the following data. Comment on the strength of the relationship. (7 marks)**

| X | 10 | 12 | 14 | 16 | 18 | 20 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Y | 25 | 28 | 30 | 35 | 38 | 42 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Tabulate sums:**

| x | y | x² | y² | xy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 10 | 25 | 100 | 625 | 250 |
| 12 | 28 | 144 | 784 | 336 |
| 14 | 30 | 196 | 900 | 420 |
| 16 | 35 | 256 | 1225 | 560 |
| 18 | 38 | 324 | 1444 | 684 |
| 20 | 42 | 400 | 1764 | 840 |
| **Σ = 90** | **Σ = 198** | **Σ = 1420** | **Σ = 6742** | **Σ = 3090** |

n = 6.

**Step 2 — Apply the raw-score formula:**

Numerator: $6 \times 3090 - 90 \times 198 = 18540 - 17820 = 720$.

X-part denominator: $6 \times 1420 - 90^2 = 8520 - 8100 = 420$.

Y-part denominator: $6 \times 6742 - 198^2 = 40452 - 39204 = 1248$.

$$r = \frac{720}{\sqrt{420 \times 1248}} = \frac{720}{\sqrt{524160}} = \frac{720}{723.99} \approx 0.9944$$

**Step 3 — Comment:** r ≈ 0.99 indicates a **very strong positive linear correlation** between X and Y.

---

**(b) Fit a regression line of Y on X for the above data. Estimate Y when X = 15. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the slope $b_{YX}$:**

$$b_{YX} = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} = \frac{720}{420} = 1.7143$$

**Step 2 — Compute the intercept $a_Y$:**

Means: $\bar X = 90/6 = 15$, $\bar Y = 198/6 = 33$.

$$a_Y = \bar Y - b_{YX} \bar X = 33 - 1.7143 \times 15 = 33 - 25.714 = 7.286$$

**Step 3 — Regression equation of Y on X:**

$$Y = 7.286 + 1.7143 X$$

**Step 4 — Estimate Y when X = 15:**

$$Y = 7.286 + 1.7143 \times 15 = 7.286 + 25.714 = 33.00$$

*Conversion logic:* Since X = 15 equals the mean of X, the predicted Y equals the mean of Y (a useful sanity check that the model is consistent).

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) The regression coefficients are $b_{YX}$ = 0.6 and $b_{XY}$ = 0.75. Find the correlation coefficient r and the angle between the two regression lines. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Use the identity $b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY} = r^2$:**

$$r^2 = 0.6 \times 0.75 = 0.45 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \sqrt{0.45} = 0.6708$$

Since both regression coefficients are positive, **r is positive**: r ≈ **+0.67**.

**Step 2 — Angle between the regression lines:**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{1 - r^2}{r} \cdot \frac{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}{\sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2}$$

Without σ values given, we report a related expression. A common KTU variant uses:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{\sigma_X \sigma_Y (1 - r^2)}{r(\sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2)}$$

If we further assume $\sigma_X = \sigma_Y$, this simplifies to:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{1 - r^2}{2r} = \frac{1 - 0.45}{2 \times 0.6708} = \frac{0.55}{1.3416} = 0.4099$$

$$\theta = \arctan(0.4099) \approx 22.27^\circ$$

**Interpretation:** The two regression lines intersect at an acute angle of approximately **22.27°**, indicating a moderate-to-strong linear association.

---

**(b) Calculate Spearman's rank correlation coefficient (with correction factor) for the following data. (7 marks)**

| Student | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Marks in Statistics (out of 100) | 80 | 60 | 70 | 65 | 90 | 75 | 85 | 70 |
| Marks in Six Sigma | 75 | 65 | 72 | 70 | 92 | 78 | 86 | 74 |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Assign ranks (with tie-handling):**

| Student | Stats (X) | Rank R₁ | Six Sigma (Y) | Rank R₂ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | 80 | 3 | 75 | 4 |
| B | 60 | 8 | 65 | 8 |
| C | 70 | 6.5 (tied with H) | 72 | 6 |
| D | 65 | 7 | 70 | 7 |
| E | 90 | 1 | 92 | 1 |
| F | 75 | 5 | 78 | 3 |
| G | 85 | 2 | 86 | 2 |
| H | 70 | 6.5 (tied with C) | 74 | 5 |

**Step 2 — Compute D = R₁ − R₂ and D²:**

| D | D² |
| :--- | :--- |
| −1 | 1 |
| 0 | 0 |
| 0.5 | 0.25 |
| 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 4 |
| 0 | 0 |
| 1.5 | 2.25 |
| **Σ** | **7.5** |

**Step 3 — Compute the correction factors:**

Tied group in R₁: C and H share rank 6.5. Tie size m = 2.

$$CF_1 = \frac{m^3 - m}{12} = \frac{8 - 2}{12} = 0.5$$

Tied group in R₂: None significant (each rank is unique). $CF_2 = 0$.

So **ΣCF = 0.5**.

**Step 4 — Apply the corrected Spearman formula:**

$$\rho = 1 - \frac{6 \left[\sum D^2 + \sum CF\right]}{n(n^2 - 1)} = 1 - \frac{6 \times 8.0}{8(64 - 1)}$$

$$\rho = 1 - \frac{48}{504} = 1 - 0.0952 = 0.9048$$

**Interpretation:** A strong positive correlation — students who perform well in Statistics also perform well in Six Sigma.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Correlation & Regression Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Correction Factor for Ties in Spearman's ρ** — Examiners routinely award **1 mark** just for spotting the ties and another **1 mark** for the CF. Skipping this costs 2 marks easily.
> 2. **Confusing $b_{YX}$ with $b_{XY}$** — $b_{YX}$ is the **slope of Y on X** (numerator same, denominator uses Σx²). Mixing them gives a wrong regression equation and a failed identity check $b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY} = r^2$.
> 3. **Wrong sign of r** — r is **positive** if both regression coefficients are positive, and **negative** if one is negative. Many students take r = +√(product) blindly, losing a mark for sign.
> 4. **Forgetting the sign of D²** — D² is always **non-negative**; the formula has ΣD², not ΣD.
> 5. **Interpreting correlation as causation** — A KTU short-answer trick question: *"Does r = 0.9 mean X causes Y?"* Answer: **No, never.** Causality requires controlled experimentation.
> 6. **Forgetting to take the square root** in $r = \sqrt{b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY}}$. The product gives $r^2$, not r.
> 7. **Skipping the interpretation line** — Always add one final sentence: *"r = 0.95 indicates a strong positive linear relationship."* This is a free 1 mark in long answers.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-know for the KTU exam):**
>
> - **Correlation** measures the *strength and direction* of a linear relationship; **Regression** models the *functional form* and enables *prediction*.
> - **Pearson's r** uses raw data; **Spearman's ρ** uses ranks; Spearman's is preferred for ordinal data or non-normal distributions.
> - Pearson's r has the range **−1 ≤ r ≤ 1**. A value of **0** means no *linear* relationship (not "no relationship").
> - The product identity **$b_{YX} \cdot b_{XY} = r^2$** is the bridge between correlation and regression.
> - **Sign of r** = sign of both regression coefficients.
> - The two regression lines intersect at the point **($\bar X$, $\bar Y$)** — the *centroid*.
> - **Coefficient of determination $R^2 = r^2$** is the fraction of variance in Y explained by X.
> - **Standard error of estimate** $S_{YX} = \sigma_Y \sqrt{1 - r^2}$ measures the average prediction error.
> - **Change of origin and scale** does not affect r — but it does affect the regression equation's intercept and slope.
> - **Correlation ≠ Causation.** This is a one-line killer question.
> - **Spearman's CF** = $\sum \frac{m^3 - m}{12}$ across all tied groups; always add it to ΣD² in the denominator term.
> - **Angle between regression lines** approaches **0°** as \vert r \vert → 1, and approaches **90°** as r → 0.
> - In a **scatter diagram**, positive slope visually = positive r; downward slope = negative r; cloud = r ≈ 0.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: One-way ANOVA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** One-way ANOVA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**One-way Analysis of Variance (One-way ANOVA)** is a parametric statistical inference technique used to test the null hypothesis that the arithmetic means of **three or more independent groups** are equal, when the data is classified according to a **single categorical factor (independent variable)** having two or more levels.

The technique, formalized by **Sir Ronald A. Fisher (1920s)**, partitions the *Total Variability* observed in the response variable into components attributable to *Between-Group* variation and *Within-Group* (residual/error) variation. The ratio of these two variances, governed by the **F-distribution**, is used to make statistical inferences.

Mathematically, it tests:

$$
H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \dots = \mu_k
$$

$$
H_1 : \text{At least one } \mu_i \text{ is different from the others}
$$

where $k \ge 3$ is the number of treatment levels (groups).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** One-way ANOVA is a cornerstone tool of the **DMAIC – Analyze Phase** in Six Sigma. It is used to identify the **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** drivers whose means differ significantly across process categories.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fertilizer Field Experiment"

Imagine a Six Sigma Black Belt working in an agricultural R&D division testing **three new fertilizers** (F1, F2, F3) on identical plots of land to maximize crop yield.

- A naive engineer would run **three separate t-tests** (F1 vs F2, F2 vs F3, F1 vs F3).
- The flaw: each t-test carries a 5% chance of a *false alarm* (Type I error). With 3 tests, the cumulative probability of *at least one* false alarm balloons to ~14%.
- **One-way ANOVA solves this** by performing a **single, unified test** that asks: *"Does the variation in crop yield across all three fertilizer plots exceed the random variation within each plot?"*

If between-plot variation is **large** compared to within-plot variation, the fertilizers truly differ. If both are similar, observed differences are just random noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** ANOVA does **not** literally compare *means* — it compares **variances** to indirectly infer whether means differ. The bigger the *between-group variance relative to within-group variance*, the more likely the group means are not all equal.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Significance Level ($\alpha$):** Typically **$\alpha = 0.05$** in KTU board problems unless otherwise specified.
- **Degrees of Freedom for F-distribution:** Numerator $df_1 = k - 1$, Denominator $df_2 = N - k$.
- **Critical Value Lookup:** $F_{\alpha, \, k-1, \, N-k}$ from the standard F-distribution table.
- **Effect Size ($\eta^2$):** Ratio of *explained variance* to *total variance*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Six-Step ANOVA Procedure

**Step 1 — State the Hypotheses**

$$
H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \dots = \mu_k \quad \text{(all group means are equal)}
$$

$$
H_1 : \exists \, i, j \text{ such that } \mu_i \neq \mu_j \quad \text{(at least one mean differs)}
$$

**Step 2 — Partition the Total Sum of Squares (SST)**

The grand logic of ANOVA is the **fundamental identity of sum of squares**:

$$
SST = SSB + SSW
$$

- $SST$ (Total) = Total variation of all observations around the grand mean.
- $SSB$ (Between) = Variation explained by the factor (treatment effect).
- $SSW$ (Within) = Unexplained random variation (residual error).

**Step 3 — Compute the Mean Squares (Variances)**

The raw sums of squares depend on degrees of freedom. Converting to **mean squares** creates unbiased variance estimates.

$$
MSB = \frac{SSB}{k - 1}, \qquad MSW = \frac{SSW}{N - k}
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the F-Statistic**

$$
F_{\text{calculated}} = \frac{MSB}{MSW}
$$

Under $H_0$, $F \sim F_{k-1, \, N-k}$ (F-distribution). Large values of $F$ indicate rejection of $H_0$.

**Step 5 — Compare with Critical Value**

$$
\text{Decision Rule:} \quad \text{Reject } H_0 \iff F_{\text{calc}} > F_{\alpha, \, k-1, \, N-k}
$$

**Step 6 — Post-Hoc Analysis (if $H_0$ is rejected)**

Use **Tukey's HSD**, **Bonferroni**, or **Scheffé** test to identify *which specific pairs* of means differ.

### 2.2 Assumptions of One-way ANOVA

1. **Normality:** Each group follows a normal distribution.
2. **Homogeneity of Variance (Homoscedasticity):** All groups have equal population variance ($\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \dots = \sigma_k^2$).
3. **Independence:** Observations are independent across and within groups.
4. **Random Sampling:** Data is collected via random sampling.
5. **Continuous Response:** The dependent variable is measured on an interval/ratio scale.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Degrees of Freedom** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Grand Mean | $\bar{X}_{..}$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} X_{ij}}{N}$ | — |
| Group Mean | $\bar{X}_{i.}$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} X_{ij}}{n_i}$ | — |
| Total SS | $SST$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$ | $N - 1$ |
| Between SS | $SSB$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$ | $k - 1$ |
| Within SS | $SSW$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})^2$ | $N - k$ |
| Between MS | $MSB$ | $\dfrac{SSB}{k - 1}$ | — |
| Within MS | $MSW$ | $\dfrac{SSW}{N - k}$ | — |
| F-Statistic | $F$ | $\dfrac{MSB}{MSW}$ | $(k-1, N-k)$ |
| Effect Size | $\eta^2$ | $\dfrac{SSB}{SST}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Never confuse $SS$ (Sum of Squares) with $MS$ (Mean Squares). Examiners award **2 marks** for correctly computing $MS$ in board problems.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Six Sigma

- **Validate CTQ differences** between supplier lots.
- **Compare alternative process settings** (e.g., temperature levels of an injection mold).
- **Analyze A/B/n testing** in product design.
- **Compare mean defect rates** of multiple shifts or machines.
- Foundational precursor to **Design of Experiments (DoE)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental ANOVA Identity

We begin with the algebraic identity for any observation $X_{ij}$:

$$
X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..} = (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.}) + (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})
$$

This expresses the deviation of any single value from the grand mean as the sum of:
- $(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})$: deviation of observation from its own group mean (within-group),
- $(\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})$: deviation of group mean from grand mean (between-group).

Squaring and summing over all observations:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})^2 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})(\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})
$$

The **cross-product term vanishes** because for each fixed $i$:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})(\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..}) = (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..}) \cdot \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})}_{= \, 0} = 0
$$

Therefore, the **fundamental ANOVA identity** is rigorously derived:

$$
\boxed{\,SST = SSW + SSB\,}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A Six Sigma Green Belt studies the effect of **three catalysts** (A, B, C) on the reaction yield (in grams). Five trials are run with each catalyst. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean yields differ.

| Catalyst A | Catalyst B | Catalyst C |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 51 | 56 | 45 |
| 49 | 53 | 44 |
| 50 | 55 | 46 |
| 52 | 54 | 43 |
| 48 | 57 | 47 |

Given Critical Value: $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$.

#### **Step 1 — Compute Group Means and Grand Mean**

Group A sum: $51 + 49 + 50 + 52 + 48 = 250 \Rightarrow \bar{X}_{A} = 50.0$

Group B sum: $56 + 53 + 55 + 54 + 57 = 275 \Rightarrow \bar{X}_{B} = 55.0$

Group C sum: $45 + 44 + 46 + 43 + 47 = 225 \Rightarrow \bar{X}_{C} = 45.0$

Grand sum: $250 + 275 + 225 = 750 \Rightarrow \bar{X}_{..} = 750/15 = 50.0$

#### **Step 2 — Compute Total Sum of Squares (SST)**

$$
SST = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \sum_{j=1}^{5} (X_{ij} - 50)^2
$$

For Group A: $(51-50)^2 + (49-50)^2 + (50-50)^2 + (52-50)^2 + (48-50)^2 = 1 + 1 + 0 + 4 + 4 = 10$

For Group B: $(56-50)^2 + (53-50)^2 + (55-50)^2 + (54-50)^2 + (57-50)^2 = 36 + 9 + 25 + 16 + 49 = 135$

For Group C: $(45-50)^2 + (44-50)^2 + (46-50)^2 + (43-50)^2 + (47-50)^2 = 25 + 36 + 16 + 49 + 9 = 135$

$$
SST = 10 + 135 + 135 = 280
$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute Between Sum of Squares (SSB)**

$$
SSB = \sum_{i=1}^{3} n_i (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})^2
$$

$$
SSB = 5(50 - 50)^2 + 5(55 - 50)^2 + 5(45 - 50)^2
$$

$$
SSB = 5(0) + 5(25) + 5(25) = 0 + 125 + 125 = 250
$$

#### **Step 4 — Compute Within Sum of Squares (SSW)**

Using $SSW = SST - SSB$:

$$
SSW = 280 - 250 = 30
$$

**Verification by direct calculation for Group A:** $(51-50)^2 + (49-50)^2 + (50-50)^2 + (52-50)^2 + (48-50)^2 = 10$ (matches). Similarly for B and C: $135 \div 5 \cdot 5$... let us recompute carefully.

For Group B: $(56-55)^2 + (53-55)^2 + (55-55)^2 + (54-55)^2 + (57-55)^2 = 1 + 4 + 0 + 1 + 4 = 10$

For Group C: $(45-45)^2 + (44-45)^2 + (46-45)^2 + (43-45)^2 + (47-45)^2 = 0 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 4 = 10$

$$
SSW = 10 + 10 + 10 = 30 \quad \checkmark
$$

#### **Step 5 — Compute Mean Squares**

$$
MSB = \frac{SSB}{k-1} = \frac{250}{3 - 1} = \frac{250}{2} = 125.0
$$

$$
MSW = \frac{SSW}{N-k} = \frac{30}{15 - 3} = \frac{30}{12} = 2.5
$$

#### **Step 6 — Compute F-Statistic**

$$
F_{\text{calc}} = \frac{MSB}{MSW} = \frac{125.0}{2.5} = 50.0
$$

#### **Step 7 — Decision**

$$
F_{\text{calc}} = 50.0 \quad > \quad F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89
$$

**Decision: Reject $H_0$.** There is strong statistical evidence that the mean reaction yields differ significantly across the three catalysts at $\alpha = 0.05$.

#### **Step 8 — ANOVA Summary Table**

| **Source** | **SS** | **df** | **MS** | **F** | **F-crit** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Between (Treatments) | **250** | 2 | 125.0 | **50.0** | 3.89 |
| Within (Error) | **30** | 12 | 2.5 | — | — |
| **Total** | **280** | **14** | — | — | — |

#### **Step 9 — Effect Size (Bonus KTU Point)**

$$
\eta^2 = \frac{SSB}{SST} = \frac{250}{280} \approx 0.893 \; (89.3\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** ~89.3% of the total variation in reaction yield is explained by the choice of catalyst — a *very strong* practical effect.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Workflow — The ANOVA Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Collect k >= 3 independent samples"] --> B["Verify ANOVA assumptions: Normality, Homoscedasticity, Independence"]
    B --> C{"Assumptions satisfied?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Use Kruskal-Wallis non-parametric test"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Compute Group Means X_i and Grand Mean X_grand"]
    E --> F["Compute SST, SSB, SSW using fundamental identity"]
    F --> G["Calculate Mean Squares: MSB and MSW"]
    G --> H["Form F-statistic: F = MSB / MSW"]
    H --> I["Look up F-critical at alpha, k-1, N-k df"]
    I --> J{"F_calc > F_critical?"}
    J -- "No" --> K["Fail to reject H0: Means are statistically equal"]
    J -- "Yes" --> L["Reject H0: At least one mean differs"]
    L --> M["Conduct post-hoc analysis Tukey HSD or Bonferroni"]
    M --> N["Identify which specific pairs of means differ"]
    N --> O["End: Report ANOVA table, F-value, p-value, eta-squared"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — ANOVA Table Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs["Input Layer"]
        X1["Raw Observations X_ij"]
        X2["Group sizes n_i"]
        X3["Number of groups k"]
        X4["Significance level alpha"]
    end

    subgraph compute["Computation Engine"]
        C1["Grand Mean Calculator"]
        C2["SST Module"]
        C3["SSB Module"]
        C4["SSW Module"]
        C5["MSB = SSB / k-1"]
        C6["MSW = SSW / N-k"]
    end

    subgraph inference["Inference Engine"]
        I1["F-statistic = MSB / MSW"]
        I2["F-distribution Lookup"]
        I3["p-value Calculator"]
        I4["Decision Logic"]
    end

    subgraph outputs["Output Layer"]
        O1["ANOVA Summary Table"]
        O2["Hypothesis Decision"]
        O3["Effect Size eta-squared"]
        O4["Post-hoc Recommendations"]
    end

    X1 --> C1
    X2 --> C1
    X3 --> C2
    X1 --> C2
    X1 --> C3
    X2 --> C3
    X1 --> C4
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C6
    C5 --> I1
    C6 --> I1
    X4 --> I2
    I1 --> I3
    I1 --> I4
    I2 --> I4
    I3 --> I4
    I4 --> O1
    I4 --> O2
    I3 --> O3
    I4 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic of Variance Partitioning

```
                    TOTAL VARIATION (SST)
                            │
              ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐
              │                           │
       BETWEEN-GROUP                   WITHIN-GROUP
       Variation (SSB)                Variation (SSW)
              │                           │
    Explained by the                Unexplained
    Factor (Catalyst)               Random Noise
              │                           │
         ┌────┴────┐                  ┌────┴────┐
         │T1│T2│T3│                  │   T1    │
         │  │  │  │                  │  ●  ●   │
         │  │  │  │                  │ ●  ● ●  │
         └────┬────┘                  │  ●  ●   │
              │                       └────┬────┘
              ▼                            │
        LARGE F-ratio                      ▼
     (Means Likely Differ)            SMALL F-ratio
                                    (Means Likely Equal)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define One-way ANOVA. State any four assumptions underlying the test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** One-way ANOVA is a parametric statistical test developed by **R. A. Fisher** used to determine whether there are any statistically significant differences between the means of **three or more independent groups** when classified by a **single categorical factor**.

**Assumptions (½ mark each, any four):**

1. **Normality:** Each group is drawn from a normally distributed population.
2. **Homogeneity of Variance:** All $k$ populations have equal variances ($\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \dots = \sigma_k^2$).
3. **Independence:** Observations within and across groups are independent.
4. **Random Sampling:** Data is collected using random sampling techniques.
5. **Continuity:** The dependent variable is measured on a continuous interval/ratio scale.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**Explain the concept of F-distribution and its role in ANOVA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**F-distribution (1.5 marks):** The F-distribution is a continuous probability distribution arising as the ratio of two independent chi-square variates divided by their respective degrees of freedom. It is **right-skewed**, defined only for non-negative values, and characterized by two parameters: numerator $df_1$ and denominator $df_2$.

**Role in ANOVA (1.5 marks):** In ANOVA, the F-statistic is constructed as the ratio of two independent variance estimates — $MSB$ (between-group mean square) and $MSW$ (within-group mean square). Under $H_0$, this ratio follows $F_{k-1, N-k}$ distribution. A large F-value indicates that between-group variance exceeds within-group variance, signaling that group means are not all equal.

---

### **PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Derive the fundamental identity of One-way ANOVA: $SST = SSB + SSW$. Discuss the significance of this partition. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For any observation $X_{ij}$, the deviation from the grand mean can be decomposed:

$$
X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..} = (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.}) + (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..})^2 = (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})^2 + (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})^2 + 2(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})(\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})
$$

Summing over all $i = 1, \dots, k$ and $j = 1, \dots, n_i$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})^2 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})(\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})
$$

The cross-term vanishes since for each fixed $i$:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})(\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..}) = (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..}) \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.}) = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{SST = SSW + SSB}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):**

1. **Variance Attribution:** This partition reveals that *all observed variability* in the data can be cleanly attributed either to the experimental factor (treatment) or to unexplained random error.
2. **Inference Foundation:** It enables the construction of the F-ratio by separating *signal* (SSB) from *noise* (SSW).
3. **Six Sigma Utility:** Provides the engine for the Analyze phase — quantifying how much process variation is *controllable* vs *random*.
4. **Degrees of Freedom Additivity:** $(N-1) = (k-1) + (N-k)$, ensuring internal mathematical consistency of the ANOVA table.

---

**(b) The tensile strength (in MPa) of three alloys is measured. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean tensile strengths differ significantly. Given $F_{0.05, 2, 9} = 4.26$. (7 marks)**

| Alloy 1 | Alloy 2 | Alloy 3 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 65 | 70 | 60 |
| 68 | 72 | 62 |
| 70 | 68 | 58 |
| 64 | 74 | 61 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3$ vs $H_1:$ At least one mean differs.

**Step 2 — Compute Means:**

$\bar{X}_1 = (65+68+70+64)/4 = 267/4 = 66.75$

$\bar{X}_2 = (70+72+68+74)/4 = 284/4 = 71.00$

$\bar{X}_3 = (60+62+58+61)/4 = 241/4 = 60.25$

$\bar{X}_{..} = (267+284+241)/12 = 792/12 = 66.00$

**Step 3 — Compute SST:**

Alloy 1: $(65-66)^2+(68-66)^2+(70-66)^2+(64-66)^2 = 1+4+16+4 = 25$

Alloy 2: $(70-66)^2+(72-66)^2+(68-66)^2+(74-66)^2 = 16+36+4+64 = 120$

Alloy 3: $(60-66)^2+(62-66)^2+(58-66)^2+(61-66)^2 = 36+16+64+25 = 141$

$$
SST = 25 + 120 + 141 = 286
$$

**Step 4 — Compute SSB:**

$$
SSB = 4(66.75 - 66)^2 + 4(71 - 66)^2 + 4(60.25 - 66)^2
$$

$$
SSB = 4(0.5625) + 4(25) + 4(33.0625) = 2.25 + 100 + 132.25 = 234.5
$$

**Step 5 — Compute SSW:**

$$
SSW = SST - SSB = 286 - 234.5 = 51.5
$$

**Step 6 — Compute MS and F:**

$$
MSB = 234.5/(3-1) = 117.25
$$

$$
MSW = 51.5/(12-3) = 5.722
$$

$$
F_{\text{calc}} = 117.25 / 5.722 \approx 20.49
$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

$F_{\text{calc}} = 20.49 > F_{\text{crit}} = 4.26$. **Reject $H_0$.**

There is strong evidence at $\alpha = 0.05$ that the mean tensile strengths of the three alloys are significantly different.

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Between | 234.5 | 2 | 117.25 | 20.49 |
| Within | 51.5 | 9 | 5.72 | — |
| Total | 286 | 11 | — | — |

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Pattern, Module Choice]**

**(a) Differentiate between One-way ANOVA and the t-test. Under what condition does One-way ANOVA reduce to a t-test? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Criterion** | **t-test** | **One-way ANOVA** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Number of groups compared | Exactly 2 groups | Three or more groups |
| Statistic used | $t = (\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2)/SE$ | $F = MSB/MSW$ |
| Distribution | Student's t | F-distribution |
| Variance partitioning | Not performed | Explicit ($SST = SSB + SSW$) |
| Type I error inflation | None for single test | Multiple comparison issue if misused |
| Output | Mean difference & p-value | F-ratio, p-value, effect size |
| Post-hoc analysis | Not needed | Required to identify differing pairs |

**Reduction Condition (2 marks):** When $k = 2$ groups, the F-statistic in One-way ANOVA is mathematically equivalent to the square of the t-statistic from an independent samples t-test:

$$
F_{1, \, N-2} = t^2_{N-2}
$$

This can be proven by substituting $k = 2$ into the ANOVA framework and simplifying. Hence, for two groups, both tests yield identical conclusions — but One-way ANOVA is **more general** and **scalable**.

**Advantages of ANOVA over multiple t-tests (2 marks):**

1. Controls the **family-wise Type I error rate** at $\alpha$.
2. Provides a **single unified test** rather than $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise tests.
3. Allows **partitioning of variance** for deeper insight.
4. Forms the foundation for **DoE and factorial ANOVA**.

---

**(b) A manufacturing process uses four machines (M1, M2, M3, M4). The output quality scores are recorded. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ if the mean quality scores differ. Given $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$. (7 marks)**

| M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 85 | 90 | 78 | 92 |
| 88 | 92 | 80 | 95 |
| 86 | 89 | 76 | 94 |
| 87 | 91 | 79 | 93 |
| 84 | 88 | 77 | 91 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:** $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \mu_4$ vs $H_1:$ At least one mean differs.

**Step 2 — Means:**

$\bar{X}_1 = 430/5 = 86.0$, $\bar{X}_2 = 450/5 = 90.0$, $\bar{X}_3 = 390/5 = 78.0$, $\bar{X}_4 = 465/5 = 93.0$

Grand total = $430+450+390+465 = 1735$, $\bar{X}_{..} = 1735/20 = 86.75$

**Step 3 — SST:**

M1: $(85-86.75)^2 + (88-86.75)^2 + (86-86.75)^2 + (87-86.75)^2 + (84-86.75)^2$
$= 3.0625 + 1.5625 + 0.5625 + 0.0625 + 7.5625 = 12.8125$

M2: $(90-86.75)^2 + (92-86.75)^2 + (89-86.75)^2 + (91-86.75)^2 + (88-86.75)^2$
$= 10.5625 + 27.5625 + 5.0625 + 18.0625 + 1.5625 = 62.8125$

M3: $(78-86.75)^2 + (80-86.75)^2 + (76-86.75)^2 + (79-86.75)^2 + (77-86.75)^2$
$= 76.5625 + 45.5625 + 115.5625 + 60.0625 + 95.0625 = 392.8125$

M4: $(92-86.75)^2 + (95-86.75)^2 + (94-86.75)^2 + (93-86.75)^2 + (91-86.75)^2$
$= 27.5625 + 68.0625 + 52.5625 + 39.0625 + 18.0625 = 205.3125$

$$
SST = 12.8125 + 62.8125 + 392.8125 + 205.3125 = 673.75
$$

**Step 4 — SSB:**

$$
SSB = 5(86-86.75)^2 + 5(90-86.75)^2 + 5(78-86.75)^2 + 5(93-86.75)^2
$$

$$
SSB = 5(0.5625) + 5(10.5625) + 5(76.5625) + 5(39.0625) = 2.8125 + 52.8125 + 382.8125 + 195.3125 = 633.75
$$

**Step 5 — SSW:**

$$
SSW = 673.75 - 633.75 = 40.00
$$

**Step 6 — MS & F:**

$$
MSB = 633.75/(4-1) = 211.25
$$

$$
MSW = 40.00/(20-4) = 2.50
$$

$$
F_{\text{calc}} = 211.25 / 2.50 = 84.50
$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

$F_{\text{calc}} = 84.50 \gg F_{\text{crit}} = 3.24$. **Reject $H_0$.**

The mean quality scores differ very significantly across the four machines.

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Between | 633.75 | 3 | 211.25 | 84.50 |
| Within | 40.00 | 16 | 2.50 | — |
| Total | 673.75 | 19 | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square deviations** before summing — direct subtraction of means gives wrong SS. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing $N$ vs $k$** in degrees of freedom: numerator uses $k-1$, denominator uses $N-k$. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Using $t$-critical instead of $F$-critical** — ANOVA always requires the F-table, not t-table. (Lose 2 marks)
> 4. **Failing to state hypotheses explicitly** — examiners award marks for $H_0$ and $H_1$ formulation before computation. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the ANOVA summary table** — it is mandatory in 14-mark questions. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Not interpreting the decision** in business context — always state what the rejection of $H_0$ means in the Six Sigma / engineering problem context.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- [x] **ANOVA = "Analysis of Variance"** — compares *variances*, not means directly.
- [x] **Use One-way ANOVA** when you have **one categorical factor** with **$\ge 3$ levels** and a continuous response.
- [x] **Five core assumptions:** Normality, Homogeneity of variance, Independence, Random sampling, Continuity of response.
- [x] **Fundamental identity:** $SST = SSB + SSW$ — this is the *soul* of ANOVA.
- [x] **Degrees of Freedom:** Numerator $df = k-1$, Denominator $df = N-k$.
- [x] **F = MSB / MSW** — large F means groups differ.
- [x] **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{\text{calc}} > F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$.
- [x] **For two groups**, $F = t^2$ — One-way ANOVA generalizes the t-test.
- [x] **Effect size $\eta^2 = SSB / SST$** measures practical significance.
- [x] **Post-hoc tests** (Tukey HSD, Bonferroni, Scheffé) are mandatory after rejecting $H_0$ to identify *which* means differ.
- [x] **In Six Sigma DMAIC**, ANOVA is the *primary statistical tool* of the **Analyze phase** for comparing multiple process categories.
- [x] **Always verify assumptions** before applying ANOVA; if violated, use **Kruskal-Wallis** (non-parametric alternative).
- [x] **ANOVA Table is mandatory** in KTU 14-mark answers — include SS, df, MS, and F columns.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two-way ANOVA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Two-way ANOVA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Two-way Analysis of Variance (Two-way ANOVA)** is a parametric inferential statistical technique used to simultaneously examine the effects of **two independent categorical variables** (called *factors*) on a single continuous *response variable*, while also quantifying whether the two factors **interact** with one another.

Unlike the One-way ANOVA which tests only one factor, the Two-way ANOVA partitions the total variability into **four** components: variability due to Factor A (rows), Factor B (columns), the interaction A×B, and the residual (within-cell) experimental error.

In the KTU 2024 Six Sigma context, this is the engine that powers **Design of Experiments (DOE)** — specifically the *Full Factorial* and *Taguchi* designs — allowing engineers to detect the optimal combination of process parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Symbols Used in KTU Notation**
> - $a$ = number of levels of Factor A (rows)
> - $b$ = number of levels of Factor B (columns)
> - $n$ = number of replicates per cell
> - $N = a \times b \times n$ = total number of observations
> - $x_{ijk}$ = the $k$-th observation in the cell at row $i$, column $j$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are the manager of a Six Sigma project trying to boost the **yield (%)** of a chemical reactor. You suspect that two knobs matter:

1. **Temperature** (Low, Medium, High)
2. **Catalyst Type** (Type-1, Type-2)

Running the reactor 3 times at every combination gives you **18 data points** in a **3×2 grid**. You now have three questions:

1. *Does Temperature alone affect yield?* → **Main Effect of Factor A**
2. *Does Catalyst Type alone affect yield?* → **Main Effect of Factor B**
3. *Does the best Temperature depend on which Catalyst is used?* → **Interaction Effect A×B**

If the best Temperature is "High" with Catalyst-1 but "Low" with Catalyst-2, that is a classic **interaction** — the factors *talk* to each other. Two-way ANOVA answers all three questions with a single set of calculations, replacing guesswork with rigorous hypothesis testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not run two separate one-way ANOVAs?**
> Running them separately ignores the *interaction* effect. Worse, you would inflate the overall Type-I error rate because you would be performing multiple tests. Two-way ANOVA solves both problems in one model.

## 1.3 Model Equation

The standard fixed-effects additive model with interaction is:

$$y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = grand (overall) mean effect
- $\alpha_i$ = main effect of the $i$-th level of Factor A (with $\sum \alpha_i = 0$)
- $\beta_j$ = main effect of the $j$-th level of Factor B (with $\sum \beta_j = 0$)
- $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ = interaction effect of the $i$-th level of A with the $j$-th level of B
- $\epsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ = random error term, i.i.d. normal

## 1.4 Hypotheses Tested

| Source | Null Hypothesis $H_0$ | Alternative $H_1$ |
|---|---|---|
| Factor A | $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \dots = \alpha_a = 0$ | At least one $\alpha_i \neq 0$ |
| Factor B | $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = \dots = \beta_b = 0$ | At least one $\beta_j \neq 0$ |
| Interaction A×B | $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0 \; \forall i,j$ | At least one $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \neq 0$ |

> [!TIP]
> A significant interaction overrides the main effects interpretation. If the interaction is significant, you must report cell means — not just the marginal (row/column) means.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logic Behind the Partitioning of Variability

The fundamental ANOVA identity states that the **Total Sum of Squares** can be decomposed into four orthogonal components:

$$SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_{Error}$$

The logic flow is:

1. **Start** with $SS_{Total}$ = variability of *every* observation around the grand mean $\bar{x}$.
2. **$SS_A$** captures how much the *row means* deviate from the grand mean (averaged across B).
3. **$SS_B$** captures how much the *column means* deviate from the grand mean (averaged across A).
4. **$SS_{AB}$** captures the extra variability *not* explained by additive row + column effects — it measures the **non-parallelism** of the cell means.
5. **$SS_{Error}$** captures the within-cell, replicate-to-replicate random noise.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used in formulas below:**
> $T_{ij.}$ = cell total, $T_{i..}$ = row total, $T_{.j.}$ = column total, $G$ = grand total, $CF$ = correction factor.

| # | Quantity | Computational Formula | Degrees of Freedom |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Correction Factor | $CF = \dfrac{G^2}{abn}$ | — |
| 2 | $SS_{Total}$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{i,j,k} x_{ijk}^2 - CF$ | $abn - 1$ |
| 3 | $SS_A$ (Rows) | $\dfrac{1}{bn}\sum_{i} T_{i..}^2 - CF$ | $a - 1$ |
| 4 | $SS_B$ (Columns) | $\dfrac{1}{an}\sum_{j} T_{.j.}^2 - CF$ | $b - 1$ |
| 5 | $SS_{Cells}$ | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i,j} T_{ij.}^2 - CF$ | $ab - 1$ |
| 6 | $SS_{AB}$ (Interaction) | $SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ |
| 7 | $SS_{Error}$ | $SS_{Total} - SS_{Cells}$ | $ab(n-1)$ |
| 8 | $MS_A$ | $SS_A / (a-1)$ | — |
| 9 | $MS_B$ | $SS_B / (b-1)$ | — |
| 10 | $MS_{AB}$ | $SS_{AB} / [(a-1)(b-1)]$ | — |
| 11 | $MS_E$ | $SS_E / [ab(n-1)]$ | — |
| 12 | $F_A$ | $MS_A / MS_E$ | — |
| 13 | $F_B$ | $MS_B / MS_E$ | — |
| 14 | $F_{AB}$ | $MS_{AB} / MS_E$ | — |

## 2.3 Decision Rule

For a chosen significance level $\alpha$ (typically **0.05** in KTU Six Sigma problems):

> **Reject $H_0$** for a given effect if the computed $F$-ratio exceeds the critical value $F_{\alpha, \, df_{effect}, \, df_{error}}$ **OR** if the corresponding $p$-value is less than $\alpha$.

## 2.4 Degrees of Freedom — Quick Sanity Check

The df partition must satisfy:

$$(a-1) + (b-1) + (a-1)(b-1) + ab(n-1) = abn - 1$$

This identity always holds and is a **common KTU valuation check** — a 1-mark shortcut question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Six Sigma and Lean Manufacturing:

- **Process Optimisation:** Identify the best (Temperature, Catalyst) combination.
- **Robust Design (Taguchi):** An $L_9$ orthogonal array generates data that *is* a two-way ANOVA.
- **Quality Control:** Quantify how much of the process variation is explained by machine, shift, batch, supplier, etc.
- **Confirmation Run:** The fitted model predicts optimal settings, which are then physically verified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Check Required (KTU frequently tests this):**
> 1. Normality of residuals
> 2. Homogeneity (equality) of variances across cells
> 3. Independence of observations
> 4. Additivity of effects (no severe interaction masking the model)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Example)

## 3.1 The Problem Statement

A Six Sigma Black Belt project investigates the **Yield (%)** of a chemical process. Two factors are studied:
- **Factor A (Temperature):** Low (1), Medium (2), High (3) → $a = 3$ levels
- **Factor B (Catalyst):** Type-1 (1), Type-2 (2) → $b = 2$ levels
- **Replicates per cell:** $n = 3$
- **Total observations:** $N = 3 \times 2 \times 3 = 18$

The data is:

| Temperature \ Catalyst | Type-1 | Type-2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** | 50, 52, 48 | 56, 58, 54 |
| **Medium** | 60, 62, 58 | 62, 64, 60 |
| **High** | 70, 72, 68 | 64, 66, 62 |

Conduct a Two-way ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$ and interpret the results.

## 3.2 Step 1 — Compute Marginal and Cell Totals

**Cell totals $T_{ij.}$:**

$$T_{11.} = 50+52+48 = 150, \quad T_{12.} = 56+58+54 = 168$$
$$T_{21.} = 60+62+58 = 180, \quad T_{22.} = 62+64+60 = 186$$
$$T_{31.} = 70+72+68 = 210, \quad T_{32.} = 64+66+62 = 192$$

**Row totals $T_{i..}$:**

$$T_{1..} = 150+168 = 318, \quad T_{2..} = 180+186 = 366, \quad T_{3..} = 210+192 = 402$$

**Column totals $T_{.j.}$:**

$$T_{.1.} = 150+180+210 = 540, \quad T_{.2.} = 168+186+192 = 546$$

**Grand total $G$:**

$$G = 318+366+402 = 1086$$

## 3.3 Step 2 — Correction Factor and $SS_{Total}$

**Correction Factor:**

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{abn} = \frac{1086^2}{18} = \frac{1{,}179{,}396}{18} = 65{,}522$$

**Sum of squares of every observation:**

$$\sum x_{ijk}^2 = 2500+2704+2304+3136+3364+2916+3600+3844+3364$$
$$+3844+4096+3600+4900+5184+4624+4096+4356+3844 = 66{,}276$$

**Total sum of squares:**

$$SS_{Total} = 66{,}276 - 65{,}522 = 754$$

## 3.4 Step 3 — $SS_A$ (Factor A — Temperature)

Using the row totals:

$$\frac{1}{bn}\sum_i T_{i..}^2 = \frac{1}{6}\left(318^2 + 366^2 + 402^2\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{6}(101{,}124 + 133{,}956 + 161{,}604) = \frac{396{,}684}{6} = 66{,}114$$

$$SS_A = 66{,}114 - 65{,}522 = 592$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — $SS_B$ (Factor B — Catalyst)

Using the column totals:

$$\frac{1}{an}\sum_j T_{.j.}^2 = \frac{1}{9}\left(540^2 + 546^2\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{9}(291{,}600 + 298{,}116) = \frac{589{,}716}{9} = 65{,}524$$

$$SS_B = 65{,}524 - 65{,}522 = 2$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — $SS_{Cells}$ and $SS_{AB}$ (Interaction)

Using the cell totals:

$$\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i,j} T_{ij.}^2 = \frac{1}{3}\left(150^2 + 168^2 + 180^2 + 186^2 + 210^2 + 192^2\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}(22{,}500 + 28{,}224 + 32{,}400 + 34{,}596 + 44{,}100 + 36{,}864)$$

$$= \frac{198{,}684}{3} = 66{,}228$$

**Interaction sum of squares:**

$$SS_{AB} = SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B = 66{,}228 - 65{,}522 - 592 - 2 = 112$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Algebraic check:** $SS_{Cells} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} \Rightarrow 706 = 592 + 2 + 112$ ✓

## 3.7 Step 6 — $SS_{Error}$

$$SS_{Error} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Cells} = 754 - 706 = 48$$

**Verification by direct cell-wise calculation:** Each of the 6 cells has within-cell SS equal to $(52-50)^2 + (48-50)^2 = 8$. With 6 cells:

$$SS_{Error} = 6 \times 8 = 48 \checkmark$$

## 3.8 Step 7 — Build the ANOVA Table

| Source of Variation | SS | df | MS | F-ratio | $F_{0.05, \cdot}$ | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Factor A (Temperature) | 592 | 2 | 296.00 | 74.00 | 3.89 | **Significant** |
| Factor B (Catalyst) | 2 | 1 | 2.00 | 0.50 | 4.75 | Not Significant |
| Interaction A×B | 112 | 2 | 56.00 | 14.00 | 3.89 | **Significant** |
| **Error** | 48 | 12 | 4.00 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | 754 | 17 | — | — | — | — |

**Sample computation for $MS_A$ and $F_A$:**

$$MS_A = \frac{SS_A}{a-1} = \frac{592}{2} = 296$$

$$F_A = \frac{MS_A}{MS_E} = \frac{296}{4} = 74.00$$

**Sample computation for $MS_{AB}$ and $F_{AB}$:**

$$MS_{AB} = \frac{SS_{AB}}{(a-1)(b-1)} = \frac{112}{2 \times 1} = 56$$

$$F_{AB} = \frac{MS_{AB}}{MS_E} = \frac{56}{4} = 14.00$$

## 3.9 Step 8 — Interpretation (Engineering Verdict)

1. **Factor A (Temperature)** is highly significant ($F = 74 \gg 3.89$). Temperature drives yield.
2. **Factor B (Catalyst Type)** is *not* significant ($F = 0.5 < 4.75$). The average effect of the catalyst is negligible.
3. **Interaction A×B** is significant ($F = 14 \gg 3.89$). The catalyst's effect **depends on** the temperature — they cannot be tuned independently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Recommendation:**
> Because the interaction is significant, we *must* look at cell means, not just row/column averages. From the data: **High Temperature + Type-1 Catalyst** gives the highest mean yield (70%), whereas **High Temperature + Type-2 Catalyst** drops to 64%. The optimal setting is **High Temp + Catalyst Type-1**, and a confirmation run should be performed.

## 3.10 Step 9 — Coefficient of Determination $R^2$

The proportion of variability explained by the model:

$$R^2 = \frac{SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB}}{SS_{Total}} = \frac{592 + 2 + 112}{754} = \frac{706}{754} = 0.9363 \approx 93.63\%$$

> The full two-way model explains **93.63%** of the variation in yield — a strong model fit, suitable for process optimisation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Two-way ANOVA

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **flow of computation** from raw data to engineering decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Data x_ijk in a x b grid] --> B1[Compute Cell Totals T_ij.]
    A0 --> B2[Compute Row Totals T_i..]
    A0 --> B3[Compute Column Totals T_j.]
    A0 --> B4[Compute Grand Total G]
    B4 --> C1[Correction Factor CF = G^2 / abn]
    B1 --> C2[SS_Cells = sum T_ij.^2 / n - CF]
    B2 --> C3[SS_A = sum T_i..^2 / bn - CF]
    B3 --> C4[SS_B = sum T_j.^2 / an - CF]
    A0 --> C5[SS_Total = sum x^2 - CF]
    C2 --> D1[SS_AB = SS_Cells - SS_A - SS_B]
    C5 --> D2[SS_Error = SS_Total - SS_Cells]
    C3 --> E1[MS_A = SS_A / df_A]
    C4 --> E2[MS_B = SS_B / df_B]
    D1 --> E3[MS_AB = SS_AB / df_AB]
    D2 --> E4[MS_E = SS_E / df_E]
    E1 --> F1[F_A = MS_A / MS_E]
    E2 --> F2[F_B = MS_B / MS_E]
    E3 --> F3[F_AB = MS_AB / MS_E]
    F1 --> G1{Compare F to F_critical}
    F2 --> G2{Compare F to F_critical}
    F3 --> G3{Compare F to F_critical}
    G1 --> H1[Decision on Factor A]
    G2 --> H2[Decision on Factor B]
    G3 --> H3[Decision on Interaction]
    H3 --> H4{Is interaction significant?}
    H4 -- Yes --> H5[Report Cell Means, ignore marginals]
    H4 -- No --> H6[Report Marginal Means of significant factors]
    H1 --> H7[Optimal Factor Level]
    H2 --> H7
    H5 --> H7
    H6 --> H7
    H7 --> H8[Confirmation Run in Pilot Plant]
```

## 4.2 Interaction Plot — Conceptual Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block illustrates how an **interaction plot** visually distinguishes *additive* from *non-additive* behaviour, which is the single most important diagnostic in two-way ANOVA.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[ADDITIVE MODEL - No Interaction]
        direction LR
        X1[Low Temp, Cat-1: 50] --> X2[Low Temp, Cat-2: 52]
        Y1[High Temp, Cat-1: 70] --> Y2[High Temp, Cat-2: 72]
        X1 -.parallel lines.-> Y1
        X2 -.parallel lines.-> Y2
    end
    subgraph S2[INTERACTION PRESENT - Lines Cross or Diverge]
        direction LR
        P1[Low Temp, Cat-1: 50] --> P2[Low Temp, Cat-2: 56]
        Q1[High Temp, Cat-1: 70] --> Q2[High Temp, Cat-2: 64]
        P1 -.steep slope.-> Q1
        P2 -.negative slope.-> Q2
    end
    S1 --> OUT[Best at High Temp - either catalyst]
    S2 --> OUT2[Best is High Temp AND Cat-1 only]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for the Three Simultaneous F-Tests

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Enter F-values and critical F] --> Q1{F_A > F_crit_A?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Factor A is significant]
    Q1 -- No --> A2[Factor A is not significant]
    START --> Q2{F_B > F_crit_B?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> B1[Factor B is significant]
    Q2 -- No --> B2[Factor B is not significant]
    START --> Q3{F_AB > F_crit_AB?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> I1[Interaction A x B is significant]
    Q3 -- No --> I2[No interaction present]
    I1 --> R1[Model is multiplicative: report cell means]
    I2 --> R2[Model is additive: marginal means are valid]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the mathematical model of a Two-way ANOVA with interaction. Explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$$

- $\mu$ → overall (grand) mean response
- $\alpha_i$ → main effect of the $i$-th level of Factor A, with constraint $\sum_i \alpha_i = 0$
- $\beta_j$ → main effect of the $j$-th level of Factor B, with constraint $\sum_j \beta_j = 0$
- $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ → interaction effect of level $i$ of A with level $j$ of B
- $\epsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ → random error term, i.i.d. normal with zero mean and constant variance

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is Two-way ANOVA preferred over two separate One-way ANOVAs in Six Sigma projects?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Interaction Detection:** Two separate one-way ANOVAs cannot estimate the A×B interaction effect, which is often the *most* important finding in a DOE study.
2. **Error Pooling:** Two-way ANOVA pools the within-cell error across all $ab$ cells, giving a more precise estimate of $\sigma^2$ with higher degrees of freedom.
3. **Efficiency:** A single experiment collects all required data; no need to run two independent experiments.
4. **Family-wise Error Control:** It avoids inflating the Type-I error rate that occurs with multiple testing.
5. **Variance Partitioning:** It explicitly quantifies *how much* variation is explained by each factor and by the interaction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting the constraint $(a-1) + (b-1) + (a-1)(b-1) + ab(n-1) = abn - 1$ — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. Using $ab$ instead of $abn$ in the Correction Factor → cascading wrong SS values.
> 3. Reporting the **marginal** means when the interaction is significant (this is a conceptual mistake worth **3 marks**).
> 4. Failing to write the verbal engineering **conclusion** — KTU awards 2 marks purely for a clear "best combination" statement.
> 5. Not showing intermediate step calculations for $F$-ratios.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A study is conducted to determine the effect of **three concentrations of a reagent (5%, 10%, 15%)** and **two solvents (S1, S2)** on the extraction efficiency (%). Three replicates are run. Data:

| Conc \ Solvent | S1 | S2 |
|---|---|---|
| **5%** | 25, 27, 23 | 30, 32, 28 |
| **10%** | 35, 37, 33 | 38, 40, 36 |
| **15%** | 40, 42, 38 | 32, 34, 30 |

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Set up the Two-way ANOVA table. State $H_0$ and $H_1$ for each effect.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Test all three effects at $\alpha = 0.05$ and recommend the optimal operating condition.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Step 1 — Marginal Totals**

Cell totals: $T_{11}=75, T_{12}=90, T_{21}=105, T_{22}=114, T_{31}=120, T_{32}=96$

Row totals: $T_{1..} = 165, T_{2..} = 219, T_{3..} = 216$

Column totals: $T_{.1.} = 300, T_{.2.} = 300$

Grand total: $G = 165+219+216 = 600$ ⇒ $CF = \dfrac{600^2}{18} = 20{,}000$

$\sum x^2 = 625+729+529+900+961+784+1225+1369+1089+1444+1600+1764+1296+1600+1449+1024+1156+900 = 20{,}844$

$$SS_{Total} = 20{,}844 - 20{,}000 = 844$$

$$SS_A = \frac{1}{6}(165^2 + 219^2 + 216^2) - 20{,}000 = \frac{114{,}042}{6} - 20{,}000 = 19{,}007 - 20{,}000 = 993 \text{ (error in arithmetic — recheck below)}$$

> *Re-evaluating:* $165^2 = 27{,}225$; $219^2 = 47{,}961$; $216^2 = 46{,}656$. Sum $= 121{,}842$. $\dfrac{121{,}842}{6} = 20{,}307$. So $SS_A = 20{,}307 - 20{,}000 = 307$.

$$SS_B = \frac{1}{9}(300^2 + 300^2) - 20{,}000 = \frac{180{,}000}{9} - 20{,}000 = 20{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 0$$

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{1}{3}(75^2+90^2+105^2+114^2+120^2+96^2) - 20{,}000$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}(5625+8100+11{,}025+12{,}996+14{,}400+9216) - 20{,}000 = \frac{61{,}362}{3} - 20{,}000 = 20{,}454 - 20{,}000 = 454$$

$$SS_{AB} = 454 - 307 - 0 = 147$$

$$SS_{Error} = 844 - 454 = 390$$

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | $F_{0.05}$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Concentration (A) | 307 | 2 | 153.50 | 4.72 | 3.89 | Significant |
| Solvent (B) | 0 | 1 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 4.75 | Not Significant |
| Interaction A×B | 147 | 2 | 73.50 | 2.26 | 3.89 | Not Significant |
| Error | 390 | 12 | 32.50 | — | — | — |
| Total | 844 | 17 | — | — | — | — |

**Part (b) Conclusions:**

- **Concentration** is significant — increasing concentration from 5% to 15% generally improves efficiency.
- **Solvent** is *not* significant — the choice of solvent has no meaningful effect on average.
- **Interaction** is *not* significant — the effect of concentration is consistent across both solvents.

**Engineering Recommendation:** Use **15% concentration** with **either solvent** (since B is insignificant, choose the cheaper solvent to minimise cost). Expected mean efficiency = $\dfrac{120+96}{6} = 36\%$.

$R^2 = \dfrac{454}{844} = 0.538$ — about **53.8%** of variability is explained; the model is adequate but replication and tighter process control are advised.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

In a Taguchi $L_4$ orthogonal-array confirmation study, two factors — **Pressure (Low, High)** and **Feed Rate (Slow, Fast)** — are tested on output thickness (mm) with **4 replicates** per cell. Data:

| Pressure \ Feed | Slow | Fast |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** | 10, 12, 11, 13 | 14, 16, 15, 17 |
| **High** | 18, 20, 19, 21 | 12, 14, 13, 15 |

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Construct the ANOVA table and verify that the $df$ partition is consistent.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Discuss whether a significant interaction exists and what this implies for the **optimal process setting**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Step 1 — Totals**

Cell totals: $T_{11}=46, T_{12}=62, T_{21}=78, T_{22}=54$

Row totals: $T_{1..}=108, T_{2..}=132$

Column totals: $T_{.1.}=124, T_{.2.}=116$

Grand total: $G = 240$ ⇒ $N = 2 \times 2 \times 4 = 16$ ⇒ $CF = \dfrac{240^2}{16} = 3{,}600$

$\sum x^2 = 100+144+121+169+196+256+225+289+324+400+361+441+144+196+169+225 = 3{,}760$

$$SS_{Total} = 3{,}760 - 3{,}600 = 160$$

$$SS_A = \frac{1}{8}(108^2 + 132^2) - 3{,}600 = \frac{29{,}040}{8} - 3{,}600 = 3{,}630 - 3{,}600 = 30$$

$$SS_B = \frac{1}{8}(124^2 + 116^2) - 3{,}600 = \frac{28{,}192}{8} - 3{,}600 = 3{,}524 - 3{,}600 = -76 \;\;\; \text{(negative — recheck)}$$

> *Recheck:* $124^2 = 15{,}376$, $116^2 = 13{,}456$, sum $= 28{,}832$, $\dfrac{28{,}832}{8} = 3{,}604$. So $SS_B = 3{,}604 - 3{,}600 = 4$. ✓

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{1}{4}(46^2+62^2+78^2+54^2) - 3{,}600 = \frac{16{,}820}{4} - 3{,}600 = 4{,}205 - 3{,}600 = 605 \text{ (recheck)}$$

> *Recheck:* $46^2=2{,}116$, $62^2=3{,}844$, $78^2=6{,}084$, $54^2=2{,}916$, sum $= 14{,}960$, $\dfrac{14{,}960}{4} = 3{,}740$. So $SS_{Cells} = 3{,}740 - 3{,}600 = 140$.

$$SS_{AB} = 140 - 30 - 4 = 106$$

$$SS_{Error} = 160 - 140 = 20$$

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | $F_{0.05}$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure (A) | 30 | 1 | 30.00 | 15.00 | 4.75 | Significant |
| Feed Rate (B) | 4 | 1 | 4.00 | 2.00 | 4.75 | Not Significant |
| Interaction A×B | 106 | 1 | 106.00 | 53.00 | 4.75 | **Highly Significant** |
| Error | 20 | 12 | 1.67 | — | — | — |
| Total | 160 | 15 | — | — | — | — |

**Degrees of freedom check:** $1 + 1 + 1 + 12 = 15 = abn - 1 = 16 - 1$ ✓

**Part (b) Discussion:**

- The interaction is the **dominant** effect ($F = 53$), far larger than the main effects.
- Looking at cell means:
  - Low P / Slow F: $46/4 = 11.5$
  - Low P / Fast F: $62/4 = 15.5$
  - **High P / Slow F: $78/4 = 19.5$ ← Maximum**
  - High P / Fast F: $54/4 = 13.5$

- The "lines" cross on an interaction plot: Pressure helps *only when Feed is slow*. With fast feed, high pressure actually *reduces* thickness.

**Engineering Verdict:** Set **Pressure = High** and **Feed Rate = Slow** for maximum output thickness. Do **not** interpret the main effects in isolation because the interaction dominates the response surface.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Pin This List**

- [x] **Two-way ANOVA** tests **two** factors and their **interaction** simultaneously.
- [x] The model is $y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$.
- [x] Three null hypotheses: $H_0^{(\alpha)}$, $H_0^{(\beta)}$, $H_0^{(\alpha\beta)}$.
- [x] $SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_{Error}$ — *the* fundamental identity.
- [x] **Correction Factor** $CF = G^2/(abn)$ is the **most common** arithmetic error point.
- [x] $SS_{AB} = SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B$ — the **shortcut** formula.
- [x] $SS_{Error} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Cells}$.
- [x] Degrees of freedom: $a-1, \; b-1, \; (a-1)(b-1), \; ab(n-1), \; abn-1$.
- [x] Always compute $F = MS_{effect}/MS_{error}$, **never** the reverse.
- [x] If the **interaction is significant**, you must interpret the **cell means**, not the marginals.
- [x] If the interaction is **not** significant, the model is **additive** and you may report marginal means of the significant main effects.
- [x] $R^2 = (SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB}) / SS_{Total}$ measures model fit.
- [x] Critical F-values required: $F_{\alpha, (a-1), ab(n-1)}$; $F_{\alpha, (b-1), ab(n-1)}$; $F_{\alpha, (a-1)(b-1), ab(n-1)}$.
- [x] Assumptions: **normality**, **homoscedasticity** (equal cell variances), **independence**, **additivity**.
- [x] Tukey's 1-df test for non-additivity is the KTU-recommended additivity diagnostic.
- [x] If the $F_{AB}$ ratio is *small* and not significant, pool $SS_{AB}$ with $SS_E$ for a *more powerful* test of main effects (advanced KTU 14-mark trick).
- [x] Tukey's HSD / Bonferroni can be used *post-hoc* to identify which specific level means differ.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Pitfall:** Never forget to **state the practical conclusion** in words (e.g., "The optimal yield is achieved at…"). KTU examiners reserve **2 marks** exclusively for a clear engineering recommendation — separate from the statistical computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive, and structured analytical methodology used in Six Sigma (specifically the **DMAIC – Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control** framework, deployed mainly in the *Improve* phase) to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and eliminate potential failure modes in a **product, process, design, or service** before they occur.
