The methodology quantifies risk through the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**, a multiplicative metric that ranks failures by combining three independent ratings: **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detection (D)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** FMEA is a high-weightage topic. Examiners frequently ask for the *definition*, the *RPN formula*, the *three rating scales*, and the *step-by-step procedure* for conducting an FMEA. Memorize the RPN formula and the 10-point rating scales — they appear almost every semester.

## 1.2 Types of FMEA (KTU Classification)

| Type | Full Form | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **DFMEA** | Design FMEA | Product/Component Design Stage |
| **PFMEA** | Process FMEA | Manufacturing/Assembly Process |
| **SFMEA** | System FMEA | Subsystem / System-level Analysis |
| **SFMEA** | Software FMEA | Software/Hardware Integration |
| **Service FMEA** | Service FMEA | Transactional/Service Processes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Car Pre-Trip Inspection**
> Imagine you are about to take a long road trip in your car. Before leaving, you don't wait for the tire to burst on the highway. Instead, you **proactively** walk around the car and think:
> - *What could go wrong?* → Tire puncture (Failure Mode)
> - *What would happen if it did?* → Car skids, accident (Effect)
> - *How bad would it be?* → Life-threatening (Severity = 9)
> - *How likely is it?* → Once every 5 years (Occurrence = 3)
> - *Would I notice it coming?* → Hard to predict (Detection = 7)
>
> **RPN = 9 × 3 × 7 = 189 → HIGH RISK → Check tire pressure NOW!**
>
> This is exactly FMEA — a **preventive "what-if" audit** instead of a reactive autopsy.

## 1.4 Origin & Standard Reference

- Originally developed by the **U.S. Military** in the 1940s (Military Procedure MIL-P-1629).
- Later formalized by the **Automotive Industry Action Group (AIAG)** and the **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** in the **J1739** standard.
- Now a global standard governed by **IEC 60812** and **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of FMEA Risk Rating

FMEA evaluates every identified failure mode using **three orthogonal 1-to-10 rating scales**:

### A. Severity (S) — "How bad is the failure?"
Measures the seriousness of the **consequence/effect** on the customer, end-user, or downstream process.

### B. Occurrence (O) — "How frequently does it happen?"
Measures the **likelihood** that the failure mode will actually occur during the expected life of the process/product.

### C. Detection (D) — "How likely are we to catch it before reaching the customer?"
Measures the ability of the **current process controls** to detect the failure mode **before** it propagates to the customer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students invert the Detection scale. **Remember:** Detection = 1 means *we almost certainly detect it* (LOW risk), and Detection = 10 means *we cannot detect it at all* (HIGH risk). It is an **inverse** scale relative to S and O.

## 2.2 The Core RPN Equation

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- $S \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$
- $O \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$
- $D \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$

**Theoretical Range:** $1 \le \text{RPN} \le 1000$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Symbol | Range | Direction | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Severity | $S$ | $1 - 10$ | Higher = Worse | Impact on customer |
| Occurrence | $O$ | $1 - 10$ | Higher = Worse | Failure frequency |
| Detection | $D$ | $1 - 10$ | Higher = Worse | Inability to detect |
| **Risk Priority Number** | $\text{RPN}$ | $1 - 1000$ | Higher = Worse | Composite risk score |
| **Action Priority (AP)** | $\text{AP}$ | Low / Med / High | Higher = Worse | New AIAG-VDA method |
| S × O (Criticality) | $C$ | $1 - 100$ | Higher = Worse | Pre-Detection severity |
| $\text{Reduction \%}$ | $\Delta$ | $0 - 100\%$ | Higher = Better | Improvement metric |

## 2.4 Standardized Rating Tables (Memorize These for KTU Exam)

### Severity (S) Scale
| Rank | Effect | Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | None | No effect |
| 2–3 | Minor | Slight inconvenience |
| 4–6 | Moderate | Loss of primary function |
| 7–8 | High | Major loss of function |
| 9 | Serious | Safety issue, with warning |
| 10 | Hazardous | Safety issue, no warning |

### Occurrence (O) Scale
| Rank | Failure Rate | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Very Low | Failure unlikely |
| 5 | Moderate | Occasional failures |
| 7 | High | Frequent failures |
| 10 | Very High | Failure almost inevitable |

### Detection (D) Scale
| Rank | Detectability | Control Strength |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Almost Certain | Highly reliable automated control |
| 5 | Moderate | Manual control with some reliability |
| 7 | Low | Weak control method |
| 10 | Absolute Uncertainty | No known control mechanism |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Mandatory for ISO/TS 16949 / IATF 16949 compliance.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** AS9100 standard relies heavily on FMEA.
- **Medical Devices:** FDA mandates FMEA in the design verification stage.
- **Manufacturing Six Sigma:** Drives the *Improve* phase of DMAIC projects.
- **Software Engineering:** SFMEA for mission-critical embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RPN Numerical Walk-Through (Exhaustive Calculation)

### Worked Example: Bolt Tightening Process

**Scenario:** In a CNC assembly cell, a critical bolt is being tightened using a torque wrench. We need to evaluate the failure mode *"Bolt under-torqued"* through a complete FMEA calculation.

**Step 1 — Identify the Failure Mode**
The failure mode is **"Bolt under-torqued"** (the bolt is not tightened to the specified torque value).

**Step 2 — Identify the Effect**
The downstream effect is: *The assembly loosens under vibration during operation, causing the bracket to detach and the machine to halt. Minor safety hazard to nearby operator.*

**Step 3 — Assign Severity (S)**
Because there is a *minor safety hazard*, we assign:
$$S = 8$$

**Step 4 — Assign Occurrence (O)**
Historical shop-floor data shows this occurs approximately **once every 200 assemblies**, indicating moderate frequency:
$$O = 5$$

**Step 5 — Assign Detection (D)**
The current control is **only a visual check** by the operator. There is no automatic torque logging or post-process audit. A visual check misses about 70% of under-torque events:
$$D = 7$$

**Step 6 — Compute RPN**
$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

Substituting the values:
$$\text{RPN} = 8 \times 5 \times 7$$

Performing the multiplication step by step:
$$8 \times 5 = 40$$
$$40 \times 7 = 280$$

Therefore:
$$\text{RPN} = 280$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the RPN**

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Threshold:** A common Six Sigma decision rule is:
> - $\text{RPN} > 200$ → Mandatory Corrective Action
> - $\text{RPN} \in [100, 200]$ → Recommended Action
> - $\text{RPN} < 100$ → Acceptable Risk (monitor)

With $\text{RPN} = 280 > 200$, this failure mode **mandates immediate corrective action**.

**Step 8 — Propose Action & Recompute**

**Action:** Install a *torque-and-angle sensor* with automated SPC logging and a rejection signal. This sensor will catch 99% of under-torque events.

After implementing the action:
- $S = 8$ (severity unchanged — cannot redesign bolt easily)
- $O = 5$ (occurrence unchanged — physical defect rate is the same)
- $D = 2$ (sensor detects almost every defect)

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 8 \times 5 \times 2 = 80$$

**Step 9 — Compute Risk Reduction**
$$\Delta\text{RPN} = \text{RPN}_{\text{old}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{new}}$$
$$\Delta\text{RPN} = 280 - 80 = 200$$

Percentage Reduction:
$$\%\,\text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta\text{RPN}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{old}}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting values:
$$\%\,\text{Reduction} = \frac{200}{280} \times 100\%$$

Performing the division:
$$\frac{200}{280} = \frac{20}{28} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143$$

Multiplying by 100:
$$\%\,\text{Reduction} \approx 71.43\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** The corrective action reduced the Risk Priority Number by **71.43%**, bringing the residual risk from the "mandatory action" zone down to the "acceptable" zone. The Six Sigma Black Belt / Green Belt would document this in the *Control* phase of DMAIC.

## 3.2 Criticality vs. RPN — An Analytical Distinction

Some industries also compute the **Criticality (C)** index, which excludes Detection because the failure has already occurred by the time we observe the effect:

$$C = S \times O$$

For our example:
$$C = 8 \times 5 = 40$$

This metric is particularly valuable in aerospace and military FMEA where detection systems are physically absent (e.g., one-shot missions).

## 3.3 Aggregate Project RPN

For an entire FMEA worksheet covering $n$ failure modes:
$$\text{RPN}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \times O_i \times D_i$$

This aggregate is used in the *Control* phase to compute the **Process Sigma Level** improvement achieved by the project.

## 3.4 Comparative Evaluation Table (Hypothetical Worksheet)

| # | Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bolt under-torqued | 8 | 5 | 7 | **280** | Install torque sensor |
| 2 | Bolt over-torqued | 9 | 3 | 6 | 162 | Use torque limiter |
| 3 | Wrong bolt inserted | 9 | 2 | 4 | 72 | Vision system |
| 4 | Bolt missing entirely | 10 | 1 | 3 | 30 | Poka-Yoke fixture |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ranking Logic:** Row 1 has the highest RPN = 280 → prioritized first. Row 4 is naturally mitigated by the inherent impossibility (Occurrence = 1) → lowest priority. This is exactly how FMEA directs resource allocation in real Six Sigma projects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 10-Step FMEA Procedure (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Project Initiation]) --> s1[Step 1: Define Scope and Boundaries]
    s1 --> s2[Step 2: Assemble Cross-Functional Team]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Identify Functions of System or Process]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4: Identify Potential Failure Modes]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5: Determine Effects and Causes]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6: Assign Severity S, Occurrence O, Detection D Scores]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7: Calculate Risk Priority Number RPN]
    s7 --> s8{Step 8: RPN Threshold Decision}
    s8 -->|RPN High| s9[Step 9: Recommend Corrective Actions]
    s8 -->|RPN Acceptable| s11[Step 11: Document and Monitor]
    s9 --> s10[Step 10: Implement Actions and Recalculate RPN]
    s10 --> s8
    s11 --> end([Project Closure and Control Phase])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The diamond node `s8` represents the **decision branch** that drives the iterative nature of FMEA. The arrow returning from `s10` back to `s8` signifies the **re-verification loop** — once an action is implemented, the team re-evaluates the failure mode's RPN to confirm it has dropped below the action threshold.

## 4.2 FMEA Functional Architecture — Inputs, Process, Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Process Map] --> core[FMEA Engine]
    inB[Customer Requirements] --> core
    inC[Historical Data and Defect Logs] --> core
    inD[Engineering Knowledge] --> core
    core --> outA[RPN Ranked Worksheet]
    core --> outB[Action Priority Table]
    core --> outC[Residual Risk Register]
    core --> outD[Updated Control Plan]
    outA --> dmaic[DMAIC Improve Phase]
    outB --> dmaic
    outC --> dmaic
    outD --> dmaic
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The four input streams (*Process Map, Customer Requirements, Historical Data, Engineering Knowledge*) are combined inside the FMEA Engine to produce four output artifacts, which then feed directly into the *Improve* and *Control* phases of the broader DMAIC cycle. This is why FMEA is called the **analytical backbone** of Six Sigma process improvement projects.

## 4.3 RPN Calculation Flow (Detailed Sub-Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fm[Identified Failure Mode] --> eff[Determine Effect on Customer]
    eff --> s_assess{Score Severity}
    s_assign[Assign S between 1 and 10] --> s_assess
    s_assess --> o_assess{Score Occurrence}
    o_assign[Assign O between 1 and 10] --> o_assess
    o_assess --> d_assess{Score Detection}
    d_assign[Assign D between 1 and 10] --> d_assess
    d_assess --> multiply[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    multiply --> rank[Sort Failure Modes by Descending RPN]
    rank --> act{Is RPN Greater Than 200?}
    act -->|Yes| action[Trigger Corrective Action Workflow]
    act -->|No| monitor[Add to Residual Risk Log]
    action --> redo[Implement and Recalculate]
    redo --> multiply
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA). Mention any two types of FMEA.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

FMEA is a systematic, proactive, and structured analytical technique used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes in a product, process, design, or service before they reach the customer. Each failure mode is rated on **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detection (D)** on a scale of 1 to 10, and the composite **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is calculated as:

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

The two main types of FMEA are:
1. **DFMEA (Design FMEA)** — applied during the product/component design phase to eliminate design-induced failures.
2. **PFMEA (Process FMEA)** — applied to manufacturing and assembly processes to eliminate process-induced failures.

---

### **Q2. List the three rating parameters used in FMEA and state what each one measures.**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The three rating parameters in FMEA are:

| Parameter | Symbol | What It Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Severity** | $S$ | The seriousness of the failure effect on the customer or end-user |
| **Occurrence** | $O$ | The likelihood or frequency of the failure mode occurring |
| **Detection** | $D$ | The probability that the existing process controls will detect the failure before it reaches the customer |

Each parameter is rated on a **scale of 1 to 10**, with **10** indicating the worst case (highest severity, highest occurrence, lowest detectability).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Module Internal Choice means you will be given either **Question A or Question B** to attempt — you do not write both.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Process FMEA. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | 7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The step-by-step procedure for conducting a Process FMEA is as follows:

**Step 1: Define the Scope and Boundaries**
Identify the process, sub-process, or system to be analyzed. Clearly state what is included and excluded from the scope.

**Step 2: Assemble the Cross-Functional Team**
Form a team including the Six Sigma Black Belt/Green Belt, process engineer, quality engineer, operator, and customer representative.

**Step 3: Identify the Functions of the Process**
List every function the process must perform. For example: *"Tighten bolt to 50 Nm torque."*

**Step 4: Identify Potential Failure Modes**
For each function, list all the ways it can fail. Example: *"Bolt under-torqued"*, *"Bolt over-torqued"*, *"Wrong bolt inserted"*.

**Step 5: Determine Effects and Causes**
For each failure mode, identify the **effect** on the customer and the **potential root cause**.

**Step 6: Assign S, O, D Ratings**
Score each failure mode on the three 1-to-10 scales.

**Step 7: Calculate RPN**
$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 8: Prioritize and Decide Action Threshold**
Typically, RPN > 200 mandates corrective action.

**Step 9: Recommend Corrective Actions**
For high-RPN failure modes, propose countermeasures targeting **occurrence reduction** (e.g., poka-yoke) or **detection improvement** (e.g., automated sensor).

**Step 10: Implement Actions and Recalculate RPN**
After implementation, recompute RPN to confirm risk reduction.

#### Part (b) — A CNC milling process exhibits a failure mode "excessive tool wear". Severity = 9, Occurrence = 6, Detection = 5. Compute the RPN and recommend an action if the threshold is 150. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | 7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Substitute values into the RPN formula**
$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2: Perform the multiplication**
$$\text{RPN} = 9 \times 6 \times 5$$
$$\text{RPN} = 54 \times 5 = 270$$

**Step 3: Compare with threshold**
$$\text{RPN} = 270 > 150 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Corrective Action Required}$$

**Step 4: Recommended Action**

To reduce the RPN below 150, we need to target either **Occurrence** or **Detection** (since Severity is fixed by physics — worn tool is always severe).

| Countermeasure | Target | New Rating | New RPN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Install tool-life sensor | Reduce $D$ | $D = 2$ | $9 \times 6 \times 2 = 108$ |
| Use harder tool material | Reduce $O$ | $O = 3$ | $9 \times 3 \times 5 = 135$ |
| Combine both | Reduce both | $O=3, D=2$ | $9 \times 3 \times 2 = 54$ |

The **combined approach** is the most robust, reducing RPN by **80%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Discuss the advantages and limitations of FMEA as a Six Sigma tool. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of FMEA:**

1. **Proactive Risk Identification** — FMEA identifies failures before they occur, shifting quality management from reactive *inspection* to preventive *design*.
2. **Prioritization of Resources** — The RPN ranking directs engineering effort to the highest-risk failure modes, ensuring optimal ROI.
3. **Cross-Functional Collaboration** — FMEA workshops bring together diverse expertise, breaking down silos between design, manufacturing, and quality teams.
4. **Documentation and Traceability** — Provides a permanent record of risk analysis, supporting ISO 9001, IATF 16949, and AS9100 audits.
5. **Cost Reduction** — Prevents expensive field recalls, warranty claims, and rework.
6. **Continuous Improvement** — FMEA is a living document, updated whenever a process or design changes.

**Limitations of FMEA:**

1. **Subjectivity of Ratings** — The 1-to-10 scales for S, O, D rely on expert judgment, leading to inter-rater variability.
2. **Time-Intensive** — A full FMEA on a complex product may take weeks of team effort.
3. **Does Not Quantify Failure Cost** — RPN is risk-based, not cost-based; two RPNs of equal value may have vastly different financial impact.
4. **Cannot Identify All Failures** — FMEA is a *what-can-go-wrong* analysis, not a *root-cause* analysis; complementary tools like **Fishbone / Ishikawa** are needed.
5. **RPN Limitations** — Two failure modes with identical RPNs may have different S, O, D combinations requiring different strategies; the **Action Priority (AP)** approach (AIAG-VDA 2019) addresses this.
6. **Static Snapshot** — Without re-evaluation, an FMEA can become outdated as the process evolves.

#### Part (b) — In a soldering process, four failure modes were identified with the following data. Compute RPN for each and rank them. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | 7 Marks]`

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold solder joint | 9 | 4 | 6 |
| Solder bridge | 8 | 3 | 5 |
| Insufficient solder | 7 | 6 | 4 |
| Component misalignment | 8 | 2 | 7 |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute RPN for each failure mode**

$$\text{RPN}_1 = 9 \times 4 \times 6 = 216$$
$$\text{RPN}_2 = 8 \times 3 \times 5 = 120$$
$$\text{RPN}_3 = 7 \times 6 \times 4 = 168$$
$$\text{RPN}_4 = 8 \times 2 \times 7 = 112$$

**Step 2: Rank in descending order of RPN**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cold solder joint | **216** |
| 2 | Insufficient solder | **168** |
| 3 | Solder bridge | **120** |
| 4 | Component misalignment | **112** |

**Step 3: Interpretation**
The highest-risk failure mode is **"Cold solder joint"** (RPN = 216) → requires immediate corrective action such as reflow profile optimization or preheat zone improvement. The lowest-risk is *"Component misalignment"* — already well controlled by existing vision systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Detection Scale:** Writing "Detection = 10 means easy to detect" — this is **wrong**. Detection = 10 means *cannot detect at all*.
> 2. **Forgetting to State the Threshold:** When a question gives "threshold = 150", you must explicitly state whether the computed RPN is greater or less than 150 and what action follows.
> 3. **Missing Re-computation:** A complete FMEA answer always shows the *post-action RPN* and computes the percentage reduction.
> 4. **Listing Only Definition:** FMEA questions often ask for *procedure* — students who define FMEA but skip the 10-step process lose 4–5 marks easily.
> 5. **RPN without Ranking:** If multiple failure modes are given, students must always produce a ranked table — not just a list of RPN values.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Full Form:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis — used in the *Improve* phase of DMAIC.
- **Three Ratings:** Severity ($S$), Occurrence ($O$), Detection ($D$), each on a 1-to-10 scale.
- **RPN Formula:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, range **1 to 1000**.
- **Detection is Inverse:** Higher $D$ = worse detection capability = worse.
- **Standard Thresholds:** RPN > 200 = mandatory action; 100–200 = recommended action; < 100 = monitor.
- **FMEA Types:** DFMEA (Design), PFMEA (Process), SFMEA (System/Software), Service FMEA.
- **10-Step Procedure:** Scope → Team → Functions → Failure Modes → Effects/Causes → Rate S/O/D → RPN → Decide → Action → Recompute.
- **Action Priority (AP):** Modern AIAG-VDA (2019) replacement for raw RPN — uses Low/Medium/High categorical ranking.
- **Criticality (C):** $C = S \times O$ — used when Detection is irrelevant (e.g., one-shot aerospace missions).
- **Origin:** U.S. Military MIL-P-1629 (1949); standardized by AIAG/SAE J1739 and IEC 60812.
- **Complementary Tools:** Ishikawa (Fishbone), Pareto Chart, 5-Why, Poka-Yoke.
- **Real-World Use:** Mandatory in automotive (IATF 16949), aerospace (AS9100), and medical device (FDA) industries.
- **Math Tip:** Always show substitution step-by-step in exam answers — KTU examiners award method marks separately from final answer marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improve Phase: Introduction to Design of Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Improve Phase: Introduction to Design of Experiment # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Design of Experiments (DOE)** is a structured, statistically rigorous methodology used in the **Improve Phase** of the DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) cycle to **plan, conduct, and analyze controlled experiments** in order to identify the **causal relationship(s)** between one or more *independent input variables* (factors) and a *dependent output variable* (response), with the objective of optimizing the process response, reducing variation, and establishing robust operating windows. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board-Standard Definition (2-Mark Favorite):** > *"Design of Experiments is a systematic, statistically based methodology for determining the relationship between factors (Xs) affecting a process and the output (Y) of that process. It enables the experimenter to manipulate several input factors simultaneously to discover which factors, and in what combination, produce the optimum output with minimum variability."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Chef" Intuition Imagine you are a chef trying to perfect a *biryani* recipe. You suspect that four things matter most: 1. **Rice soaking time** (Factor A) 2. **Spice quantity** (Factor B) 3. **Cooking flame level** (Factor C) 4. **Dum (steam) duration** (Factor D) The **taste score** (out of 10) is your **Response (Y)**. | Your Approach | Six Sigma DOE Equivalent | |---|---| | You change *only one* ingredient at a time, taste, and start over. (Time-consuming, ignores interactions) | **One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT)** — INEFFICIENT | | You test multiple combinations in parallel: "Soak 30 min + 2 tbsp spice", "Soak 60 min + 3 tbsp spice", etc. | **Full Factorial DOE** — EFFICIENT | | You record taste scores, then use statistics to find which factors actually matter and which *interact* (e.g., high flame + long dum = burnt). | **ANOVA + Interaction Analysis** | **Why DOE in the Improve Phase?** In the **Analyze** phase, you identified the *vital few* Xs (KPIVs - Key Process Input Variables). In the **Improve** phase, you must now confirm those Xs *actually drive* Y, find their **optimal settings**, and detect any **interactions** between factors. **DOE is the engine of the Improve phase** because the Analyze phase tools (regression, hypothesis testing) only describe correlation; DOE *proves causation* under controlled conditions. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):** > - *Analyze Phase tools* (Regression, Correlation, Pareto) → **Identify** potential Xs. > - *Improve Phase tools* (DOE) → **Confirm cause-effect** and **optimize** the Xs to drive Y to target. ## 1.3 Strategic Position of DOE in DMAIC The Improve phase answers three critical questions: $$\text{Question 1 (Cause-Effect):} \quad \text{Which Xs actually cause variation in Y?}$$ $$\text{Question 2 (Optimization):} \quad \text{What level of each X minimizes variation / hits target?}$$ $$\text{Question 3 (Robustness):} \quad \text{Can the optimum hold under noise conditions?}$$ DOE answers **Question 1 and 2** through **designed experiments**, and **Question 3** through **Robust Design / Taguchi methods** (advanced topic, often Part B in KTU). ## 1.4 Core Physical & Statistical Constants | Term | Standard Value / Notation | Remarks | |---|---|---| | **Significance Level (α)** | **0.05** | Industry standard; tunable to 0.01 for stricter tests | | **Power (1 − β)** | **≥ 0.80** | Minimum acceptable statistical power | | **Confidence Level** | **95%** | Conjugate of α = 0.05 | | **Degrees of Freedom (df)** | $df = n - 1$ | Where *n* = number of observations | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** DOE = **D**etermine **O**ptimal **E**ffects → **I**dentify **F**actors **A**nd **L**evels → **R**un **E**xperiments → **A**nalyze **D**ata. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Terminology of DOE (High-Frequency in KTU Papers) Before any experiment is designed, the **Black Belt / Green Belt** must define the following terms unambiguously: | # | Term | Symbol | Definition | Example (CNC Machining) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Factor (Independent Variable)** | X | A controllable input variable suspected of influencing Y | Spindle Speed, Feed Rate | | 2 | **Level** | — | The specific value(s) a factor is set to during the experiment | Speed: 1000 rpm (low), 2000 rpm (high) | | 3 | **Response (Dependent Variable)** | Y | The measurable output used to judge factor effects | Surface Roughness (Ra in µm) | | 4 | **Treatment** | — | A specific combination of factor levels in a run | "High Speed + High Feed" = Treatment 1 | | 5 | **Run** | — | A single experimental trial under one treatment | 1 observation = 1 run | | 6 | **Replication** | r | Repeating an entire experiment under identical conditions | Doing the same run 3 times | | 7 | **Replication** | r | Repeating an entire experiment under identical conditions | Doing the same run 3 times | | 8 | **Main Effect** | $E_A$ | The average change in Y when factor A is changed from low to high | Effect of Speed on Roughness | | 9 | **Interaction Effect** | $AB$ | The change in Y due to the *combined* variation of A and B, beyond their individual contributions | Speed × Feed synergy | | 10 | **Experimental Error** | $\sigma^2$ | The inherent variability of the process; estimated by replication | Run-to-run noise | | 11 | **Block** | — | A grouping of runs to control for known nuisance variability | Day-shift vs Night-shift | | 12 | **Randomization** | — | Random order of runs to neutralize time-related bias | Using a random number table | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2-Mark Definition Bank:** > - **Replicate** = Two or more **independent runs** at the *same* factor-level combination (used to estimate experimental error). > - **Repeat** = Two or more measurements on the *same* experimental unit (does *not* capture process variation; never a substitute for replication). ## 2.2 Classification of Experimental Designs DOE designs are taxonomically organized as follows: $$\text{Experimental Designs} = \begin{cases} \text{Full Factorial} \begin{cases} \text{2-Level (} 2^k \text{)} \\ \text{3-Level (} 3^k \text{)} \\ \text{Mixed Level} \end{cases} \\ \text{Fractional Factorial (} 2^{k-p} \text{)} \\ \text{Special Designs} \begin{cases} \text{Response Surface Methodology (RSM)} \\ \text{Taguchi Robust Design} \\ \text{Mixture Designs} \end{cases} \end{cases}$$ ## 2.3 The $2^k$ Full Factorial Design — The Workhorse of KTU Improve Phase A **$2^k$ factorial design** uses: - $k$ factors - Each at exactly **2 levels** (commonly coded as **−1 (low)** and **+1 (high)**) - Total number of runs: $N = 2^k$ - Requires **replication** *r* times → Total observations: $N_{\text{total}} = r \cdot 2^k$ **Coded vs. Uncoded Variables:** $$X_{\text{coded}} = \frac{X_{\text{actual}} - X_{\text{center}}}{\frac{1}{2}(X_{\text{high}} - X_{\text{low}})}$$ For example, if Temperature ranges from 80°C to 120°C: - $X_{\text{center}} = 100°C$ - Range half = 20°C - Actual 80°C → Coded −1 - Actual 100°C → Coded 0 (center point) - Actual 120°C → Coded +1 ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the consolidated formula bank for the KTU 2024 ESE Improve Phase. Memorize this — it forms 70% of Part B numerical solutions.** | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total number of runs** | $N = 2^k$ | For 2-level full factorial | | 2 | **Total runs with replication** | $N_{\text{total}} = r \cdot 2^k$ | *r* = replications | | 3 | **Main Effect of Factor A** | $E_A = \frac{\sum Y_{A^+} - \sum Y_{A^-}}{n \cdot 2^{k-1}}$ | Where $n = r \cdot 2^{k-1}$ observations at each level | | 4 | **Two-Factor Interaction (AB)** | $E_{AB} = \frac{[\text{AB}]}{n \cdot 2^{k-2}}$ | Where $[AB]$ = contrast of the AB column | | 5 | **Contrast for Factor A** | $[A] = \sum (a_i \cdot y_i)$ | $a_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ from design matrix | | 6 | **Sum of Squares — Factor A** | $SS_A = \frac{[A]^2}{N \cdot r}$ | Numerator: contrast squared | | 7 | **Sum of Squares — Interaction** | $SS_{AB} = \frac{[AB]^2}{N \cdot r}$ | Same structure | | 8 | **Total Sum of Squares** | $SS_T = \sum y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum y_i)^2}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | — | | 9 | **Sum of Squares — Error** | $SS_E = SS_T - SS_A - SS_B - SS_{AB}$ | By subtraction method | | 10 | **Degrees of Freedom — Factor** | $df_A = 1$ | For 2-level factor | | 11 | **Degrees of Freedom — Interaction** | $df_{AB} = 1$ | For 2-factor interaction in $2^k$ | | 12 | **Degrees of Freedom — Error** | $df_E = N_{\text{total}} - 1 - \sum df_{\text{factors \& interactions}}$ | — | | 13 | **Mean Square (Variance)** | $MS = \frac{SS}{df}$ | Always required for F-test | | 14 | **F-statistic** | $F_0 = \frac{MS_{\text{effect}}}{MS_E}$ | Compared to $F_{\alpha, df_{\text{eff}}, df_E}$ | | 15 | **Resolution of Fractional Factorial** | $R \geq \text{length of shortest word in defining relation}$ | R-III, R-IV, R-V | **Notation Convention for KTU:** Brackets $[\cdot]$ denote the *contrast* of a column; the contrast is the dot product of the coded column with the response vector. ## 2.5 Why DOE in Industry — Real-World Utility | Industry | Application | Y (Response) | Xs (Factors) | |---|---|---|---| | **Semiconductor** | Optimizing wafer etch uniformity | Etch depth (nm) | RF power, pressure, gas flow | | **Pharmaceutical** | Tablet dissolution optimization | Dissolution rate (%) | Compression force, binder %, granulation time | | **Automotive** | Reducing paint defects | Number of orange-peel defects | Booth humidity, spray pressure, gun distance | | **Banking** | Reducing loan processing time | Cycle time (hrs) | Approval levels, automation %, staffing | | **Healthcare** | Reducing ER wait time | Wait time (min) | Triage protocol, staff mix, shift schedule | > [!TIP] > **KTU Real-World Linkage:** When asked *"Why not just use regression?"* in the Improve phase, the canonical answer is: > *"Regression models a passive dataset; DOE **actively plans** the data collection to span the factor space efficiently, control confounders, and detect interactions — which regression *assumes away*."* # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical — $2^2$ Full Factorial Design with Replication **Problem (Modeled on KTU-style question):** A process engineer studies the effect of two factors on yield (%): - Factor A = Temperature (Low = 80°C, High = 100°C) - Factor B = Pressure (Low = 30 psi, High = 50 psi) - Replication: r = 2 - The four treatment combinations were run twice in random order. **Data Table (Responses — Y in % yield):** | Run | A (Temp) | B (Pressure) | Rep 1 | Rep 2 | Total ($T_i$) | Mean ($\bar{y}_i$) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | −1 (80) | −1 (30) | 75 | 78 | 153 | 76.5 | | 2 | +1 (100) | −1 (30) | 82 | 85 | 167 | 83.5 | | 3 | −1 (80) | +1 (50) | 80 | 83 | 163 | 81.5 | | 4 | +1 (100) | +1 (50) | 91 | 94 | 185 | 92.5 | | | | | | | **Grand Total = 668** | | ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Build the Design Matrix with Coded Columns and Contrasts The full $2^2$ design matrix in standard order (Yates order) is: | Run | I | A | B | AB | $y_1$ | $y_2$ | Total | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | +1 | 75 | 78 | 153 | | 2 | +1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | 82 | 85 | 167 | | 3 | +1 | −1 | +1 | −1 | 80 | 83 | 163 | | 4 | +1 | +1 | +1 | +1 | 91 | 94 | 185 | The **AB column** is obtained by element-wise multiplication of A and B (this is the **interaction column**, and its sign is the sign of the contrast for the AB effect). ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Calculate the Contrasts By definition, for a column vector $c$ with entries $\pm 1$ and response totals $T_i$: $$[\text{column}] = \sum_{i=1}^{4} c_i \cdot T_i$$ **Contrast for Factor A:** $$[A] = (-1)(153) + (+1)(167) + (-1)(163) + (+1)(185)$$ $$[A] = -153 + 167 - 163 + 185 = 36$$ **Contrast for Factor B:** $$[B] = (-1)(153) + (-1)(167) + (+1)(163) + (+1)(185)$$ $$[B] = -153 - 167 + 163 + 185 = 28$$ **Contrast for Interaction AB:** $$[AB] = (+1)(153) + (-1)(167) + (-1)(163) + (+1)(185)$$ $$[AB] = 153 - 167 - 163 + 185 = 8$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute the Main and Interaction Effects Recall the general effect formula: $$E_{\text{column}} = \frac{[\text{column}]}{n \cdot 2^{k-2}}$$ where $n = r \cdot 2^{k-1}$ for the *number of observations contributing to the high level* of the column. In our case, $r = 2$, $k = 2$: $$n = 2 \cdot 2^{2-1} = 4$$ And the denominator for any column in a $2^k$ design is $n \cdot 2^{k-2}$: $$\text{Denominator} = 4 \cdot 2^{2-2} = 4 \cdot 1 = 4$$ **Main Effect of A (Temperature):** $$E_A = \frac{[A]}{4} = \frac{36}{4} = 9.0 \, \% \text{ yield}$$ **Interpretation:** Increasing Temperature from 80°C to 100°C *increases* the average yield by 9 percentage points. **Main Effect of B (Pressure):** $$E_B = \frac{[B]}{4} = \frac{28}{4} = 7.0 \, \% \text{ yield}$$ **Interaction Effect AB:** $$E_{AB} = \frac{[AB]}{4} = \frac{8}{4} = 2.0 \, \% \text{ yield}$$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute the Sum of Squares **Sum of Squares for Factor A:** $$SS_A = \frac{[A]^2}{N \cdot r} = \frac{(36)^2}{4 \cdot 2} = \frac{1296}{8} = 162.0$$ **Sum of Squares for Factor B:** $$SS_B = \frac{[B]^2}{N \cdot r} = \frac{(28)^2}{8} = \frac{784}{8} = 98.0$$ **Sum of Squares for Interaction AB:** $$SS_{AB} = \frac{[AB]^2}{N \cdot r} = \frac{(8)^2}{8} = \frac{64}{8} = 8.0$$ **Total Sum of Squares:** $$SS_T = \sum y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum y_i)^2}{N_{\text{total}}}$$ First, $\sum y_i^2$ over all 8 observations: $$\sum y_i^2 = 75^2 + 78^2 + 82^2 + 85^2 + 80^2 + 83^2 + 91^2 + 94^2$$ $$= 5625 + 6084 + 6724 + 7225 + 6400 + 6889 + 8281 + 8836$$ $$= 56064$$ Correction factor: $$CF = \frac{(668)^2}{8} = \frac{446224}{8} = 55778$$ Therefore: $$SS_T = 56064 - 55778 = 286.0$$ **Sum of Squares for Error (by subtraction):** $$SS_E = SS_T - SS_A - SS_B - SS_{AB}$$ $$SS_E = 286.0 - 162.0 - 98.0 - 8.0 = 18.0$$ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Build the ANOVA Table and Conduct F-Tests | Source | SS | df | MS | $F_0$ | $F_{0.05, 1, 4}$ | Conclusion | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A (Temperature) | 162.0 | 1 | 162.0 | **36.00** | 7.71 | **Significant** | | B (Pressure) | 98.0 | 1 | 98.0 | **21.78** | 7.71 | **Significant** | | AB (Interaction) | 8.0 | 1 | 8.0 | **1.78** | 7.71 | Not Significant | | Error | 18.0 | 4 | 4.5 | — | — | — | | **Total** | **286.0** | **7** | — | — | — | — | **Verification of F-statistic for A:** $$F_0(A) = \frac{MS_A}{MS_E} = \frac{162.0}{4.5} = 36.00$$ Since $36.00 > 7.71$ → **Reject $H_0$** → Factor A is statistically significant. ### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Engineer the Optimum Both A and B are significant, AB is not. The optimum is the level of each factor that maximizes the mean response. From the run totals: - Low A (80°C): $153 + 163 = 316$ → Mean = 79.0 - High A (100°C): $167 + 185 = 352$ → Mean = 88.0 ✓ **Choose High A** - Low B (30 psi): $153 + 167 = 320$ → Mean = 80.0 - High B (50 psi): $163 + 185 = 348$ → Mean = 87.0 ✓ **Choose High B** **Predicted Optimum (A+, B+):** From Run 4 data, $\bar{y} = 92.5\%$ yield. **Mathematical confirmation (using fitted model):** $$\hat{y} = \bar{\bar{y}} + \frac{E_A}{2} x_A + \frac{E_B}{2} x_B$$ where $x_A, x_B \in \{-1, +1\}$ are coded levels and $\bar{\bar{y}} = \frac{668}{8} = 83.5$. For $x_A = +1, x_B = +1$: $$\hat{y} = 83.5 + \frac{9.0}{2}(+1) + \frac{7.0}{2}(+1) + \frac{2.0}{2}(+1)$$ $$= 83.5 + 4.5 + 3.5 + 1.0 = 92.5 \, \% \text{ yield} \quad \checkmark$$ This matches the observed mean of Run 4 exactly, confirming the model. ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Design > [!NOTE] > **Use this decision matrix in KTU's "Discuss the suitability" type questions.** | # | Factors (k) | Resources / Budget | Objective | Recommended Design | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k \leq 4$ | High | Screen factors + detect all 2-factor interactions (2FIs) | **$2^k$ Full Factorial** | | 2 | $5 \leq k \leq 8$ | Moderate | Initial screening of many factors | **$2^{k-p}$ Fractional Factorial (Resolution IV)** | | 3 | $k \geq 8$ | Limited | Find vital few from many | **Plackett-Burman (Resolution III)** | | 4 | 2 or 3 critical factors identified | Moderate | Find optimum (curvature present) | **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** — Central Composite Design (CCD) | | 5 | Any k, with noise factors | High | Optimize under noisy conditions | **Taguchi Robust Design** (Inner/Outer Array) | | 6 | Factors are proportions of a mixture | — | Optimize blend composition | **Mixture Design** (Simplex-Lattice) | ## 3.3 The Power of DOE — Quantitative Comparison To prove why DOE is mathematically superior to OFAT (One Factor At A Time), consider the *information per run*: $$\text{Information per run (OFAT)} = O(1)$$ $$\text{Information per run (DOE } 2^k \text{)} = O(k + \binom{k}{2})$$ For $k = 4$ factors, OFAT requires 8 runs minimum to test each factor at 2 levels, but it **cannot detect any interaction** even after 8 runs. A $2^4$ DOE in 16 runs estimates **4 main effects + 6 two-factor interactions + 4 three-factor interactions + 1 four-factor interaction = 15 effects** — a 15× efficiency gain. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DMAIC Macro-Flow Showing DOE's Position in the Improve Phase ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[Define Phase
