In KTU 2024-scheme terminology, DMADOV is a **closed-loop, customer-anchored, statistically-validated design protocol** that translates the *Voice of the Customer (VOC)* into engineered specifications, robust designs, and verified outputs.

## 2. Intuitive Overview: Why "Design" Needs Its Own Methodology?

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Renovating a House vs. Building a Smart Home"**
> - **DMAIC** is like *renovating an existing house*: the foundation is fixed, you can only repaint walls, fix plumbing, and improve interiors.
> - **DMADOV** is like *designing a smart home from the ground up*: you choose the soil, the architecture, the materials, the wiring, and the IoT systems *before* a single brick is laid.
>
> Renovating a leaking roof is DMAIC. Engineering a roof that will never leak in 50 years is **DMADOV**.

The "design-then-verify" approach prevents the **"creep" of defects** that occurs when Six Sigma is applied *after* a faulty product has been launched. It aligns with Joseph Juran's concept of *Quality by Design* and Genichi Taguchi's *Robust Design* philosophy.

## 3. Position of DMADOV in the Six Sigma Body of Knowledge

| Framework | Purpose | Stage of Use | Defect Handling |
|---|---|---|---|
| DMAIC | Improve existing process | After product launch | Detect & reduce |
| **DMADOV** | **Create new process/product** | **Before/at design stage** | **Prevent & eliminate** |
| DMEDI (variant) | Innovation-focused | Breakthrough design | Re-engineer |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Memory Aid:** If the question says *"the product does not exist yet"* — answer **DMADOV**. If it says *"the product exists but has problems"* — answer **DMAIC**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Six Phases — Operational Logic

DMADOV is executed as a *gated* process, meaning each phase produces deliverables that must be reviewed and approved before the next phase begins. The logical "why" and "how" of each phase is given below.

### Phase 1 — DEFINE
- **Why:** Anchor the project to customer needs and business strategy.
- **How:** Develop the *Project Charter*, identify stakeholders, map the high-level process, and capture the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)**.
- **Key Outputs:** Project Charter, SIPOC diagram, **CTQ tree** (Critical-to-Quality tree), Business Case.

### Phase 2 — MEASURE
- **Why:** Translate vague customer needs into measurable engineering specifications.
- **How:** Use **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**, also called the *House of Quality*, to convert VOC → CTQs → measurable *Critical-to-Process (CTP)* parameters.
- **Key Outputs:** QFD matrix, target specifications, Measurement System Analysis (MSA).

### Phase 3 — ANALYZE
- **Why:** Determine the design drivers and generate feasible concept alternatives.
- **How:** Apply **TRIZ** (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving), design FMEA, functional analysis, and benchmarking. Concepts are scored using **Pugh Matrix**.
- **Key Outputs:** Concept scoring matrix, Functional decomposition, Design FMEA (DFMEA).

### Phase 4 — DESIGN
- **Why:** Convert the selected concept into a detailed, ready-to-prototype design.
- **How:** Build **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, develop **Tolerance Design** (Taguchi), generate CAD models, and produce Design Verification Plan.
- **Key Outputs:** Detailed drawings, DOE results, Tolerance budget, Design Verification Plan.

### Phase 5 — OPTIMIZE
- **Why:** Fine-tune the design to achieve **Robustness** (insensitivity to noise factors) and reach Six Sigma capability.
- **How:** Use **Taguchi Robust Design**, **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**, Monte Carlo Simulation, and **Tolerance Stack-up Analysis**.
- **Key Outputs:** Optimal parameter settings, Robust design solution, Cpk ≥ 1.5 (preferably ≥ 2.0).

### Phase 6 — VALIDATE
- **Why:** Prove the design works in the real-world production environment.
- **How:** Conduct **Pilot Runs**, **Production Process Validation (PPV)**, Proving-out runs, and final **Process Capability (Cpk, Ppk)** confirmation.
- **Key Outputs:** Validated production process, signed-off capability report, transition-to-operations handover.

## 2. KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **most-tested equations** in KTU University Exams for DMADOV/DMADV topics. Memorise the relationships, not just the numbers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$$ | DPMO is dimensionless, scaled to a million. |
| 2 | **Process Yield** | $$\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100\%$$ | Expressed as a percentage. |
| 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ | Product of all sub-process yields. |
| 4 | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \bigl[1 - \Phi(Z)\bigr]$$ | Z = sigma distance from mean to nearest spec. |
| 5 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $$\text{Cpk} = \min\!\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$$ | $\bar{X}$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std. deviation. |
| 6 | **Six Sigma Target (Long-term)** | $$\text{DPMO}_{\text{Six Sigma}} \le 3.4$$ | With **1.5 σ shift** included. |
| 7 | **Taguchi Signal-to-Noise Ratio (η)** | $$\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{y_i^{2}}\right)$$ | For "larger-is-better" CTQs. |
| 8 | **Quality Loss Function (Taguchi)** | $$L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$$ | $k$ = quality loss coefficient, $m$ = target. |
| 9 | **Pugh Matrix Score** | $$\text{Concept Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (S_i \times W_i)$$ | $S_i$ = score (-1, 0, +1), $W_i$ = weight. |
| 10 | **Cpk to Sigma Conversion** | $$\text{Short-term } \sigma = \text{Cpk} \times 3$$ | Used to benchmark Cpk with sigma levels. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** Notice the use of `\min\!\left[ ... \right]` and `\sum_{i=1}^{n}` instead of `|x|` and bare `|` symbols — this preserves the KTU markdown-table integrity as mandated.

## 3. Real-World Engineering Utility of DMADOV

- **Automotive Industry:** Designing a new car platform that must survive 15 years across climate zones.
- **Aerospace:** Designing a turbine blade that must maintain Cpk ≥ 2.0 under 8 different noise factors.
- **Semiconductor:** Designing a lithography process whose defect density is < 0.01 per cm².
- **Healthcare:** Designing a blood-glucose monitor with extreme low-bias across all humidity conditions.
- **Software:** Designing an API service that must hold sub-millisecond latency under 10× peak load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This section demonstrates the **most frequently-asked DMADOV problem** in KTU examinations — converting DPMO to Sigma Level and then to a Cpk-equivalent capability target. Every algebraic step is written explicitly; *no jumps* are permitted.

## 1. Worked Derivation: From Customer Target to Engineering Specification

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A Six Sigma design team is developing a new pressure sensor. The target defect rate is **Six Sigma quality (3.4 DPMO)**. The design specification for the output voltage $V$ is target $m = 5.0$ V with USL = 5.2 V. Assuming the output is normally distributed and the design must operate with a 1.5 σ long-term shift, **calculate the maximum allowable standard deviation $\sigma$ for the design to meet Six Sigma capability.**

### Step 1 — Convert Six Sigma DPMO to the Z-value

We start from the standard **DPMO-to-Sigma** relationship:

$$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \bigl[1 - \Phi(Z)\bigr]$$

We know the Six-Sigma long-term target is DPMO = 3.4. Substituting:

$$3.4 = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \bigl[1 - \Phi(Z)\bigr]$$

Isolating the cumulative probability term:

$$1 - \Phi(Z) = \frac{3.4}{1{,}000{,}000} = 3.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

Therefore:

$$\Phi(Z) = 1 - 3.4 \times 10^{-6} = 0.99999966$$

### Step 2 — Look up the Z-value (instructor-standard table)

From the standard normal table, the value $\Phi(Z) = 0.99999966$ corresponds to:

$$Z_{\text{Six Sigma}} = 4.5$$

This is the famous **4.5 σ short-term value** that, *when shifted by 1.5 σ long-term drift*, gives 3.4 DPMO.

### Step 3 — Translate Z to allowable standard deviation

The specification spread is:

$$\text{USL} - \text{LSL} = (5.2 - 4.8) = 0.4 \text{ V}$$

(The LSL is symmetric: 4.8 V, since the design is centred at 5.0 V.)

For a centred process, the total specification width equals 6 σ (three sigmas each side):

$$\text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 6\sigma_{\text{short-term}}$$

With the long-term **1.5 σ shift** on each side becoming 4.5 σ:

$$\text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 2 \times Z_{\text{shift}} \times \sigma = 2 \times 4.5 \times \sigma$$

Substituting the numeric spread:

$$0.4 = 9.0 \times \sigma$$

Solving for $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = \frac{0.4}{9.0} = 0.0444 \text{ V}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the Required Cpk

Using the centred-process Cpk formula:

$$\text{Cpk} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \frac{5.2 - 5.0}{3 \times 0.0444}$$

Evaluating the numerator and denominator:

$$\text{Cpk} = \frac{0.2}{0.1333} = 1.50$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight for the examiner:** A Six Sigma process, by definition, must achieve a **Cpk ≥ 1.5** (long-term). The derivation above rigorously proves this benchmark.

### Step 5 — Convert to a Process Yield Estimate

Yield is the area under the normal curve between LSL and USL:

$$\text{Yield} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{0.2}{0.0444}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{-0.2}{0.0444}\right)$$

$$\text{Yield} = \Phi(4.5) - \Phi(-4.5) = 2\,\Phi(4.5) - 1$$

$$\text{Yield} \approx 2 \times 0.99999966 - 1 = 0.99999932$$

Converting to a percentage:

$$\text{Yield} \approx 99.999932\%$$

The implied defect rate is therefore:

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - 0.99999932 = 6.8 \times 10^{-7} = 0.68 \text{ ppm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the famous Six-Sigma mantra: **3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield**.

## 2. Analytical Matrix — CTQ-to-Specification Conversion Logic

The complete chain of reasoning a KTU examiner will test is captured here:

| Step | Logical Conversion | Mathematical Tool | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VOC → CTQ | Kano Model, Interviews | CTQ Tree |
| 2 | CTQ → Measurable CTP | QFD (House of Quality) | QFD Matrix |
| 3 | CTP → Target Value & Tolerance | Engineering Judgement, Benchmarking | Specification Sheet |
| 4 | Target & Tolerance → Cpk Target | $\text{Cpk} \ge 1.5$ | Capability Plan |
| 5 | Cpk Target → σ Budget | $\sigma = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3 \times \text{Cpk}}$ | Tolerance Budget |
| 6 | σ Budget → Robust Design | Taguchi DOE | Optimal Settings |
| 7 | Optimal Settings → Validation | Pilot Runs, PPAP | Capability Report |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Do **not** confuse **Cpk = 1.5** (the *Six Sigma* benchmark) with **Cpk = 2.0** (a *world-class* benchmark). KTU examiners specifically test this distinction in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. Mermaid Flow Diagram: The DMADOV Phases with Inputs and Outputs

```mermaid

    A --> A2[SIPOC Diagram]

    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    B[Phase 2 Measure] --> B1[QFD House of Quality]

    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C[Phase 3 Analyze] --> C1[Functional Analysis]
    C --> C2[TRIZ Inventive Principles]
    C --> C3[Pugh Matrix Concept Score]

    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D[Phase 4 Design] --> D1[Design of Experiments DOE]

```

## 2. Block-Level Functional Architecture: How the Six Phases Interconnect

## 3. Gated-Stage Schematic (Decision Gates Between Phases)

```mermaid

    P2 -- Gate 2 Review --> P3[Phase 3 Analyze]

    P4 -- Gate 4 Review --> P5[Phase 5 Optimize]
    P5 -- Gate 5 Review --> P6[Phase 6 Validate]
    P6 -- Final Handover --> PS[Production and Operations]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read the diagram like a map:** Each *Gate* is a formal management review where the previous phase's deliverables are signed off. In KTU viva questions, "What happens if Gate 3 fails?" is a high-frequency prompt — the answer is "regression to Phase 2 to refine the measurement system".

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme assessment blueprint**: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) with internal choice between sub-question (a) and (b).

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between DMAIC and DMADOV methodologies of Six Sigma."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | DMAIC | DMADOV |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Improve an **existing** process | **Create** a new process/product |
| Stage of use | After product launch | At design / pre-launch stage |
| Approach | Reactive (problem-solving) | Proactive (prevention-based) |
| Outcome | Stable, capable existing process | Robust, defect-free new design |
| Statistical Focus | Root-cause and process control | DOE, Robust Design, Tolerance |

> DMADOV is used in **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)**; DMAIC is used for **incremental improvement**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define the term 'Voice of the Customer' (VOC) and explain its role in the Define phase of DMADOV."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** is the structured capture of customer needs, expectations, and preferences through tools such as interviews, surveys, focus groups, complaint analysis, and the **Kano Model**. In the **Define phase** of DMADOV, VOC serves as the **input** for building the **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) tree**, which converts qualitative customer desires into measurable engineering targets. Without a robust VOC, the design risks solving the wrong problem — leading to wasted development cost and late-stage redesign.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: Part B questions carry an **internal choice** between two alternative sub-questions (a) and (b). You must attempt any **one** of (a) or (b). We model the full question with both choices provided.

### Question 3 (14 Marks)

#### ✅ Option (a) — The Classical 7-Mark × 2 Structure

**(a) Explain the six phases of the DMADOV methodology in detail. For each phase, list the key deliverable and the principal statistical or design tool used. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

DMADOV is a **six-phase Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** roadmap that systematically transforms customer needs into a robust, validated design. The phases are sequential and gated.

1. **Define (1 mark):** The project's purpose, scope, and stakeholders are documented.
   - *Deliverable:* Project Charter, CTQ Tree, SIPOC diagram.
   - *Tool:* VOC capture, Kano Model.

2. **Measure (1 mark):** Customer needs are converted into measurable engineering targets.
   - *Deliverable:* House of Quality, CTPs.
   - *Tool:* **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**.

3. **Analyze (1 mark):** Concepts are generated and evaluated.
   - *Deliverable:* Concept selection report.
   - *Tool:* **TRIZ**, **Pugh Matrix**, **DFMEA**.

4. **Design (1 mark):** The chosen concept is detailed.
   - *Deliverable:* Detailed drawings, prototype specifications.
   - *Tool:* **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, **Tolerance Design**.

5. **Optimize (1 mark):** The design is tuned for **robustness**.
   - *Deliverable:* Optimal parameter settings.
   - *Tool:* **Taguchi Robust Design**, **Response Surface Methodology**.

6. **Validate (1 mark):** The design is proven in a real production environment.
   - *Deliverable:* Signed capability report.
   - *Tool:* **Pilot Run**, **PPAP**, **Cpk/Ppk Analysis**.

**Concluding line (1 mark):** DMADOV, by virtue of its front-loaded design rigour, prevents defects at source — making it more cost-effective than detecting them after production.

---

**(b) A new pharmaceutical tablet must weigh 250 mg ± 2 mg. After collecting 30 production samples, the mean is 250 mg and the standard deviation is 0.5 mg. Calculate the process capability index (Cpk) and comment on whether the process meets Six Sigma capability. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the given values clearly.

$$\text{USL} = 252 \text{ mg}, \quad \text{LSL} = 248 \text{ mg}, \quad \bar{X} = 250 \text{ mg}, \quad \sigma = 0.5 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the upper and lower capability indices.

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \frac{252 - 250}{3 \times 0.5} = \frac{2}{1.5} = 1.333$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{250 - 248}{1.5} = \frac{2}{1.5} = 1.333$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Take the minimum.

$$\text{Cpk} = \min(1.333, 1.333) = 1.333$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Calculate equivalent DPMO (using Z = 3 × Cpk = 4.0).

$$\Phi(4.0) \approx 0.9999683 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{DPMO} \approx 32$$

This corresponds to a **4.0 σ** level.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion.

A Cpk of 1.333 represents a **4-sigma** capability with DPMO ≈ 32, which is **far short** of the Six-Sigma target of DPMO ≤ 3.4 (Cpk ≥ 1.5). The design team must **reduce σ by at least 0.07 mg** (from 0.50 to 0.43 mg) to reach Six Sigma. The process is **not** Six-Sigma capable.

---

#### ✅ Option (b) — Alternative Choice for the Same 14 Marks

**(a) Discuss the role of Quality Function Deployment (QFD) and the House of Quality in the Measure phase of DMADOV. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the central matrix of **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**, a structured planning tool used in the **Measure phase** of DMADOV to translate customer needs (the "Whats") into engineering characteristics (the "Hows"). It functions as a **conversion engine** between the qualitative and quantitative worlds.

The HOQ contains seven interlinked sub-matrices:

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — VOC expressed as 20–40 measurable desires.
2. **Engineering Requirements (Hows)** — measurable CTPs (e.g., pressure, tolerance, weight).
3. **Relationship Matrix** — strong (9), moderate (3), weak (1) relationships between Whats and Hows.
4. **Roof (Correlation Matrix)** — identifies synergies and trade-offs between Hows.
5. **Technical Importance Weights** — prioritises Hows based on their impact on customer needs.
6. **Competitive Benchmarking** — compares own design vs. competitors.
7. **Target Values** — sets engineering targets for each How.

By the end of the Measure phase, QFD provides the **engineering targets and tolerances** that drive the Analyse, Design, and Optimise phases. (7 marks allocated across explanation, components, and conclusion.)

---

**(b) A product has a target value of 100 units, USL = 110, LSL = 90, and $\sigma = 3$ units. Using Taguchi's Quality Loss Function, calculate the loss to society per defective unit if the actual measurement is $y = 105$ units. Assume the loss at the specification limit is $L = 4000$ rupees. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Determine the quality loss coefficient $k$.

From the loss function at the spec limit:

$$L = k (\Delta_0)^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{L}{(\Delta_0)^{2}}$$

The spec deviation is:

$$\Delta_0 = \text{USL} - m = 110 - 100 = 10 \text{ units}$$

Therefore:

$$k = \frac{4000}{10^{2}} = \frac{4000}{100} = 40 \text{ rupees per unit squared}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the deviation of the actual measurement from target.

$$\Delta = y - m = 105 - 100 = 5 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply Taguchi's loss function.

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^{2} = 40 \times (5)^{2} = 40 \times 25 = 1000 \text{ rupees}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Conclude with engineering interpretation.

> [!TIP]
> The loss to society is **₹1000** per such defective unit — even though the unit is still inside the spec limit. This is the philosophical core of **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function**: *being on target yields zero loss; any deviation, however small, imposes a quadratic loss*. The function proves that Six-Sigma design must minimise variance around the target value, not just stay within USL/LSL.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Cpk vs. Cp:** Cpk accounts for *centring*; Cp does not. State explicitly which formula you used.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5 σ shift:** When asked for *Six Sigma* DPMO, you **must** use the 4.5 σ short-term value, not 6 σ.
> 3. **Mixing DMAIC and DMADOV deliverables:** In DMADOV, *TRIZ, Pugh Matrix, QFD* are expected — *Pareto, Fishbone, Control Charts* belong to DMAIC.
> 4. **Skipping the unit:** Writing "Cpk = 1.5" without stating the process parameter (e.g., "tablets of 250 mg") loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the conclusion:** KTU mandates a 1-line conclusion in every long-answer numerical — without it, you forfeit 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DMADOV = Define → Measure → Analyze → Design → Optimize → Validate** — the **DFSS** framework.
- Use **DMADOV** for *new* products/processes; use **DMAIC** for *existing* ones.
- The **Define** phase anchors the project to **VOC** and produces a **CTQ Tree**.
- The **Measure** phase uses **QFD / House of Quality** to convert VOC into **CTP** specifications.
- The **Analyze** phase uses **TRIZ** and **Pugh Matrix** for concept selection.
- The **Design** phase uses **DOE** and **Tolerance Design** to detail the prototype.
- The **Optimize** phase uses **Taguchi Robust Design** and **RSM** to achieve **Cpk ≥ 1.5**.
- The **Validate** phase uses **Pilot Runs** and **PPAP** to confirm final **Cpk/Ppk ≥ 1.5**.
- **Six Sigma benchmark:** DPMO ≤ **3.4**, Yield = **99.99966%**, Z = **4.5** (short-term).
- **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ — loss is *quadratic* in deviation, *zero* at target.
- **Cpk formula:** $\min\!\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$ — minimum of upper and lower capability.
- **DFMEA** (Design FMEA) is the standard risk tool in the Analyze/Design phase — different from **PFMEA** used in DMAIC.
- **SIPOC** diagram is a high-level process map (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) used in the Define phase.
- **Gated reviews** between phases prevent moving forward with incomplete deliverables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Team Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET742 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Team Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

**Team Management** in the Six Sigma framework (DMAIC & DFSS contexts) is defined as the structured, scientifically-grounded process of organizing, leading, coordinating, and empowering a cross-functional group of individuals toward the achievement of a clearly defined project goal — typically the reduction of process variation, elimination of defects, or improvement of process capability to a target **σ = 3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**.

It encompasses the deployment of **team structures**, **role assignments**, **stage-based development models**, **leadership archetypes**, **conflict-resolution protocols**, and **motivation frameworks** that collectively convert a group of subject-matter experts into a high-performing, statistically-literate, decision-making unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board-Standardized):**
> *Team Management is the disciplined orchestration of human capital, structured communication channels, and role-based accountability matrices within a Six Sigma project, ensuring that the Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control (DMAIC) lifecycle is executed by a competent, motivated, and well-led team.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cricket Team" Analogy (Intuitive View):**
>
> Imagine a cricket team playing a Test match (a Six Sigma project is a "Test match", not a T20 — it lasts 4–6 months). The **Captain (Champion/Project Leader)** sets the field placement, decides bowling changes, and reads the pitch. The **Batsmen (Process Owners)** score runs by executing defensive and aggressive shots (running sub-projects). The **Bowlers (Black Belts)** deliver the ball (data, analysis) to take wickets (defects). The **Wicket-Keeper (Master Black Belt)** observes patterns and feeds information back. The **Coach (Sponsor/Executive)** doesn't play on the field but funds the team and reviews strategy after each session.
>
> Now, if the captain puts a bowler to bat at #4, or if the wicket-keeper starts bowling without being told — the team collapses. **Team Management** is the science of putting the *right person, in the right role, at the right time*, and giving them the *right tools and motivation*.

---

## 1.3 Core Components of Six Sigma Team Management (Hierarchical Map)

| **Level** | **Component** | **Primary Function** |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Team Structure & Hierarchy** | Defines who reports to whom (Sponsor → Champion → BB → GB → TM) |
| 2 | **Team Roles & Responsibilities** | Allocates specific tasks (Belbin Roles, RACI Matrix) |
| 3 | **Team Development Stages** | Manages group psychology over time (Tuckman's 5-Stage Model) |
| 4 | **Leadership Styles** | Directs the team through situational leadership (Hersey-Blanchard) |
| 5 | **Motivation Framework** | Sustains engagement (Maslow, Herzberg, Expectancy Theory) |
| 6 | **Conflict Resolution** | Removes friction (Thomas-Kilmann Modes) |
| 7 | **Communication Management** | Ensures clarity (3 Cs: Clear, Concise, Consistent) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Six Sigma Metric Used Throughout Team Management:**
> The target defect rate is **3.4 DPMO**, which corresponds to a process capability of **σ = 4.5 σ (short-term) = 6 σ (long-term with 1.5σ shift)**.

---

## 1.4 Why Team Management is Critical in Six Sigma

- Six Sigma projects are **cross-functional** — they span departments (production, QA, finance, supply chain).
- A **Green Belt** alone cannot solve an enterprise-level defect problem — it requires a coordinated team.
- **Defect reduction** of the order of **10× to 100× improvement** is statistically impossible without structured human collaboration.
- KTU examiners frequently test **"why teams fail"** — the answer is overwhelmingly: *lack of role clarity, poor leadership, and misaligned motivation*.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Team Failure Causes — "RRR-CLM":**
> **R**ole ambiguity, **R**esource starvation, **R**eward absence, **C**onflict unmanaged, **L**eadership mismatch, **M**easurement void.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Six Sigma Team Structure — The Hierarchy

The Six Sigma organizational pyramid is a tiered structure, each level having specific responsibilities, authority, and certification requirements.

| **Tier** | **Role** | **Certification** | **% Time on Project** | **Key Responsibility** |
|----------|----------|-------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Top** | **Executive / Sponsor** | Trained in Six Sigma Awareness | 10% | Approves project, allocates budget, removes organizational barriers |
| **Upper** | **Champion** | Six Sigma Champion | 15–20% | Scopes project, selects BB, ensures alignment with strategy |
| **Mid** | **Master Black Belt (MBB)** | MBB Certified | 100% (full-time coach) | Coaches BBs, trains GBs, mentors statistical tools |
| **Operational** | **Black Belt (BB)** | BB Certified | 100% (full-time leader) | Leads DMAIC project, applies tools, drives results |
| **Frontline** | **Green Belt (GB)** | GB Certified | 25–50% | Leads sub-projects, collects data, executes tasks |
| **Support** | **Team Member (TM)** | Project-specific training | 10–25% | Subject-matter expertise (SME), data gathering |

---

## 2.2 Tuckman's Five-Stage Team Development Model (High-Yield)

Developed by **Bruce Tuckman (1965)**, updated with **Adjourning (1977)**. This is a **guaranteed KTU question**.

| **Stage** | **Characteristics** | **Team Member Behavior** | **Leader's Action** |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| **1. Forming** | Orientation, politeness, testing boundaries | Dependent, seeking guidance | Direct, provide clear goals & structure |
| **2. Storming** | Conflict, frustration, power struggles | Resistance, intra-group friction | Coach, facilitate conflict resolution |
| **3. Norming** | Cohesion, agreement, shared norms | Trust building, collaborative | Support, encourage participation |
| **4. Performing** | High productivity, autonomous function | Self-managed, task-focused | Delegate, monitor only loosely |
| **5. Adjourning** | Disbandment, project closure | Reflection, knowledge transfer | Recognize, document, celebrate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"At which stage is conflict maximum?"* — the answer is **Storming**. When asked *"When should the leader delegate?"* — the answer is **Performing**.

---

## 2.3 Belbin Team Role Theory (High-Yield)

Dr. **Meredith Belbin** (1981) identified 9 roles clustered into 3 categories. A balanced team requires a mix.

| **Category** | **Role** | **Strength** | **Weakness** |
|--------------|----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Cerebral (Thinking)** | Plant (PL) | Creative, imaginative | Too abstract |
| | Monitor Evaluator (ME) | Analytical, strategic | Lacks drive |
| | Specialist (SP) | Deep technical knowledge | Narrow focus |
| **Action-Oriented (Doing)** | Shaper (SH) | Challenging, dynamic | Provokes conflict |
| | Implementer (IM) | Disciplined, reliable | Slow to adapt |
| | Completer Finisher (CF) | Detail-oriented, conscientious | Perfectionist, anxious |
| **People-Oriented (Feeling)** | Coordinator (CO) | Mature, confident chairer | Offloads creative work |
| | Teamworker (TW) | Cooperative, perceptive | Indecisive in crisis |
| | Resource Investigator (RI) | Explores opportunities, extroverted | Loses interest post-enthusiasm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "Brain-Body-Heart":**
> **Brain** = Plant, Monitor Evaluator, Specialist (THINK)
> **Body** = Shaper, Implementer, Completer Finisher (DO)
> **Heart** = Coordinator, Teamworker, Resource Investigator (FEEL)

---

## 2.4 Leadership Styles in Six Sigma (Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership)

The leader's style must adapt to the **competence** and **commitment** of the team member.

| **Competence Level** | **Commitment Level** | **Leader Style** | **KTU Tag** |
|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Low | High | **Directing (S1)** — Tell | D1 |
| Low–Moderate | Low | **Coaching (S2)** — Sell | D2 |
| Moderate–High | Variable | **Supporting (S3)** — Participate | D3 |
| High | High | **Delegating (S4)** — Observe | D4 |

---

## 2.5 Motivation Theories (High-Density Summary)

### 2.5.1 Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (1943)

| **Level** | **Need** | **Six Sigma Example** |
|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| 5 (Top) | Self-Actualization | Becoming a Master Black Belt |
| 4 | Esteem | Black Belt certification, recognition |
| 3 | Love/Belonging | Team membership, project group |
| 2 | Safety | Job security through Six Sigma |
| 1 (Base) | Physiological | Salary from project completion bonus |

### 2.5.2 Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (1959)

- **Hygiene Factors** (extrinsic): Salary, policy, supervision → absence causes dissatisfaction, presence does NOT motivate.
- **Motivators** (intrinsic): Achievement, recognition, growth → drive satisfaction.

### 2.5.3 Expectancy Theory (Vroom, 1964)

$$\text{Motivation} \;=\; \text{Expectancy} \times \text{Instrumentality} \times \text{Valence}$$

> **Where:**
> - $Expectancy$ = Belief that effort will lead to performance ($0 \le E \le 1$).
> - $Instrumentality$ = Belief that performance will lead to reward ($0 \le I \le 1$).
> - $Valence$ = Value placed on the reward ($0 \le V \le \infty$, but typically $-1 \le V \le 1$).

---

## 2.6 RACI Matrix (Responsibility Assignment)

A core KTU-exam tool for clarifying roles in any Six Sigma project.

| **Letter** | **Role** | **Meaning** |
|------------|----------|-------------|
| **R** | Responsible | Does the work |
| **A** | Accountable | Ultimately answerable (only ONE person) |
| **C** | Consulted | Provides input before/during |
| **I** | Informed | Told after the fact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Every task in a RACI must have EXACTLY ONE Accountable (A) — never zero, never multiple. This is a frequent board-exam marking point.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Team Management Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Use `\vert` for absolute values to preserve table formatting.**

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Unit / Range** | **Use** |
|------------|-------------|------------------|---------|
| **Six Sigma Quality Level** | $\sigma_{LT} = Z_{\text{short}} + 1.5$ | Sigma | Long-term shift correction |
| **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | defects / $10^{6}$ | Baseline & final metric |
| **Process Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | fraction $\vert 0,1 \mid$ | Track improvement |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | fraction $\vert 0,1 \mid$ | Multi-stage processes |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Currency | Project ROI |
| **Project ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100$ | % | Sponsor justification |
| **Motivation (Vroom)** | $M = E \times I \times V$ | scalar | Individual drive |
| **Team Maturity Index** | $\text{TMI} = \dfrac{\text{Performing Members}}{\text{Total Members}}$ | ratio $\vert 0,1 \mid$ | Stage assessment |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Manufacturing:** Six Sigma teams reduce defect rates on assembly lines (e.g., automotive industry: 3.4 DPMO target).
- **Software Engineering:** CMMI + Six Sigma teams reduce post-deployment bugs.
- **Healthcare:** Teams reduce hospital-acquired infection rates.
- **Banking/Finance:** Teams reduce loan-processing errors to single-digit DPMO.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Benchmark:** Motorola (1986) achieved a **150× defect reduction** in 5 years using Six Sigma teams — this is the historical anchor examiners love to quote.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation Note:** Because Team Management is a behavioral/management science, the "derivations" below are exhaustive **analytical frameworks**, **decision matrices**, and **scenario evaluation logs** — the exact KTU-board equivalent of mathematical derivations.

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Framework: Selecting the Right Leadership Style (Hersey-Blanchard)

**Problem Statement:** *A Six Sigma Green Belt has just completed her training. She is highly enthusiastic (high commitment) but has zero statistical software experience (low competence). What leadership style should the Black Belt apply?*

### **Step 1: Diagnose Competence Level**

$$\text{Competence}_{\text{GB}} = \text{Knowledge} + \text{Skills} + \text{Experience}$$

- Knowledge of DMAIC: Moderate (just trained).
- Skill in Minitab: **Low** (never used).
- Experience in projects: **Zero**.

$$\Rightarrow \text{Competence} = \text{Low}$$

### **Step 2: Diagnose Commitment Level**

- Enthusiasm: **High** (eager to apply learning).
- Confidence: **Moderate-to-High**.
- Anxiety: **Moderate** (typical of new GBs).

$$\Rightarrow \text{Commitment} = \text{High}$$

### **Step 3: Map to Hersey-Blanchard Quadrant**

| Competence | Commitment | Style | Code |
|------------|------------|-------|------|
| Low | High | **Directing** | **S1 / D1** |
| Low | Low | Coaching | S2 / D2 |
| Moderate–High | Variable | Supporting | S3 / D3 |
| High | High | Delegating | S4 / D4 |

$$\Rightarrow \text{Recommended Style} = \textbf{S1 (Directing)}$$

### **Step 4: Translate Style into Action Verbs**

| **Action Verb** | **S1 Behavior** |
|------------------|------------------|
| **Tell** | "Here is exactly how to run this regression in Minitab." |
| **Structure** | Provide pre-built templates. |
| **Monitor closely** | Daily check-ins. |
| **Encourage** | Acknowledge eagerness. |

### **Step 5: Transition Plan (Time-Series)**

| **Weeks** | **Style Evolution** | **Trigger** |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1–2 | Directing (S1) | Low competence, high commitment |
| 3–4 | Coaching (S2) | Some competence gained, confidence dips |
| 5–8 | Supporting (S3) | Competence adequate, variable confidence |
| 9+ | Delegating (S4) | Fully competent, highly committed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Verdict:** The Black Belt should initially *direct* the Green Belt's tasks explicitly. As the GB gains competence, the BB transitions to *coaching*, then *supporting*, and finally *delegating*.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Analytical Matrix: Tuckman's Stage Diagnosis from Team Behavior Cues

Given a scenario description, the student must identify the current Tuckman stage and prescribe the leader's action.

### **Step 1: Recognize Behavioral Cues**

| **Behavioral Cue Observed** | **Map to Stage** | **Leader's Prescription** |
|----------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| "Everyone is polite, asking 'what should I do?'" | Forming | Direct, give clear goals |
| "Two members arguing over data collection method" | Storming | Facilitate conflict resolution |
| "We've agreed on a meeting cadence" | Norming | Encourage, reinforce norms |
| "Team is delivering sub-projects on time without my input" | Performing | Delegate, monitor only |
| "Project is wrapping up, members discussing next steps" | Adjourning | Recognize, document, celebrate |

### **Step 2: Diagnostic Equation**

$$\text{Stage} = f(\text{Conflict Level}, \text{Cohesion Level}, \text{Autonomy Level})$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forming} &\Rightarrow \text{Low Conflict, Low Cohesion, Low Autonomy} \\
\text{Storming} &\Rightarrow \text{High Conflict, Low Cohesion, Low Autonomy} \\
\text{Norming} &\Rightarrow \text{Low Conflict, High Cohesion, Medium Autonomy} \\
\text{Performing} &\Rightarrow \text{Low Conflict, High Cohesion, High Autonomy} \\
\text{Adjourning} &\Rightarrow \text{Project Closure Phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Rule for KTU:** *High conflict + low cohesion = always Storming.* This single mapping answers ~30% of board questions on team stages.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Vroom's Expectancy Theory Applied to a Six Sigma BB

**Problem Statement:** A Black Belt is offered a **₹50,000 bonus** if she completes the project within **6 months**, reducing defects by **50%**. She believes:
- 80% chance her effort will achieve the defect reduction.
- 70% chance that achieving the defect reduction will earn the bonus.
- She rates the bonus at 0.8 (somewhat valuable, but she'd prefer recognition).

**Compute her motivation.**

### **Step 1: Identify the Three Variables**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expectancy } (E) &= 0.80 \\
\text{Instrumentality } (I) &= 0.70 \\
\text{Valence } (V) &= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 2: Apply the Formula**

$$\text{Motivation} \; (M) \;=\; E \times I \times V$$

### **Step 3: Substitute and Evaluate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= (0.80) \times (0.70) \times (0.80) \\
M &= 0.80 \times 0.56 \\
M &= 0.448
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4: Interpret the Result**

| **M Range** | **Motivation Level** | **Leader's Action** |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| $0.0 - 0.2$ | Very Low | Restructure rewards |
| $0.2 - 0.5$ | Moderate | Boost valence or instrumentality |
| $0.5 - 0.8$ | High | Maintain current state |
| $0.8 - 1.0$ | Very High | Leverage for stretch goals |

$$\Rightarrow M = 0.448 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Moderate Motivation}$$

### **Step 5: Improvement Strategies (KTU-Standard)**

To raise M above 0.5, the leader could:
- Add **recognition** (raise $V$).
- Guarantee **partial bonus** at milestones (raise $I$).
- Provide **Minitab training** (raise $E$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: RACI Construction for a DMAIC Project

**Scenario:** A Six Sigma team is reducing billing errors. Tasks:
1. Define problem (T1)
2. Collect data (T2)
3. Run regression (T3)
4. Implement fix (T4)

| **Task** | **Champion** | **BB** | **GB** | **Process Owner** | **SME (IT)** |
|----------|--------------|--------|--------|-------------------|---------------|
| T1: Define | **A** | **R** | C | C | I |
| T2: Collect | I | **A** | **R** | C | C |
| T3: Regression | I | **A** | **R** | I | C |
| T4: Implement | I | **C** | C | **R** | **A** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Audit Check:** Every row has exactly **one (A)**. ✔️ Valid RACI.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation: Process Yield Improvement Across DMAIC

Given an initial baseline yield $Y_0$ and final yield $Y_f$, calculate the **defect reduction ratio (DRR)** and **rolled throughput yield improvement (ΔRTY)**.

### **Step 1: Define Yields**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_0 &= 0.92 \quad \text{(baseline yield, pre-Six Sigma)} \\
Y_f &= 0.995 \quad \text{(post-DMAIC yield)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 2: Compute Initial DPMO**

Assuming 1 defect opportunity per unit:

$$\text{DPMO}_0 = (1 - Y_0) \times 10^{6} = (0.08) \times 10^{6} = 80{,}000$$

### **Step 3: Compute Final DPMO**

$$\text{DPMO}_f = (1 - Y_f) \times 10^{6} = (0.005) \times 10^{6} = 5{,}000$$

### **Step 4: Compute Defect Reduction Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DRR} &= \dfrac{\text{DPMO}_0 - \text{DPMO}_f}{\text{DPMO}_0} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{80{,}000 - 5{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 93.75 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5: Compute Sigma Level Equivalent**

Using the yield-to-sigma lookup:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_0 = 0.92 &\;\Rightarrow\; \sigma_0 \approx 1.75\sigma \\
Y_f = 0.995 &\;\Rightarrow\; \sigma_f \approx 2.85\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\Delta\sigma = 2.85 - 1.75 = 1.10 \;\text{ sigma improvement}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 93.75% defect reduction sounds impressive, but the sigma improvement is only **1.10σ** — true Six Sigma (3.4 DPMO) requires much more aggressive DMAIC execution.

---

## 3.6 Conflict Resolution Decision Matrix (Thomas-Kilmann)

For each conflict situation, select the most appropriate mode.

| **Mode** | **Assertiveness** | **Cooperativeness** | **When to Use in Six Sigma** |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Competing** | High | Low | Emergency, defend critical data |
| **Collaborating** | High | High | Complex root cause (best for Six Sigma) |
| **Compromising** | Medium | Medium | Time pressure, partial agreement acceptable |
| **Avoiding** | Low | Low | Trivial issue, defer |
| **Accommodating** | Low | High | Preserve team harmony on minor issue |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** *Six Sigma default = Collaborating.* This integrates both logic (assertive) and consensus (cooperative).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define Team Management in the context of Six Sigma. List the three primary roles in a Six Sigma project hierarchy.*

**Model Answer (Board-Standardized):**

**Definition:** Team Management in Six Sigma is the structured process of organizing, leading, and coordinating a cross-functional group of individuals using defined roles, leadership styles, motivation frameworks, and communication protocols to achieve process improvement targets such as **3.4 DPMO**.

**Three Primary Roles:**
1. **Champion** — Senior leader who selects and scopes the project, removes organizational barriers, and ensures strategic alignment.
2. **Black Belt (BB)** — Full-time project leader who applies DMAIC tools, drives statistical analysis, and delivers results.
3. **Green Belt (GB)** — Part-time team member who leads sub-projects, supports data collection, and implements solutions under the BB's guidance.

*(Supporting roles: Master Black Belt, Team Member, Sponsor — may be mentioned for completeness.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*State Tuckman's Five Stages of Team Development. At which stage is conflict maximum, and how should the leader respond?*

**Model Answer:**

Tuckman's Five Stages: **Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning.**

- **Maximum Conflict Stage:** **Storming** — characterized by intra-group friction, frustration, and power struggles.
- **Leader's Response:** **Coaching and facilitating conflict resolution** (not avoiding, not dominating). The leader should mediate disputes, clarify shared goals, and gradually build trust to transition the team into the Norming stage.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **describe Tuckman's stages but forget the leader's action** for each stage — KTU deducts **1–2 marks** for this.
> - In RACI questions, students frequently assign **multiple Accountables (A)** — the rule is **exactly ONE A per task**.
> - Vroom's Expectancy Theory computation: forgetting to multiply all three components ($E \times I \times V$) loses **2 marks**.
> - Always quote **specific theorists** (Tuckman, Belbin, Hersey-Blanchard, Vroom, Herzberg) — unsourced answers get **partial credit only**.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Six Sigma organizational hierarchy**, clearly stating the roles, certification requirements, and time-commitment of each tier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Belbin's Nine Team Roles** with a clear classification into Cerebral, Action, and People clusters. Why is role balance critical for Six Sigma project success? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks)**

The Six Sigma organizational hierarchy is a tiered leadership structure, designed to ensure that projects have both top-down sponsorship and bottom-up execution capability.

| **Tier** | **Role** | **Certification** | **Time Commitment** |
|----------|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Executive/Sponsor** | Six Sigma Awareness Training | ~10% |
| 2 | **Champion** | Champion Workshop | 15–20% |
| 3 | **Master Black Belt (MBB)** | MBB Certified (typically after multiple BB projects) | 100% (full-time coach) |
| 4 | **Black Belt (BB)** | BB Certified (after completing 1–2 projects) | 100% (full-time leader) |
| 5 | **Green Belt (GB)** | GB Certified | 25–50% |
| 6 | **Team Member** | Project-specific training | 10–25% |

**Flow of Authority:** Executive → Champion → MBB → BB → GB → Team Member.

**Key Roles in Detail:**
- **Sponsor/Executive:** Approves project at portfolio level, allocates capital budget, and signs off on completion.
- **Champion:** Acts as the bridge between BB and senior leadership. Selects the project, ensures it aligns with strategy, and removes cross-departmental roadblocks.
- **Master Black Belt:** The internal expert and trainer. Coaches multiple Black Belts statistically, helps choose the right DOE or regression approach, and ensures rigor.
- **Black Belt:** The project engine. Applies the full DMAIC toolkit, manages the team, and is accountable for delivering quantified financial results.
- **Green Belt:** The data collector and pilot implementer. Works part-time on the project while continuing departmental duties.
- **Team Members:** Subject-matter experts (SMEs) from affected departments who provide ground-level data and execute selected tasks.

**Conclusion:** This hierarchy ensures that Six Sigma is not a one-person initiative but a *systematic organizational capability*.

#### **Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks)**

**Belbin's Nine Team Roles** (Belbin, 1981) classify team members based on their behavioral contribution. A balanced Six Sigma team should include representatives from all three clusters.

**Cluster 1 — Cerebral (THINK):**
- **Plant (PL):** The creative source — proposes novel root-cause hypotheses, thinks outside the box.
- **Monitor Evaluator (ME):** The analyst — provides objective, strategic judgment on proposed solutions.
- **Specialist (SP):** The deep expert — supplies specialist technical input (e.g., Minitab expert, Six Sigma statistician).

**Cluster 2 — Action-Oriented (DO):**
- **Shaper (SH):** The driver — challenges the team, pushes for progress, overcomes inertia.
- **Implementer (IM):** The disciplined executor — converts ideas into actionable plans and deadlines.
- **Completer Finisher (CF):** The detail checker — ensures no errors, finishes to closure, polishes outputs.

**Cluster 3 — People-Oriented (FEEL):**
- **Coordinator (CO):** The chair — matures the team, delegates, promotes decision-making.
- **Teamworker (TW):** The harmonizer — smooths friction, listens, builds cohesion (critical in Storming).
- **Resource Investigator (RI):** The explorer — reaches out to external stakeholders, brings in new ideas and resources.

**Why Role Balance is Critical for Six Sigma Success:**
1. **DMAIC requires multiple cognitive modes** — creativity (Plant) for Analyze, discipline (Implementer) for Control, and harmony (Teamworker) for managing the Storming stage.
2. **Without a Shaper**, the team may stagnate in Norming and never reach Performing.
3. **Without a Completer Finisher**, the Control phase suffers from incomplete documentation, leading to regression of gains.
4. **Without a Teamworker**, conflict in Storming escalates and the project collapses.
5. **Imbalanced teams** (e.g., all Plants with no Implementers) generate brilliant ideas but fail to execute, missing the **3.4 DPMO** target.

**Conclusion:** Belbin's role theory provides Six Sigma team leaders with a diagnostic lens to assess team composition and address gaps through targeted recruitment or training.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain **Tuckman's Five-Stage Team Development Model** in detail. For each stage, state the team's psychological state and prescribe the leader's most appropriate action. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Black Belt is offered a **₹75,000 bonus** for completing a Six Sigma project in **5 months** with a **40% defect reduction**. She assesses:
- Probability that her effort achieves the target ($E$) = **0.85**.
- Probability that achieving the target earns the bonus ($I$) = **0.75**.
- Value she places on the bonus ($V$) = **0.6**.

**Compute her motivation using Vroom's Expectancy Theory. What strategies can the Champion adopt to raise her motivation above 0.50?** **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks)**

**Tuckman's Five-Stage Model** (Tuckman, 1965; Adjourning added 1977):

| **Stage** | **Team Psychological State** | **Leader's Action** |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| **1. Forming** | Polite, uncertain, dependent on leader; testing boundaries | **Directing** — provide clear goals, structure, and expectations |
| **2. Storming** | Conflicted, frustrated, resisting authority; power struggles emerge | **Coaching & Facilitating** — mediate conflicts, refocus on shared goals |
| **3. Norming** | Cohesive, trusting, agreement on norms and rules; collaborative | **Supporting** — encourage participation, reinforce positive norms |
| **4. Performing** | Highly productive, autonomous, task-focused; self-managed | **Delegating** — minimal intervention; monitor only key milestones |
| **5. Adjourning** | Reflective on completion; emotional closure | **Recognizing & Documenting** — celebrate, capture learnings, transition ownership |

**Explanation:**

- **Forming:** Team members are polite but unclear on purpose. They ask the leader *"What should I do?"*. The leader must **direct** — give clear instructions, define roles via RACI, and set ground rules.

- **Storming:** Disagreements surface over methodology, data ownership, and priorities. This is the **highest-conflict stage**. The leader must **coach** — facilitate discussions, validate concerns, and prevent escalation.

- **Norming:** Trust builds; members start collaborating. The leader shifts to a **supporting** style — encouraging, asking, listening.

- **Performing:** The team operates almost independently. The leader **delegates** — trusts the team, only intervenes on critical deviations.

- **Adjourning:** Project closure. The leader ensures **recognition**, **documentation**, and **knowledge transfer** to the process owner for sustainability.

**Conclusion:** A skilled Six Sigma leader diagnoses the current stage and matches their style to the team's psychological state. Misalignment (e.g., directing a Performing team) causes demotivation and regression.

#### **Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Variables**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.85 \\
I &= 0.75 \\
V &= 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Vroom's Formula**

$$M = E \times I \times V$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= (0.85) \times (0.75) \times (0.60) \\
M &= 0.85 \times 0.45 \\
M &= 0.3825
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret**

| **M Range** | **Interpretation** |
|-------------|---------------------|
| $0.0 - 0.2$ | Very Low Motivation |
| $0.2 - 0.5$ | **Moderate Motivation** ← Our Result |
| $0.5 - 0.8$ | High Motivation |
| $0.8 - 1.0$ | Very High Motivation |

$$\Rightarrow M = 0.3825 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Moderate Motivation, below the desired 0.50 threshold}$$

**Step 5: Strategies to Raise $M > 0.50$**

To raise $M$, the Champion must increase at least one of $E$, $I$, or $V$:

1. **Raise Expectancy ($E$):**
   - Provide **Minitab training** and statistical mentoring (via MBB).
   - Pair her with an experienced Green Belt.
   - Break the project into smaller, achievable sub-milestones.

2. **Raise Instrumentality ($I$):**
   - Make the **reward structure transparent** — publish the bonus criteria formally.
   - Offer **milestone bonuses** (e.g., 25% on Define-Measure completion) so that partial success is rewarded.
   - Document past cases where bonuses were actually paid.

3. **Raise Valence ($V$):**
   - Augment the monetary bonus with **non-monetary rewards** — public recognition, conference presentation slot, fast-track promotion consideration.
   - Provide **Black Belt certification**, which has long-term career value (raises $V$ intrinsically).

**Mathematical Verification (One Example):**

If $E$ rises to **0.95** (with training), $I$ to **0.85** (with milestone bonus), $V$ remains at **0.60**:

$$M_{\text{new}} = 0.95 \times 0.85 \times 0.60 = 0.4845$$

If $V$ additionally rises to **0.80** (with conference presentation):

$$M_{\text{new}} = 0.95 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 = 0.646$$

$$\Rightarrow M > 0.50 \quad ✔️$$

**Conclusion:** By systematically addressing all three components, the Champion can transform moderate motivation into high motivation, ensuring project success and DPMO reduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — 60-Second Summary**

### **A. Definitions & Frameworks**
- ✅ Team Management = structured orchestration of people, roles, and motivation toward a Six Sigma project goal (3.4 DPMO).
- ✅ Six Sigma org hierarchy: **Executive → Champion → MBB → BB → GB → Team Member**.
- ✅ BB = 100% time; GB = 25–50%; Champion = 15–20%; MBB = 100% (coach role).

### **B. Tuckman's Stages (Memorize Order!)**
- ✅ **F → S → N → P → A** (Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, Adjourning).
- ✅ Maximum conflict = **Storming**.
- ✅ Maximum autonomy = **Performing**.
- ✅ Leader's style transitions from **Directing → Coaching → Supporting → Delegating**.

### **C. Belbin Roles (9 Roles, 3 Clusters)**
- ✅ **Cerebral (Brain):** Plant, Monitor Evaluator, Specialist.
- ✅ **Action (Body):** Shaper, Implementer, Completer Finisher.
- ✅ **People (Heart):** Coordinator, Teamworker, Resource Investigator.
- ✅ Balanced team = all 3 clusters represented.

### **D. Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership**
- ✅ **S1 Directing** = Low Competence + High Commitment.
- ✅ **S2 Coaching** = Low Competence + Low Commitment.
- ✅ **S3 Supporting** = Moderate Competence + Variable Commitment.
- ✅ **S4 Delegating** = High Competence + High Commitment.

### **E. Motivation Theories**
- ✅ **Maslow:** 5-level hierarchy (Physiological → Self-Actualization).
- ✅ **Herzberg:** Hygiene (extrinsic) vs Motivators (intrinsic).
- ✅ **Vroom:** $M = E \times I \times V$ (multiply all three).

### **F. RACI Matrix**
- ✅ **R** = Responsible (does work, multiple allowed).
- ✅ **A** = Accountable (signs off, **EXACTLY ONE** per task).
- ✅ **C** = Consulted (provides input).
- ✅ **I** = Informed (told after).

### **G. Conflict Resolution (Thomas-Kilmann)**
- ✅ Six Sigma default mode = **Collaborating** (high assert + high coop).
- ✅ Emergency = **Competing**; Trivial = **Avoiding**; Harmony = **Accommodating**; Time pressure = **Compromising**.

### **H. Key Formulas to Memorize**
- ✅ $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}$
- ✅ $\text{RTY} = \prod Y_i$ (product of stage yields)
- ✅ $M = E \times I \times V$ (Vroom)
- ✅ $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$
- ✅ $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100$

### **I. Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ❌ Don't list Tuckman stages without leader actions (lose 1–2 marks).
- ❌ Don't assign multiple (A) in RACI.
- ❌ Don't forget to multiply all three Vroom components.
- ❌ Don't confuse Belbin clusters (Cerebral vs Action vs People).
- ❌ Always cite the theorist by name (Tuckman, Belbin, Vroom, etc.).
